actions.thought: UTG in a 6-handed game with Tc5c is a very weak open. Although suited, 10-5 has poor raw equity, weak connectivity, and will be dominated by many hands (Txs, Axs, higher clubs) — plus I’ll be out of position vs the button and blinds. Limping costs 100 into a marginal spot and invites raises/isolations; opening also creates difficult postflop decisions with a weak hand. With a 100BB stack I should wait for a stronger, positionally-suited spot, so I’ll fold and conserve chips for better opportunities.

We’re in middle position with 8♦3♥ offsuit, a dominated, low-connectivity hand that plays poorly multiway and realizes equity badly out of position versus later positions. With no dead money beyond the blinds and a full 100bb stack depth, there’s no compelling reason to limp or open; folding preserves stack and avoids tough postflop spots.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-King suited, a premium hand. Since the action has folded to me, I will make a standard opening raise of 3 times the big blind to build the pot and take initiative in the hand.

89o on the button vs a CO raise. Decent connected hand with position. Too weak to 3-bet for value, folding is too tight with this playability and position. Calling to see a flop in position is the standard play here.

Qd Jd is a premium suited Broadway hand with excellent playability, straight and flush potential. Pot odds are outstanding: 250 to call into 750 (3:1), requiring only ~25% equity, which we have plenty against a likely wide CO opening range and speculative BTN call. SB defense calls for continuing with this hand. Postflop, we can realize equity OOP multiway if BB calls, with deep stacks providing implied odds for draws. 3-betting risks a bloated pot OOP against two opponents who may continue wide, and folding is far too tight on hand 1 with no reads. Calling keeps the pot manageable while keeping opponents' ranges wide.

I hold a pocket pair of fours (4s4h) in the Big Blind. There has been a raise to 300 by the Cutoff, followed by calls from the Button and Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 1000, offering me excellent pot odds of 5:1. With three other players in the hand, the implied odds for 'set mining' (trying to hit three-of-a-kind on the flop) are high. My hand plays poorly post-flop without improvement, so my plan is to call to see the flop cheaply. If I do not hit a set, I will likely fold to any significant aggression; if I do hit, I stand to win a large pot.

Preflop call with QdJd was standard for pot odds and playability in SB vs CO open and BTN flat multiway deep. Flop Ah6d2s is ace-high and dry, missing us entirely—no pair, no straight draw (gutshot at best to 5-T, unlikely), backdoor flush draw at most. Facing CO raiser, BTN caller, and BB multiway OOP first to act, queen-high has minimal showdown value and poor playability. No reason to bet/raise as bluff (dry board favors value from Ax/broadway in villain ranges, multiway reduces bluff efficacy). Check to realize equity if checked behind or control pot; prepared to fold to any bet as equity too low (~10-20% vs typical ranges). Early in tournament, no need for heroics without reads.

I hold pocket fours on an A-6-2 rainbow board. I missed my set, which was the primary goal of calling pre-flop. The Ace on the board hits the ranges of the pre-flop raiser (Flash) and the callers significantly harder than mine. Being out of position with a weak underpair in a multi-way pot (4 players), betting achieves nothing as I unlikely to fold out better hands and I am drawing very thin against any Ace. My best course of action is to check. If faced with a bet, I will likely fold unless the sizing is extremely small, as my hand has very little showdown value.

I have top pair with the best kicker (Ace-King) on a very dry board (A-6-2 rainbow). After the blinds checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, I should place a continuation bet to extract value from weaker Aces and other hands that might call. A bet of approximately 40% of the pot (500 into 1200) is effective here to keep weaker hands interested while maintaining control of the hand.

Completely missed this flop with 89o - no pair, no real draws besides a weak backdoor straight. Flash's c-bet on an Ace-high board is standard and likely has Ax or better. Two players behind me who could still wake up with hands. No reason to call 500 with no equity here. Clean fold.

Flop Ah6d2s completely misses QdJd: no pair, no straight draw (at best gutshot unlikely), only backdoor diamond flush and spade draw. Preflop calling range was wide multiway deepstacked, but Flash's CO preflop raise + flop bet into checked pot represents strong value-heavy range: primarily Ax (Aces dominate us), sets unlikely, some broadway bluffs possible but board texture favors value (ace-high dry rainbow). Equity realization OOP vs Flash: roughly 15-20% max against polarized range (worse if value-dense). Pot odds: 500 into 2200 (~4.4:1, need ~18% equity), marginal at best and denied by poor playability/showdown value post-turn OOP. Pro checked BB, could have anything weak but no reason to inflate pot with marginal hand. Claude folded correctly. Early tournament, no reads/history, preserve stack for better spots; folding queen-high is standard discipline here. Cannot raise as pure bluff (villain range capped below top pair, multiway history). Check-fold strategy as pre-planned.

I hold a pair of Fours on an A-6-2 rainbow board. The pre-flop raiser (Flash) has continuation bet 500 into a multi-way pot after two checks. This board texture heavily favors the aggressor's range (Ax hands). My set mining plan failed, and I am currently beating almost nothing except total air, while being out of position and drawing to only 2 outs. Pot odds of 3.4:1 are insufficient to justify chasing a 2-outer or floating out of position with an underpair. The disciplined play is to fold.

UTG 6-max with 3d2s offsuit is the weakest class of hands: poor high-card strength, dominated when it makes a pair, and little playability without position. Limping invites multiway trouble and raising is pure spew this early with deep stacks. Best EV is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

Holding Ace-Deuce offsuit in middle position. This hand is quite weak and often dominated by other Ace-X hands. In a 6-max game, raising with A2o from this position is generally too loose, and calling is even worse. Standard professional strategy dictates a fold here to avoid difficult post-flop situations with a weak kicker.

KQs is a premium hand in the cutoff with two folds in front. This is a clear raise spot for value. I'll open to 250 (2.5x) as a standard raise size to build the pot while still giving myself good fold equity.

Q7o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit with no strong potential. Facing a cutoff raise to 2.5x from Claude, who showed aggression last hand by raising and c-betting flop. On the button with deep stacks (97BB effective), calling is loose due to poor playability and frequent domination by better Qx or broadways in his range. 3-betting is not justified as it's outside a balanced bluff range. Folding preserves stack early in tournament (hand 2/150) for better spots. Standard discipline maximizes EV long-term.

I hold Td 5c in the Small Blind. Facing a raise to 250 from the Cutoff (Claude), my hand is significantly below the strength required to defend, especially considering I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. While the Cutoff's range is wide, T5 offsuit is trash with poor playability and equity. There is no merit in calling or 3-betting here. The correct play is to fold.

Hand is J7 offsuit — weak, unsuited, and poor postflop playability out of position. Claude opened to 250 from the cutoff; the pot is 400 and I need to call 150 more (total committed 250). Those odds (~2.7:1, ~27% equity required) justify a cheap defend against a likely wide cutoff opening range. I don't want to 3-bet with this rag (too thin for a value or reliable bluff 3-bet deep-stacked early in the tourney), and folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call to see a flop and only continue with a pair or a strong draw; fold to significant aggression on dry flops to preserve my ~99bb stack.

I have J♣7♠ (Jh7s) on K♥7♥3♠ — middle pair with a decent Jack kicker but out of position to the preflop raiser (Claude). This hand fits my preflop plan: continue with a pair but avoid bloating the pot OOP. Checking preserves pot control, lets Claude c-bet with a wide range (and we can call to realize equity against many overcards/bluffs), and avoids building a big pot when we’re likely behind to Kx/sets/overpairs. If Claude fires a small-to-medium c-bet I’ll call to see the turn; I’ll fold to large bets or any raise that strongly polarizes to value. For now: check.

I flopped top pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on K73 board. Minni called from BB and checked to me. This is a textbook c-bet spot for value and protection against the heart flush draw. Betting around 60% pot to extract value from worse kings, sevens, and draws.

I have Jh7s on K♥7♥3♠ — middle pair with a decent kicker but out of position to the cutoff. Claude opened a wide cutoff range preflop and his 350 c-bet into the 550 pot (900 total) is a medium-sized continuation bet. Calling 350 to win 900 requires roughly ~28% equity; against a wide C/O c-bet range my pair has enough showdown value to justify the price. Raising OOP with J7 is too thin — I’ll either get called by better Kx/sets or face a shove/raise that I can’t comfortably continue against. Plan: call to keep the pot controlled and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to significant turn aggression that polarizes to value, continue if I improve (two pair/full house) or if villain shows weakness.

I have J♥7♠ — second pair (sevens) with a decent Jack kicker but I’m out of position to Claude (cutoff). The turn (5♥) brings a third heart to the board, creating a real flush threat vs. the wide cutoff range and also leaves many Kx/sets/overpairs that dominate me. There’s no bet to face right now, so I’ll check to keep the pot small and allow Claude to barrel with a wide range; I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a vulnerable one-pair hand. Plan: check and call a small-to-medium turn bet (realizing showdown value), but fold to heavy raises or large polarized action that strongly indicate made flushes/sets/overpairs. Betting or raising here would be too thin and will often get me into uncomfortable spots.

Third heart hit on turn completing flush draws. Minni has been check-calling which is consistent with a drawing hand that may have just got there. I have top pair good kicker but no heart. Checking back for pot control - if Minni has a flush they'll likely bet river, if they have a weaker King or pair I can potentially value bet a safe river.

Board: 7♥ K♥ 3♠ 5♥ 3♣. I hold J♥7♠ — that’s two pair (7s and 3s) after the river pairing the treys. Claude (cutoff) opened preflop and made a big flop c-bet, then checked the turn (and checked back the river), which looks like a wide/blocked range: Kx, single-pair K or missed heart draws, some 7x/3x slowplays, and occasional stronger full-house/quad combos. My two pair now beats most Kx and missed draws and gets called by worse hands, but it does lose to full houses/quads (77, 33, KK, 55, etc.). Pot is 1,250 and my stack is deep enough to apply pressure, but I want to avoid bloating the pot with a vulnerable hand. I’ll make a small value bet (~1/3 pot = 400) to extract from worse pairs and missed bluffs while keeping the sizing small enough to fold to significant raises (which would indicate a full house/quad). If Claude raises large, I’ll respect the polarization and fold; if he calls or folds, proceed accordingly to showdown or collect the small pot.

Minni's small river bet (400 into 1650, ~24% pot) after check-calling flop and checking turn is interesting. This sizing often indicates a thin value bet or blocking bet. With the flush completing on turn and board pairing on river, a strong hand (flush, trips) would likely bet bigger. My KQ is top pair good kicker - I beat weaker kings, sevens, and bluffs. Getting 5:1 pot odds, this is a clear call.

I am Under the Gun (UTG) with 5h 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Given the poor positional disadvantage and the low strength of my hand, folding is the standard and correct play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.
AKo is a premium hand in middle position. With one fold ahead, I should raise to build the pot and thin the field. Standard raise to 250-300 is appropriate.

Preflop in cutoff facing a raise to 275 (2.75x) from Claude in middle position. Pot is 425, calling requires 275 for pot odds of about 1.55:1. My hand 7d5s is offsuit, unconnected, and ranks among the bottom 30% of hands with very poor playability postflop. No strong blockers or suited connectors to justify a call or 3-bet bluff. Deep stacks (~100BB) and early in tournament (hand 3/150) favor folding weak hands to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Claude's range is strong, and I have position but not enough to speculate with trash.

I am on the Button with ATo facing an open raise to 2.75BB from the Middle Position (Claude). While I have position, ATo is easily dominated by the opening range of a competent player in Middle Position (AK, AQ, AJ, TT+). Flat calling invites the blinds to enter, creating a multi-way pot where offsuit broadways play poorly. 3-betting turns my hand into a bluff that has to fold to a 4-bet. Given it is early in the tournament (Hand 3), preserving chips and avoiding reverse implied odds situations is prudent. The most disciplined play against an MP open is to fold.

I’m in the small blind with 8♣4♣ facing Claude’s MP open to 275. Calling 225 more into a 425 pot would leave me needing ~34.6% equity (225/(425+225)) to break even, and 8-4 suited has poor raw equity and is frequently dominated by a raiser’s range. I’d be out of position postflop and have weak playability — a min 3-bet bluff to 450 is possible but thin and likely to either get called by better hands or fold equity stripped by a cold 4-bet. With deep stacks but no compelling reason to contest this open with a weak, easily dominated holding, I’ll conserve chips and fold.

Claude’s MP open to 2.75bb is a fairly strong range. Q2o is heavily dominated by all better Qx and most Ax/Kx holdings, and it plays very poorly postflop even getting a decent price. With no suitedness and little playability, defending here will bleed chips over time. I’ll take the disciplined fold and wait for better spots.
KQo is a strong hand in UTG. Standard open raise to 250-275 is appropriate to build the pot with this premium holding while maintaining fold equity against weaker hands.

KTo offsuit is a weak hand facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has shown strength by raising UTG in previous hands (Hand 3) and getting folds. UTG ranges are strong: high pairs, big aces, broadways. KTo has poor playability postflop, little fold equity if raising, and bad implied odds. Deep stacks don't help this marginal hand. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold 7c Tc in the Cutoff. Claude has opened from UTG for 2.5bb. While Claude has been active (raising 3 hands in a row), an UTG range is typically strong, and T7s is easily dominated and plays poorly against that range. Calling invites squeezes from the Button or Blinds, and 3-betting is too loose here. Folding is the standard disciplined play.

I'm on the button with Qh9c — a marginal but playable hand in position. Claude opened UTG to 250; versus an UTG range Q9o is often dominated, so a 3-bet would be too light and risks bloating the pot out of position. I prefer to call 250 to use my positional advantage and see a flop cheaply; if the blinds fold I'll play heads-up postflop, and if I face a 4-bet I'll fold. Plan: float and evaluate the flop — continue aggressively with top pair or strong draws, fold to sustained heavy aggression if I miss.

UTG opened and the Button flatted, giving both players strong/condensed ranges. J6o is dominated frequently, plays poorly multiway, and we’ll be out of position postflop with the BB still to act. Squeezing here is too ambitious with this hand and stack depths; calling creates a low-EV spot. Best is to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad 3c. Claude raised to 250 from UTG, and Minni called from the Button. The pot is currently 650, and it costs me 150 to call. This gives me pot odds of roughly 4.3:1. While A3o is a weak hand and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the price is good enough to see a flop. I must play cautiously post-flop, as I am likely dominated if an Ace hits, and I'm facing a strong range from an UTG raiser.

I have completely missed the flop with Ace-high and no real draws on a board that heavily favors my opponents' ranges (broadway cards and heart draws). With two opponents still in the hand, including the UTG raiser, the best play is to check and fold to any bet.

I flopped top pair with a good kicker (KQ on K-J-9 board). This is a strong hand but the board is coordinated with heart flush draws and straight draws possible. Flash checked, and I'm in position against two opponents. I should bet for value and protection - around 450-500 (55-60% pot) is appropriate to charge draws and extract value from worse kings and Jx.

Preflop I called Claude’s UTG open to float and evaluate. On J♥ 9♥ K♣ I have middle pair (9♣) with a Q♥ kicker and a very small backdoor heart possibility. Claude opened UTG so his range is fairly strong (Kx, Jx, overpairs, AK/AQ), but his bet size here is not polarizing and could include c-bets/bluffs. I’m on the button (in position) so the +EV, low-variance line is to call: keep worse hands and bluffs in, control the pot size, and re-evaluate on the turn. Folding is too weak with a made pair and the price is reasonable; raising would bloat the pot and commit me with a vulnerable hand that is often behind. Plan: call the 450, see the turn, and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy continued aggression if I don’t improve, or extract/raise if I catch a good card (9/Q/heart) or get a favorable action.

I have completely missed the flop with Ad 3c on a board of Jh 9h Kc. Both Claude (UTG) and Minni (Button) have shown interest in the pot, with Claude betting and Minni calling. My Ace-high has very little equity here, as this board hits their pre-flop ranges hard. I have no draws and would likely be behind even if an Ace fell. Folding is the only sensible move.

Turn brought 8c, completing QT straight and adding club flush draw to existing heart draw. I still have top pair good kicker. The board is getting dangerous but checking allows all draws to see a free river. I should bet for value against worse Kx, Jx hands and to charge the many draws. Sizing around 65% pot (1100) is appropriate - large enough to deny proper odds to draws while extracting value.

I fold. Summary: I have Qh9c — second pair (9s) with a Queen kicker, but my backdoor heart draw is gone after the 8c turn. Claude (UTG) has shown a strong, consistent line: open UTG, c-bet the flop, and now a sizable turn raise to 1100 into the pot (pre-turn pot ~1700; current pot 2800). That sizing and sequence are strongly weighted to value (Kx, Jx, two pair, sets) from an UTG opener. To call 1100 I need roughly 28% equity (1100 into a 3900 pot), but my realistic equity vs his value-heavy range is far lower. I have only two clean outs to make trips (the two remaining 9s) and three queens that often won’t be good vs Kx or two-pair hands. Raising is too thin and would bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand out of line. Preserving my 82bb stack for better spots is the +EV play here, so I fold.

UTG position in a 6-handed game requires a tight opening range. K9o is offsuit, dominated by better kings (KQ, KJ, KA), and plays poorly post-flop from early position. No prior action, blinds posted (pot 150, to call BB 100). Folding preserves stack for premium spots early in the tournament with deep stacks (~100BB). Standard GTO play is to fold this hand UTG.

I am in Middle Position with 9d 5d. While suited, this is a three-gapper and a very weak hand to open with from early/middle position given that there are four players left to act behind me. The risk of being dominated or 3-bet is high, and playing this out of position is unprofitable. With a stack of nearly 100BB, there is no pressure to play marginal hands. The standard play is to fold.

6♦3♣ is one of the weakest possible hands — unsuited, very disconnected and easily dominated. I’m in the cutoff with three players still to act behind (Button, SB, BB), so I’ll be out of position and likely face a defend or 3‑bet. There’s no reason to invest 100 chips with such poor playability; limp/calling is speculative but this hand has almost no post‑flop equity, and raising as a steal with these cards is unnecessarily risky. I’ll conserve my stack and fold.

Folds to us on the Button with K8o. This is a profitable steal spot versus blinds; we have decent high-card value and position, and we don’t want to limp and invite the blinds to realize equity cheaply. A standard small open keeps risk controlled while applying pressure and building initiative for postflop c-bets.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (Td 2s), which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo has raised from the Button. While button opens are typically wide, T2o is not strong enough to defend, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand and with an active Big Blind (Claude) still to act behind me. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have AcTc in the BB facing a button open of 250. This is a strong suited ace that plays well both as a 3-bet or a call. Against a button steal attempt, ATs is firmly in my 3-bet range. I'll 3-bet to around 700 to put pressure and potentially take it down preflop or build a pot with position-advantage equity.

We opened K8o as a standard button steal, but versus Claude’s 3-bet from the big blind this hand becomes a poor continue. K8o is dominated by a large part of his value/continue range (AK/KQ/KJ, better kings, big pairs), and calling creates reverse implied odds even with position. A 4-bet bluff is also too expensive at these stacks versus an aggressive opponent who can continue or shove. Best EV is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I am holding 5c 2s in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em (low, unsuited, unconnected). Being in early position requires a strong range to enter the pot, as I have to act first and will be out of position against most callers. With 50/100 blinds and a solid stack of 9650, there is absolutely no incentive to get involved with this trash hand. The standard play is to fold immediately.

I have 8♥6♣ in middle position with ~82.5bb. There’s been no raise yet (pot 150, to call 100, min-raise 200), but this is a weak, unsuited, gap hand with poor playability out of position and several players left to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB). Limping invites multi-way pots where 8-6o has low equity and raising from MP will likely run into a 3-bet from the aggressive raisers at this table (Claude has shown frequent strong raises). Pot odds for a call (~40% break-even) don’t justify playing this hand OOP. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to play, I’ll conserve chips and fold preflop.

Jh2d is too weak to open from the cutoff against a competent/active button and strong SB; it has poor playability, is often dominated when it flops a jack, and doesn’t realize equity well when called. With no dead money beyond blinds and plenty of better steal candidates, folding preserves EV and avoids tough 3-bet/defend spots.

I am on the button with 89o. While this is a marginal opening hand in many situations, the Small Blind (Claude) has shown significant aggression in almost every hand played so far, including frequent raises and 3-bets. Opening with a weak offsuit connector like 89o into an aggressive chip leader who has the positional advantage post-flop (relative to the Big Blind) and a high propensity to 3-bet is risky. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand to challenge Claude's aggression.
Folded to me in the SB with QJs - a strong hand heads-up. I should raise to put pressure on the BB. Standard raise to 3x BB.

T4o is a complete trash hand with no high card strength, suitedness, or playability. Facing a standard raise to 300 from the SB after multiple folds, Claude's range is wide but still favors folding this unprofitable hand. No position postflop, deep stacks, early in tournament—preserve chips for better spots. Standard fold.

Fold. Jc9h is a marginal offsuit hand and I'm UTG in a short-handed game — too weak to open from early position. Several aggressive players remain to act (Claude on the button has been opening frequently and others will squeeze), so raising invites 3-bets and calling leaves me out of position in multiway pots where my hand is often dominated. Limping is passive and creates tough decisions postflop. With a healthy ~82BB stack I’ll preserve chips for a better spot later.

Folded to me in middle position with only blinds in the pot. As4o is not a premium, but it has an Ace blocker (reduces frequency of AA/AK/AQ in the blinds) and can win the pot preflop a high percentage of the time. I prefer raising over limping to maximize fold equity and keep initiative; if I face a sizeable 3-bet from the players behind, I can comfortably fold and avoid dominated Ace-high spots.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Ten offsuit. FiveTwo, opening from middle position, raised to 250. While QTo is a decent hand, it is marginal against an opening range from middle position, especially with Claude (who has been very aggressive) still to act on the button. Calling here risks being squeezed by the button or blinds, and QTo does not play particularly well post-flop when dominated. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

T3 suited is a weak hand even on the button. While I have position, calling a raise with this hand is marginal - the 3 doesn't connect well and I'll often be dominated. Not a good 3-bet bluff candidate either. Best to fold and wait for better spots.

Facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in middle position, pot is 400, and I need to call 200 more in the small blind (total 250 from me). My T8d is suited connectors with good playability and implied odds on deep stacks (95BB+). Equity against an MP opening range is around 38-40%, and pot odds are favorable at roughly 3:1 after calling (plus implied). BB may defend or fold, but OOP multiway potential is playable with flush/straight draws. Folding is too tight early in tournament; raising is overplaying. Standard defend here for value realization.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 250 from Middle Position and a call from the Small Blind. The pot odds are favorable (getting 5:1 on a call), but my hand (Jd 2c) is extremely weak, unsuited, and unconnected. Even closing the action, J2o has very poor playability and equity realization out of position against two opponents with stronger ranges. Folding is the disciplined decision to preserve chips.

Flop of 6h Jh 5d completely misses my Td8d. No pair, no strong straight draw (minor gutshot possibilities like hitting a 7 or 9 for long-shot straights), backdoor diamond flush draw, and ten overcard for weak showdown value. Heads-up OOP against FiveTwo's MP raising range (which likely includes pairs, broadway, suited aces/connectors that connect better here), my equity is low ~25-35% tops. Pot is small (600, ~6BB), deep stacks (~94BB effective), early in tournament. Best play is check to realize fold equity: opponent may check back weak hands or air, giving free card; if bets (likely c-bet), easy fold with no fold equity post-flop OOP. Donk betting has no value and poor bluff value OOP; check-raise bluff too thin early without reads. Prioritize survival and multi-street playability.

Heads-up in position after opening preflop, I can apply pressure with a small range c-bet. This flop (6h Jh 5d) connects with a chunk of SB’s calling range, so I don’t want to go big, but a ~1/3 pot bet should fold out a lot of his air/overcards (KQ/QT/AT without a pair) and deny equity. With As4h I also retain some backdoor potential (heart turns, A/4 turns to improve) while keeping the pot manageable if called.

Flop completely misses Td8d: no pair, only backdoor diamond flush draw and weak gutshot to 7 (5-6-7-8 straight), with overcard ten for minimal showdown value. Facing FiveTwo's 200 bet into 600 (pot now 800) after my check, this is a standard continuation bet from their MP opening range, which hits this connected 6h-Jh-5d board better (overpairs, sets, two-pair combos like 65/J6/56/J5, broadway draws, heart flush draws). My equity is ~25-30% at best heads-up OOP, but pot odds of 4:1 aren't sufficient given reverse implied odds and poor playability multi-street (easy to get outdrawn or face further aggression). Deep stacks (~94BB) don't help without equity. Calling bloats pot OOP with air; raising has no fold equity as bluffs look strong and get snapped by value. Consistent with pre-flop plan: check-fold to c-bet. Preserve stack early in tournament (hand 7/150).

We’re UTG 6-handed with 84o, a hand that plays poorly out of position (dominated when we make weak pairs, limited straight potential, no suited equity). With several aggressive opponents behind, opening or limping just invites isolation and tough postflop spots for minimal upside. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better UTG opens.

I am in middle position with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a six-max tournament, A7o is generally too weak to open from this position, especially with an aggressive player like Claude still to act behind me in the Cutoff. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid being out of position with a marginal hand.

76o is a weak hand - not suited and only connected. While I'm in late position with two folds ahead, this hand doesn't have good playability postflop. Better to wait for stronger spots rather than open with this marginal holding.

Preflop on the button with QcTc suited, a strong speculative hand with good playability, straight and flush potential. Three folds ahead (UTG, MP, CO), so position on both blinds with initiative. Standard play is to raise for value, isolation against blinds, and to thin the field. Deep stacks allow for postflop play. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is a balanced size based on observed opening ranges in prior hands (250-300 common). Folding or limping is too passive; calling BB risks multiway pot OOP for blinds.

Facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button while in the Small Blind. My hand, J9s, has some playability but suffers significantly from being out of position (OOP) without the betting lead. The pot odds to call are approximately 36% (calling 250 to win a pot that will be around 700), but realizing that equity OOP is difficult, likely dragging my realized equity below the required threshold. While a 3-bet is an option to isolate, against a 3x open it requires risking a significant portion of my stack (~11-12%). A disciplined fold is the most +EV decision here to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.

Elon opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the big blind with A♣6♠. With ~81 BB effective this is a clear defend — A6o has decent equity vs a button opening range and the Ace blocker adds value. Calling costs 200 into a 450 pot (200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8% pot odds), which is reasonable to see a flop. I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a thin 3-bet; I’ll call, see the flop, and proceed only with top pair or strong draws while exercising pot control on dry boards. If faced with a large 3-bet preflop I will fold.

I have A♣6♠ on 4♦6♣7♥ — middle pair with the best possible kicker vs many hands. I’m out of position to the BTN and the board is fairly connected (5/8 straight outs, lots of 7x/6x in a BTN opening range). Preflop I called intending to exercise pot control on non-threatening flops, and this is exactly that spot: betting OOP with a marginal made hand would bloat the pot and put me in tough decisions vs ranges that dominate me or have strong draws. I’ll check to control the pot, induce bluffs, and call reasonable continuation bets to realize equity. If Elon checks back I’ll take a free turn; if he c-bets small/medium I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn; if he applies large sizing or raises and I haven’t improved I’ll fold to preserve ~79–80 BB.

On the flop of 4d-6c-7h, as the preflop raiser from the button with position on the big blind who checked. My QcTc is Queen-high with two overcards, a weak club flush draw (three to flush with 6c on board), and backdoor straight potential (e.g., 58 or 89). No made hand, but excellent playability deepstacked. Board is low and coordinated (straight draws like 35/53/58/85 possible), somewhat drawy but rainbow-ish (d,c,h suits), favoring my button raising range which includes Ax, broadway pairs, suited connectors that connect better (e.g., 56,67,45,78). BB defended pre OOP likely wide (suited connectors, Ax, small pairs), but check indicates weakness. High fold equity; c-bet to deny equity, take pot immediately (~45% pot size standard for polarized range), or get called by worse (weaker Ax, gutshots) while building pot for turns that improve me. Checking back risks giving free card to draws that beat me. Tournament stage early (hand 8/150), stacks deep (90bb+), value bet sizing 300 fits observed pot sizes and prior actions. Plan to cbet wide here for initiative.

I have A♣6♠ — middle pair with the best possible kicker on 4♦6♣7♥. Elon opened from the button and made a small/standard C‑bet (300 into a 950 pot). This is a classic defend spot: calling keeps the pot controlled out of position, realizes equity against a wide BTN C‑betting range (many bluffs, weaker pairs and draws), and the Ace blocker reduces some premium A‑X combos he might have. A 300 call into the current pot is inexpensive relative to the equity I have and avoids committing more with a marginal made hand. Plan: call to see the turn; if I improve (two‑pair/top pair) or he checks I can proceed aggressively, but if he barrels large on a blank turn and I haven’t improved I will fold.

I have A♣6♠ — middle pair with the best possible kicker on 4♦-6♣-7♥-2♠. Elon was the preflop raiser and c-bet the flop; he now checked the turn so there’s no bet to call. Betting OOP with a marginal made hand here would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots against hands that dominate me (7x, better 6x) or that will raise for value. The turn 2 is largely a blank for my hand (it only helps a few very specific combos) and checking keeps the pot under control while allowing bluffs to show themselves on the river. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the river — call small river bets with showdown-value hands or fold to large aggression if I haven’t improved.

Turn board 4d-6c-7h-2s brings a low spade that doesn't complete obvious draws against my range but keeps the board coordinated with possible gutshots and wheel potential. Minni's OOP check-call pre and flop suggests a medium-strength hand like a pocket pair (55-99), weak Ax, or stubborn draws, but her turn check indicates no strong hand or missed draw, giving me high fold equity. My QcTc has a strong club flush draw (9 outs, ~19% equity standalone), Queen/Ten overcards for 3-4 additional outs, and backdoor straight potential, totaling solid equity (~25-30% vs villain range). As preflop raiser with position, I represent top pair+, sets, straights (58, 35 wheel-ish), and continue betting my polarized range to deny equity to draws/pairs that beat me sometimes while value-owning worse Ax/draws. Checking back risks villain improving or free showdown, suboptimal with position and initiative. Pot 1250, bet ~55% pot (700) balances value from worse and protection, observed sizing fits prior actions (300-1100 range), deep stacks allow multi-street planning (barrel river if check-raise or call). Early tournament, building stack by exploiting passive BB defense.

I have A♣6♠ — middle pair (6s) with the best possible kicker vs a lot of the BTN’s range. Elon opened BTN, c‑bet the flop and now bets the turn 700 into a 1250 pot (current pot 1950). Calling 700 requires ~26.4% equity (700 / (1950+700) ≈ 26.4%), which is a low threshold and well within the equity A6o has versus a wide BTN continuation/barrel range that includes many Ax, Kx and medium pairs or bluffs. I’m out of position, so raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and often get me into dominated or uncomfortable spots versus value hands (7x, 8x, better pairs). Folding would give up too much equity for poor pot odds. Plan: call the 700 to control the pot and see the river; if he checks the river I can take a free card or bet for thin value; if he makes a small/medium river stab I’ll call for showdown in many cases, but I will fold to a large shove on a blank river unless I improve.

I hold A♣6♠ — middle pair (6s) with the best possible kicker. The pot is 2650 and Elon (BTN) was the preflop raiser and barreled flop and turn but apparently checked the river. That passive river action signals either weakness or pot control rather than a locked-in strong Q/7/6 two‑pair/set. With a marginal made hand I want thin value without bloating the pot. A small bet (~1/3 pot) targets worse Ax/Kx/Jx and failed draws that will call, while folding out pure air and denying a free showdown. I avoid checking (which gives up value) and avoid a large bet/overcommitment that would force tough calls against Qx/7x. I will make a modest value bet of 900 chips. If Elon raises big (a shove or large raise), I will fold; if he makes a small raise I’ll re-evaluate but will be inclined to fold to heavy aggression with only middle pair.

River Queen pairs my QcTc for top pair with Ten kicker, a solid but vulnerable holding on this 2-4-6-7-Q board with possible straights (3-5 for 3-4-5-6-7, 5-8 for 4-5-6-7-8) and two-pair combos (67, Q6, Q7, Q4, Q2, 46, etc.). Minni's line is capping strength: OOP limp-call vs button raise (calls wide pre), check-call flop 300 (~50% pot on drawy 4-6-7), check-call large turn 700 (~55% pot into coordinated board post-2s), now leading river 900 (~34% into 2650). This is a strong value lead, unlikely bluffs (OOP called large turn without improving flush/straight likely), favors monsters like 67 two pair, sets (22/44/66/77), straights (35/58), or slowplayed Qx/trips. No flush hit. Pot odds 900-to-4450 (~20%) require 18.5% equity, but vs her capped strong range, TPQK has ~10-15% (loses to all made value, few outs). Deep stacks (80BB), early (hand 8/150), preserve stack; fold preserves image for future aggression. Prior thoughts noted high equity turn but river missed draw, hit only medium pair.

Holding a pocket pair (5h, 5d) under-the-gun in a 6-max game is a standard opening range hand. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take the initiative, potentially thin the field, and set up a profitable situation if I flop a set. While small pairs can be tricky out of position, folding is too conservative and limping is generally suboptimal in this structure.
T6 suited is too weak to call a UTG raise from middle position. Poor hand that doesn't play well postflop, and there are still players behind who could squeeze. Easy fold.

T5o is a speculative offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raiser's range. Flash has shown aggression from early position in prior hands (e.g., Hand 1, Hand 4), suggesting a strong range here. In cutoff with deep stacks (80BB), calling or raising risks committing chips to a dominated spot multiway potentially. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities early in the tournament (hand 9/150).

I am on the Button with Kc 6h. The UTG player (Flash) has opened to 300 (3 BB). An opening raise from UTG typically indicates a strong range (top 15-20% of hands), heavily dominating my K6o. K6o has very poor playability and equity against a strong range. Calling is not profitable due to reverse implied odds and lack of equity realization. 3-betting as a bluff is ill-advised against a UTG open without better blockers or playability. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I have A♦Q♦ in the small blind facing a 300 UTG open. With ~95 BB I have a strong, suited broadway that plays well both preflop and postflop. Out of position vs an opener I prefer to 3-bet for value and to isolate rather than limp/call into multiway pots. A 3-bet to 900 (a standard ~3x sizing vs a 300 open) is large enough to fold out many worse hands and discourage multiway flops, but keeps worse Ax and broadway hands in. If I get a cold 4-bet from UTG I will re-evaluate (likely fold to a big, committed 4-bet); if called I’ll C-bet/plan postflop as the aggressor. Calling 250 is too passive here and folding is overly tight — raise to take the initiative.

We’re in the BB with 9h8h facing an UTG open (300) and an SB 3-bet to 900. 98s is too playable to just fold getting a reasonable price, especially with good implied odds when we flop strong draws/two pair/straights. A 4-bet bluff from out of position versus a UTG range + SB 3-bet range is spewy, and folding would be over-tight given stack depth (~90–100bb). Calling keeps in dominated hands, realizes our suited connector equity, and avoids bloating the pot without initiative OOP.

I opened UTG with 55 and faced a 3-bet from the SB and a cold-call from the BB. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds to call 600 more into a 2100 pot. With the effective stacks around 90BB, the implied odds for set-mining are excellent. Additionally, as the UTG player against the SB and BB, I will have the advantage of position throughout the rest of the hand. Folding would be too conservative given the price and position, and 4-betting is unnecessary with a small pair. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here.

I 3‑betted preflop and the Js Ac 4h flop gives me top pair with the best kicker (A♦Q♦) in a three‑way pot. As preflop aggressor I should continue for value and to charge worse Axs, Jx and broadway hands, but the pot is multiway and the board is relatively dry so I want a modest size to keep worse hands in. I’ll c‑bet ~1/3 pot to get value while avoiding committing too much OOP — 900 into 2700. If I face a large raise I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a strong check‑raise representing two pair/set); if called I’ll plan my turn line based on the card and opponents’ actions.

We’re in a 3-bet pot, multiway, out of position versus the SB 3-bettor and an UTG caller. On A♣ J♠ 4♥ our 9♥8♥ has essentially no made hand and only weak backdoor equity (backdoor flush/runner-runner straights), and pairing a 9 or 8 is rarely good versus these strong ranges. Even though the 900 bet is small into 3600, calling is likely to face more pressure on later streets with poor realization. Raising as a bluff multiway is also high-risk and low-fold-equity. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

I entered this pot pre-flop with 55 primarily for set-mining value. The flop of Js-Ac-4h is highly unfavorable for my hand, as it features two overcards that align perfectly with Minni's Small Blind 3-betting range (which heavily includes hands like AK, AQ, AJ, and high pocket pairs). Minni has fired a continuation bet into two players, which typically shows strength on an Ace-high board. Given that I have only two outs to improve and no backdoor draws, calling the 900 chips is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
9s4d is a very weak offsuit hand in UTG position - the worst position at the table. No playability, no high card value. Easy fold.

5c9d is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards with poor playability and equity. In middle position with 6 players and deep stacks (80BB effective), standard play is to fold preflop without a premium hand or playable suited connector. Limping or raising risks position and pot OOP. Previous hands show aggressive opens, so folding preserves stack for better spots early in tournament.

I hold T6o in the Cutoff. This is a very weak hand with no playability. Although the Cutoff is a late position where opening ranges can be wider, T6o is below the bottom of a reasonable opening range, even for a steal, especially with three players left to act (Button, SB, BB). My stack is healthy (9500), and there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such poor holdings early in the tournament. The correct play is to fold.

7♣2♠ is the absolute worst starting hand — virtually no playability even in position. Calling 100 to see a flop would require ~40% equity to break even against a single random hand and far less vs two defenders; 7-2o has well below that versus typical blind defending ranges. Open-raising as a pure steal would demand very high fold equity from both the SB and BB (and both players have shown they will defend/three‑bet often), so a small raise would just get called and leave me in a dominated postflop spot. With a healthy 11.4k stack and many hands left, this is not a spot to gamble — conserve chips and wait for a much better opportunity.

Folded to us blind-vs-blind with a very weak offsuit connector. Raising risks building a pot out of position vs a BB who can defend/3-bet wide, and 34o realizes equity poorly when we get called. Completing keeps the pot small, protects our limping range, and lets us realize our equity with good immediate price (need only 50 more into 150).

I am in the big blind with Queen-Six offsuit. The action folded to the small blind, who merely called the blind. With a marginal hand like Q6o, there is no need to bloat the pot by raising. Checking allows me to see a free flop and potentially hit a pair or better without risking additional chips.

We flopped bottom pair (4s on board, 4h in hand) plus a gutshot to the wheel if a 5 comes. In a limp/check blind-vs-blind pot on a dry A-4-2 rainbow board, BB has plenty of Ax that will continue versus a lead, and if we get raised we’re in a miserable spot with a weak pair. Our hand has decent showdown value and can profitably check-call some reasonable bets, so keeping the pot small and realizing equity is best.

I am in position in a limped pot with Queen-high on an Ace-high board. My opponent in the small blind has checked to me. While I could attempt a small stab to take the pot, Q6o has very little equity if called, and checking back allows me to see a free turn card. There is no need to risk chips in this small pot without a made hand or a draw.

In this limp/check blind-vs-blind pot, our pair of 4s has some showdown value and a small amount of backup equity (gutshot to the wheel on a 5). Leading turn mainly folds out BB’s air, but when we get called it’s often by Ax (or better 4x) that dominates us, and we also expose ourselves to a raise that forces a tough fold/call decision with a marginal hand. Checking keeps the pot small, lets us realize equity, and allows BB to bluff his missed hands on the turn/river where we can consider bluff-catching depending on sizing.

The board is Ace-high and the pot remains limped. FiveTwo has checked to me twice. While I could try to represent the Ace with a small bet, my hand (Q6o) has very little value if called. In a small 200-chip pot, it is safer to check back and take the free card to the river, hoping to either improve or win a cheap showdown if the opponent also has air.

We river A-A-7-4-2 with 4h3d, giving us two pair (Aces and Fours). In this limp/check BvB line, Flash’s range is still extremely wide, but the double-ace runout makes it likely he checks back a lot of worse hands (any non-A, non-4) that we beat at showdown. A small blocker/value bet targets exactly those hands that will “look us up” with just the board Aces + kickers, while also setting a cheap price versus the portion of his range that slowplayed an Ace (or has a better 4x) — and if he raises, we can comfortably fold without donating more. Pot is small, so taking a thin, controlled value stab is best.

In this limped pot, the board has paired Aces on the river (4s 2h Ad 7s Ah). FiveTwo has led out for 100 into a 200-chip pot after checking every previous street. While this could be a bluff or a thin value bet with a small pair (like a 4 or 7) or even a weak Ace, my hand (Queen-high) has very little showdown value. Even if FiveTwo is bluffing, they could easily hold King-high. I am getting 3:1 odds to call, but Queen-high is simply too weak to justify a call here given the lack of information. I will fold and preserve my stack.

78o is a marginal hand at best, and completely unplayable from UTG in a 6-handed game with deep stacks. No suitedness, poor playability multiway, and no fold equity if raising. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots early in the tournament.

I hold A3o in middle position with 9500 chips (95 BB). UTG has folded. A3o is a weak hand to open from early/middle position, especially with aggressive players like Minni and Claude still to act behind me. The hand has poor playability post-flop as it is easily dominated and unsuited. Folding is the standard and most profitable play in this spot to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Cutoff with QhTs — a standard open-raise hand. There are three players left to act (button, SB, BB) but my position and hand equity justify taking the initiative to steal the blinds and build pots when I’m likely ahead. Stacks are deep (~100+ BBs) so I can play postflop, but I don’t want to give free cards or face cheap multiway flops — I’ll raise to 3x the big blind to 300. This sizing gives good fold equity versus the blinds, keeps SPR manageable if called, and is in line with the table’s tendencies. Plan: if I get single-called I’ll C-bet appropriate flops; fold to a large 3-bet from a tight player (e.g., Claude) but consider calling a small 3-bet from looser opponents depending on sizing and reads.

Minni opens 3bb from the cutoff and we’re on the button with 9♠6♣ offsuit (very weak, poorly connected, dominated often). Calling invites squeezes from the blinds and creates tough postflop spots even with position; 3-betting as a bluff with this hand is too loose given its lack of blockers and playability. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better button spots.

I am in the Small Blind holding 2c 3d, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised to 300 from the Cutoff. Calling here out of position with a junk hand is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

KJs is a strong hand facing a cutoff open. Minni raised to 300, and I have a premium suited broadway. I can 3-bet for value here - KJs plays well against a cutoff opening range. 3-betting to around 850-900 puts pressure on Minni and builds the pot with a strong hand.

I opened to 300 from the cutoff with QhTs — a standard steal attempt — but Claude 3‑bet to 850. QTo is marginal against a 3‑bet range: it’s often dominated (AQ+, AK, KQ, mid pairs) and has poor playability out of position in a bloated pot. I must call 550 into a 1,200 pot, which requires ~31% equity just to break even preflop, and with deep stacks this would commit me to difficult multi‑street decisions. 4‑betting is out of the question with QTo. I planned explicitly to fold to a large 3‑bet from Claude, so I’ll preserve my stack and fold here.

I am in UTG position holding Ace-Ten offsuit (Ad Ts). In a 6-handed game, this hand is borderline and often folded from early position to avoid domination and difficult post-flop situations out of position. I have observed that Claude (currently in the Small Blind) has been playing very aggressively, frequently raising and 3-betting. Opening light into an aggressive player who will have position on me or 3-bet me is risky. Given my tight image so far, I could potentially steal, but the risk of playing a dominated hand out of position outweighs the benefit. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

6♣5♣ is a speculative, suited connector with good implied odds deep (I have ~111BB). There is no preflop raise yet (pot 150, to call 100, min-raise 200). With several players yet to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB) who have shown aggression previously, open-raising from MP with this hand risks getting 3‑bet or playing bloated pots out of position. Calling 100 is the prudent play: it’s a relatively small investment to see a flop and realize equity, while preserving stack depth to extract value when I hit. Plan: call to the flop, continue with pairs/strong draws, and fold or proceed cautiously facing significant preflop/turn aggression (re‑evaluate if contacted by a sizable 3‑bet).

Pocket fives plays well in position versus a limp: we can isolate Minni, often take it down preflop or on favorable boards, and still have good set-mining value when called. Limping behind invites the blinds in and lowers our ability to realize equity cleanly. I’ll iso-raise to a size that pressures the limp and discourages the blinds from coming along.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has raised to 450 over a limp from Minni. Calling or raising with this hand would be a significant mistake, especially against a raise from the cutoff. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

T7o is a weak hand, especially out of position in the small blind facing a raise from the cutoff. I'd be calling 400 into a 700 pot with a speculative hand that doesn't play well multiway or OOP. Easy fold.

My hand is Jd3h offsuit, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a raise to 450 from FiveTwo in the cutoff after a limp from Minni, who has shown some calling tendencies but also folds to aggression. Pot is 700, to call 350 gives about 2:1 odds, but J3o has negligible equity against a typical cutoff raising range (which includes pairs, broadways, suited aces, etc.), and postflop playability is poor. Standard BB defense folds this; no reason to speculate with 80BB stacks deep. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I limped with 6♣5♣ to see a flop when there was no open — that was the plan. FiveTwo then raised to 450 (I must call 350 more). This hand is a speculative, small suited connector and I'm out of position to the CO post‑flop. Calling another 350 into a 700 pot OOP against a single raiser will put me in difficult multi‑street decisions with a hand that fares poorly vs a raiser’s typical range; 3‑betting as a bluff from MP with 6‑5s is also suboptimal. Sunk cost is 100 already; I will preserve my deep stack for better spots and fold here.

A♣Q♠ is a strong UTG open in a 6-max game — worth raising for value and initiative. With 110BB effective I want to discourage too many callers and build the pot with a premium hand, so I’ll open to 3x the big blind. If I face a small 3-bet I can call and play postflop; I’ll fold to an unexpectedly large 3-bet depending on the aggressor. Total to put in the pot: 300.

Facing an UTG open in a 6-handed game, 8♠5♥ offsuit is too weak and poorly playable (dominated, low high-card value, and makes second-best pairs/straights). With deep stacks, calling invites tough postflop spots versus a strong range, and it’s not a good 3-bet bluff candidate. Preserve stack and fold.

I am in the Cutoff with 8h 3h. Minni raised from UTG to 300. 83 suited is a very weak hand to play against an early position raise, especially with three players left to act behind me. There is no reason to risk chips with such a low equity hand. I will fold.

KQo on the button vs UTG open from Minni. This is a premium hand with position - excellent spot to 3-bet. Minni has shown some aggression but also folds to 3-bets (folded to my 3-bet in Hand #11 with KJc). A raise to ~850 puts pressure on them and lets me play a bigger pot in position with a strong holding.

9d2s is a complete trash hand with no playability, high card value, or suitedness. Facing an UTG open from Minni (tight-aggressive tendencies from history) and a button 3-bet from Claude (frequently aggressive raiser who isolates and wins pots), this is a clear fold. Pot odds are poor (calling 800 into 1300 pot for set-mining or speculative value which this hand lacks), and out of position in SB with no fold equity on a raise. Preserve stack for better spots early in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with J4 offsuit, facing a raise from Minni (UTG) and a 3-bet from Claude (Button). This indicates strong ranges from my opponents. My hand is extremely weak, and I would be out of position against both players in a bloated pot. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a poor holding against this action. Folding is the only reasonable option.

I opened UTG to 300 with A♣Q♠ — a strong hand for pressure and value. Claude’s button 3-bet to 850 is sizable but, given his frequent preflop aggression, likely contains a lot of broadways and weaker Ax/Kx hands. Calling 550 keeps me out of position and surrenders initiative; folding is too weak for AQs. I prefer to 4-bet to isolate and extract value/fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with ~107BB effective. I’ll 4-bet to 2,600 total — large enough to apply pressure and fold out worse hands, but small enough to retain maneuverability postflop if called. Action: raise to 2600.

Minni 4-bet to 2600 after I 3-bet with KQo. This is a clear fold. KQo is not strong enough to continue against a 4-bet range which typically includes AA, KK, QQ, AK, and maybe JJ/AQs. Against this range I'm either dominated (by KK, AK) or a significant underdog. Not strong enough to 5-bet jam and calling leaves me in a tough spot even with position. Easy fold and save my chips.

99 is a strong UTG open in 6-max: we’re ahead of most calling ranges and can profitably play for value while denying equity to overcards. Limping invites multiway pots and tough decisions versus aggressive players behind. I’ll open with a standard sizing that builds the pot but doesn’t overcommit against 3-bets.

I am holding King-Jack of clubs, a strong suited broadway hand that performs well in multi-way pots and has significant post-flop equity. FiveTwo has raised to 2.5x from under-the-gun. While KJs is a candidate for a 3-bet, calling is a very solid play here to see a flop without over-committing against a potentially strong UTG opening range, while also leaving room to navigate if the aggressive players behind me (Claude or Minni) decide to squeeze.

A6o in the cutoff facing a UTG raise and a cold call. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly multiway. With 3 players still to act behind me and facing a UTG raise (typically tight range), A6o is too weak to continue. The ace is dominated by better aces, and this hand doesn't have great post-flop playability. Easy fold.

2d8s is a complete trash hand: low, offsuit, disconnected, with minimal playability post-flop. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo (who has shown aggression in prior hands) and a flat call from Flash, the range is strong. Pot odds are ~2.6:1 (250 to call into 650), but multiway with poor equity and position doesn't justify a call. Button position is nice, but not for speculative junk like this. Folding preserves stack for premium spots in a long tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit (8h Td). Facing a raise from UTG (FiveTwo) and a call from Middle Position (Flash), I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 850), but my hand has poor playability, especially out of position in a multi-way pot. T8o is easily dominated by the ranges of an UTG opener and a caller. Folding is the prudent long-term play to preserve chips for better spots.

I’m in the big blind with 8♣6♣ facing an UTG open to 250 and one caller. Calling costs me 150 more (250 total) into a 650 pot, roughly 4.3:1 pot odds. 8-6 suited is a speculative hand with good multiway equity and strong straight/flush potential, and stacks are deep enough (~120BB) to realize implied odds. A min 3-bet to 400 would be out of position versus the opener, give little fold equity against two players, and is too small to credibly fold out stronger ranges. I’ll call to see a flop and continue only with a pair, strong draw, or a favorable board texture; otherwise I’ll concede to significant aggression.

Flop: 4♣ 3♠ Q♣. I hold 8♣6♣ — a four-card club flush draw (9 club outs) plus a gutshot to a 5 (4 outs), with one overlap (5♣) for 12 unique outs. That’s ~25.5% to hit on the turn and ~45% to hit by the river. This is a multiway pot (UTG opener + one caller), so bluffing or barreling is thin and I should prioritize pot control and equity realization. Stacks are deep enough that implied odds justify seeing another card, so I’ll check and see the turn. Plan: call small continuation bets (roughly ≤1/3 pot) to chase the draw; fold to very large bets/raises that don’t give correct odds or that show heavy strength.

Multiway on Qc-4c-3s, 99 is an underpair but still has decent showdown value and benefits from protection versus two overcards and random floats. Both callers have plenty of Qx/clubs/sets, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a big sizing, but a small c-bet leverages my UTG range advantage, denies equity, and can still get folds from A/K-high and some weak pairs. If I’m raised, I can comfortably fold this hand.

On a flop of 4c 3s Qc, I have the second-nut flush draw with Kc Jc, plus two overcards (King and Jack). The pot is 1075 and FiveTwo has made a small continuation bet of 275 (about 25% of the pot). This small sizing often indicates a range-wide continuation bet. With Minni still to act behind me, calling is a strong play; it allows me to see a turn card cheaply while keeping my range wide and potentially drawing Minni into the pot with a weaker hand. My equity is very high (around 35-45% against most of FiveTwo's range), making this an easy continuation.

I’ll call 275. With 8♣6♣ on 4♣-3♠-Q♣ I have a strong club draw (9 clean outs). That’s ~19.1% to hit on the turn and ~35% to hit by the river — well above the ~16.9% equity required by the current pot odds (275 into a 1,350 pot). This is a multiway pot, so I don’t want to bloat it by raising out of position vs the UTG opener; a small c-bet like this is exactly the spot to realize equity cheaply. Plan: see the turn — fold to heavy strength if I miss and face large aggression, but continue/raise if I hit the flush (or pair up to a safe board) to extract value.

Turn: board 4♣ 3♠ Q♣ A♣. I hold 8♣6♣ — a made club flush (A‑Q‑8‑6‑4 using the board Ace and Queen). This is a strong value hand in a multiway pot: most of my opponents’ continuing ranges (top pair Q, any A, weaker made hands, and some draws) will call a bet, while the only hands that currently beat me are two‑club hole combos that include clubs higher than my 8 — those combos are relatively rare. If I check I give a free card (a club on the river) that can turn one‑club hands into a better flush, so I should charge draws and extract value now.

Plan: bet for value and protection — big enough to get called by worse and price out some turn/river clubs, but not so huge that I’m committed to call a large raise from a very narrow two‑club range. A bet of roughly 55% pot (~900 chips into a 1625 pot) meets those goals. If I face a large raise or shove I’ll strongly consider that I’m up against a superior two‑club combo and will re‑evaluate (call small raises, fold to extreme action).

The Ac is one of the worst turns for 99: it puts an overcard to the Q, completes the front-door club flush, and heavily improves the BB’s flop check/call range (Ax, two-club hands, Qx with a club). Minni now donk-leads 900 into 2525 multiway, which is strongly value-weighted, and I have no club blocker plus only ~2 outs to a set on the river. Calling also invites Flash to continue/raise behind, creating nasty reverse implied odds. Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I have the absolute nut flush on this board. My hole cards are the King and Jack of clubs, and the board shows the Ace, Queen, and Four of clubs. Since I hold the King-high flush and the Ace of clubs is on the board, no player can have a higher flush. Minni has 'donk-led' into two players on the turn, which typically indicates a very strong hand like a set, two pair, or a smaller flush. I am raising to 2400 to build the pot and extract value from these hands while I am guaranteed to be ahead. This sizing is designed to be called by her strong made hands and sets up a potential all-in on the river.

I have the club flush (A‑Q‑8‑6‑4 using my 8♣6♣), but Flash's large turn check‑raise is highly polarized and strongly represents a turned two‑club flush that beats my 8‑high flush. Pot = 4,925 and I must call 1,500, so I need roughly 1,500/(4,925+1,500) ≈ 23.3% equity to justify a call. There are 8 unseen clubs remaining (K,J,T,9,7,5,3,2) → C(8,2)=28 two‑club hole-card combos; of those 28, 22 include at least one club higher than an 8 and therefore beat my flush, while only 6 are clearly behind. Flash called preflop and the flop and then made a big raise on the turn after the A♣ came — a line that matches turning a stronger two‑club flush. Given the sizing and line, his range is weighted toward value (many combos that beat me), so my equity versus his raising range is likely well below the ~23% needed. Raising myself would commit a lot of chips when I’m probably dominated, and calling would be a marginal / negative‑EV call against a value‑heavy range. Per my earlier plan (call small raises, fold to extreme action), this is an extreme action — I fold to preserve stack and wait for a cleaner spot.
I am UTG with AKo, which is a premium starting hand. I want to raise to build the pot and narrow the field. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate in this 50/100 blind structure to gain initiative.

Ad3d is a suited ace with nut flush potential. Against Flash's UTG raise, this is a marginal hand but playable. I have position on the raiser and the suited ace gives me good implied odds if I hit a diamond draw or top pair. Calling is reasonable here - 3-betting would be too aggressive with this hand and folding seems too tight.

79o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially facing an UTG raise from Flash (who frequently opens aggressively) and a call from Claude. In cutoff position, while I have position on early actors, calling 300 into 750 pot multiway risks poor post-flop equity and reverse implied odds. Stacks are deep (~80BB), so preserve stack for premium spots rather than speculative calls. Standard fold to avoid tough decisions OOP or multiway.

I am on the Button with T6s, facing an UTG open to 3bb and a flat call from Middle Position. Although I have the best position, T6s is a marginal speculative hand dominated by many hands in the UTG opener's range (like AT, KT, or better suited spades). With two players already involved showing strength and the blinds still to act behind me, the risk of domination or being squeezed is too high to justify the call. The pot odds are decent, but the reverse implied odds and low playability of T6 against early position ranges make folding the disciplined choice.

I’m on the small blind with 2♦7♣ — essentially the worst possible starting hand. Flash opened UTG to 300 and Claude cold‑called, so this is a multiway pot against strong ranges. I’ve already posted 50 and would need to call 250 more. Pot is 750, so calling 250 only gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity), but 7‑2o has almost no equity vs an UTG raiser plus a caller and plays terribly out of position postflop. Raising is not sensible either. With a healthy stack and many hands left, the correct play is to conserve chips and fold this hand.

We’re getting a good price (200 more to win 750 already) and 9♠8♦ has enough raw equity multiway. While it’s offsuit and we’ll be out of position, it plays well when we make strong straights/two pair and we don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet bluff versus an UTG open plus a caller. Take the pot odds and see a flop.

Multiway flop on 2c-6d-4c, our 9s8d has essentially no immediate equity (no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw) and is a poor candidate to lead or check-raise bluff. While the low board hits the BB range, we should realize equity by checking and continue only versus very small bets or with better backdoors; otherwise we can comfortably fold later.

I opened with AKo and got two callers. The flop is 2c-6d-4c, which is a low board that mostly misses my range but is also unlikely to have hit my opponents hard, though it favors their calling ranges more than mine. I have two overcards and the backdoor nut flush draw (as I hold the Ace of Spades, not clubs, I don't actually have a club draw, but I do have overcards). FiveTwo checked. I will place a continuation bet of about 500 into the 950 pot to take down the pot now and represent a big pair, as my opponents' ranges are likely capped.

I have Ad3d on a 2c6d4c board. I have ace high, a gutshot straight draw (need a 5), and a backdoor diamond flush draw. Flash c-bet 500 into ~900. Getting about 3:1, I need ~25% equity. With just a gutshot (~8%), backdoor flush (~2%), and 3 aces (~6%), I'm around 16% equity - not enough to continue. The board also has a club draw which Flash could have. Folding is the disciplined play here.

We’re facing a small c-bet (500 into 1450), but 9♠8♦ on 2♣-6♦-4♣ has essentially no real equity: no pair, no flush draw, and no direct straight draw—only very thin backdoor improvement (mainly a 7 turn to pick up an OESD). We also won’t realize our overcard equity well out of position versus an UTG range that can comfortably continue with overpairs, top pairs (A6/A4), and sets. Raising as a bluff is unattractive because we have poor equity when called and would need to fire multiple barrels. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack for higher-EV spots.
T7o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and I'm in the worst position at the table. No need to get involved with marginal holdings from early position.

6d3c is a trash hand, offsuit, disconnected low cards with no playability. In middle position pre-flop after UTG fold, standard play is to fold to avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop. Pot is small (150), but committing chips with this hand has negative EV long-term. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament.

I am in the Cutoff with A6s (Ace-Six of Clubs). This is a strong enough hand to open-raise from late position in a 6-handed game. My goal is to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. Folding is too tight, and limping is weak. A standard open size of 2.5BB (250 chips) is appropriate to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable if 3-bet.

I’m on the button with A♥T♠ facing a CO raise to 250. With ~105BB effective and position on my side, A‑T offsuit is strong enough to continue but vulnerable to being dominated by bigger A‑X hands. I prefer to call rather than make a thin, small 3‑bet (min‑raise to 400) which won’t fold out many better hands — calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves position post‑flop, and lets me realize equity and apply pressure on favorable flops. Plan: play the flop aggressively when I connect (top pair, good draws) and proceed cautiously or fold to heavy resistance on dry misses; re‑evaluate if blinds 3‑bet.

Facing a CO open and a BTN flat, A7o is a dominated, low-realization hand from the small blind. Calling invites the BB to come along, leaves us multiway and out of position, and when we make top pair (A-high) we’re often in kicker trouble versus stronger Ax that exist in both ranges. A squeeze would need a very high fold rate to show profit given the likely continuing ranges and the aggressive BB behind. Best is to preserve stack and wait for cleaner spots.

I am in the big blind with 8c 4d, which is a weak hand. The pot is 650 and it costs me 150 more to call Pro's raise of 250, as I already have 100 invested. While the price is 4.3 to 1, this hand performs very poorly post-flop and I will be out of position against both Pro (the raiser) and Minni (the caller). Given the strength of the opponents and my desire to conserve my stack for better opportunities, I will fold.

I am the pre-flop aggressor playing out of position against the Button (Minni) on a Jd-5s-3d board. My hand is Ac-6c, giving me Ace-high, a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4), and backdoor club potential. The board texture connects reasonably well with a Button calling range (which includes flush draws, sets, and Jx hands). Betting here doesn't accomplish much as better hands (pairs) likely call, and worse hands (like K-high) might fold, but I don't need much protection against them. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size with a marginal hand OOP. I intend to check and evaluate; if Minni checks back, I realize my equity for free. If Minni bets, I will likely fold unless the sizing is very small, as my Ace-high is vulnerable.

I missed this dry J‑3‑5 rainbow board with A♥T♠ — no pair, no diamond or straight draw. CO (the preflop raiser) checked, which looks like pot control or a potential trap, so with position and a marginal holding I want to control the pot size and realize equity rather than bluff into possible Jx or a check‑raise. Checking keeps the initiative to act on the turn, lets me call down cheaply if I pick up equity (an A or T) or apply a delayed bluff when favorable, and avoids getting raised off a dry miss. Plan: check here, re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed aggressively only if I improve or the table gives a clear fold equity window.

On the turn, the board develops a flush texture with the 9d (3d 5s Jd 9d). I hold Ace-high (Ac 6c) with no diamond, meaning I have no draw to the flush and my straight potential is negligible. After checking the flop and getting a check-back, my Ace-high still has some showdown value against random broadway floats. However, betting here is risky as I represent very little having checked the flop, and I don't hold a diamond blocker to bluff effectively. I want to get to showdown cheaply. If I check and the opponent bets, I can easily fold. If they check back, I maintain my equity. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size out of position.

I have A♥T♠ on 3d-5s-Jd-9d — currently only ace-high and I hold no diamonds. The board already has three diamonds (3d, Jd, 9d), so any two-diamond hand is already beating me; many hands in the CO's raising range (Jx, 9x, sets, two diamonds) will either call a bet or raise me. Pot is 650 with ~9k effective stacks (SPR ≈ 14), so I don't want to build a big pot with a marginal hand. I checked the flop and the CO checked back on the turn, which looks like pot control or weakness — perfect to realize equity in position rather than bluff into possible made hands. Plan: check the turn, see the river; if I pair my A or T I can bet for thin value, if the river bricks and he shows aggression I'll fold; if he checks again I can consider a small probe on a safe river. Therefore: check now to keep the pot manageable and use position on the river.

I hold Ace-high (Ac6c) on a board of [3d 5s Jd 9d Qd], which puts four diamonds on the board. I do not hold a diamond. The pot is 650. The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn. Minni's range likely consists of marginal pairs, weak flushes, or missed straight draws. Betting here achieves little; hands that beat me (any diamond, any pair) will likely call, and hands that I beat (lower high cards) will fold. Therefore, betting turns my hand into a bluff with low success probability. Checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown where Ace-high might win against missed draws, or easily fold if Minni bets, as she likely has the flush or a pair if she puts money in on this river.

Board: 3♦ 5♠ J♦ 9♦ Q♦ — I hold A♥ T♠: just ace‑high and no diamond. Pro was the preflop raiser and checked flop and turn and checked the river as well. With four diamonds on board any diamond in his range crushes me, and many 1‑pair hands (Jx/9x/Qx), two pair, or straights/flushes either beat me or will call a probe. Pot is 650 with ~9k effective stacks (SPR ≈ 14). I have virtually no value to extract and a bluff here is low EV — most better hands will call and I risk getting raised or called by dominating Ax hands. I’ll check and take the showdown, keeping the pot small. If Pro were to bet a sizable river bet I’d fold; only an extremely tiny probe would justify a call as a price call, but the correct default is to check.

UTG in a 6-handed game with J♦3♠ offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity, and no suited or connected value. Standard tight opening range preflop doesn't include this. Pot is 150 from blinds, to call 100, but limping or raising would be a mistake as it invites multiway pots where this hand performs poorly. Folding preserves stack for better spots, aligning with long-term maximization over 150 hands.

In a 6-handed game, King-Jack offsuit (KJo) is a standard opening hand from Middle Position (MP) after the UTG player has folded. I have a healthy stack of 91 BBs. While there are aggressive players behind me (specifically Claude in the Big Blind who has shown a tendency to 3-bet), KJo blocks strong value hands like AA, KK, JJ, AK, and KQ. A raise to 2.5x (250 chips) allows me to take the initiative and potentially steal the blinds or play the pot in position against the blinds. If I face significant aggression or a 3-bet, I can easily fold this unsuited hand without committing too much of my stack.

Fold. 4♣10♦ is a weak, offsuit hand with poor playability and is often dominated by a middle‑position opener. Facing Pro's raise to 250 with the button and blinds yet to act creates a good chance of getting squeezed or playing a multi‑way pot out of position. I’ll preserve a healthy stack (10925) and wait for a better spot or a chance to attack the button/blinds, rather than committing chips with a marginal hand.

Pro opens to 2.5bb from middle position and we’re on the Button with 9♦3♣ offsuit. This hand is too weak to profitably call (dominated, poor playability, reverse implied odds) and it’s not a good candidate to 3-bet bluff given how little equity it has when called. Preserve stack and wait for better spots in position.

I am in the small blind with 8-6 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Pro has raised to 2.5x the big blind from middle position. Playing this hand out of position against a raise is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to maintain my chip lead.

74o is a very weak hand. Even getting decent pot odds (150 to win 400), defending with 74 offsuit against a middle position raise is -EV. This hand plays poorly post-flop and rarely makes strong hands. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position with 7h 4c, which is a statistically very weak hand (7-4 offsuit). With 5 players left to act behind me, the probability of someone holding a better hand is extremely high. Opening from early position requires a strong range (typically pairs 77+, strong broadways, suited aces/connectors). 74o falls well outside any reasonable opening range. Given the deep stack depth (92.5 BB) and early stage of the tournament, there is no incentive to make a loose speculative play here. Folding is the standard and correct decision.
