No reason to bet here with just ace high and a weak draw - checking to see a free card makes sense. If I get bet into, I can fold as my equity isn't great.

Multiway pot on a J64 flop with two hearts. QTo has queen-high overcards and a gutshot straight draw to broadway (needing KT), but minimal showdown value OOP vs FiveTwo's CO raising range (which hits Jx well) and Claude's speculative SB call. No flush draw or strong pair. Pot odds irrelevant with free check. Best play is to check, pot control, realize equity cheaply on turn if check-folded behind, or induce bluffs. Avoid bluffing multiway OOP early in tournament with deep stacks; folding equity low and reverse implied odds if hit queen. Consistent with preflop flat strategy of playing postflop control.

Flop is Jh-6c-4h three-way after both blinds defend and check. With Kc9h we have only K-high, no immediate straight draw, and just a backdoor heart draw. In a multiway pot, a c-bet gets called very often by any Jx, 6x/4x, pocket pairs, and heart draws, leaving us in a tough spot to continue barreling with low equity. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity/position, and lets us respond profitably on many turns (K/9 for a pair, heart for additional equity, Q/T for strong backdoors) while also protecting our checking range.

The 7d on the turn gives me an open-ended straight draw (need a 3 or 8). I now have 8 outs for the straight. The flop checked through, suggesting weakness from all players. I could lead out here as a semi-bluff, but being OOP with just a draw is tricky - if I bet and get raised, I'm in a tough spot. Better to check and see a free river card to try to hit my straight. If someone bets small, I can call with my 8-out draw getting decent implied odds.

On the turn, the board is Jh6c4h7d, bringing a 7 that completes some straight draws (e.g., 45, 35) and adds a diamond, but my QcTd has only queen-high with no pair, flush draw, or strong straight draw remaining (backdoor broadway faded). Multiway pot (vs. FiveTwo's CO wide raising range and Claude's speculative SB call) after all checks on flop suggests weakness from both. Pot odds are irrelevant with a free check. Checking pot-controls my marginal showdown value, realizes thin equity cheaply to river, induces bluffs from missed draws, and avoids bloating pot OOP with poor playability if raised. Bluffing multiway turn OOP early tournament has low fold equity against CO/SB ranges and exposes stack unnecessarily (7300 effective). Consistent with preflop/flop passive strategy exploiting opponents' tendencies to check weak hands (seen in prior hands like 14,26). Plan to showdown or fold to river aggression.

Turn goes check-check to us again in a 3-way pot after we checked back flop as PFR. With K-high and essentially no real draw, our showdown value is very thin and checking just concedes the pot too often to any pair or even random A-high that decides to stab river. However, the double-check line from both blinds caps them toward a lot of weak one-pair and overcard holdings, and a small delayed c-bet is credible from our range (overpairs/Jx/sets) while risking little. I’ll take a low-risk stab to fold out their air and deny equity to random overcards/draws; if called, I can mostly shut down unless I improve (K/9) or get a very favorable bluff card/runout.

I have an open-ended straight draw (need 3 or 8 for the straight). Getting 1350:300 = 4.5:1 pot odds. With 8 outs I'm about 5:1 against, so pot odds are close. With implied odds if I hit a hidden straight, this is a clear call. FiveTwo's small bet (40% pot) suggests he's likely weak or on a draw himself after checking the flop.

Facing a turn bet of 300 from FiveTwo into a 1350 pot (~4.5:1 direct odds, better implied multiway) after all checks on flop, with Claude flat-calling behind. QcTd holds only queen-high on Jh6c4h7d board, no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (backdoor broadway missed), and poor showdown value OOP multiway vs. CO's turn betting range (often Jx, 7x, sets, completed draws like 45/53/58, bluffs) and SB's calling range (pairs, draws). Equity likely <25% vs. villain ranges, insufficient even with pot odds given reverse implied odds if river bricks and we face further aggression. Consistent with passive preflop/flop/early turn strategy of pot control OOP in multiway pot; folding marginal hand avoids spew early tournament (hand 36/150, deep stacks ~70BB). FiveTwo shows turn aggression when checked to (e.g., Hand 26, 35), but calling risks bloating pot OOP with no improving outs. Preserve stack for better spots, exploit opponents' check-folds elsewhere.

I hit my straight on the river! The 3d completes my 3-4-5-6-7 straight using my 5 and the board's 3-4-6-7. This is a strong hand that I need to value bet. FiveTwo showed aggression on the turn with a 300 bet. I should bet for value - around 65-70% pot to get called by Jx hands or weaker made hands. Betting 900 into 1350 is a solid value sizing.

We’re facing a 2/3-pot river lead (900 into ~1350). With K-high we have essentially zero showdown value, so calling requires Claude to be bluffing at least ~29% of the time.

Given the line (SB defend → check flop → check/call my small delayed c-bet on turn → lead river), Claude’s range is heavily weighted to made hands: Jx/7x/6x/4x that decided to bluff-catch turn, plus hands that improved on the 3d (notably any 5x that now makes a straight 3-4-5-6-7). Missed heart draws exist, but my 9h blocks some of the natural bluff combos, and population tendency from this seat/line is not to overbluff rivers after calling turn.

Raising as a bluff is too optimistic: his value region (straights/two-pair/sets and some Jx that won’t love folding) will call often enough, and we don’t have strong blockers to the key value hands (5x). Best play is to fold and preserve stack.

UTG with 5♠6♣ (offsuit) is a very weak, low-card hand that’s easily dominated and has poor postflop playability out of position. Calling 100 (limping) risks a multiway pot and tough decisions against the frequent open-raising players at this table; raising as a bluff from UTG with this hand is unnecessary and thin. I have a healthy stack (14525), so there’s no need to gamble with a marginal holding — fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re first in from middle position after UTG folds, facing only the blinds (pot 150, no raise). 7h3h is too weak to open from MP in a 6-max/high-roller dynamic, and limping is especially bad since button/cutoff/SB can isolate frequently, forcing us to play a dominated hand out of position with poor realization. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for stronger opens/spots.

I am in the Cutoff with 5h 6d. This is a very weak starting hand. While I could attempt to steal the blinds with a raise, the players behind me (especially Claude on the Button) have shown a willingness to play back. Given that I have a healthy stack and it's relatively early in the tournament (Hand 37), there's no reason to take a high-variance risk with such a weak hand. Folding is the standard and most prudent play.

On the button with Q9o and everyone has folded to me. This is a prime stealing spot. Q9o is a solid hand for a button steal attempt against the blinds. Standard 2.5x raise to put pressure on the blinds.

Facing a late position button raise from Claude, who has shown aggression in previous hands. My holding is T4 suited, a speculative hand with some post-flop potential but poor playability out of position in the small blind. Pot odds are about 2:1 (200 to call into 600), but implied odds are uncertain OOP against a wide button range. Defending this wide OOP risks difficult spots post-flop with a short-suited connector. Better to fold and preserve stack for stronger hands, given deep stacks and tournament goal to maximize over 150 hands. Pro in BB may defend or squeeze.

I am in the Big Blind with 42o, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (Claude). The pot is 400, and I need to call 150. This gives me pot odds of roughly 27%. Although 42o has raw equity close to this against a wide button range, it is one of the worst starting hands in hold'em. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressor is historically a losing play due to poor playability and realization of equity. Folding is the standard and correct decision here.

UTG 6-max with 6c5h (offsuit) is too weak to open profitably. We’ll be out of position against multiple players who can 3-bet and isolate, and this hand doesn’t realize equity well without suitedness. Limping creates multiway, low-EV spots. Best play is to preserve stack and wait for stronger opens.

I am in Middle Position with Jack-Three offsuit (J3o). This is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low high-card value. In a 6-handed tournament, opening J3o from MP is not profitable and deviates significantly from standard winning ranges. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.
4d 8h is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and no high card value. Even in the cutoff position with only 3 players to act behind me, this is not a profitable opening hand. Standard fold.

Folds to the button in an unopened pot. Q9o is a standard opening hand from the button due to position, decent playability, and high card strength. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) to steal the blinds or build the pot with initiative while maintaining fold equity against the blinds, who have shown passive tendencies in previous hands but can defend wide.

I hold 6h 8s in the Small Blind. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300. My hand is a weak off-suit one-gapper. Playing this hand out of position against a Button raiser is generally -EV due to poor equity realization and the positional disadvantage. While Button ranges are wide, 68o is too weak to flat call or 3-bet profitably in this spot. The pot odds of roughly 36% are borderline against a wide range, but my realization factor will be low. Folding is the standard and prudent play.

I’m in the big blind with pocket 7s facing a button open to 300. Effective stacks are deep-ish (I ~14425, Elon ~6950 → ~69bb effective). Calling the extra 200 (300 total committed) gives correct pot odds (~200 into a 650 pot ≈ 30.8%) and good implied odds to set-mine against a likely wide button opening range. A min 3-bet to 500 would concede position and offer little fold equity; a larger 3-bet is unnecessary and too high-variance with a middle pair. I’ll call and see a flop — if I hit a set I’ll build the pot; if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously out of position, folding to heavy aggression or controlling the pot with checks/calls depending on texture and opponent tendencies.

Check. I have middle pair (77) out of position on A♦ K♦ 5♦ — two overcards plus a dangerous three-diamond flop. Betting OOP would bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand and get called by better or fold worse; checking controls the pot and lets me call reasonable continuation bets (and fold to large aggression). Plan: check to induce c-bets/bluffs, call small bets for showdown value, and fold to heavy pressure unless board/runout changes favorably.

Preflop, standard button steal raise with Q9o. Flop Ad Kd 5d is a scary, draw-heavy board that hits my perceived strong range (AK, Adx, strong Ax, flush draws, sets). I have zero equity or showdown value, but this is an ideal bluff spot: position, initiative, opponent's check indicates weakness or trapping (unlikely given BB defense). Minni's history shows passive calling tendencies, but a c-bet will fold out better aces, weak pairs, and many floats. Bet 60% pot (~400) to charge draws, build pot for turns where I improve, or take it down now. Deep stacks early tournament, exploiting wide BB defense. +EV bluff.

Board is A♦ K♦ 5♦ — a very wet, three-diamond flop with two overcards to my 7♠7♥. I’m out of position to the BTN who opened and now c-bet 400 into the pot. Raising OOP with middle pair into a three-diamond Ace‑King board is high-variance and gives little fold equity against a likely wide button range (many A/K/diamond combos). Folding here would be too tight given the pot odds: calling 400 into a 1050 pot (~27–28% equity required) is reasonable since I still have showdown value vs bluffs and missed broadway/connector hands. Plan: call the 400 to see a turn, fold to large turn aggression if the board pairs A/K or runs out a diamond (or if he jams), and otherwise proceed cautiously (check-call small turns, extract value if I improve to a set).

Turn pairs my 7s — I’ve made a set (7♠7♥) on A♦ K♦ 5♦ 7♣. The board has three diamonds, so the one hand that currently beats me is a made diamond flush (opponent would have had to hold two diamonds on the flop), which is possible but not extremely common. Button opened and c‑bet the flop, so his range is heavy in A‑x, K‑x, diamond combos and some bluffs. My set is ahead of most of that range and I should extract value while charging single‑diamond hands and denying a free river. I’ll lead for value with a meaningful but non‑committing size (≈2/3 pot) to get called by top pairs and many draws while keeping the pot manageable if he has a flush. If he check‑raises large, I’ll strongly consider that he may have a made flush and will re‑evaluate (call small/medium raises, fold to an overjam depending on sizing).

Preflop button raise with Q9o was standard. Flop c-bet on scary AdKd5d board as planned bluff representing Ax/Kx/flush, got called by BB who defends wide. Turn 7c bricks my hand completely—no pair, no draw (Qc9s misses diamonds, no straight draw). Minni's donk of 900 (38% pot) after check-calling flop strongly indicates value: likely weak Ax (A5/A7), Kx, two-pair, or missed draw turning into semi-bluff/protection. My range is polarized, but continued aggression lacks equity (dominated by any pair/draw) and fold equity against her range (she called flop c-bet and now leads). Pot 2350, calling 900 commits ~14% stack with 0% showdown equity and poor implied odds. Raising min to 1800 risks stack on pure air vs her strong range. Tournament hand 38/150, deep stacks, better spots exist to bluff. Standard fold to preserve stack and avoid spew.

I am dealt 8s 2d in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and is unplayable from early position. With a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds, there is no reason to risk chips on a marginal or poor hand. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

Q9o in middle position is a marginal hand. With Flash folding UTG and 4 players behind, I could fold or make a standard open raise. Given my comfortable stack (100BB) and the opportunity to pick up blinds, I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to 250.

Preflop in cutoff facing a raise to 250 from Claude in middle position after UTG fold. My hand is Ac4h offsuit, a weak ace with poor postflop playability, dominated by better aces and vulnerable to any pair or suited connectors in Claude's raising range. Pot odds to call are about 1.6:1 effective (250 into 400), requiring too much equity against a strong range. 3-betting is not justified without a stronger hand or read suggesting a fold-heavy opponent. Stacks are deep (65BB+), so folding preserves stack for better spots later in the tournament. Claude has been aggressive historically but no specific exploitative read to deviate from standard play.

I am on the Button with 4s3s, a very weak suited connector (one-gapper). The action is folded to Claude in Middle Position who raises to 250 (2.5 BB). With 82 big blinds, I have a deep stack, but 43s is generally too weak to cold call an MP open, even in position, due to poor equity realization and the threat of squeezes from the blinds. 3-betting is an option but risky against an MP range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

Qc5s is a weak, unsuited hand that is often dominated by a MP opener's range. From the small blind I'll be out of position to the raiser and the big blind, so calling 200 to play a multiway pot OOP (pot 400) is low-EV. A light 3-bet to 400 would commit too many chips with little fold equity and likely get called or isolated by better hands. With a healthy 15,225 stack and many hands left in the tournament, preserve chips and fold here.

Claude opens from middle position to 2.5bb. From the big blind we can defend fairly wide due to pot odds, but T4o is near the bottom of the range: dominated when we make top pair, poor playability postflop, and we’ll struggle to realize equity out of position in a heads-up pot. Not a good candidate for a 3-bet bluff either. Best is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.
9c4d is a trash hand from UTG. No playability - offsuit, disconnected, no high cards. Easy fold from early position.

A4s is a profitable opening hand from middle position, especially suited ace with flush and straight potential, good playability postflop. UTG folded, so wider range appropriate. Standard 3x raise to 300 for fold equity and to build pot in position against blinds and button. Deep stacks support speculative hands like this. Opponents from history call loosely but no recent aggression preflop here.

I am holding 6s 8h in the Cutoff facing a 3bb raise from Middle Position (Elon). 68o is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand with very poor playability and equity realization. It is well below the range of hands I should continue with against an early-to-middle position open. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here to preserve my 82bb stack for better spots.

Hand is 9h3d — a very weak, unsuited, unconnected holding with almost no postflop playability. Elon opened to 300 from middle position (a standard 3BB open). Calling here (300 into ~450) requires ~40% equity to be correct heads-up and far more in a likely multiway pot once the blinds act; 9-3o’s equity against an opener’s range is well below that. Raising as a bluff is possible in theory, but this is too poor a hand to isolate or apply pressure profitably. With a healthy 152 BB stack I should preserve chips and wait for a real opportunity. Fold.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3x open from Elon with the big blind still to act, and T3o has terrible playability and domination issues (can’t realize equity OOP, rarely flops strong, and makes dominated top pairs). Calling is -EV and invites squeezes; 3-bet bluffing is better reserved for hands with blockers or suited connectivity. Best decision is to let it go and preserve stack for higher-quality spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding pocket eights (8d 8c). Elon has raised to 300 from middle position. In a 6-max game, 88 is a strong hand, but playing it out of position against an opener suggests that a call is a solid, conservative play. This allows me to set-mine and see a flop without bloating the pot unnecessarily. A 3-bet is also an option, but given the stack sizes and the fact that Elon's range is reasonably strong from that position, calling the 200 additional chips is the most balanced approach.

I have an underpair to the Queen on a flop of 5c-Qd-2d. Given that I was the caller from the Big Blind pre-flop, the standard play is to check to the original aggressor, Elon. This allows me to gauge the strength of his hand based on his continuation bet sizing. My pocket eights are ahead of his unpaired overcards (like AK, AJ, or KJ) but behind any Queen or higher pocket pair. Checking is the most prudent move to control the pot size out of position.

Heads-up on flop after my preflop raise from MP and Flash's BB call. Flop 5c Qd 2d is dry, queen-high with two low cards and diamond flush draw possible. My A4s has ace-high (often best), gutshot straight draw to the wheel nuts (4 outs to 3x), and backdoor spade flush draw. Board favors my strong preflop range (AQ, KQ, pairs, etc.). Flash's check suggests weakness; BB defense range misses this board frequently (overcards, suited junk). Standard continuation bet to apply pressure, protect equity, and deny equity to drawing hands. Half-pot sizing balances value from trapped hands and fold equity. Deep stacks allow flexibility postflop.

I have an underpair with pocket eights on a Queen-high board. Elon's bet of 325 into a 650 pot is a standard continuation bet that he would make with a wide range of hands, including overcards like AK or AJ which I currently beat. Folding is too weak given the pot odds and the strength of my hand relative to a typical C-betting range. Raising would likely only get called by better hands. Therefore, calling is the most appropriate action to see a turn and re-evaluate the strength of my hand.

I am currently holding an underpair (pocket eights) on a board of 5c-Qd-2d-7s. The 7s on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Elon, I will stick to a check-call strategy to control the pot. Leading out here (donk betting) would likely only be called by better hands (like a Queen or higher pocket pairs) and would force out the bluffs I am currently beating (like AK, AJ, or diamond draws). By checking, I give Elon the opportunity to check behind with his marginal hands or continue bluffing with his air, while I minimize my losses if he has a strong holding.

Heads-up pot on turn after my flop c-bet continuation, which Flash called from BB. Board remains dry: 5c Qd 2d 7s, no strong draws completed except possible diamond flush draws or weak straights unlikely. My A4s has ace-high with minimal equity now (backdoor spade flush draw only, gutshot missed). Ace-high has decent showdown value against BB calling range, which misses this board often (overcards, weak pairs, missed draws). Flash checked turn again, signaling potential weakness but could be trapping medium strength like Qx or mid pair. Deep stacks and tournament stage favor pot control here; betting risks getting raised off equity or called by better, while checking realizes showdown value, controls pot size, and keeps pressure for river if he shows weakness. No need to bluff into a sticky spot when free card is low risk and preserves stack for better spots over 150 hands.

I have pocket eights on a board of 5c-Qd-2d-7s-Qc. The river paired the Queen, which is generally a good card for my hand as it reduces the combinations of Queens Elon could hold. Elon checked the turn after C-betting the flop, which often indicates a marginal made hand or a missed draw. My eights have solid showdown value, beating his bluffs and small pairs. However, a bet from me is unlikely to get called by many worse hands, whereas checking allows Elon to potentially bluff with his missed diamond draws or check behind with a hand like A5 or 66. Therefore, checking is the most effective way to reach showdown and realize my hand's equity.

River pairs the queens, making top pair QQ or better very strong, but Flash's triple check (flop, turn, river) from BB defense range suggests capped range: likely missed draws (diamond flush draws bricked, backdoors missed), weak pairs (55,22,77 unlikely given board texture), overcards like KJ/JT/Ax without diamond/spade, or air. My A4s has ace-high showdown value, which beats most of his checking range (bluffs, weak aces, missed floats). No strong value in my hand to bet for value (behind QQ/TQ/etc. if he has it, but unlikely since he'd bet turn/river). Bluffing thin is unnecessary and risky with deep stacks; he can hero-call with Qx or snap off with better. Checking realizes equity, controls pot, and wins at showdown frequently vs BB calling station tendencies from history (loose calls but folds to aggression postflop). Tournament phase favors preserving stack for premium spots over marginal river bets. Optimal pot control line after turn check.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 84o, an offsuit gapped low hand with no high card value or playability. This is a standard fold preflop from early position. No steal incentive as stacks are deep (59BB) and multiple players behind including aggressive ones like Claude and Minni. Preserve stack for premium hands to maximize long-term EV.

I hold 5c 7s in Middle Position, which is a very weak off-suit disconnected hand (7-5 offsuit). With four players left to act behind me, including the aggressive blinds and button, opening this hand has negative expected value. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand 100% of the time from this position. My stack is healthy at 82 big blinds, so there is no pressure to make desperate moves.

Cutoff with A♦K♥ — a premium hand and perfect for an open-raise. There’s only the blinds in so far (pot 150, to-call 100). I’ll open to 300 (3x the BB / the standard sizing at this table) rather than min-raising to 200: 300 gives good fold equity against the button and blinds, reduces the chance of multiway pots, and keeps plenty of post‑flop maneuverability with ~150BB effective stacks. If I face a 3‑bet from an aggressive opponent I’ll re-evaluate (4‑bet or call depending on sizing and player), and if called I’ll C-bet favorable flops to fold out weaker hands and extract value from worse aces/kings.

Cutoff opens to 3bb and we’re on the Button with J6o. This hand is heavily dominated by Minni’s opening range (better Jx, broadways), plays poorly multiway, and calling invites squeezes from the blinds. It’s not a profitable 3-bet candidate either (no blocker value beyond a weak J, poor playability when called). Preserve stack and wait for better spots in position.

I am in the small blind with 6-4 offsuit. Minni has raised from the cutoff to 300. This is a very weak hand to defend with, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand. The pot odds are not favorable enough to justify a call with such a low-equity hand.

JTs suited is a strong hand in the BB. Minni has been opening aggressively from late position. I have a nice suited connector that plays well both as a 3-bet and a call. Given I'll be OOP postflop, 3-betting allows me to take initiative and potentially win the pot now. A 3-bet to 850 puts pressure on Minni who has shown ability to fold to 3-bets.

I opened the cutoff with A♦K♥ (standard 3x open to 300). Claude (BB) 3‑bet to 850 — a common BB 3‑bet size that can include both strong hands and some wider bluff/steal candidates. With AKo and deep stacks (~149BB vs his ~93BB) I want to seize the initiative: 4‑betting isolates the pot heads‑up, gains fold equity against weaker broadways and medium pairs, and lets me build value when I’m ahead. A small 4‑bet (to the minimum 1400) would be too weak; I’ll make a clean, constructive 4‑bet to 2600 total. If Claude folds I take the pot; if he calls we play post‑flop OOP with a premium hand and good equity; if he 5‑bets shove I’ll evaluate his sizing/range but AKo has enough equity to continue in many jam scenarios.

Minni 4-bet to 2600 after my 3-bet. This is the same pattern as Hand #13 where Minni 4-bet me with KQo and I folded. JTs is a nice hand for 3-betting but doesn't have the equity to continue against a 4-bet range. Even suited connectors can't profitably call here - I'd be crushed by the likely QQ+, AK range. Clear fold.

I am in UTG position with 7s 9h, which is a weak off-suit one-gapper. Given the early position, this hand is well below the standard opening range. I will fold immediately to conserve chips and wait for a better spot or position.

Pocket tens in middle position are a clear open — strong, value-heavy hand that plays well multiway but is best when I take the initiative. UTG (Pro) folded so I can open and apply pressure to the blinds; the table’s usual open size has been ~300. Min-raising to 200 is too small and invites too many callers; I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to build a pot, get folds, and define ranges. My stack is deep enough to continue postflop, and this size is standard at this table for value and protection.

Minni is opening frequently to 3x and has shown she can fold to 3-bets, while a flat-call here (Q8s) invites a squeeze from the Button/SB and gives us a dominated top-pair problem in a multiway pot. Qh8h is a reasonable light 3-bet candidate: suited for playability when called, blocks some Qx continues, and benefits from position to apply pressure postflop. If Minni 4-bets, we can comfortably fold and lose a controlled amount; if she calls, we can c-bet selectively and leverage position.

I am on the button with 7c4c. Minni raised to 300 from middle position and FiveTwo 3-bet to 900 from the cutoff. My hand is a weak suited connector and does not warrant a call or a cold 4-bet against a raise and a re-raise, especially with two players still to act behind me in the blinds and the original raiser likely to continue. I will fold.

A2o facing a raise and a 3-bet is a clear fold. Out of position with a weak ace against two players showing strength - one of whom (Minni) has been aggressive and shown willingness to 4-bet. A2o plays poorly multiway and is often dominated by stronger aces in their ranges.

Preflop in the big blind facing a raise to 300 from Minni in middle position and a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo in the cutoff. My hand is J5o, which is a weak, offsuit, gapped hand with poor playability and low equity against a strong 3-bet range. Pot odds require calling 800 into 1350 (about 37% equity needed), but J5o has far less against typical 3-bet ranges from an aggressive cutoff over an open. No position post-flop, and risk of Minni continuing makes it multiway. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament (hand 42/150). Prioritizing selective defense to maximize long-term stack growth.

I opened to 300 from middle with TT and got 3-bet to 900 from the cutoff. TT is a hand I want to play for value/isolation against a later-position 3-bet: it has good equity versus a wide 3-bet range (AK/AQ, medium pairs, suited broadways and some bluffs) and it plays well postflop. I’m out of position, so I prefer to either take the pot down preflop or build it on my terms. A 4-bet to a meaningful size achieves both — it folds out some of his weaker broadways/Ax hands, isolates heads-up, and keeps postflop SPR manageable. I’ll 4-bet to 2,500 total (well above the 1,500 minimum) — big enough to apply pressure and get value from worse, but not so large that a 5-bet shove becomes trivially correct for either player. Plan: if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I proceed with standard postflop strategy (c-bet dry boards for value/protection, check/control on coordinated boards); if he 5-bet shoves I will usually fold TT to a clear shove since that range is likely polarized to AA/KK/AK.

Minni’s 4-bet sizing to 2500 is small and she’s shown aggression in these spots, but Q8s is still a poor continue versus any reasonable 4-bet range: we’re dominated when we make top pair, and we won’t realize equity well even with position because SPR will be low (~1.3) and she can pressure us off marginal holdings. A 5-bet jam also isn’t attractive—fold equity is limited given her price to call and our blockers are weak. Best EV is to cut losses and preserve a playable stack.

UTG with pocket sixes (6c6d) in a 6-max game: a small/medium pocket pair that’s worth opening for value and to avoid a multiway pot where my set-mining equity is diluted. Pot is 150 and calling 100 (limping) is passive and gives too good odds to the blinds/late callers. Table open sizes have commonly been ~250–300, so a standard 3x BB to 300 is size-consistent, provides fold equity and builds a pot I can set-mine. My stack (17175) is deep enough to play postflop. Action plan: open-raise to 300. If I face a small/medium 3-bet I’ll call to pursue sets and postflop play; if I face a large 3-bet/4-bet shove from a big stack I’ll strongly consider folding to avoid getting stacked with a vulnerable pair.

UTG has been opening frequently and is capable of putting in big 4-bets, so defending too loose can get expensive. JTo (offsuit) is dominated by a large portion of a reasonable UTG range (AJ/KJ/QJ/JJ/TT/AT, etc.), and flatting invites squeezes from CO/BTN and leaves us in tough reverse-implied-odds spots when we make one-pair hands. 3-betting would be a marginal bluff into a player who can 4-bet aggressively, forcing us to fold and burn chips. With ~85 BB effective, the disciplined +EV play is to pass and preserve stack for better spots.

Minni has been playing very aggressively and opening frequently, but K5s is a weak hand to play against an UTG raise, especially given Minni's recent tendency to 4-bet. Folding is the most prudent move to avoid getting into a marginal spot with a dominated hand.

I have 98o on the button facing a UTG raise from Minni (who has been quite active). While 98o isn't premium, I have position and this is a playable suited-connector type hand. Minni has shown aggression with 4-bets before (hands 13, 41), so 3-betting light is risky. Calling to see a flop in position is reasonable - we can outplay post-flop or fold if we miss.

AQ offsuit is a premium hand, top-tier for 3-betting. Facing an UTG raise from Minni (tight-aggressive range) and a button call from Claude (wider range), 3-betting from the SB isolates against Minni's strong range where we have good equity (dominates AJs-ATs, KQs, etc.) and thins the field to reduce multiway pots OOP. Calling OOP multiway risks tough postflop spots with position disadvantage. Folding is too tight for our goal of maximizing stack in a tournament. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) builds the pot for value while maintaining fold equity against lighter calls. Min raise is 500, but 900 is standard. Our 57BB stack supports this.

I hold Kc 2d in the Big Blind. The action has been heavy: UTG opened to 300, Button called, and the Small Blind squeezed to 900. K2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly in multi-way 3-bet pots. I am getting poor odds to call (requiring 800 more chips into a pot of 1600), and the original UTG raiser still has to act behind me, presenting a threat of a 4-bet. Given the strength shown by the SB and the vulnerability of my hand, folding is the only correct play to preserve my healthy stack of 81BB.

I opened UTG to 300 with 6c6d and Elon (SB) 3‑bet to 900. The pot is ~1,600 and I must call 600 more (total = 900) or 4‑bet to 1,500. Pocket sixes are a classic set‑mining hand — facing a small/medium 3‑bet I prefer to call and realize equity rather than 4‑bet out of position. I only have ~11.8% to flop a set, but Elon's effective stack (~4k behind his 900) gives reasonable implied odds if I hit. A 4‑bet here would be thin and could commit me with a vulnerable pair; folding is too conservative versus this 3‑bet size. Plan: call to keep the pot size reasonable, fold to heavy aggression on dry flops, and extract value if I hit a set.

I have 98o on the button facing Elon's squeeze to 900. Minni called. Pot is 2200, I need 600 more. Getting about 3.7:1 pot odds (27% equity needed). 98o is speculative but I have position on both players. With implied odds if I hit a straight or two pair, calling is marginally profitable. Folding is fine too but I'll take the flop given position.

AQo flops top pair top kicker on a dryish board with clubs (A-5-3cc). Strong value hand that dominates most Ax in ranges and beats draws/bluffs. Multiway pot OOP after my preflop 3-bet, which Minni (tight UTG) and Claude (wide BTN) called, so their ranges are capped somewhat (Minni: JJ+, AK/AJs, Claude wider). Betting protects against clubs/straight draws (2-4, 4-6, 6-7), builds pot for value, and takes advantage of fold equity vs worse Ax (AJ, AT). Check risks giving free cards to draws or facing check-raises. Size to ~1/2 pot (1400) balances value/protection without overbetting multiway; larger risks foldout from worse hands while smaller invites multiway. My 4k effective stack supports this aggression to grow stack in tournament.

I will call 1400. Holding 6♣6♦ on 3♣5♣A♥ I have a medium pocket pair with very limited direct improvement (only 2 outs to make trips; ~8.4% to hit by the river, backdoor club flush is tiny). Elon 3‑bet preflop and now c‑bet 1,400 into the pot — calling 1,400 into the 4,200 pot (making it 5,600) requires ~25% equity. Raising here would be thin and I’d often be playing a bloated pot out of position against a range that contains many Ax and overpairs; folding now surrenders too much after my preflop investment. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity (and get additional callers for implied odds if I hit), and preserves the option to fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve to a clear value hand (trips) or the turn changes dynamics in my favor.

I have 9c8h on an A-5-3 board with two clubs. I completely missed this flop - no pair, no real draw (only a weak backdoor flush with the 9c). Elon bet 1400 and Minni called, showing strength from both players. I'm drawing nearly dead here against likely aces or better hands. Even getting ~4:1 pot odds, I need about 20% equity and I have essentially 0%. Easy fold.

TPTK remains very strong on this turn board (3c5cAh8c), blocking nut flush draws with Ad. Minni's UTG raise + 3bet call + flop call range is polarized: nutted hands (sets, two pairs, strong Ax like AK/AQ which we dominate, nut flush possible but less likely as AcX might raise flop) or stubborn medium strength (AJ-AT, QQ-JJ). Shove commits ~2/3 pot (3525 into 5600), extracting value from all worse Ax, overpairs, and any stubborn calls while denying equity to flush draws (e.g., 4c6c, JcTc), gutshots (46), and combo draws. Checking OOP risks free cards to draws completing or facing a bet we call anyway; shoving realizes equity maximally in tournament spot (hand 43/150, growing stack). Her large stack (14k) allows calls, but fold equity exists vs bluffs/draws. Line consistent with flop aggression.

Fold. Pot is 9,125 and I must call 3,525, so I need ~3,525 / (9,125+3,525) ≈ 28% equity to break even. I hold 6♣6♦ on 3♣5♣A♥8♣: I have a pair of sixes and a one-card (river) club draw. Unique outs are the two remaining sixes + the 9 remaining clubs = 11 outs → raw chance ≈ 11/46 ≈ 24%. Many of those outs are not clean: a river club often still loses to hands that already have a club-flush (two clubs in hand), and hitting a 6 (trips) still loses to sets/full houses and won’t beat made flushes. Elon’s line (preflop 3-bet, big flop bet, shove on turn) strongly favors Ax, sets, or made flushes — ranges that dominate or heavily crush my hand. My estimated effective equity is well below the ~28% required, so calling would be +EV negative. I fold to preserve stack and wait for a better, cleaner spot.

6-handed UTG, 6♠3♠ is simply too weak to open profitably. It makes dominated pairs/weak top pairs and relies on rare straights/flushes with poor reverse-implied-odds, and limping invites frequent isolation from the more aggressive players behind. With ~85bb effective, preserving EV by folding is best.

I am in Middle Position with King of Diamonds and Four of Clubs. This is a very weak hand (offsuit, low kicker) and does not warrant an open or a call. With aggressive players like Minni and Claude behind me, there is no value in playing this hand. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening.
In cutoff with 89o after two folds. This is a reasonable spot to open raise with a connected hand that has decent playability. Standard 2.5x open to put pressure on the remaining players.

Preflop on the button with 2s9d offsuit, a complete trash hand with no playability, straight potential, or suitedness. Claude's cutoff raise to 250 (2.5BB) indicates a strong range, likely including all pairs, broadways, suited connectors, and Ax. My hand has negligible equity against this range (~20-30% at best heads-up, less multiway). Pot odds to call 250 into 400 are poor for a speculative hand, and even in position, postflop playability is awful without improvement. Folding preserves stack for better spots. No history suggests Claude is overly wide here; he's shown aggression but folding trash avoids tough decisions.

I hold 7s 3h in the Small Blind, facing a raise to 250 from Claude in the Cutoff. 7-3 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker, with no connectivity or suited potential. Playing this hand out of position against a raise is significantly negative EV. The cost to call is 200 chips into a pot of 400, offering 2:1 odds, but my equity realization will be terrible. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Claude opened to 250 from the CO. I’m on the BB with A♠4♥ and stacks are deep (I have 14,775; Claude 8,150). Pot is 400 and I need to add 150 more to call, which would make my total committed 250. Calling here is the standard and lowest-variance play: A4o is worth defending from the BB vs a CO open — decent showdown value, an ace-blocker and reasonable equity vs a CO opening range. The call price gives me ~27% break-even equity (150 into a 550 pot), which A4o will generally meet vs a typical CO range. 3-betting is an option as a bluff, but it bloats a pot out of position against a somewhat capable opponent who can 4-bet or shove; with deep stacks I prefer to realize equity postflop. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed based on texture (value bet/raise when I hit an ace or strong draw, fold to big aggression when clearly dominated).

I have A♠4♥ on 8♣5♥4♦ — I’ve paired the board (pair of 4s) with an Ace kicker. It’s checked to me heads‑up; pot is 550 and stacks are deep (I: 14,625; Claude: 8,150). This is a marginal value hand out of position on a very dry, rainbow board with few strong draws. Betting here would get value from some worse A‑high and broadway hands but also bloats the pot and invites check‑raises or big turn action from overpairs/sets that currently beat me. I prefer pot control: check and see how Claude reacts. If he bets a small C‑bet I’ll call to realize equity and plan to fold to heavy aggression; if he checks back I can take a free turn (and target an Ace or improve later). If I improve (two pair/top pair) I’ll be willing to build the pot or raise, but for now keeping the pot small OOP is the lowest-variance, +EV line.

Flopped top pair (8s) with a 9 kicker on 8c5h4d. Minni checked to me. This is a clear value bet spot - I need to charge draws (6-7 for straight) and get value from worse hands. Betting around 60% pot for value and protection.

I have A♠4♥ on 8♣-5♥-4♦ — bottom pair with a strong ace‑kicker. Claude opened CO and now makes a modest ~350 c‑bet into a 900 pot (~38% pot). The price to call (350) gives me ~28% break‑even equity (350/(900+350) ≈ 28%), which A4x will generally meet vs a typical CO continuation range that contains many overcards and floats as well as some pairs. With deep stacks but out of position I want to keep the pot under control and realize equity rather than inflate the pot with a check‑raise or bluff; calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets me fold to big turn aggression that usually denotes an overpair/set. Plan: call the flop, see the turn, fold to very large/committing action, call or continue vs small turn bets, and value‑build if I improve (Ace or two‑pair). Total committed after this call will be 600 (250 preflop + 350 flop).

Board is 8♣‑5♥‑4♦‑3♦; I hold A♠4♥ — bottom pair with a very good kicker. Claude opened CO and c‑bet the flop; I called and he now checks the turn after the 3♦. That line (bet flop, check turn) usually represents a medium/uncertain range: floats, medium pairs or hands that don’t want to build a big OOP pot. I’m out of position with a marginal one‑pair hand and deep stacks — my priority is pot control and realizing equity rather than bloating the pot where I’m likely behind to overpairs/sets. Checking preserves showdown equity, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me fold to clear strength while still extracting value if I improve on the river (A or 4) or calling small river bets. Plan: check behind the turn, re‑evaluate on the river (value‑bet if I pair up, call small bets with my kicker, fold to large overbets).

Still have top pair (8s) with 9 kicker. Turn 3d creates a 2-6 straight draw and second diamond. Minni checked again. I should continue betting for value and protection - there are plenty of draws I need to charge. Betting around 65% pot.

I checked the turn to control the pot and see a river, but Claude now bets 800 into a 2050 pot. Calling here requires ~28% equity (800 / (2050+800) ≈ 28%), while my hand (A♠4♥ – bottom pair with a strong kicker) has very little improvement equity: only 5 clean outs to trips/two‑pair (3 Aces + 2 Fours) ≈ 11% to hit the river, and my current showdown equity versus Claude’s continuing range (preflop CO opener who c‑bet the flop and barrels the turn) is likely well below the required 28%. Claude’s line heavily contains overpairs, top pairs and sets that beat me and won’t fold to this bet size. Raising as a bluff OOP is a poor risk/reward spot — his range has many calling combos and a raise would bloat a pot I’m likely behind in. With deep stacks and better spots ahead, the correct low‑variance +EV decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the UTG position with 8c 6h, which is a very weak hand. In a 6-max tournament, opening from early position requires a much stronger range to account for the players behind me. Given the aggression shown by players like Minni and Claude in previous hands, playing a speculative and weak hand like 86o out of position is a losing strategy. Folding is the standard and correct play.

K4 suited in middle position. Suited king has playability with flush potential. Standard open raise to 2.5x is reasonable here - not a premium hand but playable with position on the blinds if I get called.

AQo is a strong offsuit hand in cutoff position facing a middle position raise to 250 into a 400 pot. This hand has excellent equity against Claude's likely raising range (which includes worse aces, broadway, and some suited connectors based on history). 3-betting applies pressure, builds the pot for value, and leverages my position postflop. Button, SB, and BB can still act, but my stack depth (91BB) allows for this play. Standard sizing around 3x the raise (750) is appropriate to isolate or get folds from weaker hands while denying equity to speculative calls.

I hold 5c 9d on the Button. The action began with Claude opening to 250 from Middle Position, followed by Elon 3-betting to 750 from the Cutoff. My hand is unsuited and disconnected with very low equity, especially against a 3-bet range. Cold-calling a 3-bet with 95o is strictly -EV, and bluffing (4-betting) here is ill-advised without blockers against players showing strength. The only standard play is to fold.

Claude opened to 250 and Elon 3-bet to 750. I’m in the small blind with K♥3♥. To call I must put in 700 (total 750) into a pot of 1150, so I need roughly 37.8% equity to justify a call (700/(1150+700) ≈ 37.8%). K3s is heavily dominated by a typical 3‑bet range and has poor playability out of position (and the BB can still act). A min‑raise to 1250 as a bluff is too risky OOP against an aggressive 3‑better. Preserve my 14,225 stack for better spots — fold here.

We’re facing an UTG+? open (250) and a cold 3-bet to 750 with action still potentially reopening behind us (Claude can continue). 8♠6♣ offsuit plays very poorly multiway and out of position versus a tight/strong 3-bet range: low equity, lots of reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs, and no suitedness to realize straight potential efficiently. Despite the pot odds, our realization is too low in this spot, so we preserve stack and wait for better defend/3-bet candidates.

Elon 3-bet to 750 after my open. K4s is not strong enough to continue against a 3-bet. I'm out of position with a marginal hand that doesn't play well postflop against a 3-betting range. The pot odds of ~2.3:1 aren't good enough for a suited king with a weak kicker. Folding is the disciplined play here.
8s2s is a very weak hand, especially from UTG position with 5 players to act behind me. This is a clear fold - the hand has almost no playability and poor equity against calling ranges.

Preflop in middle position after UTG fold. Holding 9d2d, a weak suited hand with poor playability and low equity against typical calling ranges. Pot is 150 with blinds posted, facing a to-call of 100. Standard play is to fold trash hands like this early in the hand to avoid difficult postflop situations and preserve stack for premium spots. No steal incentive given position and opponent stacks.

I am in the Cutoff with 7c 9h, which is an off-suit one-gapper and generally considered a weak hand. With three players left to act behind me, including the Button and Blinds, the likelihood of stealing the blinds is not high enough to justify opening with such low equity. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from this position.

On the button with 3♠T♦ — a weak, unsuited hand and not a standard open-raise candidate. Calling 100 into a 150 pot is cheap relative to my 14,225 stack and keeps positional advantage vs the blinds. Plan: see the flop and only continue with meaningful equity (top pair, strong draws); fold to significant preflop 3-bets or aggressive action that suggests I’m dominated. This preserves chips while giving me a chance to take the pot post-flop or realize equity when the board is favorable.

Button limp ranges are typically capped/weak in this lineup, and completing with J7o invites a tough multiway pot out of position versus the BB. I’ll iso-raise to take initiative and win the dead money often; if I face a 3-bet I can comfortably fold this hand. If called, I’ll play a controlled pot and c-bet/selectively barrel on boards that favor my perceived range.

I have Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in a 6-max game. After the Button limped and the Small Blind raised to 450, I should 3-bet to build the pot, take initiative, and potentially narrow the field. A raise to 1400 (roughly 3x the previous raise plus the dead money) is a standard size that puts pressure on both opponents and asserts the strength of my holding.

Holding 3♠T♦ on the button is a weak, unsuited hand with poor equity against a 3‑bet. I already planned to fold to significant preflop aggression with marginal holdings. Calling 1,300 to a pot of 1,950 is not profitable — I’ll be out of position in a multiway pot and often dominated by the 3‑bettor’s range. A 4‑bet bluff to 2,350 is too risky here. Preserve the 14k+ stack and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

We iso-raised a limper with a hand that’s fine as a one-and-done steal, but once the BB 3-bets, our Jh7d is in very bad shape: dominated by all Jx that continue, crushed by the value range (AJ/KJ/QJ, big pairs), and it realizes equity poorly out of position. Calling creates a low SPR pot where we’ll be forced into tough, often losing, postflop decisions without the ability to credibly apply pressure. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive with weak blockers and deep enough stacks that we can get played back at. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

82 offsuit is an extremely weak hand from UTG in a 6-handed tournament with deep stacks (~95BB). It has no high card value, no suitedness for flush potential, poor connectivity for straights, and is easily dominated. Folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV in the tournament.

I hold Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o) in Middle Position. With roughly 80 big blinds, I am deep-stacked. A2o is a weak hand to open from early/middle position because it is easily dominated by better Aces and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against calls from the Cutoff or Button. With four active players left to act behind me, the likelihood of running into a better hand is significant. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Cutoff with K♠7♥ — a marginal but playable hand in position. UTG and MP folded, so this is a good steal spot: I want to take the initiative and deny good odds to the blinds. Raise to 300 (3x the BB) is a standard sizing here — enough fold equity to pick up the blinds and isolate the button while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: if everyone folds I take the blinds; if called I’ll continuation-bet favorable dry/high-card flops and play cautiously with a single pair or weak K; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold (K7o is poorly positioned vs a 3‑bettor’s range).

Minni’s cutoff open to 3bb represents a fairly wide range, but K6o is too dominated and plays poorly as a flat (lots of reverse implied odds when we make a weak K). Turning it into a 3-bet bluff is also unattractive given Minni has shown willingness to 4-bet/put pressure on reraises, and the blinds (especially Flash) can be active, increasing the chance we get squeezed or forced into tough spots. With no antes and plenty of better button defends available, this is a disciplined fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Q8o. Minni, who has been playing quite aggressively, raised to 300 from the Cutoff. Q8o is a weak hand to play out of position, and calling 250 more into a 450 pot doesn't offer great value, especially with Claude still to act in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

5h3s is a very weak offsuit hand. Minni has been active with raises. I'm getting 2.25:1 odds but 53o plays poorly out of position - hard to make strong hands even when hitting. Fold is the disciplined play.

I am holding 7-2 suited (7c 2c) in the UTG position. This is widely considered one of the weakest starting hands in poker due to its lack of connectivity and low rank. Being in early position (UTG) with 5 players left to act makes this an easy fold. There is no strategic merit to playing this hand; entering the pot would be a significant negative expected value play.

8♠6♦ is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability out of position. There are several players yet to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB) and the table shows frequent raisers — calling 100 into a 150 pot gives poor immediate pot odds and leaves me vulnerable to raises or playing a difficult multiway flop. With a healthy ~143bb stack there's no reason to gamble with this marginal holding; folding preserves my stack for a better spot.

With 6h5h in the cutoff (two folds ahead), this is a standard open: suited connector with good playability, can make strong disguised hands, and benefits a lot from position. Raising also pressures the blinds and avoids going multiway via a limp where our equity realization drops. I’ll use a normal CO sizing to keep my range consistent and not bloat the pot unnecessarily against the more aggressive players behind.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. A player in the cutoff (FiveTwo) has opened with a standard 2.5x raise. T7o is a weak hand that does not play well against a raise, even with the advantage of position. While I have a healthy stack, there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand here and risk chips unnecessarily. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play in this situation.

K3o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a cutoff raise. While pot odds are decent (200 to win 400), K3o plays poorly postflop - often dominated by better Kx hands, poor connectivity, and I'll be out of position. This is a standard fold.

Facing an isolated cutoff raise after multiple folds, FiveTwo's opening range is wide, including many suited connectors, broadways, and pairs. 5c4c is a suited connector with excellent playability postflop for straights, flushes, and combo draws. Deep effective stacks (94BB/78BB) provide strong implied odds. Pot odds are favorable: calling 150 more into 400 (27% equity needed), and 54s realizes ~38% vs. a typical CO range while dominating many worse suited hands. Folding wastes BB defense equity; 3-betting risks playing out of position with a mediocre hand. Standard defensive call to realize equity multiway potential if others wake up, but heads-up here.

Preflop call with 54s was standard BB defense vs CO raise for playability and implied odds deepstacked. Flop 3s Th Td completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot would require 62 for wheel, but irrelevant), no flush draw. Pure air with minimal backdoors. Heads-up OOP vs C-range which hits this board decently (TT, Txb, Ax suited, pairs). Pot 550, first to act: check-fold optimal as c-bet likely (~60-70% freq), no fold equity or semi-bluff value without draws. Raising dead money suicidal OOP with no equity. Preserve stack for better spots; folding to bet doesn't lose value since we realize 0 EV here.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on a paired, high-card flop (TT3) after BB checks. This texture is generally good for my range advantage (overpairs, broadway highs, some Tx), while Elon has a lot of unpaired hands that will have a tough time continuing versus even small pressure. With 6h5h I have little showdown value but some backdoors (hearts + wheel/straight runouts), so a small range c-bet is the best way to win the pot immediately and keep my line consistent without bloating the pot. If called, I can barrel selectively on good turns (A/K/Q/J, heart, 4/7) and otherwise shut down.

Facing a small continuation bet of 150 into a pot that will be 700 after the call OOP on a paired Td Th 3s flop with 5c4c (complete air: no pair, no flush draw, gutshot straight draw at best to a 2 or 6 but poor equity ~10-15% vs. CO cbet range heavy on TT, T9o/s, ATs, KQs, mid pairs, some bluffs but board favors value). Preflop call was fine BB defense with suited connector for playability deep (~92BB effective vs 76BB villain), but flop misses entirely. Small bet sizing could be trapping strong hands or inducing, but check-fold preserves stack as we have no fold equity to raise, bad odds to float OOP (need ~18% equity, have less), and backdoors too speculative. FiveTwo's CO range hits this board reasonably well; folding realizes 0 EV spot without bloating pot for turn shenanigans. Better spots ahead with 100+ hands left.

UTG in a 6-max table with 9♠7♠ — a playable suited connector with good postflop playability and implied odds given deep stacks (≈143 BB). I want to take the initiative and isolate the blinds rather than limp and invite multiway action. Standard open size here is ~3x the big blind to both gain fold equity and keep the pot manageable if called. Plan: open to 300. If I face a small 3-bet from the blinds I can consider calling and playing postflop; fold to a large 3-bet that prices out profitable implied-odds play or commit too much of my deep stack.

AKo is a premium hand vs a 6-max UTG 3x open. I want to 3-bet for value (dominating a large part of Minni’s opening range like AQ/AJ/KQ and medium pairs), take initiative, and reduce the chance of tough multiway spots with players behind (CO/BTN/SB/BB). Given the 300 open and stacks ~80bb, a sizing around 3.5x pressures flats while keeping Minni’s worse hands in. If Minni 4-bets, I’ll reassess based on sizing/tendencies, but this is a clear value 3-bet.

Minni opened from UTG and FiveTwo 3-bet from Middle Position. My hand, Qc 9c, is a decent speculative hand but it is not strong enough to call a 3-bet in this configuration, especially with the original raiser still to act behind me. FiveTwo's 3-bet against an UTG open typically represents a very strong range. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.
32o is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a raise and a 3-bet, this is a clear fold. No reason to call 1050 with trash.

Preflop in the small blind with A7o facing an UTG open-raise to 300 from Minni and a 3-bet to 1050 from FiveTwo in middle position. The pot is 1500, and it costs 1000 more to call (10% of my 9225 stack), with the big blind (Pro) yet to act. A7o is an offsuit ace with a weak kicker, lacking the strength or playability to profitably defend against two aggressive actions suggesting strong ranges (e.g., QQ+, AK from 3-bettor). Folding is standard to avoid difficult postflop spots OOP with marginal equity, preserving stack for superior opportunities in this deep-stack tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with Q8o, facing a UTG open to 300 and a 3-bet from Middle Position to 1050. This action indicates strong ranges from my opponents (typically JJ+, AK, AQs for the 3-bettor against UTG). Q8o is dominated and plays very poorly out of position in a 3-bet pot. The price to call (950 chips) is far too high for this hand strength. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I opened UTG to 300 with 9♠7♠ to take the initiative, but FiveTwo has 3-bet to 1050. Calling 750 more to play out of position is marginal — the immediate pot odds (~33%) require more equity than 9♠7♠ typically has vs a MP 3-bet (likely weighted to strong value hands). Postflop play will be difficult OOP and a 4-bet bluff (min to 1800) is too thin here given the risk of a shove or a call from better hands. With ~140 BB I can preserve my stack and wait for a better, more favorable spot, so I fold.

6-handed and 88bb deep, A3s is a standard UTG open: it has good playability (nut-flush potential, wheel/straight possibilities) and benefits from taking initiative. It also blocks strong Ax holdings, making it a reasonable candidate to continue against 3-bets at some frequency. I’ll use a consistent 2.5x sizing to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable out of position versus later seats.

I am holding Jack-Seven offsuit in middle position. FiveTwo has opened from under-the-gun for 2.5x the big blind. J7o is a weak hand that does not play well against an UTG range, and there are still three players left to act behind me. Calling or raising here with such a marginal holding would be a mistake. The optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

J7 suited in the cutoff facing a UTG open. While suited gives some playability, J7s is too weak against an UTG opening range. The gap between J and 7 limits straight potential, and if I hit my flush draw I could easily be dominated. Standard fold here.

93 suited is a speculative hand with poor high-card strength and gapped connectors. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who shows some aggression in history but UTG suggests a strong range (top 15-20%: pairs, broadway, suited aces). Position on button is advantageous for speculative hands, but pot odds are 250 to call into 400 (38.5% pot, requires ~25% equity), and 93s has roughly 30-35% equity vs tight UTG range but poor playability OOP if blinds call/raise. Blinds (Pro SB, Minni BB) may squeeze or call, complicating. Deep stacks but tournament phase favors discipline over marginal calls. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I hold 4s 5s in the Small Blind, facing a 2.5x raise (250) from FiveTwo in UTG. While 4s 5s is a suited connector with potential implied odds, playing it from the Small Blind against an UTG range is generally -EV due to being out of position for the rest of the hand. The pot odds are roughly 3:1, but the risk of being squeezed by the aggressive Big Blind (Minni) and the difficulty of realizing equity out of position make this a fold. FiveTwo has been showing aggression recently, making it less likely I can steal pots post-flop easily.

I’m in the big blind with pocket tens facing an UTG open to 250. TdTs is too strong to just fold and calling keeps the raiser’s range wide and lets speculative hands see a cheap flop; however I want to take the initiative, isolate the opener and get some fold equity. With deep stacks (we ~138bb, opener ~85bb) a standard 3‑bet for value is appropriate. I’ll 3‑bet to 900 total — large enough (≈3.5x the open) to fold out weaker Ax and small pairs, while still leaving room to play post‑flop if called. Plan: if FiveTwo folds we pick up the pot; if called I’ll C‑bet on many dry flops and proceed cautiously on ace‑high or coordinated boards. If FiveTwo 4‑bets big (heavy shove/very large 4‑bet) I will reassess and likely fold to avoid getting it in badly behind against JJ+/AK.

Minni’s BB 3-bet versus an UTG open is typically value-heavy, so a light 4-bet bluff will run into a lot of continues (calls/jams) and creates an inflated pot with a hand that doesn’t love getting put to the test for ~85bb. A3s is still a reasonable defend: we have position, strong playability (nut-flush potential + wheel/straight boards), and an ace blocker that slightly reduces the top of Minni’s range. Calling keeps our range protected and lets us realize equity without overexposing our stack.

Heads‑up vs the UTG caller; I hold TT — an overpair to this flop (2♠‑9♥‑8♠). The board is somewhat coordinated (9‑8 connected and two spades), so there are some sets (88/99/22) and multi‑card spade draws to worry about, but as the preflop 3‑bettor my range is strong and I should apply pressure. A half‑pot continuation bet (~50%) achieves three things: extracts value from worse hands (we beat Kx, Ax without a pair, single overcards and many small pairs), prices/charges spade and straight draws, and preserves pot control against the worst made hands. I’ll bet 900 into the 1850 pot. Plan if called: re‑evaluate on the turn — proceed cautiously if the turn brings a spade or pairs the board; if villain raises big on the flop I’ll assume sets/two‑pair or a very strong draw and fold to large aggression (call smaller, non‑committal raises only to see a safe turn).

We flop the nut flush draw (A♠3♠ on 2♠9♥8♠) plus some overcard value, and Minni’s ~1/3-pot c-bet gives us excellent direct odds: calling 900 to win ~3650 means we need only ~25% equity, and the nut FD alone is often ~35%+ versus overpairs/high cards. Raising is an option as a semi-bluff, but versus a BB 3-bet range that’s typically value-heavy (overpairs/AK) the fold equity is limited and we risk getting jammed off our draw for a big portion of our stack. Best is to call and realize equity, keeping in bluffs and allowing us to apply pressure on favorable turns (spade, A, some runouts).

Turn: my TdTs have improved to trips with the Tc on the turn — an extremely strong hand against the opponent's calling range. FiveTwo called my preflop 3‑bet and then called my flop c‑bet, so his range is weighted toward medium pairs (99/88/22), 9x/8x, some Ax/Kx, and spade/straight draws. Very few hands in that range beat trips (the one remaining Ten is the main worry and is very unlikely). I should be building the pot for value and charging draws rather than checking and giving a free river. A value/denial sizing of roughly 2/3 pot makes sense here: big enough to charge spade and straight draws and to get calls from worse pairs and sets, but not so large that too many of those hands fold. Pot is 3650, so I will bet 2400 (≈66% pot). If called I’ll re‑evaluate the river and extract again on a blank or pot‑control if a scary spade/paired card arrives. If I face an unusually large check‑raise that commits most of his stack I’ll weigh the quads possibility (very rare) but generally lean toward calling down with trips unless the line is extremely polarized. Action: bet 2400 for value and to deny/charge draws.

Turn brings a bigger sizing (2400 into 3650) and our direct price to call is poor: we’d be calling 2400 to win 8450 on the river (~28% needed), while the nut flush draw is ~19.6% and A-high outs aren’t always clean versus a BB 3-bet range that contains AK/AQ/sets.

Given stack-to-pot (if we call we’ll have ~4575 behind into ~8450), the hand is naturally heading toward stacks anyway. The best way to realize our equity and avoid getting put in a miserable spot on brick rivers is to turn the draw into a semi-bluff jam: we apply maximum pressure to one-pair hands (JJ/QQ/KK without a spade) and unpaired big cards (AK/AQ without a spade) that may barrel once and then hate facing an all-in. When called, we still retain strong equity with 9 outs to the nut flush (and occasional extra wins with an Ace).

So I’m shoving to maximize fold equity + equity realization.

I have trips (TdTs with Tc on board) on the turn — an extremely strong holding. Villain (UTG) called my preflop 3‑bet and my flop c‑bet, so his range is weighted toward medium pairs (99/88/22), some Kx/Ax and suited broadways/draws. I bet 2,400 on the turn to get value and charge draws; his raise to 6,975 is an all‑in. Calling costs me 4,575 more to win a pot of ~17,600 (after my call), so I need only ~26% equity to break even. Trips dominate the vast majority of his plausible range; only a small subset (made straights such as JQ/J7 or very unlikely two‑card combos) currently beat me, while sets/single pairs are behind or need a pairing river to overtake me. Given the pot odds and my hand equity, this is a +EV call. I will call the all‑in and go to the river.
2h8h is a very weak hand from UTG position. Even being suited doesn't make this playable from early position. I need to play tight here and wait for better opportunities.

Preflop, UTG+1 position after Claude folds UTG. Hand is Ts8d offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor playability from early position. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better opportunities. Pot unraised (blinds only), but limping or raising with this hand is suboptimal. Opponents have decent stacks; no need to get involved here.

I am on the Button with 6h 9s, which is a very weak unsuited hand (9-6 offsuit). Although the Button is a strong position to steal blinds, the Small Blind (Minni) has a very large stack and has demonstrated aggressive tendencies (3-betting and applying pressure). Opening with trash like 96o is unprofitable here as I will likely have to fold to a 3-bet or play a weak hand out of position against a strong range if called. Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve my 79bb stack for better opportunities.

I’m in the small blind with 5♠8♣ — a weak, unsuited hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 more (total commit 100) to see a flop, which gives me ~3:1 pot odds and is a cheap way to try to hit. Raising to 200 to attempt a steal is tempting, but Flash (BB) has shown willingness to defend and play big pots, so a raise will often be called and I’ll be playing a marginal hand OOP. Folding would just dump my posted 50 with no information. I’ll call the 50 (total 100) and re-evaluate on the flop — continue if I pair or have a good draw, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Ah 3h. After the Button and UTG players folded, the Small Blind (Minni) just completed the blind. In a blind-vs-blind situation, Ah 3h is a premium hand. While checking would allow me to see the flop for free, raising allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or build value against a wider range from the Small Blind. I will raise to 350 to exert pressure and leverage my positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

I’m in the small blind with 5♠8♣ facing a raise to 350; I must call 250 more (total commit would be 300). This is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand and I’ll be out of position for the whole hand against a single raiser (Flash). Calling 250 into a 450 pot gives ~2.8:1 pot odds (I’d need ~26% equity), but 5-8o is usually dominated by a raiser’s range and has poor postflop playability OOP. Raising to attempt a steal is also a bad look with this holding. With a deep stack I can wait for better spots and avoid getting involved in a bloated pot with a marginal hand. I’ll fold and preserve chips.

A9s is a strong suited ace hand with good playability, equity against calling ranges, and a strong ace blocker. In a 5-handed game from UTG, it's well within the opening range. Raising builds the pot, takes initiative, and denies equity to weaker hands. Standard open-raise size of 3x the BB (300 total) is appropriate given deep stacks and no prior action. Limping is too passive UTG; folding is too tight.

I am holding J3 suited in UTG+1 facing a raise from UTG (Elon). J3s is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a standard UTG opening range. With 79 BBs, there is no pressure to play marginal or weak hands. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I’m on the button with A♣ Q♥ facing an UTG open to 300 in a 5-handed game. This is a strong hand in position with a deep stack (227BB). I want to take the initiative, gain fold equity and isolate the opener rather than allowing the blinds to come along for cheap. A standard 3-bet to ~3x the open (900 total) accomplishes that — it prices out many speculative hands, sets up a heads-up pot where my hand plays very well, and leaves plenty of postflop maneuvering room. If I face a large 4‑bet I’ll re-evaluate (often fold to heavy 4‑bet sizes), but if called I’ll use position to c‑bet appropriate flops and extract value. Therefore I 3‑bet to 900.

I am in the Small Blind holding King-Seven of spades. The action started with an UTG open from Elon to 300, followed by a 3-bet from Minni on the button to 900. While suited Kings have some playability, calling here would put me out of position against two players, one of whom has shown significant aggression recently. Cold-calling a 3-bet from the SB with a marginal hand like K7s is generally a losing play in the long run. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.
Holding 4c6h offsuit facing a 3-bet from Minni. This is a trash hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range. I'm out of position with a weak holding - clear fold.

A9s remains a premium 4-bet hand against Minni's button 3-bet range, which is likely wide including broadway hands, suited aces, suited connectors, and bluffs given her aggressive history (frequent raises and 3-bets in spots like hands 13, 41, 42, 49, 50). It blocks AA/AK, has strong suited playability, and ~40-50% equity when called. Folding is too tight UTG with this strength. Calling OOP risks difficult postflop spots without initiative, especially deepstacked. 4-betting to 2400 (~2.7x her 3-bet) applies pressure, denies equity, and builds a pot when ahead. Minni folds often to aggression (e.g., folded to 4-bets/raises previously). If called, we proceed with range advantage and blockers.

Elon 4‑bet to 2400 after my 3‑bet to 900. The pot is 3450 and calling costs 1500, so I’d need ~1500/(3450+1500) ≈ 30.3% equity to break even. A♣Q♥ is frequently dominated vs a UTG 4‑bet range (QQ+/AK, maybe a couple of bluffs) and its realistic equity is right at or below that threshold. A 5‑bet here would commit a lot more chips into a likely value-heavy range and isn’t worth the risk with a deep stack — I’d rather preserve my deep stack and wait for a clearer spot. Therefore I fold to the 4‑bet.

I am in UTG position with King-Queen offsuit (KQo) in a 5-handed game. With 79 BBs, this hand is strong enough to open-raise for value, as UTG in 5-handed play is effectively Hi-Jack. It blocks strong hands like KK/QQ and plays well post-flop. I will raise to 2.5x BB (250) to build the pot and thin the field, while being cautious of the aggressive chip leader Minni to my direct left.

Pro opened to 250 from UTG. I’m UTG+1 with Ts6c — a weak, offsuit ten with poor playability and high likelihood of being dominated by an UTG opener. There are three players to act behind (button, SB, BB), increasing chances of a multiway pot where this hand performs poorly. Calling 250 into a 400 pot gives ~38% immediate pot odds, but my equity vs an UTG range is well below that and I don’t want to 3‑bet light out of position with stacks and players behind. Fold and wait for a better spot in position or with a stronger hand.

I am on the button with 5-4 offsuit. Pro has raised to 2.5 BBs from UTG. This hand is extremely weak and does not play well against an UTG opening range, which is typically quite strong. Even with the advantage of position, there's no reason to call or bluff 3-bet here with such low equity and two active players still to act behind me in the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
6h7d offsuit in the SB facing a UTG raise. This is a weak hand out of position against an early position raiser. Poor implied odds and playability OOP. Clear fold.

6d4c is a complete trash hand with no playability, low cards, offsuit, and poor connectivity. Facing an UTG raise from Pro (2.5x BB, standard sizing from early position with a strong range like pairs, Ax, broadway), defending from BB would be a loose call. Pot odds are 150 to call into 400 (2.67:1), but equity against UTG range is very low (~20-25% at best, often less). No fold equity here as I'm out of position postflop. Tournament stage is mid-early (hand 53/150), deep stacks (~100BB effective), so preserve stack for better spots. Standard fold.

UTG with pocket 4s on a 5‑handed table and very deep effective stacks (I have ~218bb). Small pocket pairs are best played by raising here to take the initiative, build a pot when I hit a set, and get some fold equity versus overcards. Limping invites multiway action and often reduces my ability to realize equity cleanly; a standard 3x open to 300 is consistent with table tendencies and keeps my options vs. 3-bets (I can fold to a large 3-bet, or call a reasonable-sized 3-bet to set-mine given deep stacks). Raise to 300 to isolate/take the lead preflop and protect my hand.

I am holding 8d 6h in UTG+1. Minni, who has a significant stack and has shown aggression in previous hands, has raised from UTG to 300. 8-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play against an UTG open, as it is easily dominated and does not play well post-flop. With three players still to act behind me, calling or raising would be highly disadvantageous and likely lead to a loss of chips. The optimal play to preserve my stack for better opportunities is to fold.
Q4 suited is too weak to call an UTG raise, even from the button. Minni has been active but Q4s lacks good playability - weak kicker, poor straight potential. Easy fold.

Preflop in the small blind facing an UTG raise to 300 (3x) from Minni, who has been aggressive in early position in prior hands and often isolates successfully. My hand is 4s3s, a speculative suited connector but very weak and low, with poor playability out of position postflop against a strong UTG range (likely high pairs, broadways, suited aces, etc.). Calling 250 into 450 pot commits ~2.5bb OOP with a hand that rarely makes strong hands and is easily dominated or outdrawn. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no fold equity squeeze viable here with BB behind and Minni deep-stacked at 21.5k. Raising is a clear bluff jam or nonsense without position. Optimal play is fold to maximize EV long-term.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 300 from Minni, who is UTG and deep-stacked. The pot is 450 (300 raise + 50 SB + 100 BB) and I need to call 200 to see a flop, giving me pot odds of roughly 3.25:1 (requiring ~31% equity). My hand, 9s2s, is extremely weak, performing poorly against a standard UTG opening range. Additionally, playing a low suited hand out of position against a big stack creates difficult post-flop scenarios and negative implied odds. The equity realization for this hand is low, making a fold the correct strategic decision.

I am dealt Ten of Hearts and Four of Diamonds in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak offsuit hand that does not belong in any standard opening range, especially from early position in a five-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 110 big blinds, and there is no reason to attempt a speculative play or a bluff with such poor equity and no positional advantage. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
A9 suited is a solid opening hand from UTG+1. This is a standard raise spot - suited aces play well with the potential for nut flushes and decent high card strength. I'll open to 2.5x.

Preflop on the button facing a 2.5BB raise from UTG+1 (Claude). A7o is a marginal hand but benefits greatly from position.
