It blocks strong Ax hands like AA/AK, and has good playability postflop if we hit top pair or draw. Pot odds are favorable (calling 250 into 400 for set mining/implied odds). Claude has been aggressive in history but often bets postflop; we can realize equity well in position. Blinds may fold or isolate poorly. Folding is too tight here; 3-betting risks playing OOP if called/shoved. Prefer calling to control pot and play postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with QJo facing a raise from UTG+1 (Claude) and a call from the Button (Elon). Although the pot odds are tempting (calling 200 to win 650), QJo is a hand that is easily dominated by the opening range of an early position player (AQ, AJ, KQ, KJ) and potentially the calling range of the button. Playing a marginal off-suit hand out of position against multiple opponents is a recipe for losing a larger pot post-flop due to reverse implied odds. Folding is the disciplined and correct long-term play here.

Holding 3♣6♦ in the big blind vs an UTG+1 open to 250 and a button call: this is a very weak, easily dominated hand. However the price to see a flop is cheap — I need to call 150 more to put a total of 250 in and the pot is already 650, so my immediate pot odds are roughly 4.3:1 (~19% equity required). With deep stacks I can afford to see a flop cheaply but I don’t want to inflate the pot with a 3‑bet bluff multiway or take marginal postflop lines out of position. Plan: call the 150 (total commit 250) to see the flop, and fold to significant aggression unless I hit a pair or develop a strong draw. No raise — hand lacks equity and blockers for a profitable 3‑bet here.

Flop 2♥ 5♠ T♠ with 3♣6♦ — I completely missed. I have no pair and only a gutshot to a 4 (4 outs, ~16% to hit by river). This is a multiway spot (Claude opened UTG+1, Elon called), so my hand plays very poorly vs continuation bets. Pot is 800 and there’s currently no bet to me, so the prudent line is to check and control the pot. Plan: check and fold to meaningful aggression (only consider calling a very small probe that gives correct pot odds); if checked through I take a free turn to try to hit the 4 or re-evaluate. No bluff/raise — preserve my 21,700 stack with such weak equity.

I was the preflop raiser with Ac9c. Board is 2h5sTs - relatively dry with a spade draw possible. I have two overcards and backdoor clubs. Standard c-bet spot to represent strength and take down the pot. Betting around 1/2 pot.

On the flop of 2h5sTs, I hold Ah7s for pure Ace-high with no pair, no flush draw (only one spade in hand plus two on board), no straight draw, and minimal backdoor potential. Pre-flop call with A7o was positionally reasonable for playability and blockers, but this board misses me completely. Claude's UTG+1 raise and flop bet (400 into 1200, ~33% pot) represents strength: likely overpairs (TT+, AJ+), Tx, or strong draws (spade flush draws like KJs, QJs, or straight draws like 67/89). Minni's check from BB suggests weakness. Pot odds are 1200:400 (3:1), requiring ~25% equity to call, but my equity is low (~20-30% at best vs his polarized range, less if value-heavy). Raising as a bluff has merit with position, Ace-blocker (blocks AA/AK/AT), and fold equity against Claude's possible one-pair hands, but risks Minni waking up and bloating pot OOP postflop with nothing. Stacks are deep (mine 98BB, Claude 78BB eff), but folding preserves stack for better spots. History shows Claude aggressive but callable in position; here, no showdown value or draw justifies continuing. Discipline: fold marginal air postflop to avoid spew.

Board 2♥-5♠-T♠, I hold 3♣6♦ — no pair and only a straight by the river with a 4 (4 outs, ~16% to hit by river). Pot is 1200 and Claude has just bet 400, so calling 400 only gets me ~25% pot odds (I need ≈25% equity). My actual equity (~16%) is well below that, and Claude opened UTG+1 then barreled the flop, so his range is heavily weighted to T‑x, overpairs or better — hands that dominate mine. I’m out of position, have poor blockers, and raising as a bluff is thin against an opener/continuation bet from UTG+1. With a healthy stack I should preserve chips for better spots rather than calling a +EV negative shot. Fold.

J8o is a weak hand from UTG position. In early position with 4 players to act behind me, I need a stronger range. This hand doesn't have good playability and will often be dominated by better Jacks. Folding is the correct play here.

Preflop, UTG+1 position after UTG fold. Holding K6h suited, which is a marginal hand at best. Early position means acting first with many players behind, likely multiway pot. K6s plays poorly postflop out of position, often dominated by better kings or playable hands. Deep stack (98BB), no need to speculate. Pot odds for limp (100 into 150) don't justify entering with this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium opportunities and build over 150 hands.

I am on the Button with 9s3h, which is a statistically very weak hand (disconnected, offsuit, low ranks). UTG and UTG+1 have folded. While the Button is a good position to steal blinds, the Small Blind (Minni) has a very large stack (21,650) and has shown aggression, making a steal attempt with trash like 93o negative EV. The risk of getting 3-bet or called is too high for a hand with such poor post-flop playability. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

Heads-up vs Flash (only BB remains). I hold 3♣6♠ — a very weak hand — but the price to see a flop is small (I need to put in 50 more). With deep stacks and being on the button (I act first preflop but last on later streets), I prefer to keep the pot small and see a flop in position rather than inflate the pot with a marginal hand or fold away 50 chips. Flash has shown both defensiveness and occasional aggression in earlier hands, so raising could induce a 3‑bet or commit me out of position to bigger action I don’t want with 3‑6. Plan: call cheaply to 100 total; on the flop I’ll continue only with a real improvement or a favourable, low‑variance board texture and otherwise fold to significant pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 5h. The Small Blind (Minni) has just completed the blind. With a speculative suited hand like this, I have the option to raise to take the pot immediately, but checking allows me to see a free flop and realize my hand's equity without increasing the pot size unnecessarily against the chip leader. Given Minni's active playstyle, I prefer to keep the pot small and see if I can catch a strong piece of the flop.

I have 3♣6♠ on Q♦8♠5♠ — no pair and only weak backdoor equity (need runner–runner spades or a straight). I’m out of position in a limp pot and have very little fold equity OOP, so a c‑bet here would be low EV: Flash will call with a wide range of better hands or apply pressure that I can’t comfortably handle. I’ll check to control the pot and try to see a free turn. Plan: if Flash fires a small bet giving correct pot odds I may call to realize backdoor equity; if he makes a sizable bet or raises I’ll fold.

On a board of Qd-8s-5s, I have flopped middle pair (eights) and bottom pair (fives), giving me two pair. The pot is very small (200) because it was a limped pot pre-flop. Minni checked from the small blind. While my two pair is vulnerable to a Queen or spade draws, betting here helps define the hand and protects against free cards for overcards or draws. I will bet 150 into the 200 pot to build value from weaker hands like a single eight, a five, or flush draws.

Board is Q♦ 8♠ 5♠; I hold 3♣6♠ — no pair, only a weak backdoor (runner‑runner) flush/straight and a few pair outs. Flash’s 150 into a 350 pot is a relatively small bet (calling 150 would make the pot 500, so I’m getting ~30% pot odds). My raw equity to improve (runner‑runner flush/straight + pair outs) is low, but the cost to see the turn is tiny relative to my 21.6k stack and matches my preflop plan to call small flop bets with junk to keep the pot small and realize any backdoor equity. Raising would be bluffing out of position with almost no fold equity and is poor here. Plan: call the small bet now, evaluate the turn — fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve, continue only with real improvement (pair, a spade on turn giving a single‑card flush draw, or a very cheap check‑back).

Check. The turn (J♠) gave me a real, low‑variance improvement: I picked up a one‑card spade flush draw (I hold 6♠ with 8♠–5♠–J♠ on board). That’s ~9 spade outs to the flush and a few extra pair outs (combined roughly ~30% to improve by the river). There’s no bet to face right now and I’m OOP, so betting would bloat the pot with a drawing hand and little fold equity versus Flash’s wide calling/continuation range. I’ll check to control the pot and either see a free river or call a small bet (good pot odds) to realize equity; I’ll fold to large turn/river aggression unless I hit.

The Jack of Spades is a highly coordinated card that completes several potential draws. It completes the spade flush and is also an overcard to my two pair (8s and 5s). Minni called my flop bet, which suggests she has at least a piece of the board or a draw. Since Minni checked to me again, I will check behind to control the pot size and realize my equity. Betting here is risky because any spade or a hand containing a Queen or Jack beats me, and I want to see a cheap showdown or a safe river card.

River is a 2♥ — my 3♣6♠ never improved (no pair, missed the spade flush). Flash bet small on the flop and then went passive on turn, so his range contains many pairs and medium-strength hands that will call a river bluff. The pot is only 500 and I have essentially zero showdown value, so bluffing OOP here is low EV and unnecessary with a 21.45k stack. I’ll check and concede to a value bet — preserve chips for a better spot.

On the river, the board is Qd-8s-5s-Js-2h. I have two pair (8s and 5s). Minni has checked for the third time (flop, turn, and river). While my two pair is relatively strong for a limped pot, the board has three spades and two overcards (Queen and Jack) to my pairs. If I bet, it is unlikely Minni will call with a worse hand, and she might check-raise with a flush or a better two pair. Checking back allows me to take my hand to showdown and realize its value without risking more chips on a board that is quite coordinated.

8s4s is a speculative suited hand but very weak from UTG in a 5-handed game. It performs poorly against likely calling or raising ranges and lacks domination resistance. Position is poor for post-flop play. Standard play is to fold and wait for stronger hands to build the stack without unnecessary risk early in the tournament.

I am in UTG+1 (MP) with 6s 4d, an off-suit one-gapper. This is a very weak hand with low equity and poor playability. With 79 BBs, there is no pressure to make desperate moves, and this hand falls well outside of any standard opening range from early position. Folding is the only standard option.

Pocket kings (Kd Kc) on the button — premium preflop. With only the SB and BB to act and deep effective stacks (my 21,450; Flash ~11,675; Claude ~8,950), standard is to raise for value and to isolate the blinds rather than limp/call. I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300 total): large enough to build the pot and discourage cheap multiway flops, but not so large as to commit my whole stack or invite unnecessary overbets. Plan: if both fold I take the blinds; if called I proceed for value postflop; if faced with a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing/tendency (call smaller 3-bets, 4-bet or shove against obvious squeezes).

I am in the Small Blind with 8-6 offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300 (3x the big blind). 8-6 offsuit is a weak hand to defend out of position, especially against the chip leader who has been playing aggressively. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff with this hand is low-frequency and risky here. I will fold to preserve my stack.
Ah5c in the BB facing a min-raise from Minni who has been very active. Getting 2.25:1 odds (200 to win 450). A5o is a reasonable defend against an aggressive player. Calling to see a flop.

I flopped trip fives with an ace kicker on 2d5d5s - an excellent hand. Minni has been very active preflop. Checking here to let them c-bet is optimal. This disguises my hand strength and allows them to continue bluffing or betting worse hands. I can then decide whether to call and trap or check-raise depending on their bet size.

I have KK on the button and opened to 300 preflop; Claude (BB) cold-called so the pot is 650 heads-up. Flop 2d-5d-5s gives me a clean overpair. The only real hands that currently beat me are 5x (including quads) or 22 (full house), which are unlikely; the more common threats are diamond draws and weaker one-pair hands (A5, small pairs, A2) that I want to charge. I’ll bet for value and protection — a ~60% pot bet to deny free cards to draws while keeping worse hands and worse pairs calling. If Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises heavily I’ll assume a very strong holding (set/quads/full house or a strong disguised draw) and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

I flopped trip fives with the ace kicker - an extremely strong hand. Minni c-bet 400 into 650. With two diamonds on board creating a flush draw, I have a decision: raise to charge draws or call to trap. Minni has been very active, so calling keeps their bluffs and weaker hands in. If I raise, they likely fold unless they have a strong holding. Best to smooth call and let them continue betting on later streets.

I have trip fives with ace kicker - a monster hand. The Queen on the turn is a safe card. Minni has been very aggressive throughout the tournament. If I bet now, they likely fold their bluffs. By checking, I give them another chance to bluff or value-bet worse hands. If they check behind, I can lead river for value. Trapping the aggressive player is optimal here.

Heads-up vs Claude (BB). Pot is 1,450 and I hold KK on 2♦-5♦-5♠-Q♣. I bet the flop and he called, so his range includes diamond draws, medium pocket pairs, some overcards, and the occasional 5x or weird hand. The turn Q gives him some new Qx combos that now beat me, but his frequency of Qx is still limited. My objectives: (1) charge diamond draws and worse pairs, (2) deny free rivers that could outdraw me, and (3) get thin value from hands like 22/33/44/A5/A2 and leftover draws. Betting again is the right play for value and protection. I choose a ~60% pot turn bet — 900 chips — large enough to charge draws and weaker pairs but not so huge as to commit me unnecessarily before river. If Claude raises big I’ll heavily discount bluffs and assume trips/Qx; I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a polarizing shove or call a small raise depending on sizing).

I have trip fives with ace kicker - a monster hand. Minni bet 900 into 2350. Time to raise for value. They could have Qx (now top pair), a flush draw, or a bluff. Raising extracts value from all these hands and protects against diamond draws. A raise to ~2200 builds the pot nicely and sets up a river shove with my remaining stack. Minni has been aggressive so they may call with Qx or draws.

I opened BTN and bet the turn (900) with KK on 2♦-5♦-5♠-Q♣ to charge draws and get value. Claude called the flop and now check‑raised my turn bet to 2,200, leaving me 1,300 to call into a 4,550 pot (≈22% pot odds). His line is very polarized and matches the hands that currently crush me: any hand with a 5 in the hole (trips/quads), pocket 22 (full house), or 2/5 combinations — these value combos are numerous versus the relatively small set of missed-draw bluffs. My equity against a heavy “5x/22” value range is well below the ~22% needed to call, and raising would only commit more chips into a range that very often beats me. I’ll preserve my stack and fold this spot.

I am holding 3c 4d (43o) from Under the Gun (UTG). This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Opening from early position with such a weak hand is a significant strategic error as it leaves me vulnerable to 3-bets and playing out of position against stronger ranges. My stack is healthy (79 BBs), so there is no pressure to make a move. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Fold. K♥2♥ is a very weak hand from UTG+1 — highly dominated by A/Kx and with poor post‑flop playability out of position. With five players still to act, deep stacks and opponents (Claude/Elon/Flash) who have shown willingness to 3‑bet or apply big pressure, opening or calling 100 here is unnecessarily speculative. Preserve the ~198BB stack and wait for a better, positionally profitable spot.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. While it is a potential stealing position, 9-6 offsuit is too weak to open with against players like Claude and Elon who have shown they are willing to defend or re-raise. I have a healthy stack and there is no need to play such a marginal hand from this position.
Folded to me in SB with K9o. This is a standard open from the SB heads-up against the BB. K9o plays well as a raise here to take initiative and potentially win the pot preflop. Raising to 250.

QcTc is a strong suited broadway connector, excellent for defending the BB against a wide SB steal-raise range from Claude, who has been aggressive in position. We have great playability post-flop with flush and straight potential, dominate many of Claude's likely calling hands (e.g., Ax suited, broadways, suited connectors), and have fold equity on a 3-bet given the dead money in the pot (SB+BB). Acting second post-flop provides positional advantage. 3-betting to 800 (roughly 3.2x the raise) balances value and bluff, maximizing fold equity while keeping SPR reasonable (~10:1 effective). Calling risks playing out of position passively; folding is too tight for this spot.

K9o is too weak to continue facing a 3-bet from the BB. I'm out of position and this hand doesn't play well against a typical 3-bet range. The pot odds of ~2:1 aren't sufficient to overcome the positional disadvantage and hand weakness. Clear fold.

UTG with J♦4♥ — a weak, offsuit rag with very poor playability and lots of dominated combos. There are four players to act after me, so I’ll be OOP postflop against callers/raisers. Calling 100 to see a flop isn’t justified (hand has low equity and reverse implied odds), and raising is a losing, marginal move from UTG. I’ll conserve my deep stack for better spots and fold preflop.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit in UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. This is a premium opening hand. I have consistently opened for 300 (3x the big blind) with my strong hands in previous rounds, and I will maintain that sizing here to build the pot and take control of the hand. Minni has already folded, so I only have to worry about the three players behind me.
J5o is too weak to defend against an UTG+1 open even from the button. While position is good, this hand has poor playability and will often be dominated by Flash's opening range. Easy fold.

5s7h is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an early position raise from Flash (UTG+1). In the small blind, out of position postflop, defending this trash hand risks chips unnecessarily. Pot odds require calling 250 into 450 (~36% equity needed), but 57o has minimal equity vs. a strong opening range. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in this tournament.

I hold Q4o in the Big Blind facing a raise to 300 from Flash in UTG+1. This is a very weak hand, easily dominated by an early position opening range (AQ, KQ, QJs, pairs). Although I am getting decent pot odds calling 200 into 450, my equity realization out of position with this trash hand will be very poor. I will likely miss the flop or hit a weak pair that is hard to play. Discretion is the better part of valor here; folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position with King-Seven offsuit (K7o). In a 5-handed game, K7o is generally considered too weak to open from early position, as it is easily dominated by the calling ranges of the players behind me and plays poorly post-flop. With a healthy stack of over 110 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands. I will fold and wait for a more favorable starting hand.

J2o is a very weak hand, especially from early position (UTG+1). This is a clear fold - it's not in any reasonable opening range from this position. The jack is easily dominated and the deuce provides no backup.

Preflop on the button with 2h4c, an extremely weak offsuit hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Early position players (Flash UTG and Claude UTG+1) have folded, leaving SB (Pro) and BB (Minni) to act after me. While button is a stealing spot, this hand has zero postflop playability and is easily dominated or outdrawn. Folding preserves my 10075 stack for better opportunities in a tournament where survival and stack maximization matter over 150 hands. No value in limping or raising such trash.

I am in the Small Blind with T4s, a weak suited hand with poor connectivity (large gap). The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Minni) has a significant chip lead (nearly 200 BBs) and has shown a tendency to defend the big blind and play actively post-flop in previous hands. While the price to complete the blind is cheap (50 chips into a pot of 150), playing a weak hand Out of Position against a deep-stacked, active opponent is likely -EV due to difficulty in realizing equity. Folding preserves my stack of ~77 BBs for better opportunities.
T4o is a weak hand in UTG position. This is an easy fold - the hand has poor playability, no connectedness, and I'm out of position against the entire table. No reason to open this.

Pocket deuces (22) is a profitable opening hand from UTG+1 in 5-handed play. It has good equity against calling ranges and set-mining potential with deep effective stacks (mine 100BB+, others similar). Raising protects against overcards and takes initiative. Standard sizing is 3x the BB to 300, which is controlled aggression early in the tournament (hand 61/150). Folding is too tight; limping invites multiway pots OOP which is suboptimal postflop.

The current hand is Q8s on the Button. The player Elon (UTG+1) has opened for a 3x raise (300 chips). While I have position, Q8s is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by an early position opening range (hands like AQ, KQ, QJs, 88+). The raise size of 3BB offers poor immediate pot odds (requiring about 40% equity to break even purely on pot odds, excluding implied odds/positional advantage). Given Elon's active and aggressive profile (showing 4-bets and strong post-flop aggression in previous hands), floating with a dominated hand is risky. 3-betting is also unappealing as Elon has shown willingness to 4-bet. Conserving the 77BB stack for better spots is the prudent play.

I have A♣7♣ in the small blind facing an UTG+1 open to 300 (pot 450, 250 to me). A7s is a playable hand with good equity and blocker value, and with deep stacks I prefer to realize equity rather than 3-bet light out of position. Calling keeps the pot manageable, keeps worse hands in, and lets me play flops where I can continue on an Ace or club/strong draw. If the big blind squeezes or Elon 3-bets large, I will fold unless the sizing is small and I can get good odds. Plan: call preflop, evaluate flop texture and opponent actions, continue aggressively on favorable boards (A or clubs/draws), otherwise play cautiously OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 7c. Elon raised to 300 from UTG+1, and Minni called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 to call. While the pot odds are attractive (3.5:1), 97o is a very weak hand to play multi-way and out of position, especially against a range from an early-position raiser like Elon. My position between Minni and Elon post-flop is also disadvantageous. I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than potentially losing more chips on later streets with a dominated or marginal hand.

Flop: K♠ 6♣ 6♦. I hold A♣7♣. I completely missed — this is just Ace‑high with only remote backdoor club potential (I have 2 clubs in hand but only one on board, so no 1‑card flush draw). Raw clean outs are limited (three Aces + three 7s = 6), and many of those are dead or weakened if Elon holds an Ace or has Kx (e.g. AK beats me if an Ace comes). Elon opened UTG+1, so his continuing range is relatively strong (Kx, broadways, pocket pairs) and he’s shown aggression in earlier hands — my bluffing equity OOP is thin and he’s likely to call or raise too often for this to be profitable.

I’ll check to control the pot and keep my options. If Elon checks back I get a free turn to realize equity. If he bets small (around ≤ ~1/2 pot) I’ll consider a call to see a turn since I can still hit an Ace/7; I’ll fold to large pressure because my hand has poor showdown value without improvement. If I hit an Ace on the turn I’ll apply pressure for value/raise depending on his turn action.

Preflop raiser checked to IP on a dry paired flop (Ks6c6d). Pocket 2s is a vulnerable underpair but blocks some 22 bluffs and has set-mining potential remaining. Villain's SB calling range (to UTG+1 raise) includes Kx, Ax suited, mid pockets (77-JJ), suited broadways/connectors. 22 has ~35-45% equity overall vs this range (strong vs air/missed draws/pockets, weak vs Kx/66). Standard c-bet for thin value (vs weak Kx/pairs), protection (vs overcards/gutshots), and to deny equity. OOP villain checks suggest possible weakness or trapping; half-pot size balances with value hands like KK/AK/KQs/66 from range. Deep stacks (eff ~9k), early in tourney (61/150), controlled aggression builds stack. Checking risks giving free card to overcards/draws or allowing villain to rep strength.

Call 350. On K♠6♣6♦ with A♣7♣ I have only Ace‑high plus a very thin backdoor club draw (need both turn+river). Raw immediate outs are ~3 Aces + 3 sevens = 6, but many are blocked or still lose to Kx/AK, so my equity is modest. Elon opened UTG+1 (relatively strong range) and bets ~1/2 pot (350 into ~700) — a standard c‑bet that represents Kx, pocket pairs and some broadways. Calling 350 into a 1050 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds, so it’s cheap enough to see a turn and try to realize equity or hit an Ace. Raising OOP as a bluff here is thin — poor fold equity and I'd be committing chips with no pair. I’ll call to see the turn, but if the turn doesn’t improve me (no A/7 and no realistic draw) I will fold to significant turn aggression; if I pair my Ace on the turn I’ll look to extract value depending on Elon’s action.

Turn analysis: the 4♠ bricks my backdoor club draw — with A♣7♣ on K♠6♣6♦4♠ I now have only Ace‑high and single‑card outs (three Aces, three 7s). Elon opened UTG+1 and barreled the flop, so his continuing range is heavy on Kx, pocket pairs and strong broadways; he’s unlikely to fold Kx/overpairs to a bluff OOP. There is no meaningful fold equity and betting here would just bloat the pot with a very weak hand and invite a raise. With no bet to call I prefer to control the pot and see the river for one more chance to pair up. Plan: check and fold to large turn/river aggression if I don’t improve; if the river pairs my Ace or 7 I’ll reassess for value/possible thin‑bluff shove depending on Elon’s action.

Pocket 22 remains a strong underpair on this paired board (Ks6c6d4s), blocking villain's possible 22 bluffs and beating weaker pairs, missed draws, and some weak Kx hands in Minni's SB calling range (e.g., KJo+, lower pockets like 55-33, suited connectors that missed). The turn 4s adds spade flush draws and gutshot straight draws (e.g., 53, 75), which we want to charge. Minni's double check OOP suggests possible weakness, capping her value range somewhat (less likely KK/66/AK here without aggression), but she could have traps. Equity vs. calling range is ~40%+, with showdown value. Deep effective stacks (~94BB for me), early tournament (61/150), pot 1400. Betting ~2/3 pot (900) for thin value, protection against draws/overcards, and to deny equity/build pot. Checking risks free cards to improving hands or allowing villain to take line as strength. Continues controlled aggression from pre-flop/flop initiative, balancing with stronger hands like KQ/KJ/66.

Fold. On K♠ 6♣ 6♦ 4♠ with A♣7♣ I have only Ace‑high — my backdoor club draw was killed by the 4♠. I have ~6 raw outs (3 Aces + 3 sevens) but several of those are weakened or dead when Elon shows up with the kind of UTG+1 continuing range that barrels flop and turn (Kx, pocket pairs, AK, strong broadways). The maths are clear: the pot is 2,300 and the bet is 900, so calling requires ~900/(2300+900) ≈ 28.1% equity to be break‑even. My raw river equity is 6/46 ≈ 13.0%, well below the required threshold. Raising is a poor option — I have almost no fold equity OOP and would be bloating the pot with a very weak hand. Given opponent type (UTG+1 open, consistent aggression this hand) and stack considerations, folding and preserving chips for a better spot is the correct play.

Pocket 3s (33) from UTG in 5-handed 50/100 NLHE. This is a playable hand with good set-mining potential and decent equity against calling ranges. Standard opening range from early position includes small pairs. Given frequent preflop raises of 250-300 in history, I'll open-raise to 300 (3x BB) to build the pot, apply pressure, and take initiative. My stack is deep (108BB), and position allows fold equity if reraised. Limp too passive, fold too tight for tournament ICM considerations.

I am holding Ad8d in UTG+1 facing a raise to 300 (3bb) from Elon in UTG. A8 suited is a marginal hand against a UTG opening range, often dominated by better Aces (AK-A9). Calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from players behind and puts me in a spot with reverse implied odds if an Ace flops. 3-betting is an option as a bluff but risky against an UTG range. Given the stack depth (77bb) and position, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing an UTG open to 3bb in a 5-handed game, Elon’s opening range is reasonably strong and often dominates small, disconnected offsuit hands. I have 6♠4♣ — very poor playability, frequently dominated, and unlikely to realize equity profitably post‑flop even with position. Calling 300 would put me in many marginal or multiway spots with little fold equity; 3‑betting as a bluff with this hand is thin and likely to get called or 3‑bet by the blinds/UTG. With a healthy 19250 stack I’m better off preserving chips and waiting for a better button spot. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight offsuit. Elon, acting from UTG, has raised to 300. King-Eight is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by an early position raising range (which typically includes AK, KQ, KJ, and pocket pairs). Defending the Small Blind out of position against an UTG raiser with this hand is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
73s is a weak hand even though suited. Facing an UTG raise, this is too weak to defend from the BB. Poor equity against a typical UTG range and I'll be out of position. Easy fold.

I am holding 42 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Positioned Under the Gun (UTG), I have zero strategic incentive to enter the pot with this hand. The correct and only reasonable play is to fold immediately.

I have Q♠2♦ in early position (UTG+1) with three players to act behind (Button, SB, BB). This is a very weak offsuit hand that will be dominated or have poor playability post-flop; calling 100 to see a flop or making a tiny open to 200 is not profitable out of position. I’ll preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot to open — fold.

I am on the button with pocket nines, a strong hand in a 5-handed game. My standard opening raise size has been 300 throughout the session, and since it has folded to me, I will raise to 300 to either take down the blinds or play a pot in position against the small or big blind.

I have pocket Aces - the best starting hand in poker. Flash raised to 300 from the button. This is a clear 3-bet for value. I want to build the pot while I have the strongest possible hand. Standard 3-bet sizing is around 3x the open, so I'll raise to 900.

T3h suited is a speculative hand with some flush and straight potential, but it's weak and gapped against a button open and small blind 3-bet. Calling 800 OOP into a 1300 pot requires ~38% equity, which T3s lacks against strong 3-bet ranges from Claude and potential calling range from Flash on the button. Postflop play would be difficult OOP multiway. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in this tournament phase with 87 hands left.

I opened to 300 with pocket nines on the button, and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. Claude has shown a high frequency of 3-betting against late-position openers. Pocket nines are well ahead of a wide 3-betting range and are too strong to fold. While a 4-bet is an option, calling allows me to play a controlled pot in position and keeps his bluffs in. I will call the additional 600 to see a flop.

Flopped top set with AA on A54 with two hearts. This is a dream flop. There's a flush draw and some straight potential, so I need to bet for value and protection. Flash called my 3-bet so they likely have a decent hand - possibly an Ace, pocket pair, or suited connectors with hearts. I'll bet around 60% pot to extract value while keeping their range wide.

Claude 3-bet me pre-flop and has now led out with a substantial bet on an Ace-high board. As the 3-bettor, Claude has a significant range advantage on this board texture, as his range is heavily weighted toward strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) and high pocket pairs. My pocket nines have been demoted to a mediocre underpair, and I am essentially only beating total bluffs or lower pocket pairs, many of which he might not 3-bet pre-flop. Given that I have very few ways to improve my hand (only two nines remain in the deck) and the likelihood that Claude will continue his aggression on future streets, I will fold to preserve my stack and avoid a difficult guessing game in a bloated pot.

UTG with Js7c is a weak offsuit hand with poor postflop playability and a bad kicker. I can limp for 100 or open to at least 200 (typical opens at this table are often ~300), but limp invites multiway pots where this hand performs poorly and an open invites 3-bets from the aggressive players behind (Claude/Elon). With deep stacks and plenty of better spots to apply pressure later, it's correct to avoid a marginal, high-variance spot and fold.

In a 5-handed game, Ace-Seven offsuit is at the very bottom of a standard opening range from this position. Given that Claude (on the Button) and Elon (in the Small Blind) have shown significant pre-flop aggression in previous hands, opening with a weak Ace is likely to lead to a difficult spot if I am 3-bet or called and forced to play out of position. I will choose the conservative route and fold this marginal hand to wait for a better opportunity.

J8o on the button with both UTG players folded. This is a standard button steal spot against just the blinds. J8o is playable here for a raise - not a premium hand but good enough to open from the button. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to try to take down the blinds.

My hand is 3c Jh, a weak offsuit gapped hand with poor playability and low equity against Claude's button opening range, which is likely wide but still favors him. I'm out of position in the SB, and calling 200 into a 400 pot offers poor implied odds given the hand's trash nature. Folding preserves my deep 108 BB stack for better spots; no need to defend this wide preflop.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd3d facing a standard 2.5x open from the Button (Claude). The pot odds are favorable; I need to call 150 to win a pot of 550, requiring roughly 27% equity. Q3s has approximately 34-36% equity against a typical wide Button opening range. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the suited nature of the hand and the cheap price make this a standard defense (call) rather than a fold. A 3-bet is not necessary here as the hand plays reasonably well post-flop as a flat call.

I am out of position with Queen-high and a backdoor flush draw on a very dry, unconnected board (7-4-2 rainbow). The board texture favors the pre-flop raiser slightly in terms of overpairs, though it hits the BB defense range reasonably well for pairs. Standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out serves little purpose here as I have no strong equity or immediate draw to protect or semi-bluff with.

I completely missed this dry 7-4-2 rainbow flop with J8o, but this is an excellent c-bet spot. The board is very low and dry, unlikely to have connected with Pro's BB calling range. I was the preflop aggressor and have position. A small c-bet of around 1/3 pot should take this down often enough to be profitable.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with Qd3d on a dry Flop of 7s 2c 4h. The pot is 550 and my opponent (Claude, Button) bets 200. While the bet is small (36% pot), offering me decent pot odds (requiring ~21% equity), my hand is very weak. I only have Queen-high with a weak kicker and a backdoor flush draw. Against a standard Button c-betting range (which includes all sets, overpairs, top pairs, and better high cards), my equity is likely below the threshold to call profitably. Furthermore, playing Q-high out of position against an aggressive opponent who is capable of firing multiple barrels (as seen in Hand #4) will be difficult on future streets. Without a pair or a strong draw, the disciplined play is to fold.

I am in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position with 2s 7c, which is statistically the weakest hand in Texas Hold'em. Given the early position and the total lack of hand strength or connectivity, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. The correct play is to fold and preserve my stack.
2c6s offsuit is a trash hand in early position. No playability, no equity against calling ranges. Easy fold.

Preflop on the button in an unraised pot (pot 150, blinds posted 50/100, Flash and Claude folded ahead). ThJd is a strong button raising hand with good equity, straight potential, and suited-ish playability in position. Raising steals blinds often against SB Pro (7450 stack) and deep BB Minni (19150), while building pot if called. Standard raise size 3x BB to 300 total for value/steal. Position allows controlling postflop. Folding too tight, calling too passive/multiway likely.

I am holding 3s 2d in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from Elon on the Button, I am out of position and have poor equity against any reasonable opening range. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.4:1, the poor playability of this hand and the disadvantage of being out of position make calling unprofitable. There is no strategic merit in defending the Small Blind with this hand. Folding is the only standard play.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button with Ad5d in the big blind. Pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (total commitment to 300) — cheap given my 19,150 stack and Elon's 10,525. A5s has strong postflop playability: nut-flush potential, wheel straight possibilities, and it blocks many Ace combinations Elon might open. 3-betting OOP with A5s is unattractive (I’d lose initiative and risk a fold or a costly fight vs stronger Aces); a tiny min-reraise to 500 won’t buy much fold equity either. I’ll call 200 to see a flop, plan to play straightforwardly: look to realize equity on diamond/Ace/5 flops, pot-control on dry boards, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I miss.

I have A♦5♦ on J♠5♣7♠ — a marginal made hand (middle pair with the best possible kicker) and only a backdoor diamond draw. I’m out of position vs the BTN open-raiser who shows a wide, often aggressive range that contains many Jx, 7x, spade draws and overcards. Betting here would deny equity to draws but also opens me up to check-raises from stronger Jx hands and bloats the pot OOP with a hand I don’t want to build big. My priority is pot control and realizing equity: check and call reasonable continuation bets, fold to large aggression, and re-evaluate on the turn (lead or value-bet if an Ace/5/diamond arrives, or fold to heavy action if the turn is hostile). This preserves my deep stack and keeps future exploitative options against Elon.

Preflop button raise with ThJd was standard for steal/value/position. Flop Js5c7s gives me top pair (Js) with T kicker, decent equity heads-up OOP vs BB caller Minni. Board draw-heavy (spade flush draw, gutshot straight draws like 46/89), so bet for value/protection against Ax/draws/pairs. Minni checked, pot 650, my stack deep (10.5bb effective? Wait, blinds 50/100, stacks deep ~100bb). Bet 2/3 pot (~400) balances value/thin value, denies equity to draws, builds pot OOP range advantage. Position allows check-back control if check, but betting exploits better hand, maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

I have A♦5♦ on J♠5♣7♠: middle pair with the best possible Ace-kicker, plus backdoor diamond/straight potential and I block many A-high combos Elon might open. Pot is 1050 and Elon bets 400 (~38% pot) — I’m getting ~27–28% required equity to call (400 / (1050+400) ≈ 27.6%), which is a very reasonable price vs a BTN c-bet range. I’m out of position and deep-stacked, so I want to control the pot rather than inflate it with a marginal hand (3-betting or check-raising is unattractive). Call to realize equity, keep weaker hands and draws in, and re-evaluate on the turn: fold to a large hostile raise/shove unless I improve; call small/medium turn bets; if an Ace/5/diamond hits I can get aggressive for value.

I hold A♦5♦ on J♠5♣7♠5♠ — trips with the best possible kicker. Elon opened BTN and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn; his turn-check narrows his range toward medium-strength hands (Jx, 7x, some Ax, and missed or turned spade draws) but could also include polarized traps (pocket JJ/77 → now full houses). This is a clear value-bet spot: I want to charge pairs and worse A-highs and deny a free river to any remaining straight/spade backdoors. A small-to-medium sizing will maximize calls from Jx/7x while keeping worse hands and weaker pairs in the pot. I’ll lead for value but keep it constructive — bet ~1/3–1/2 pot. If Elon check-raises large, his range is polarized toward full houses or made spade flushes; I’ll re-evaluate and proceed cautiously (call small/medium raises, fold to a polarizing shove unless the sizing or read suggests a bluff).

On the turn, the board is Js5c7s5s, pairing the 5s and bringing a third spade. I have top pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker from ThJd, which is still a strong hand in position against Minni's BB check-call flop range. She checked flop (two spades, drawy board), called my 400 bet, and now donk-bets a small 500 into 1450 pot (~1/3 pot size), which looks weak and capped—likely a weak pair (like 7x, 5x now paired), stubborn draw (now flush or str), or air trying to pot control/steal. Stronger hands (sets, two-pair, turned flush) would likely bet larger or check-raise flop. Flushes possible (two spades in her range like A♠x/K♠x that check-called flop), but small bet size doesn't scream nuts; it polarizes toward medium strength or draws. Pot odds to call are excellent (500 into 1950, ~20% equity needed, I have ~60-70% vs her range). Raising risks folding out worse (pairs/draws) while getting snapped by 5x/flush; calling keeps pot small OOP for her, realizes equity, controls pot size with deep stacks (my 10k, her 18k), and position on river to bet/fold/call draws. Folding too tight given strength/history. Call balances value extraction long-term in tournament.

I hold Ad5d on Js 5c 7s 5s Tc — trips (three 5s) with the nut Ace kicker. Elon (BTN) opened, c-bet the flop and then checked the turn and called my modest lead of 500. His line is consistent with a lot of single-pair hands (Jx, 7x), some Ax, and a few pocket pairs; full houses that beat me (JJ, 77, TT or J5/T5/75 combos) are possible but relatively uncommon in his calling range. I want to extract thin value from worse Jx/7x and A-high hands while minimizing my downside to the few full-house combinations. A small-to-medium river bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that: it gets called by many worse hands and is small enough that a raise is highly indicative of a full house (in which case I can fold). Checking risks giving up value to hands that will call a small bet. I will bet ~800 chips (≈1/3 of the 2450 pot) to maximize thin value and keep my options if Elon shows heavy aggression.

River Tc pairs the board's Ten, pairing my Th to give me two pair: Jacks and Tens (Js/Jd/Th/Tc/7s), a very strong hand on this paired board (5s paired earlier). This beats nearly all of Minni's one-pair hands (like 7x, weaker Jx, Tx), many two-pair combos (J7, J5), and bluffs/draws, while only losing to trips+ (55 full house, JJ/TT sets, 89 straights, spade flushes). Minni's BB check-call flop (draw-heavy Js5c7s) + small turn donk 500 (~1/3 pot after flush/straight draw completes) + small river bet 800 (~1/4-1/3 into 3250) screams medium strength/pot control (pairs like 77/JT/J9/87/A-high draws) or weak value, not nuts (flushes/sets/straights would bomb larger post-turn or check-raise). Her history shows calling stations but folding to aggression when behind (e.g., Hand 4,14,35). Pot odds to call are great (800 into 4050 ~16% equity, we have 70-80% vs range), but raising extracts max value from worse (top of her capped range) while denying draws/implied odds. Deep stacks (my 9625, her 17250), mid-tournament (65/150), position allows. Raise to 2400 (3x her bet, ~60% of pot-if-called) sizes well: gets called by 1-pair/medium two-pair, folds better but protects against floats. Folding/calling too passive; bet unavail. Builds stack EV+.

I hold A♦5♦ on J♠–5♣–7♠–5♠–T♣: trips (three 5s) with the nut Ace kicker. I led the turn for value and he check‑called; I bet the river 800 for thin value and he raised to 2400. I must call 1600 more. Pot is 5650, so calling costs ~22% equity (1600 / (5650+1600) ≈ 22%).

Elon’s line is polarized — it can represent turn/full houses (JJ/77), rivered full houses (TT or 5x combos), or completed spade flushes — but also includes a fair number of missed spade draws and single pair Jx/Ax hands that will raise or shove as bluffs or thin value. Trips are very strong versus a lot of his continuing range, and folding now surrenders too much value. Raising myself would only bloat the pot when many of his value hands (flush/full house) crush me and could commit me to a much bigger loss.

Given the pot odds and the fact the raise isn’t an immediate shove, the correct line is to call the extra 1600 (matching to 2400 total) to get to showdown and re‑evaluate only if he jams further — a shove after my call would be highly polarized and I would fold to an all‑in unless I put him on a very specific bluff frequency.

Ac8c is a solid suited ace from UTG. With 116 BBs effective, this is a standard open-raise hand. Suited aces play well postflop with flush potential and blocker value. Raising to 2.5x (250) is standard sizing.

J3s is a marginal suited hand with low cards and weak playability. Facing a raise from UTG (Claude), who typically has a strong range, from early position (UTG+1), folding is standard to avoid difficult post-flop situations out of position with a speculative hand. My stack is healthy at 72BB+, no need to speculate here. Prioritize solid play to build stack long-term.

I hold 4s 2s on the button facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. A UTG opening range is typically strong, consisting of high cards and pairs against which my low suited one-gapper performs poorly. While I have position, the hand has very low equity and suffers from reverse implied odds (e.g., making a small flush vs a bigger flush). Additionally, calling risks a squeeze from the blinds. Given the stack depths and the tight configuration, this is a clear fold.

Claude opened UTG to 250 — a range that will be fairly strong from early position. I hold 5♠6♣: a weak, unsuited connector with poor playability and bad blockers. From the small blind I would be out of position to the raiser and the big blind on every post‑flop street. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity just to break even preflop, which 5‑6 offsuit does not reliably have vs an UTG opening range; 3‑betting would be a pure bluff with little reason here. With deep stacks I can wait for better spots and preserve chips, so I will fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 4c. Claude raised to 250 from UTG. This hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position against a standard UTG opening range. Despite getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 550), the lack of playability and equity makes this a clear fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

UTG position with 5d7c offsuit is a complete trash hand. Poor connectivity, no high card strength, low playability post-flop, and easily dominated. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Deep stack (72BB) allows patience. Opponent tendencies from history don't change this fundamental decision.

I am in UTG+1 (Hijack) in a 5-handed game with K5s. This is a marginal hand. While suited Kings have some playability, K5 is weak due to the low kicker. With the chip leader (Minni) on the button and active players in the blinds, opening this hand carries the risk of being 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position with a dominated hand. Given my stack size of ~74BB, I prefer to fold this marginal holding and wait for a better spot or position to open.

Button with Td6h facing only the blinds — this is a marginal hand but excellent positional spot with deep stacks. I want to apply pressure and steal the blinds rather than limp. Raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to maximize fold equity and price out loose calls; a min-raise would invite more defense. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet; if called I’ll play postflop aggressively in position, c-betting favorable textures and pot-controlling on high-card/connected boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni, who is the chip leader and has been playing very aggressively, has raised from the button. Jack-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand to play out of position, especially against an active chip leader who can apply significant pressure on later streets. Given the stack sizes and the need to preserve my chips for better spots, folding is the most prudent play here.

KQo is a strong hand in the BB facing a button raise from Minni. Minni has been very active raising, so their range is wide. I should 3-bet for value and to take initiative. Standard 3-bet to around 850.

I opened to 300 on the button to steal the blinds and Claude responded with a 3-bet to 850 from the big blind. Td6h is a marginal, non-blocker hand — not a good candidate to 4‑bet bluff — but I have position and very deep stacks (≈225 BB). Calling (putting 850 total in) is relatively cheap (550 more into a 1,200 pot), keeps the pot controllable, allows me to realize equity and play postflop in position. Plan: see the flop and proceed only with a real piece (T/6/top pair, or strong draw); fold to large, persistent pressure on dry boards. I’ll reserve 4‑bets/shoves for much stronger holdings.

I 3-bet preflop with KQo and got called. This 2-2-7 board is very dry and doesn't connect well with either of our ranges. As the preflop aggressor, I should continue with a small c-bet to pressure Minni's medium-strength hands like small pairs, suited connectors that missed, and A-high. A 1/3 pot sizing is efficient here.

Call 575. Board: 2♠ 2♥ 7♥ — my T♦6♥ has no pair but does have a backdoor heart and two cards that can pair (T or 6) = ~6 clean outs (~24% to hit by river) plus a small runner-runner flush chance. Pot is 2325; calling 575 would make the pot ~2900 so I’m getting ~5:1 (need ~16.7% equity). Claude 3‑bet preflop so his range is weighted to value, but his flop stab is very small (~33% pot), which suggests he’s also betting light or probing. With position, deep stacks, and sufficient pot odds, a call to realize equity and keep the pot controllable is the +EV line. Raising would be a thin bluff without good blockers and would bloat the pot out of line with my equity; folding would be too tight given the odds. Plan: see the turn and proceed only with a real improvement (pair or strong heart draw); fold to large turn aggression if I miss.

Turn brought the 8s which doesn't significantly change the board. I c-bet flop and got called by Minni. Their calling range likely includes pocket pairs (77, 88 now got there, small/medium pairs), 7x, heart draws, or occasionally a 2. I have K-high with no draws. Firing a second barrel with just K-high against a calling station-ish opponent who flatted a 3-bet and called flop seems -EV. I'll check to control pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or improve on the river.

Check. I have Td6h with no pair on 2♠ 2♥ 7♥ 8♠ — after Claude 3‑bet pre and made a small flop stab then checked the turn his line skews toward a made hand or pot‑control with a reasonably strong range. I have only 6 clean outs (three Tens + three Sixes) and ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity to improve on the river, so betting now as a bluff would bloat a pot in which I'm a large underdog and will get called by many hands in his range. Pot control and realizing equity in position is the +EV line: check and see the river. Plan: if the river checks through I only win when I pair; if Claude fires a meaningful bet on the river I will fold most of the time — mathematically I only get the correct pot odds to call very tiny river bets (on the order of ≈15% of the pot, ≈430 chips or less).

River brought the third deuce, making trip deuces on the board. With KQ, I have trip deuces with K-Q kickers. Any pocket pair in Minni's range (77, 88, or anything else) now has a full house and beats me. Minni flatted a 3-bet and called flop - consistent with pocket pairs. I can't value bet since worse hands won't call, and I have showdown value against missed draws. Check and hope to win against Ax or busted heart draws.

Check. The river (2s 2h 7h 8s 2d) bricks for Td6h — I missed completely and have no showdown value. Claude 3‑bet pre and made a small flop stab, then checked the turn and river; his line is weighted toward a value/pot‑control range (pairs/overcards that turned into medium hands, or a full house if he holds a 7/8). Bluffing into a 3‑bettor on a trip board with no blockers is low EV and will get called by many hands that beat me. Pot control and preserving chips is optimal: check through to showdown and fold to any river bet; there’s no justification to build the pot here.

I am in UTG position with 9d 4h, which is a very weak off-suit hand (9-high, no connectivity). With four players left to act behind me, the probability that someone has a better hand is very high. My stack is healthy (74.5 BB), so there is no pressure to make a move with a garbage hand. Opening here would be a significant mistake. The only correct play is to fold.

UTG+1 with Ace of Spades (As) and Nine of Hearts (9h). With a deep stack (~214bb) in a 5-handed game this is a playable ace and I want to take the initiative rather than limp/call. I’ll open-raise to 300 total (≈3x BB) to pick up the blinds, get heads-up more often, and give myself c-bet opportunities on many flops. Plan: fold to a large, polarized 3-bet from a tight opponent; against a single caller continue with a standard c-bet on favorable textures and proceed cautiously when my kicker looks dominated.

I have Ad Qs on the button, which is a very strong hand in a 5-handed game. Minni, the chip leader, has opened to 300 from early position. While Minni has been active, a 3-bet here is standard to take control of the hand, potentially win the pot immediately, or build value against a range that I dominate. A raise to 900 is a standard size for a 3-bet in this position.
I have 2h8s - one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a 3-bet to 900, I need to call 850 with a junk hand. This is a clear fold. No playability, no equity worth defending.

2s7h is one of the worst possible starting hands in poker, with extremely low equity against any reasonable range. Minni opened from UTG+1, which suggests strength, and Flash 3-bet from the Button to 900, indicating a strong hand like premium pairs, broadway, or suited connectors. Facing a 3-bet after an early position open, my hand has negligible fold equity if I shove or raise, poor implied odds if I call due to reverse domination, and bad pot odds multi-way. Pot odds are about 800 to call into 1350 (needing ~37% equity, but 27o has ~20% vs tight 3-bet range). With 71BB stack and 82 hands left, preserving chips for better spots is optimal. Opponents are deep-stacked and aggressive based on history (Minni frequent raiser, Flash active 3-bettor), so no reason to speculate. Clear fold.

Call 600 more (900 total). As9o is a playable ace against a Button 3-bet and with deep stacks I prefer to realize equity and see a flop out of position rather than fold a hand with showdown value or turn it into a marginal 4‑bet. The pot is 1,350 so calling 600 gives ~31% pot odds; versus a reasonably wide/aggressive 3‑bet range A9 has enough equity to continue. Postflop I’ll proceed cautiously OOP: c‑bet small on dry/ace‑high flops, avoid bloating the pot on wet/high‑card textures where my kicker looks dominated, and fold to large, polarized aggression or a shove preflop/turn if it’s clear I’m crushed.

I have A♠9♥ on 8♣5♣8♥ — only ace‑high with no club backdoor and I’m out of position to a button 3‑better. Flash’s 3‑bet range contains lots of hands that fare better on this paired, two‑club board (pocket pairs, 8x, 5x, and club draws) so a c‑bet here is likely to get called by better or check‑raised. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than bluff into a polarized range from OOP. I’ll check and see the turn for free; plan is to fold to a large flop/turn stab, call very small probes when the pot odds and villain tendencies justify it, and only start betting when I pick up additional equity (an A, 9, or a safe runout).

I am the pre-flop 3-bettor and the board is 8c 5c 8h. This board is relatively neutral but favors my range slightly more in terms of high overpairs (AA, KK, QQ). Minni checked to me. While a continuation bet is a standard option to exert pressure, I have strong showdown value with Ace-high and two overcards. Checking back allows me to control the pot size against the chip leader and see a turn card for free. If Minni has a medium pocket pair (66, 77, 99), she is unlikely to fold to a single bet on this flop. By checking, I protect my range and can potentially catch an Ace or Queen on the turn.

Check. The turn A♠9♥ on 8♣ 5♣ 8♥ A♥ gives me top pair but a vulnerable 9-kicker out of position to a Button 3-better. Flash's 3-bet range contains many hands that fare better here (AK/AQ, A8, 88, some overpairs) and he checked the flop — so his range is likely capped or controlling; betting now would bloat a ~1,950-chip pot and get called by better A-x and medium pairs while folding only pure air. With deep stacks I want pot control and to realize equity: check and let him show aggression first. Plan: call a small-to-medium turn bet for value, but fold to a large, polarized raise/shove that strongly represents two pair/trips or a dominating ace. If Flash checks back I take a free river and re-evaluate (thin-value bet safe runouts, check to showdown on scary cards).

I have improved to top pair with a Queen kicker on the turn (Ah). The flop was checked through, and Minni has checked again on the turn. Since the Ace is very much in my 3-bet range and I have now paired it, I have a strong hand that should be betting for value. Minni might have a middle pocket pair, a smaller Ace, or a club draw. Betting here protects my hand against draws and extracts value from hands that are now second best. A bet of around half the pot is a standard sizing to accomplish this.

I have top pair (A♠9♥) but a vulnerable kicker on a paired board (8♣5♣8♥ A♥). Flash was the button 3‑better preflop, checked the flop and now fires a half‑pot turn bet (1,000 into a 1,950 pot before his bet; pot is 2,950 now). This sizing is a small‑to‑medium delayed c‑bet that can be made with a wide range (AK/AQ, A8/A5, medium pairs, 8x, some club draws and bluffs). Per my pre‑turn plan I want pot control OOP and to realize equity versus that range rather than bloating the pot with a marginal kicker. Calling 1,000 to win a 3,950 pot after my call requires ~25% equity (1,000/3,950 ≈ 25.3%), which A9 has versus a reasonably wide 3‑bet/check‑call range. Raising OOP here is thin — it commits us and is often only good versus air or worse aces, and folding is too weak given our showdown value and the price. I will call the 1,000 to keep the pot manageable and re‑evaluate on the river: if Flash shows strong, polarized aggression (large overbet or shove) I will fold; if he checks or makes a small river bet I will call thin for value or make a small value bet on safe runouts.

Board: 8c 5c 8h Ah 2d. I hold As9h — two pair (Aces and Eights) with a 9 kicker. Flash was the preflop 3‑better, checked the flop and fired a delayed turn c‑bet of 1,000 (I called). His check on the river indicates a capped or medium‑strength range, but his line still contains many hands that beat me (trip 8s/quads, full houses from pocket 5s/2s, and stronger A‑x like AK/AQ), while the worse Ax combos I can extract value from are limited. A river bet here is thin: it will often be called by hands that beat me or fold only the parts of his range I already beat, so betting bloats the pot with little positive EV and risks a check‑raise. I’ll follow my earlier pot‑control plan and check to take this to showdown. If Flash now leads out small I’ll call; I’d fold to a large polarized shove.

On the river, the board is 8c 5c 8h Ah 2d. I have top pair, Queen kicker (Aces and Eights). The pre-flop action was a 3-bet from me, followed by a check-through on the flop. On the turn, the Ace hit and I bet 1000, which Minni called. On the river, the Deuce of diamonds is a complete brick. Minni has checked to me. Given the action, Minni likely has a hand like a medium pocket pair (99-JJ) or possibly a weaker Ace. It is unlikely she has an 8 given the flop check and the turn call. Since I have showdown value and the pot is already quite large (3950), checking back is a safe play to realize my equity. While I could bet for thin value, checking ensures I don't get check-raised by a trapped monster and wins the pot often enough against her calling range.

We're in a 5-handed game UTG with A♠4♠ — a playable, suited ace with good postflop play and blocker value. In short-handed play this is a hand I want to open rather than limp: it has fold equity and nut-flush/ace-up potential. I’ll open to 300 (3bb) — a standard 3bb raise at this table that buys the blinds some respect, keeps the pot manageable with ~195bb effective stacks, and matches my prior open sizing so I remain balanced. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable/A-high or flush-draw flops, fold to heavy multiway pressure or large 3-bets from aggressive opponents, and call smallish 3-bets to realize equity and play postflop when appropriate.

I have Ace-Five suited in UTG+1. Minni has been opening frequently from UTG. A5s is a premier 3-bet bluffing candidate because the Ace blocks strong hands like AA, AK, and AQ, while the hand itself has good post-flop potential with flush and straight draws. By raising to 900, I take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or isolate Minni in a pot where I have a range advantage.
5h7s is a weak offsuit hand facing a raise and 3-bet. With two players showing strength, this hand has poor equity and no real playability. Easy fold.

T5o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against the strong ranges of Minni (UTG raiser) and Flash (3-bettor from UTG+1). Facing significant action preflop from early positions, calling 850 into a 1350 pot offers insufficient odds and exposes my stack to further aggression postflop where this hand performs poorly. Folding preserves chips for better spots, aligning with tight-aggressive strategy to maximize stack over 150 hands.

I am holding 7c 2c in the Big Blind, facing a UTG raise to 300 and a UTG+1 3-bet to 900. 72s is one of the weakest hands in poker. Facing a raise and a re-raise from early positions indicates very strong ranges from my opponents. I am out of position and getting poor pot odds (calling 800 to win 1350) given my hand's extremely low equity against 3-betting ranges. There is no strategic merit to calling or bluffing here. The only correct move is to fold immediately.

I opened UTG with A♠4♠ — a suited ace with blocker value and good postflop playability. Flash 3-bet to 900 after my 300 open; pot is 1,350 and I need to call 600 more (900 total). With effective stacks ~118bb and a small/standard 3-bet size, I don’t want to 4-bet light out of position and build a big pot with a marginal hand. Calling keeps worse hands in, gives me reasonable equity (plus backdoor flush/draw potential and ace-high combos), and fits my preflop plan to call smallish 3-bets and realize equity. Plan: see a flop and continue on A-high or strong draw flops (small c-bet or check/call), fold or concede to large 4-bets or heavy aggression that commits too many chips OOP.

Board is 6♠-3♣-5♠ and I hold A♠4♠ — I have the nut-flush draw plus the ace blocker and Ace-high right now. Flash 3-bet preflop, so his range is weighted to high cards/pairs and many of those hands are unimproved on this board. This is a textbook spot to semi-bluff: I want to apply pressure and build a pot with a drawing hand that has excellent equity and blocks the strongest nut-spade combos. I’ll c-bet around half the pot to force folds from many overcards and charge weaker draws while keeping the pot manageable OOP. If he calls I’ll continue when I improve (flush or pair) and re-evaluate on turn; if he raises large I’ll usually fold (or only call very small raises to realize equity).

Minni has led out (donk bet) for 1000 into a 1950 pot on a 6s-3c-5s flop after calling my pre-flop 3-bet. I have bottom pair (5s) with an Ace kicker. While her lead often indicates a hand like a 6, a medium pocket pair, or a draw, I cannot fold here given the pot odds and my position. Raising would likely only get called by better hands, so I will call to maintain pot control and evaluate the turn, where an Ace or Five would significantly improve my hand.

Board: 6s-3c-5s-6h. I hold A♠4♠ (nut spade draw + Ace blocker) and am facing no bet on the turn with the pot at 3,950. I c-bet the flop and was called, then the turn paired the board which increases the chance villain has a 6/set and reduces my fold equity. I have 9 spade outs to make the flush (9/46 ≈ 19.6%) plus 3 aces and 3 fours as additional pair outs — 15 total outs (15/46 ≈ 32.6%), though some of those outs don’t cleanly beat trips/quads. Against a preflop 3-bettor who called the flop, betting the turn would bloat the pot out of position and get me into difficult spots (check-raises, value bets from made hands). I prefer pot control and to realize equity: check and call small-to-medium bets to see the river, fold to large turn check-raises/overwhelming value, and if the river completes my flush or pairs my Ace I will pursue value then.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Six of Hearts. After leading the flop, Minni's check could indicate either a trap with a set/trips or a realization that her marginal hand or draw is now in a tougher spot. My hand, bottom pair (Fives) with an Ace kicker, has decent showdown value but is unlikely to get called by worse if I bet. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, see the river for free, and keep her range wide, which includes missed draws and smaller pairs that I currently beat.

I missed my flush and didn’t pair the river (Kc). I’m currently showing only Ace-high. Villain 3‑bet preflop and called my flop aggression, then checked the turn — a line more consistent with a marginal made hand or a missed draw than with strong value (a turned/set would likely have been played more aggressively). Betting the river would be a pure bluff that needs to fold out hands I already beat (weak showdown hands), but it will get called by Kx and beaten by any pair or better; given he’s shown willingness to call earlier, a bluff is unlikely to be reliably profitable. My original plan was pot control when I didn’t improve; checking to showdown preserves chips and avoids bloating a pot I’m unlikely to win. I’ll check and see the showdown.

On the river, the King of Clubs completes the board. Minni has checked for the second time in a row after leading out on the flop. My pair of fives has some showdown value against missed draws (like spades) or small pocket pairs like 44. Betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and it's risky to bluff here given that Minni's flop lead suggests she has a piece of the board or a marginal made hand. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and get to showdown for free in a pot that is already significant.

I am in the UTG position in a 5-handed game. Holding Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o), this hand is marginally below the standard opening range for the first player to act. While it is an Ace, the weak kicker and the fact that I would be out of position against most of the table if called makes this a standard fold in a high-roller environment. Given the stack sizes and the aggressive play observed from Minni and Claude, I will wait for a stronger hand to enter the pot.

A8o in UTG+1 is a reasonable opening hand. With Flash already folded, I have a decent ace that plays well heads-up if called. Standard raise to 250 to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position against late position callers.

Preflop on the button with ATo offsuit, a strong hand in position. Claude raised from early position (UTG+1) to 250, which is a standard raise size into a 150 pot. His range is likely tight: pairs 88+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways, etc. However, button position allows me to 3-bet profitably for value and isolation. ATo has good equity against his calling range (blocks AA, has ace dominance over AJ/KQ, plays well postflop). SB and BB stacks are deep but will fold light often to a 3-bet. Raising to 800 (about 3.2x his raise) is a good size: builds pot, applies pressure, and maintains fold equity while keeping SPR reasonable (~10:1 effective). Folding or calling is too passive; flat risks multiway pots OOP dominations.

I am in the Small Blind with [Kd, Qc] facing an open raise from Claude (UTG+1) to 250 and a 3-bet from Elon (Button) to 800. KQ offsuit is a trouble hand to play out of position against a 3-bet, as it is often dominated by AQ, AK, and pocket pairs like QQ-AA in the 3-bettor's range. Cold calling 750 chips is roughly 10% of my stack and puts me in a difficult spot post-flop sandwich-ed or heads-up OOP. A cold 4-bet bluff is too risky given the stack sizes and lack of information suggesting Elon is 3-betting light. The prudent play is to fold.

I have pocket eights in the big blind facing an UTG+1 open to 250 and a button 3-bet to 800. With deep stacks (my stack 17,525 ≈ 175 BB) 88 is a strong, playable hand — especially because I will act last post-flop. Calling the additional 700 (total 800 committed this street) is cheap relative to my stack and keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to realize equity (set-mining and post-flop play). A minimum 4-bet to 1,350 is too small to credibly fold out stronger parts of their ranges and risks getting 5-bet or inflated into a big pot unnecessarily; folding would be too weak. Pot odds: calling 700 into a 1,200 pot means I need ~36.8% equity to break even, which is reasonable given the situation and positional advantage. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively if I hit a set or a very favorable board, otherwise be ready to concede to strong pressure on overcard-heavy or coordinated runouts.

A8o facing a 3-bet from Elon and a cold call from Minni. Cold calls typically indicate strong hands like pocket pairs or suited broadways. I'm OOP against both players with a mediocre ace. While pot odds are ~3.5:1, A8o plays poorly multiway out of position. This is a clear fold - don't want to bloat the pot with a dominated ace or weak kicker.

I’m OOP with pocket eights on Jh 9h 6d against a player who 3‑bet preflop (Elon). This is a marginal middle pair on a two‑heart, two‑overcard board — many hands in his 3‑bet range (Jx, 9x, TT+, AK/AQ) dominate me and there are heart draws to worry about. My priority is pot control and to realize equity; I don’t want to bloat an out‑of‑position pot with a hand that is easily crushed by his value range. I’ll check to induce and to keep the pot manageable, plan to call a standard c‑bet (small/medium sizing) and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy/clearly polarized aggression unless I improve or pick up a read. If he checks back I can take a stab on a safe turn or continue to control the pot.

Heads-up on the flop with A♥T♦ against Minni in BB who called my preflop 3-bet. Board is J♥9♥6♦, giving me the nut flush draw (Ah blocks higher hearts) plus Ace overcard for gutshot blocker and showdown value. Minni checked, representing weakness or a trap, but her BB calling range vs button 3-bet includes suited connectors, broadways, pairs that hit or draw. This is a strong spot: I have ~40% equity vs most calling ranges even if behind, massive vs bluffs. Betting builds the pot with my draw (SPR ~5:1 post-bet), denies equity to her draws/overcards, and gets value from Jx, Tx, 9x, pairs, weaker draws. Size to 1200 (~2/3 pot) charges max while keeping stacks committed and fold equity vs air. Check risks giving free cards to chase; overbet unnecessary with position.

I’m OOP with pocket 8s on Jh 9h 6d facing a button 3‑better’s flop bet. Elon’s 3‑bet range is weighted to value (JJ/99/TT+, AK/AQ, Jx, some suited connectors) and the flop contains two hearts, so there are both many hands that dominate me and heart draws to worry about. The bet is 1200 into a 3100 pot — I’m getting ~27.9% break‑even equity to call (1200/(3100+1200)). With only middle pair and just two outs to a set, I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position by raising or check‑raising; folding is too tight given the price and his likely wide continuation frequency with this sizing. I’ll call to control pot size and realize equity, keep bluffs in, and re‑evaluate on the turn: fold to heavy, clearly polarized aggression unless I improve (or pick up a strong read); if I hit an 8 I’ll look to extract value; if he checks the turn I may take a stab or continue pot control.

Board: Jh 9h 6d 2h. I hold 8♠8♦ (middle pair) and am out of position. The turn is dangerous: it brings a third heart so any two‑heart combo in Elon’s 3‑bet range has completed a flush, and his preflop 3‑bet range also contains many Jx/9x/TT+ hands that already beat me. I have only two outs to a set (very low equity) and don’t want to bloat a pot OOP with a marginal hand. Checking preserves pot control, keeps his bluffs in his range, and lets me fold to clear polarization or large aggression on the turn/river without risking a big portion of my 15625 stack. Plan: check — if Elon bets a small/medium size on the turn I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to large, polarized bets; call very small bets for pot control), and if he checks back I’ll decide on the river whether to take a free card, block‑bet small for fold equity, or check to showdown depending on the final card and his action.

Heads-up on the turn vs Minni who has checked twice after calling preflop 3-bet and flop bet of 1200 on a draw-heavy J♥9♥6♦ flop. Turn 2♥ brings a third heart, keeping my A♥T♦ nut flush draw live (~18% to hit on river, but combined with ~50% equity overall due to blockers, overcard, and position). Board extremely coordinated (flush draw, open-enders like T8/78/57), her BB calling range vs button 3-bet includes suited connectors (87s♥, T8s♥ etc. that hit draws or weak pairs), broadway, Ax suited—many improved or drawing but vulnerable. Her double-check represents weakness (no strong monster likely to check twice), so bet for thin value from top pair (AJ/KJ/JT), two-pair, sets, inferior flushes (lower hearts), and to charge max draws/bluffs. Pot 4300, SPR low (~1.2:1 effective), betting 2800 (~2/3 pot) builds to ~10k pot, leaving ~2275 behind for river shove/value if checked to/brick, denies equity, and exploits her passivity. Checking risks free river card to her draws or check-raise trap, suboptimal with 50%+ equity and initiative.

Fold. Elon has 3‑bet preflop, fired the flop and now barrels the turn big (2800 into a 7100 pot). After his turn bet the pot would be 9,900 and calling 2,800 requires ~28.3% equity (2800/9900). I have pocket eights on Jh 9h 6d 2h with no heart and only two outs (two remaining eights) → 2/46 ≈ 4.35% to hit a set on the river. His line and sizing are weighted to value (Jx/9x/TT+/made heart flushes) that dominate me, and my raw equity is far below the pot odds. Even allowing for some bluffs, the bet is large and polarizing; calling would risk a substantial portion of my stack with almost no chance to improve. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.
J4o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand that doesn't play well postflop, and UTG requires a tight opening range. No reason to get involved here.

Q♦9♥ is an offsuit, non-connected hand with limited playability from UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game. Preflop pot is 150 with 100 to call, suggesting a possible limp or small bet ahead, but regardless, this hand lacks the strength to call or raise profitably from early position against deep-stacked, aggressive opponents observed in prior hands. Standard play is to fold and wait for better opportunities to build the stack over remaining hands.

I am on the Button with Jack-Six offsuit (Js 6h). The action has folded to me. My stack is 73 BBs. J6o is a very weak hand that falls outside of a standard button opening range, even for stealing the blinds. It plays poorly post-flop and is dominated by many calling ranges. With the blinds having significant stacks (155 BBs and 146 BBs), opening this hand risks facing 3-bets or difficult post-flop spots. The most prudent play is to fold.

I have 4s 2d — essentially the worst kind of hand. I'm in the small blind and would be out of position against Flash (big blind). Although calling an extra 50 into a 150 pot offers decent pot odds, this hand is rarely profitable postflop and is frequently dominated; raising would be a poor play since it will be called or 3-bet by better hands. With a healthy stack and many hands left, I prefer to avoid marginal, OOP spots and preserve chips for better opportunities. Fold.

J℃8c is a suited connector with some playability, but from UTG in a 5-handed game at deep stacks (~94BB), it is below the standard opening range. UTG opens should be around 15-20% of hands: pairs 22+, broadway hands like AJo+, KQo+, suited aces, and higher suited connectors like JTs+.
