J8s risks being dominated or outdrawn multiway and is vulnerable to 3-bets from later positions (Pro has 73BB, others deeper). Folding preserves the stack for premium spots to maximize EV over 150 hands. Opponents show aggressive tendencies in history (frequent raises/3bets), so no limp either.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively Cutoff in 5-handed play) with 8h4h. This is a suited two-gapper, which is generally too weak to open from early/middle position, even with 5 players. My stack is 73 BB, the shortest at the table, so I should avoid spewing chips with marginal holdings. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with K♠3♦, stacks are deep (15,575) and I’m facing only the blinds (pot 150, 100 to call). K3o is a marginal hand, but on the button it’s a reasonable stealing candidate — I want to take the initiative and avoid playing a dominated K-high out of position. I’ll open-raise to 300 (a standard 3x-ish size here) to apply pressure and pick up the blinds or isolate one opponent. Plan if action changes: fold to a large, committed 3-bet (K3o has poor equity vs 3-bet ranges); consider a call only to a very small 3-bet from the SB and play cautiously postflop. If called, use position to c-bet small on favourable flops and check/fold when I completely miss. Overall goal: win the blinds or seize position with minimal risk.

I am in the Small Blind with T5 offsuit. Minni, who is on the button, has raised to 300. T5 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button player. Fold is the most disciplined and correct choice here.

Facing a button open from Minni with Q7o in the BB. This is a steal spot but Q7o is a weak hand that plays poorly OOP. Getting 2.25:1 but Q7o doesn't have enough equity to defend profitably here. Fold is the standard play.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Six offsuit (Qh 6c). This is a statistically weak hand that plays poorly from early position due to the high probability of domination and the disadvantage of acting first post-flop if called. My stack is healthy at 73 BBs, so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. The standard and optimal play here is to fold.

Holding Kc2c UTG+1 with a deep ~157BB stack: this is a marginal, easily-dominated hand (most callers/raisers will have better Kx or broadway cards). Limping for 100 is cheap but invites raises and likely a multiway, where K2s has poor playability. Raising to seize the pot is overly aggressive with a weak kicker and will get me into tricky post‑flop spots OOP vs the Button/BB (both have shown aggression in prior hands). With lots of better spots to pick from and plenty of stack depth, I conserve chips and fold this early weak holding.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit. After the UTG and UTG+1 players folded, the action is on me. A9o is a strong hand to open with from the button in a 5-handed game, as it is ahead of the average defending range of the blinds. I will make a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure and build the pot, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes in this tournament.

I have pocket 7s in the SB facing a button raise to 300. 77 is a solid hand for set mining with good implied odds given the stack depths (128bb effective). Calling 250 to try to flop a set is the standard play here. 3-betting is an option but calling to set mine is more profitable OOP against a button open.

9d2s is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness (offsuit), or high card value. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has a wide range, and a call from Claude in the small blind, which adds strength to the pot. As BB, I have position post-flop if I call, but my hand has negligible equity against their ranges and poor playability on any board. Pot odds are about 3:1 (700:200), but implied odds are minimal due to hand strength. Best to fold and preserve stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament phase (93bb effective).

I missed my set with 77 on Jd9cJs. This is a paired board with two jacks. My pocket 7s are an underpair with minimal showdown value. I'm OOP against the preflop raiser. Standard play is to check and likely fold to aggression - Flash will have plenty of Jx, overpairs, and better hands in their button range.

On a board of Jd-9c-Js, I have flopped two pair (Jacks and Nines) with the best possible kicker (Ace). While the paired board increases the probability that my opponent could have a Jack, my hand is currently very strong against most of Claude's calling range from the small blind (pocket pairs, suited connectors, and broadway hands). Claude checked to me, which is standard. I will place a continuation bet of 350 (half the pot) to get value from straight draws (like T8 or QT), smaller pairs, or hands with one overcard. This bet also helps define where I stand if Claude decides to check-raise, as a Jack is the primary hand I need to worry about.

I have pocket 7s on Jd9cJs - an underpair on a paired board. Flash bet 350 into 700 (half pot). My 77 has minimal showdown value here. Flash's button range contains plenty of Jx, 99, overpairs like TT/QQ/KK/AA. I'm basically drawing to two outs for a set. Getting 3:1 pot odds but way behind vs his betting range. Clean fold.

Fold. Qc6 is a weak, offsuit hand from UTG in a five‑hand game: poor playability, frequently dominated by opponents’ Qx and broadway/medium pairs, and I’ll be OOP against 4 players with a table that opens to 300+ often. Limping invites raises and calling out of position creates difficult, marginal post‑flop decisions despite deep stacks. No reason to invest chips here — preserve the stack for a better spot.

I have pocket Jacks (Jd Js) in UTG+1. Minni (UTG) folded. I should definitely raise for value and to thin the field. A standard raise to 3x big blinds (300) is appropriate here to build the pot while not committing too much in case of a significant re-raise from the blinds or the button, although JJ is a very strong hand.

7c3c is a very weak suited hand. While I have position on the button, calling a raise with this hand is -EV. The suited aspect gives some backdoor flush potential, but the gap between cards makes straight possibilities poor. Against an UTG+1 open, this is a clear fold.

Facing an early position raise from Flash after UTG fold. 3s6s is a weak suited connector, speculative at best, but OOP in SB against likely strong range. Pot odds 450:250 (~1.8:1, need ~35% equity) are insufficient given post-flop playability issues and BB still to act. Flash has been aggressive historically. Preserve stack by folding a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with K7o facing a raise to 300 from Flash in the Cutoff (5-handed play). The pot odds are 450:200, requiring about 30.7% equity to call. Against a Cutoff opening range (likely 25-30%), K7o has roughly 38% raw equity. However, I will be playing out of position with a hand that suffers from reverse implied odds (often dominated by better Kings). Applying an equity realization factor for being OOP (approx 0.7-0.8), my realized equity drops below the threshold needed to call profitably. Preserving my stack of 72bb is preferable to playing a marginal hand OOP against an active opponent.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Combined with being in the UTG (Under the Gun) position, where I am the first to act and have the entire table behind me, this hand is an easy and mandatory fold. There is no strategic value in playing this hand from this position.
Pocket Jacks is a premium hand in UTG+1. With Flash folding, I should open raise to build the pot and narrow the field. Standard raise to 2.5x BB (250) is appropriate here.

Qh6c is a trash offsuit hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or blocking value. Facing an early position raise from Claude (UTG+1 in a 5-handed game), who frequently raises with decent hands based on history. Pot odds are 400:250 (~1.6:1), but postflop playability is poor OOP against the raiser, and blinds behind can still act and squeeze. Equity against a typical ~15% open range is ~30%, insufficient for a profitable call or 3bet bluff. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots on the button.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 6s. The UTG+1 player (Claude) has raised to 250. Q6 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly, especially out of position against an early position raiser who likely has a stronger range. The pot odds do not justify a call (need ~33% equity, have less than 30% against a reasonable range), and I would be at a positional disadvantage post-flop. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Claude opened to 250 from UTG+1 and I'm in the big blind with 8h4d. Calling 150 into a 400 pot would make it 550 total, so I need roughly 27% equity to break even. 8-4 offsuit has very poor equity and postflop playability vs a standard UTG+1 opening range, and I'll be out of position. No reason to gamble with this hand — fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.
J3 offsuit in UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak, unconnected hand that plays poorly out of position. No reason to open this from early position.

94s is a suited hand with some potential for flush draws or straight draws, but it's gapped and weak from UTG+1 (early position) in a 5-handed game. Facing a standard BB of 100 into a 150 pot, limping risks playing OOP postflop with a marginal hand against ranges that can include strong holdings. Raising lacks value and fold equity isn't reliable early. Standard play is to fold and wait for better spots to maximize stack long-term over 150 hands.

I am on the Button with Ten-Eight offsuit (Td 8h) in an unopened pot. While the Button allows for a wide opening range to steal the blinds, T8o is generally below the threshold for a profitable open, typically falling outside the top 50% of hands. Furthermore, the players in the blinds (Minni and Flash) have large stacks and have demonstrated willingness to play back or call, making a steal attempt with a marginal hand like this less attractive (negative EV). With 71 BBs, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The standard and disciplined play is to fold.

I’m on the small blind with Ac2c heads-up vs only the big blind. A2 suited is a very playable heads-up holding (nut ace + backdoor club equity) and I don’t want to let the BB see a free flop with position. With deep stacks (~155bb) I’ll open to the minimum (raise to 200) to take the initiative, build a smallish pot, and apply fold equity. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate/fold; if called I’ll play cautiously out of position and c-bet appropriate flops. Completing/passively calling would cede initiative, and folding is too weak here — so raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Five suited. Minni, in the Small Blind, has made a minimum raise to 200. Given the excellent pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 400) and the strength of a suited King in a blind-vs-blind battle, a call is the most standard and profitable play. This allows me to see a flop in position with a hand that has good playability and potential.

Heads‑up out of position vs the BB after my preflop raise. I have A♣2♣ on 6♠ 3♥ 8♣: nut ace as a single overcard, plus a backdoor club runner‑runner draw and an A‑blocker. The flop is dry and my hand fares well versus a wide defending range — many Kx/Qx/Jx and weaker suited hands will fold to pressure. A medium continuation bet (~50% pot) takes the initiative, charges hands that currently beat me (pairs) and the few turn/river draws, and preserves fold equity. Plan: bet 200 to win the pot now or get called and continue selectively (turn ace/club or pot‑control on bricks). If Flash raises big, I’ll re‑evaluate and will often fold since my ace‑high has poor equity vs two‑pair/sets.

On a dry, low board of 6s-3h-8c, I am holding King-high with a backdoor spade draw. Minni, in the Small Blind, has continuation-bet 200 into a 400 pot. While I am in position and have two overcards, this board does not connect with my hand at all, and I have very little equity to continue against a standard c-bet from an aggressive opponent who has shown a willingness to barrel. Given my healthy stack and the long-term goal of maximizing it over 150 hands, I prefer to fold this marginal hand now rather than getting involved in a potentially expensive and difficult spot on later streets.

Pocket Kings is a premium hand, second only to AA pre-flop. As UTG with 5 players and deep stacks, a standard raise is optimal to build the pot, get value from worse hands, and thin the field to reduce multi-way pots where KK is vulnerable. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is a balanced size that has been common in prior hands and doesn't overcommit early.

I hold J3o (Jack of Clubs, Three of Diamonds) in middle position (UTG+1) facing a raise to 300 from the UTG player (Elon). This is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability, often dominated by the opener's range. With three players yet to act behind me and facing a raise, the pot odds do not justify a call, and the hand is too weak for a bluff 3-bet. The standard and only reasonable play is to fold.

I’m on the button with 4♦3♣ — a very weak, unsuited small connector. Elon opened UTG to 300 in a 5-handed game, which represents a fairly strong, and often dominating, range. Calling 300 into a 450 pot gives me ~40% immediate pot odds (300/(450+300)); 4-3o doesn’t have that equity vs an UTG opening range and plays poorly postflop. The SB and BB act after me and can squeeze or isolate, so calling or min-raising invites difficult spots and potential large losses. With a healthy stack (15,825) and no compelling reason to gamble here, folding to preserve chips and wait for a better opportunity is the +EV line.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. Elon has opened from UTG with a 3x raise. Q5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a player opening from early position, as it is easily dominated and lacks post-flop playability. Given the tournament context and my current stack size, there is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff 3-bet. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision here.
T7o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. UTG opening range is typically tight, and T7o plays poorly postflop with limited equity. Not worth defending 200 chips with this holding.

I am in UTG position with Tc 2c. This is a very weak hand with poor playability due to the large gap and low kicker. Opening from early position requires a strong range (typically pocket pairs, strong broadways, or premium suited connectors) to avoid playing weak hands out of position against multiple opponents. T2s is well outside of any reasonable opening range. Therefore, the only correct play is to fold.

5-handed, UTG+1 — A6o is marginal but worth opening in short-handed play. Limping is passive and surrenders initiative to the blinds; I want fold equity and to define ranges. I’ll make a standard 3xBB open to 300 (total), which is consistent with table sizing. Plan: take the lead postflop against single callers, but be prepared to fold to a large 3-bet since A6o is often dominated versus tight 3-bet ranges.

I am on the button holding Js 2s. While the hand is suited, it is structurally very weak to be playing against an early position raise from Minni, who has been showing a lot of aggression recently. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without a strong relative hand strength, and a 3-bet would be too speculative. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.

7d4h is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high card value. Minni raised from UTG+1, and I'm out of position in the SB with Elon still to act. Clear fold - no reason to defend this garbage hand.

73o is a complete trash hand with no playability. Facing a raise from early/mid position (UTG+1 in 5-handed game), Minni's range is strong: pairs, broadways, suited connectors, etc. I'm OOP in BB, and even though pot odds are ~2.25:1, my equity is dismal (~20-30% at best vs tight range, worse vs loose). No fold equity for a 3bet, and calling sets up tough postflop spots OOP with dominated hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Long-term EV positive.

I'm UTG with 9h8h in a 5-handed, deep-stacked game (~160 BB). 9-8 suited is very playable in position postflop and has good implied odds, so I want the initiative rather than limp and invite a multiway pot. A standard 3x BB open (300) applies pressure to the blinds, narrows the field, and gives me leverage postflop. If I face a large/clear 3-bet (e.g. ~800+ from Claude/Elon) I'll fold; against a smaller 3-bet I can consider a call and play postflop for implied odds. Plan: c-bet favorable/connected heart boards, proceed cautiously on dry high-card boards without improvement.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Minni has opened from under-the-gun (UTG) with a raise to 300. Given my poor hand and being in early position with several players yet to act, there is no strategic reason to play this hand. Folding is the only viable professional play.

K9o on the button facing a UTG open from Minni who has been opening frequently. This is a decent spot to flat call in position - K9o has enough playability to see a flop with position advantage. 3-betting is an option but K9o isn't ideal for that. Calling 300 to play in position is the best line here.

Pocket Aces is the premium starting hand, far ahead of Minni's UTG raising range and Claude's button calling range. In the small blind, 3-betting is standard to build the pot, charge draws, protect equity, and potentially isolate. Pro in BB has 70bb but our hand plays well multiway if necessary. Sizing to 1200 (4x the initial raise, considering dead money and pot of 750) is aggressive yet balanced for deep stacks (~90bb effective), denying equity to worse hands and getting value from broadways and pairs.

I am in the Big Blind with QTo facing a UTG open from Minni, a flat from the Button, and a large squeeze raise to 1200 from the Small Blind. My hand is dominated by the ranges of the players involved, especially the squeezer and the original UTG opener who is yet to act. The pot odds are poor (less than 2:1), and I would be playing out of position in a bloated pot with a marginal off-suit hand. Furthermore, the original raiser (Minni) could still 4-bet, forcing me out after putting chips in. This is a clear fold situation.

Fold. I opened to 300 with 9h8h because it’s a playable, speculative hand in position — but Elon’s 3‑bet to 1,200 (I must call 900 more) is a sizable response and Claude has already called, creating a multiway pot where I’ll be out of position. 9‑8♥ is worth seeing flops in the right spots (heads‑up or in position against a wide 3‑bettor), but here calling 900 commits chips into a bloated pot with poor positional dynamics and little fold equity. Re‑raising to the 2,100 minimum is too loose with this holding (it risks a 5‑bet or expensive spots versus strong ranges). This matches my preflop plan to fold to a large/clear 3‑bet (~800+), so I’ll conserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon in the SB after I flatted Minni's open. K9o is too weak to continue against a 3-bet - I'm getting ~2:1 odds but K9o plays poorly against a 3-bet range. Even with position, I'll often make second-best hands (second pair, dominated kings). This is a clear fold.

I am dealt 10-2 offsuit in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and is not worth playing from any position, especially not from early position where I have several players behind me yet to act. Given my healthy stack size and the stage of the tournament, there is no reason to attempt a high-risk bluff or enter a pot with such poor equity. Folding is the standard and correct play here.
8-7 offsuit in UTG+1 is a weak hand that doesn't play well from early position. Not suited, no high card value. Standard fold here - no reason to open this hand with 4 players still to act behind me.

Preflop on the button with Jd2h, a trash hand with poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and dominated by better jacks if called. Folds from UTG and HJ increase steal potential, but J2o is outside a profitable button shoving or raising range here due to reverse implied odds and bad postflop prospects. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are marginal (2.5:1), but hand unplays postflop. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots with 100BB depth, hand 80/150.

Action is folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold 7h8h, a strong suited connector. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, this hand is too strong to fold and benefits from raising to deny equity to the Big Blind's weak holdings while building a pot with a hand that has excellent post-flop playability. My history shows a preference for opening to 250 (2.5BB) when entering pots, which offers a good balance of fold equity and risk management against the chip leader in the Big Blind.

I have J♠4♣ — a weak, unsuited hand, but calling is cheap. The SB opened to 2.5bb (250); I need to call 150 into a 350 pot, so calling gives ~30% pot odds to see a flop. With only the SB vs me remaining I'll have positional advantage post‑flop (I act after him), and Pro's SB opening range is likely wide, so I can realize equity or fold to aggression. I don't want to 3‑bet bluff out of position with this hand or inflate the pot unnecessarily with marginal holdings. Plan: call to see the flop; fold to large turn/river aggression if I miss, or play for value if I pair my J or pick up good equity.

I hold 7h8h on a K-3-2 rainbow board. Although I raised pre-flop from the Small Blind, this dry King-high texture hits the Big Blind's defending range reasonably well (Kx, pocket pairs). My hand has no showdown value (7-high) and no immediate draws. The opponent, Minni, is the chip leader and has demonstrated 'sticky' tendencies in previous hands, often calling down with made hands. C-betting with zero equity against a sticky big stack is risky, especially as the short stack at the table. Checking is a prudent play to preserve chips; I will fold to any aggression.

I completely missed the flop with J♠4♣ — no pair, only a very weak backdoor spade possibility and negligible equity. Pot is 500 and there's nothing to call on this street (Pro checked), so the lowest-risk, highest-expected-value line is to check and use my position to see the turn for free. Betting here would be a pure bluff that bloats the pot with a marginal holding and exposes me to a check-raise or a call from better hands. Plan: check and evaluate the turn — fold to sizable aggression if I still miss, call small bets to realize equity, and only start betting/raising if I pair or pick up credible equity.

On the Turn, the board reads [3s, Kc, 2d, 6s]. I still hold just 7-high with [7h 8h] and no draws (not even a gutshot). My flop check was intended to control the pot size against the chip leader who has sticky tendencies. Minni's check-back on the flop suggests weakness or a trap, but given the dry board, likely marginal showdown value (weak pair) or air. The turn card [6s] brings a potential flush draw but doesn't improve my hand or clearly improve my range advantage enough to justify a zero-equity bluff against a calling station. Betting here would be spewing chips. Checking is the disciplined play; I will fold to any bet.

I check. Holding J♠4♣ on 3♠ K♣ 2♦ 6♠ I have essentially missed — no made hand and no realistic draw. A five‑card spade flush is impossible (there are only three spades among my card + board and only the river remains), and my only outs are pairing the J or the 4 (3 Jacks + 3 Fours = 6 outs → 6/46 ≈ 13%). The pot is 500 and there is nothing to call on this street, so betting would be a thin bluff that bloats the pot with very little equity and risks a check‑raise or a call from better hands (Kx, small pair, or even showdown cards). Best line is to keep the pot small, see the river for free, and only continue with a pair or to fold to meaningful river aggression.

On the River, the board is [3s, Kc, 2d, 6s, Ah]. The pot is 500. The Ace on the river is a significant card. It improves my range (which includes many Ax hands that might check-call or check-back flop/turn) but also hits the opponent's range of floaty Ace-highs. My hand (7 high) has absolutely zero showdown value, so the only way to win is by bluffing. However, the opponent (Minni) is the chip leader and has demonstrated significant 'sticky' tendencies in previous hands, frequently calling down with marginal holdings. Bluffing into a station on a board that completes a double-broadway look (A and K) is risky, as they might hero-call with any piece of the board (Kx, 6x, or even a pocket pair). As the shortest stack, preserving chips is crucial. Attempting to bluff a calling station for a small pot is negative EV. The prudent play is to give up, check, and fold to any bet.

The river completed 3♠ K♣ 2♦ 6♠ A♥ and I hold J♠4♣ — I missed entirely. The pot is 500 and there is nothing to call (to‑call 0). Pro (the preflop raiser) checked every street, which is a line consistent with either a weak pair, showdown‑worthy but passive Kx/Ax, or a missed draw. Betting here would be a pure thin bluff: to make a small bet of ~250 profitable I’d need the villain to fold >33% of the time, which is optimistic given his passive line. There’s also the risk of a check‑raise or getting called by any ace, king, or pair. With essentially no showdown equity and good stack preservation, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play is to check and take the free showdown.
4d5h is a weak offsuit hand from UTG position. This is well outside my opening range from early position. Easy fold.

T8h suited is a speculative hand with some potential for implied odds and straight/flush draws, but in UTG+1 position with 4 players behind (including button Pro with 68BB, deep SB Minni, and BB Flash), it's too vulnerable to domination by better suited connectors, broadway, or pairs. Standard play is to fold marginal suited connectors early to preserve stack and avoid playing out of position postflop. Tournament mid-phase (hand 81/150), stacks deep (~100BB), prioritize premium opens to maximize EV.

I am on the Button with 6d 5s (65o). The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds from, 65o is typically below the standard opening range threshold, even for a wide Button strategy. Suited connectors (like 65s) would be a standard open, but the off-suit variant realizes equity poorly post-flop and is easily dominated. With effective stacks around 68 BBs, there is no pressure to make marginal shoves or loose opens. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

Heads-up to the big blind with As6c — a very playable hand in the small blind. Everyone folded to me so I want initiative and fold equity rather than just completing. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) — big enough to apply pressure and isolate Flash, but small enough to keep postflop decisions comfortable with deep stacks. If called I’ll c-bet favorable ace/range textures and proceed cautiously when I face significant resistance or a 3-bet; if folded I pick up the blind and the pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with 42 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has raised from the Small Blind to 300. While I would have position post-flop and the price to call is only 200 more into a 400 pot (giving me 3:1 odds), 42o realizes its equity very poorly and is rarely worth defending against a standard open, even from the Small Blind. Given Minni's active play, I expect her to continue frequently on various textures, making it difficult to win the pot without a strong hand. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Th2h is a very weak suited hand with poor playability. From UTG in a 5-handed game, it's standard to fold trash like this pre-flop to avoid playing out of position with a hand that rarely makes strong hands. Pot is small (150 from blinds), no need to speculate with 10-high suited connector wannabe. Preserve stack for premium hands to maximize long-term EV in the tournament.

Playing 5-handed, sitting in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with Ace-Jack offsuit. This is a strong starting hand that falls well within the standard opening range for this position. With a healthy stack of 68 BBs, I want to take the initiative. Folding is too passive, and limping allows opponents to see a cheap flop. I will raise to 250 (2.5x BB), a standard sizing to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately or play in position against the blinds.

I’m on the button with 6♣5♥ facing an UTG+1 open to 250 (pot 400, to-call 250). Calling requires ~38% equity (250/(400+250)), and 6-5 offsuit has poor equity vs an early-position opener’s relatively strong range and plays badly multiway. Two players (SB/BB) are yet to act so I risk a squeeze/isolation and I have little fold/backdoor equity to make a light 3‑bet profitable. With a deep stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot from the button — fold here.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Five offsuit. Pro has opened with a raise to 250 from UTG+1. Q5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a standard opening range, even with the pot odds offered. I will fold and wait for a better spot to protect my stack.

AJs is a strong hand in the big blind facing a UTG+1 open. Pro has a shorter stack (6550) and opened to 250. This is a clear 3-bet spot for value - AJs plays well heads up and I can extract value or take down the pot preflop. 3-betting to around 750 is standard sizing.

I opened to 2.5bb with AJo from the Cutoff (UTG+1 in 5-handed) and facing a 3-bet to 7.5bb from the Big Blind (Claude). Claude has been active and has shown 3-betting capability previously. AJo is a marginal but defendable hand in this spot, particularly because I will have position on the aggressor post-flop. The price is reasonable (calling 500 into a pot of roughly 1050), and folding would be too tight given the active dynamic and short-handed play. 4-betting is likely overplaying this hand as a bluff. I will call and navigate post-flop in position.

Flopped top pair top kicker on Jd 8c 7d. This is a great result for AJs. Board has some draws (diamond flush draw, straight draws with 9T), so I want to bet for value and protection. Pro called my 3-bet, so they likely have medium pairs, broadway hands, or suited connectors. I should c-bet around 60-65% pot to get value from worse Jx hands, pocket pairs, and charge draws.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-Jack on a J-8-7 board in a 3-bet pot. The board is wet with flush and straight draws, which justifies my opponent's larger c-bet sizing (approx 60% pot) whether they have value (Overpairs, Sets, AJ) or a semi-bluff (Flush draws, straight draws). Folding TPTK here is too weak given the board texture and my relative hand strength; I am ahead of bluffs and chop with AJ, only losing to overpairs and sets. Raising would likely only get action from better hands and fold out bluffs. Calling keeps my opponent's range wide and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn with a manageable Stack-to-Pot Ratio (~1.5).

Turn Ts is a dangerous card - it completes 9x straights (7-8-9-T-J). Pro called my 3-bet and flop bet, which could include hands like 98s, T9s, or even just 9x suited connectors. JT also made two pair. My TPTK is still good against flush draws and weaker Jx, but I should check to control pot size on this scary runout. If Pro bets, I can call and evaluate river. If they check back, I can potentially value bet river on safe cards.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AJ) on a board of J-8-7-T. The turn card (T) makes the board more coordinated, bringing possible straight completions (for 99 or 9x holdings) and sets (TT). Claude 3-bet pre-flop and fired a large continuation bet on the flop, but has now checked the turn. His check could indicate pot control with an overpair (QQ/KK/AA) that fears the wet board, a hand like AJ looking to chop/pot-control, or a give-up from a semi-bluff (like a flush draw). Given the stack-to-pot ratio is roughly 1.5, betting here risks bloating the pot against a range that beats me (sets, straights, overpairs) or folding out hands I crush (bluffs). Checking back controls the pot size, realizes my equity, and allows me to evaluate the river decision with more information, potentially inducing bluffs on the river from missed draws.

River Qd completes the flush draw and keeps straight possibilities alive (any 9 makes 8-9-T-J-Q). My AJs is now just a bluff catcher - I have TPTK but the board is very scary. Key point: I have the Ad which blocks the nut flush. Pro checked turn showing weakness, but could have been slow-playing or had a draw that just got there. Betting for value is risky as I only get called by better (flushes, straights, QJ, JT). Checking and potentially calling a small bet makes more sense given my blocker.

I hold pair of Jacks with an Ace kicker on a very coordinated board of [Jd 8c 7d Ts Qd]. The river Queen is a scare card that completes potential flush draws (3 diamonds) and connects straights (AK makes broadway, 9x makes lower straight), while also being an overcard to my Jack. Claude's line of 3-bet pre, bet flop, check turn, check river suggests either pot control with a hand like AA/KK/AJ or a give-up. My hand has showdown value against missed bluffs but loses to most of his value range (Overpairs, AQ, Straights, Flushes). Betting achieves little as better hands won't fold and worse hands won't call. I will check back to realize my equity and see a cheap showdown.

I am in UTG position with 6c 2s, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em (6-2 offsuit). Given the poor hand strength and the disadvantage of being first to act pre-flop, there is no justification for entering the pot. My stack is healthy (68 BB), so I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Q♦5♥ is a weak offsuit hand with poor postflop playability and a high chance of being dominated. I’m effectively out of position (UTG+1) and facing a raise (to call 100, min-raise to 200). Calling 100 or turning this into a raise would be speculative and often a losing proposition against typical opener ranges here. Stack sizes are deep enough to wait for a better spot — preserve chips and fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten of spades, which is a strong starting hand. It has been folded to me, so I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and value-bet my position. This size is consistent with my previous opening raises.

Holding 8d9d suited connectors in the small blind facing a button raise to 300. This is a decent speculative hand with straight and flush potential. Flash is raising from the button which suggests a wide range. Suited connectors play well with implied odds and I only need to call 250 more. Calling to see a flop is the best play here - folding is too tight and 3-betting doesn't play as well with this hand type.

Qh9h is a strong suited connector with excellent playability, flush and straight potential. Pre-flop pot odds are excellent: calling 200 more into 700 (pot becomes 900), getting over 4:1 direct odds with strong implied odds deep-stacked. Flash's button raise is likely wide after two folds, and Claude's SB call suggests a speculative range. Calling from BB keeps the pot multi-way where my hand realizes equity well, and I can play post-flop effectively from position in a bloated pot. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks isolation with a hand better multi-way and allows opponents to fold better. Standard call to realize equity.

Flopped nothing on K-J-4 with two spades. I have 8d9d - just backdoor flush draw (one diamond on board) and backdoor straight draws. This board hits Flash's button opening range well (broadway cards). With 8-high and only backdoor equity, I should check and see what develops. No reason to bet into this dry board with no made hand.

The flop of Ks Jd 4s completely misses my Qh9h. I have no pair, no straight draw (queen-high with a gutshot at best but unlikely), and no flush draw since the board has spades and my suit is hearts. Flash's button raise pre-flop is wide, likely including many king-x, suited broadways, and speculative hands that connect well with this king-high board. Claude's small blind check suggests weakness, but Flash acts last and can represent strength with position. Betting here as a bluff is poor; the board favors calling stations and value hands behind me, and with two opponents, my fold equity is low while risking a large pot I have no equity in. Checking preserves my stack, allows pot control, and lets me fold to any bet without committing more chips. Multi-way pots post-flop with a miss warrant passive play to avoid spewing chips long-term in the tournament.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have the button. The flop of K-J-4 is relatively good for my range, and I hold the Ace of Spades, providing me with the nut flush backdoor potential. Both the Small Blind and Big Blind checked to me, showing weakness. I will place a standard continuation bet of slightly more than half the pot to represent a King or a strong Jack and try to take the pot down now.

I have 8d9d on Jd-4s-Ks - just 8-high with only a backdoor diamond flush draw. Flash c-bet 500 into 900 on a board that heavily favors his button opening range. I have no real equity here - calling 500 with backdoor draws only is burning chips. Easy fold.

Facing Flash's 500 bet into a 1400 pot on a Ks Jd 4s flop, my Qh9h has completely missed: queen-high no pair, no flush draw (hearts), no straight draw (gutshot to T for unlikely straight unlikely given board). Pot odds are about 2.8:1 (500 to call for 1900 pot), but my equity against Flash's button opening range + continuation bet is near zero (<10%) as he has frequent kings (Kx ~15-20% of range), Jx, sets, and draws, while my hand is a pure bluff-catcher with no blockers. Claude's fold confirms weakness, isolating vs Flash who bets wide but strong here. Calling commits to a bloated pot OOP with no hand, no fold equity for raise, and poor implied odds deep-stacked but no improvement path. Folding preserves stack (9675, calling ~5%) for better spots in tournament; spewing chips multi-way pre but now heads-up is still -EV long-term. Standard discipline: fold marginal hands that miss entirely.

I’m UTG with J♠8♦ — a weak, offsuit jack. In 5‑handed play UTG needs a fairly tight opening range; J8o is frequently dominated by higher jacks, broadways and many Ax/Kx hands and plays poorly OOP. With 160bb stacks at the table and several aggressive players behind, I’m likely to face 3‑bets or multiway action that this hand doesn’t handle well. The cost to fold is small (forfeiting the chance to contest a 150 pot for a 100 call), so I preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am in UTG+1 with 89s. In a 5-handed tournament, this is a standard opening hand from almost any position. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage the post-flop playability of suited connectors.

5d9d on the button facing a 300 raise from Flash. Suited one-gapper with position - playable but marginal. Flash has been active throughout the tournament. I'll call and play in position, looking to connect with the flop or fold to aggression when I miss.

Preflop in the small blind with A♣4♠ offsuit facing a raise to 300 from Flash in UTG+1 (early position) and a flat call from Claude on the button. A4o is a weak ace, offsuit, with poor postflop playability out of position. The early position raise suggests strength from Flash, and Claude's call adds multiway pot risk. Pot odds are about 3:1 (calling 250 into 750), but equity against typical raising range is insufficient, especially OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament with 84/150 hands played and deep stacks.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four offsuit (T4o), facing a raise to 300 from Flash (UTG+1) and a call from Claude (Button). The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 950, roughly 4.75:1), but T4o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. Flash's early position raise suggests a strong range, and even against Claude's button flatting range, I am likely dominated. The reverse implied odds are significant. Preserving chips is better than defending a trash hand in this spot.

I raised pre-flop and was called by Claude on the button. The flop of 2c-Kh-7d is quite dry and generally favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, as I am more likely to hold a King (AK, KQ, KJ) than the caller. I have very little equity with 9-high, so a continuation bet is the most effective way to win the pot immediately. Claude has shown a willingness to fold to continuation bets on similar boards in previous hands (like Hand #83). I will bet approximately 60% of the pot to represent strength.

I have 5d9d on a 2c Kh 7d board - completely missed. Only one diamond on board so no real flush draw, no straight draw, just 9-high. Flash's c-bet of 450 into 750 is standard. With no pair and no meaningful draws, this is a clear fold.

I am holding Ace-Six suited (Ac 6c) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. In a 5-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to open for a raise, as it has good post-flop playability and nut-flush potential. My stack is deep (159 BB), allowing me to play aggressively and put pressure on the shorter stacks like Pro. I will continue my established pattern of opening for 3x the big blind to 300.
Holding 2c 5h - a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Flash opened from UTG to 300. This is a clear fold - no reason to play this hand out of position against an UTG open.

53o is an extremely weak hand with poor connectivity and no high card strength. Facing a UTG raise from Flash, who has shown aggression in early position in past hands, their range is strong (likely pairs, broadway, suited connectors). Calling 300 into a 450 pot from the button offers poor implied odds for such a speculative hand, as it plays poorly multi-way against strong ranges and has low equity. Raising has no fold equity with this holding. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in position with 96bb depth.

I hold 5c 2s in the Small Blind, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 300 from the Under-the-Gun player (Flash), I am out of position with garbage cards. Even though the raise is standard size (3bb), my equity is extremely low, and I have no fold equity or playability post-flop. Folding is the only correct decision here to preserve my stack of 6675.

Flash opened to 300 from UTG and everyone else folded — I’m in the BB with Kh9c and would need to put another 200 (total 300 committed). Calling 200 into a 450 pot is cheap and makes the pot ~650; break-even equity is ~200/(450+200) ≈ 31%. K9o is marginal vs a tight UTG range but has a king blocker and reasonable playability OOP. A 3-bet bluff is possible occasionally, but against a likely sticky/aggressive opener and with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll call to see a flop cheaply and proceed based on texture: fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I miss, continue if I hit top pair or a strong draw, and use position/stack to apply pressure selectively.

Heads-up vs the UTG opener (Flash). I called preflop and the flop comes 2♦ J♠ 6♦; I hold K♥9♣ — no pair and no diamond draw. Pot is 650 and action is checked to me. Out of position with a marginal hand and deep stacks, I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bluff into a range that will call or raise with many hands (Jx, Kx, Ax). A bet could sometimes take it down, but the risk of being raised or called by better hands makes leading here unattractive. I’ll check and see a turn; plan is to fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I improve or pick up a clear read to bluff.

I have middle pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker on a board of 2d Js 6d. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play here. This bet allows me to take down the pot immediately or extract value from weaker hands and draws while protecting my hand from potential overcards on the turn.

Flash opened UTG and I defended the BB with K♥9♣. Flop is 2♦ J♠ 6♦, pot 1100 and Flash bets 450 (~40% pot). Calling 450 puts 450 into a pot that will be ~2000, so my break-even equity is only ~22.5%. K9o has borderline but workable showdown equity against a wide UTG c-bet range (Jx, some Kx/Ax, diamond draws and bluffs). I have no diamond draw and no pair, so I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand — a raise would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity and high risk of a shove or a call by better hands. I’ll call the small bet to realize equity and see a turn, then fold to significant turn/river aggression unless I pair up or pick up a clear read/bluffing opportunity.

I never improved — K♥9♣ on 2♦ J♠ 6♦ T♠ has no pair and no flush/straight draw. Flash bet the flop and then checked the turn, which looks like pot control or a missed card; his range still contains many Jx, Tx, Kx and diamond draws that will call a turn bluff. Pot is 1,550 and stacks are deep, so I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot and lets me fold to meaningful aggression; if he checks back I can reevaluate the river, if he bets big I'll fold. I will check and preserve my stack.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a board that is becoming quite coordinated (2d-Js-6d-Ts). After Minni called my flop continuation bet, she likely has a piece of the board or a draw. The Ten of spades is a card that could have easily improved her range (hands like JT, QT, or even a pair of tens). By checking behind on the turn, I control the pot size and ensure I get to a river with a hand that has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet for three streets. This also protects me from a potential check-raise.

I have K♥9♣ on a river of 2♦ J♠ 6♦ T♠ T♣. I missed everything — no pair and no relevant draw — so I have virtually no showdown value. Flash opened UTG, c-bet the flop and then checked the turn; that line is consistent with hands that will call a river bet (Jx, Tx, A/K type hands) as well as missed draws. To bluff river profitably I’d need an unrealistically high fold frequency from an UTG opener (given the line), and a raise/call from better hands would cost me a lot. Policing the pot with a small “blocking” bet is unlikely to change ranges meaningfully. The +EV, lowest-variance play is to check and fold to any sizable river bet; if Flash checks back I’ll lose the showdown but avoid throwing away extra chips on a marginal bluff.

I have two pair, Tens and Sixes, with an Ace kicker. The board is quite coordinated with a Jack and two Tens. Minni has checked to me on the river after calling my flop continuation bet and checking through the turn. While my hand has decent showdown value, betting here is unlikely to extract value from worse hands, as most hands that would call a river bet (like a Jack or a Ten) currently beat me. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and reach showdown without risking a check-raise.
Ac9c is a solid suited ace in UTG position. With 117 BBs effective, this is a standard open. A9 suited has good playability with flush potential and high card value. Opening to 2.5x (250).

KQ offsuit from UTG+1 facing an UTG raise to 2.5BB. This is a marginal hand at best early position. Calling puts me out of position post-flop with poor implied odds against a strong UTG range (likely pairs, AK, AQ, broadway). 3-betting risks too much equity denial or getting 4-bet. Folding preserves our deep stack (96BB) for better spots with position or premium hands. Opponent tendencies show Claude raises strong from UTG and defends well. Goal is long-term stack maximization.

I hold Ten of Diamonds and Three of Hearts (T3o) on the Button. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against an Under-the-Gun (UTG) opening range. Claude raised to 250 from UTG, signaling strength. Even with the positional advantage, T3o is far too weak to call or 3-bet bluff profitably. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I have 9♦2♣ in the small blind facing an UTG open to 250 (pot 400, to call 200). 9‑2 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands — highly dominated and with almost no playability out of position against an UTG opening and the big blind still to act. Calling 200 here would be investing with very poor equity and create difficult multi‑street decisions; 3‑betting is not reasonable. Best play is to fold and preserve the stack for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Three suited (Kd 3d). Claude has opened from UTG for 250. Everyone else has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of roughly 3.6:1, meaning I need about 21.5% equity to make a profitable call. K3s is a reasonable defending hand in this spot, especially given the suitedness and the attractive price. I will call to see a flop and play cautiously out of position against an UTG range.

I am first to act on the flop after defending my big blind pre-flop. The board is 8c-4s-2h, which is very dry and disconnected. My hand, Kd-3d, has missed the flop entirely, though I have a backdoor diamond draw and one overcard. In this situation, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) and see how they react. Leading out (donk-betting) on this texture doesn't make much sense for my range, so I will check.

I have Ac9c on 8c4s2h - ace high with backdoor flush draw. Dry board that misses most of Flash's BB calling range. I have position and preflop initiative. Standard c-bet spot to take it down or set up for backdoor equity. Betting around 40% pot.

I missed the flop entirely with my Kd 3d. Claude opened from UTG and followed up with a continuation bet on a dry board of 8c-4s-2h. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive (4.4:1), I have very little equity against a standard UTG opening range. Floating out of position with just one overcard and a weak kicker is a losing play here. I will fold and preserve my stack.

UTG in a 5-handed game with K5o offsuit. This hand has poor playability, low equity when called or raised, and is easily dominated by better kings. Standard fold from early position to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Pot is small (150), but risking chips preflop with this hand is -EV given the positions behind me.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1 in 5-handed) with K6o. This hand is too weak to open-raise profitably from this position given the stack depths and the players behind (Minni, Flash, Claude) who have shown capability to defend and 3-bet. K6o is dominated by most calling ranges. It is a standard fold.

I’m on the button with Qd3h — a weak offsuit hand but great positional opportunity against only the blinds. With UTG and UTG+1 folded I want to apply pressure and steal the blinds rather than limp or just call; a 3x open (to 300 total) gives decent fold equity and keeps the raise meaningful against wide defending ranges. If either blind 3-bets I’ll fold this hand (dominated and not worth a bloated pot vs a strong range). If called I’ll play post‑flop in position: c‑bet when I connect or the board gives me backdoor equity, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy resistance. Raise to 300 balances aggression and risk while exploiting my positional advantage.

I am holding Ten-Deuce offsuit (Ts 2h) in the small blind. Minni has raised from the button to 300. Ten-Deuce is an extremely weak hand, and calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against an active player. Given the poor hand strength and positional disadvantage, folding is the standard and most prudent play.
4c7h is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the button, and while I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds, 74o plays poorly postflop and is not a profitable defend even at these odds. Fold.

I am holding 7h 4d in the UTG position, which is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low rank. Being first to act with four opponents behind me, opening with such a weak hand is a losing strategy. With 67 BBs, I need to preserve my stack for better spots. The correct play is to fold immediately.

8♣5♣ is a marginal suited connector and I'm in early position (UTG+1) with three players left to act (BTN, SB, BB). Calling 100 will put me out of position in likely multiway pots against opponents who have shown frequent raising/3‑betting; open‑raising to 200 is too loose from this seat and will get me into awkward spots. With a healthy 153 BB stack I should conserve equity and wait for a better spot — fold preflop.

I am on the button with 8s 5d. This is a very weak hand. While the action has folded to me and I could potentially steal the blinds, both Claude and Elon have shown aggression and a willingness to defend or 3-bet in previous hands. Given the tournament context and my current healthy stack of 164.25 big blinds, there is no need to take a high-variance, low-equity spot with a weak hand like 85o. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
JTd is a strong suited connector, and with everyone folding to me in the small blind, I should raise to put pressure on the big blind. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Pre-flop in BB facing a 3x raise from SB after three folds. J5s is a suited one-gapper with good playability: potential for nut flush draws, wheel straights, broadway straights, and pair+draw combos. Claude's SB raise range is wide (suited aces, broadway, pairs, suited connectors), so J5s has decent equity (~35-40% vs typical open range) and realizes well multi-way or heads-up. Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 into 400 (pot will be 600 after call, immediate odds ~3:1, need ~25%). Folding is too tight in BB defense spot; raising OOP with this hand is suboptimal as it plays poorly if re-raised. Plan to call, see flop, and play draws aggressively or fold to c-bets on bad boards. Stack depths are deep (~95BB), so speculative hands like this profit long-term.

Flop is 3h Kc 3c - a paired board with a king. I completely missed with JTd - no pair, no diamond draw. As the preflop aggressor, I should make a small continuation bet to represent the king. Elon's BB calling range could have many hands that miss here too. A 1/3 pot c-bet of around 200 is appropriate - cheap enough to fold if raised but maintains aggression.

Flop is a complete whiff for J5s: no pair, no flush draw (board has clubs and hearts), no straight draw (paired treys and king block most possibilities). Claude's 200 bet into 600 (now pot 800) from SB after wide open range is likely value with top pair (Kx), overpairs, two-pair, sets, or even bluffs/draws, but my jack-high has minimal equity (~10-20% at best) and poor playability going forward. Pot odds are 4:1 but implied odds are weak OOP heads-up with reverse implied odds on scary boards. Raising has no fold equity with air and plays poorly if called. Folding preserves stack for premium spots; speculative pre-flop call didn't connect, standard discipline.

UTG with 8♠2♠ — this is one of the weakest starting hands. Out of position against four players to act, it’s frequently dominated and has very poor multiway playability. Calling 100 to limp gives bad pot control and invites raises; opening (min-raising) with this holding is overly aggressive and easily exploited. With a deep but healthy stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am holding Ace-Queen suited (Ac Qc) in UTG+1. This is a premium starting hand, particularly in a 5-handed game. Since Minni (UTG) has folded, I am the first player to act on the pot. My strategy is to open-raise to build value and narrow the field. Throughout the tournament, I have consistently used a 3x open (300 chips), so I will maintain that sizing to remain balanced and represent a strong range.

I have A9o on the button facing a 3x open from Flash in UTG+1. This is a good spot to 3-bet - I have position, a decent Ax hand, and can either take down the pot preflop or play a raised pot with position. A standard 3-bet to around 850 should work here.

Facing a raise from Flash in early position and a 3-bet from Claude on the button. My hand J5o is very weak, offsuit, poor playability, and low equity against likely strong ranges. Pot odds are not favorable for calling 800 into 1300 with trash. Pro acts behind in BB, but no value in continuing. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 3d, a very weak hand. The action pre-flop has been heavy: Flash raised to 300 from early position, and Claude 3-bet to 850 from the Button. Facing a raise and a re-raise, my hand has virtually no playability or equity. Cold calling a 3-bet with 5-3 offsuit out of position is a losing play. I must fold immediately to preserve my stack.

I am holding Ace-Queen suited (Ac Qc), a premium hand in a 5-handed game. I opened for 300 and faced a 3-bet from Claude on the button to 850. While 4-betting is an option to regain initiative, calling is often more effective with AQ suited to keep the opponent's range wide, including potential bluffs and hands I dominate like AJ or KQ. Calling also prevents me from being forced to fold a high-equity hand if Claude 5-bet jams. I will proceed to the flop and play from there, keeping the pot manageable since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

On this dry, paired board of 3-9-3, I have Ace-high with two overcards and a backdoor nut flush draw. Since I was the caller of the pre-flop 3-bet and am out of position against the button (Claude), the standard and most balanced play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out here would rarely fold out better hands and would likely only be called by hands that currently beat me. By checking, I can see how Claude responds to this board and evaluate whether to call a continuation bet based on his sizing.

I flopped top pair with top kicker (A9 on 3h9s3c). Flash checked to me after calling my 3-bet. This is a great spot to value bet - I have the best hand most of the time against Flash's calling range (underpairs, broadway hands, suited connectors). The paired board is dry and favorable. Betting around 60% pot for value and protection.

On this dry, paired board of 3-9-3, I am facing a continuation bet of 1100 into a pot that is now 2950. Holding Ace-Queen suited, I have the best Ace-high hand, two overcards to the board, and a backdoor nut flush draw. Given the 5-handed dynamics and Claude's position on the button, his 3-betting and C-betting range includes many hands I still beat or have significant equity against, such as Ace-King or potential bluffs. Folding here would be too exploitable on such a static texture. Raising doesn't accomplish much as it likely only gets called by better hands. Therefore, calling is the most balanced approach to see a turn card and evaluate further based on the board development and Claude's subsequent actions.

The 7h on the turn is largely a blank card that doesn't change the equity of the hands significantly. As I am out of position against Claude, who was the pre-flop 3-better and the flop aggressor, the most prudent play is to continue with a check. I have established a check-calling line with my Ace-Queen suited, and leading out now would likely only be called by better hands or force me into a difficult spot if raised. By checking, I allow Claude to either check behind with hands like Ace-King or smaller pairs that I may still have equity against, or continue his aggression with bluffs or value hands. My decision on whether to call a further bet will depend on his sizing and my perceived pot odds to hit an Ace or Queen on the river.

I have top pair top kicker (A9 on 3h9s3c7h). Flash called my flop c-bet and checked again on the turn. The 7h is a blank that doesn't change the board texture much. I should continue betting for value - Flash likely has underpairs, Ax hands, or floats. Betting around 65% pot to extract value and charge draws.

I am holding Ace-Queen suited (Ac Qc) on a board of 3h-9s-3c-7h. I was the pre-flop caller of Claude's 3-bet and have check-called a flop continuation bet of 1100. Claude has now fired a second barrel of 2600 into a pot of 4050. This bet represents about 64% of the pot and a significant portion of Claude's remaining stack. While Claude has shown a tendency to be aggressive (3-betting with hands as weak as J8 in Hand #67), this second barrel on a relatively static, paired board is very polar. Claude's range likely includes overpairs (JJ, QQ, KK, AA), trip 3s, or a strong 9x that he is betting for value. Although I have Ace-high and two overcards, I am only drawing to roughly 6 outs if I am behind, and those outs may not even be clean if Claude has Ace-King or a set. Calling 2600 to potentially face an all-in on the river with only Ace-high is not a winning strategy in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack, which remains healthy, to find a better spot.

In a 5-handed game, pocket fives are strong enough to open for a raise from under the gun. A standard 3x big blind open allows me to take initiative and build a pot, while also giving me the potential to win the blinds immediately. Given my stack size and the aggressive nature of the table, raising is preferable to limping.
Q2o is a very weak hand. Facing a 3x open from UTG, I should fold. This hand has poor playability and I'm out of position. Easy fold.

On the button with J♠ 8♥ offsuit facing a 3bb UTG open from Flash in a 5-handed tournament spot. Deep stacks (92bb effective). J8o is a playable defending hand from the button due to excellent position, straight potential (97T, T97, etc.), and decent blocker to broadway. Pot odds are favorable: calling 300 into 750 (pot + call). Blinds (Pro 65bb SB, Minni deep BB) likely fold wide, isolating heads-up in position vs Flash's likely polarized UTG range including some speculative hands. Folding would be too tight; 3-betting marginal without strong blockers or suited value. Plan: flat call, play postflop aggressively if hits or as bluff in position.

I am holding 92o in the Small Blind. Flash raised from UTG and Elon called on the Button. This is a multi-way pot where I would be out of position with one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. My equity against a UTG opening range and a Button calling range is very poor, and playing this hand post-flop OOP is not profitable. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a weak holding. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I’m in the big blind with A9 offsuit facing an UTG open to 300 and a button cold-call. A9o is too marginal to 3-bet for value vs an UTG opener and a caller — I’d be OOP to both and likely dominated by UTG’s range. Pot is 750 and it’s only 200 more to see a flop (total 300 committed), so the price is correct to defend. I’ll call and see a flop; plan is to continue only with a clear Ace, strong draw, or favorable texture/cheap c-bet — otherwise fold to heavy aggression to preserve my deep stack and keep fold equity for later hands.

Flop: J♥5♣7♣ — I have A♥9♠ (ace‑high only). This is a dry, non‑paired board (two clubs) and I missed — no pair, no meaningful draw. I’m out of position to the UTG opener and the button, so barreling OOP with a marginal ace is a losing line more often than not. I’ll check for pot control and to see actions from the preflop aggressor(s). Plan: call a small c‑bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot, ~300) to realize equity vs wide c‑bets, but fold to larger single bets or any raise (≈≥1/2 pot ~475) since I’m likely behind to UTG’s range. If it checks through I’ll take a free turn and only continue aggressively if I pair the Ace or pick up a strong draw.

I have flopped a set of fives on a board that contains a Jack and two clubs. This is a very strong hand, but the board is somewhat coordinated with potential flush and straight draws. I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against these draws. A bet of around two-thirds of the pot (600 into 950) is a standard sizing here to extract value from one-pair hands like AJ, KJ, or QJ, as well as to charge any club draws.

Flop of Jh5c7c gives me top pair (Js) with a weak 8 kicker in position on the button in a multiway pot (Flash UTG raiser, Minni BB). Flash's UTG range is strong but he c-bets ~2/3 pot after Minni checks, which could be value (AJ+, sets, overpairs) or protecting against clubs/draws. Board has flush draw (clubs) and minor straight draw potential (e.g., 89 OESD for 56789, 68 gutshot), but my top pair has good showdown value and blocks top pair. Pot odds excellent (600 to call into 1550, ~28% implied odds needed, I have more equity). Raising risks isolation with weak kicker vs strong range and Minni behind; folding too tight in position deepstacked (90bb). Plan: call, reassess turn with position, potentially bluff or value bet if improves or Flash weakens. Minni likely weak (check), can fold draws if check-fold.

Out of position with A♥9♠ on J♥‑5♣‑7♣. I checked the flop for pot control as planned. Flash’s bet of 600 is well above the ~300 I’d tolerate — and Elon called behind, making this a multiway pot where my Ace‑high has very little fold equity and poor implied odds. Calling 600 into a 2150 pot (pot ≈2750 after my call) requires ~21.8% equity; I only have ~6 outs (~24% to hit by river) in gross terms, but multiway action and the likelihood opponents have pairs or better means my realized equity is substantially lower and I risk facing larger bets OOP on later streets. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a cleaner spot — fold.

The turn Ten of Clubs is a very coordinated card that completes several potential draws. It brings a third club to the board, making a flush possible, and also completes a straight for any hand containing 8-9. While I have a set of fives, the board texture has become significantly more dangerous since the flop. Elon called my flop bet on the button, which suggests he has a piece of the board or a draw. By checking, I am exercising pot control and looking to see how he reacts to the flush-completing card. If he checks back, I can re-evaluate on the river. If he bets, I still have 10 outs (three 7s, three Jacks, three 10s, and the last 5) to improve to a full house or quads, which gives me enough equity to likely call a reasonably sized bet.

Heads-up on the turn with top pair (Js) and weak 8 kicker on a draw-heavy board: Jh5c7cTc (three clubs, potential straights like 68,89). Flash (UTG raiser) c-bet flop for value/protection but now checks turn, suggesting possible weakness (missed draw, medium strength checking behind for pot control, or trapping). My hand blocks top pair Js, has decent showdown value, and position. Pot 2150 deepstacked (~40bb effective vs Flash). Betting ~2/3 pot (1400) targets his Ax/high cards/draws that missed clubs, gets value from worse pairs (e.g., overpairs without J, 9x, Tx), protects vs river draws, and denies equity. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs; betting maintains initiative and builds pot with one-pair value in tournament spot (hand 90/150). Plan: bet 1400, c/c river thin or fold to raise.

I have a set of fives, which is a very strong hand, but the turn card (Ten of Clubs) is highly problematic as it completes potential flushes and straights. However, with 10 outs to improve to a full house or quads on the river (three Jacks, three Tens, three Sevens, and one Five), I have significant equity even if I am currently behind. Elon's bet of 1400 into a 2150 pot provides decent pot odds, and considering implied odds if I do fill up on the river, a call is mathematically sound. I am also potentially still ahead of hands like AJ or KJ that Elon might be betting for value or protection.

On the river, the board texture is extremely dangerous for my set of fives. The Jack-Five-Seven-Ten-Queen board with three clubs means that any two clubs, any 8-9, and now any K-9 or A-K have made a better hand. My set, while strong on the flop, has been relegated to a mid-strength hand that is likely beaten by Elon's range, especially since he showed aggression on the turn when the flush and straight draws started completing. Betting here would likely only result in getting called by hands that beat me or forcing out hands I already beat. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or to evaluate a potential bet from Elon, though I must be prepared to fold if he bets heavily, as the board runout was nearly worst-case for my specific holding.

River Qh brings stronger top pairs (Qx into range), possible straights (9TJQK), and Flash's range that cbets flop on wet board, checks turn, but calls my turn bet on flush-completing Tc includes many clubs (flushes), better Jx (AJ/KJ), Tx pairs+draws, sets, and two-pair combos, most of which beat my J8 top pair weak kicker. While some Ax, K-high, or missed draws might fold to a value/bluff bet, the risk of getting called/raised by better hands outweighs thin value or fold equity here, especially as he checked river again (weakness but traps possible). Checking realizes showdown value against his medium strength/checking range, maintains stack preservation in late tournament (hand 90/150), and avoids bloating pot OOP post-check with marginal hand. Position allows free showdown; betting half-pot+ commits too much (~3k into 5k leaves ~4k, poor risk/reward vs polarized calling range). Consistent with turn plan: c/c thin or check-behind river for pot control.
5h 2s is a trash hand, especially from UTG position where I need to act first against 4 opponents. No reason to invest chips here.

UTG+1 (early position) with A3o, a weak offsuit ace with poor post-flop playability, low equity against ranges, and dominated by better aces. Standard fold to avoid tough spots multiway or OOP. Preserve stack for premium hands, especially with 70BB effective stack and tournament progression.

I am on the Button with J4s. It is folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds, J4 suited is generally below the standard opening range, even for a wide Button range (typically J6s+ or J5s might be the bottom). The blinds (Minni and Flash) have large stacks (150bb+) and can easily defend or 3-bet, putting my 65bb stack in a marginal spot with a weak hand. Folding is the most prudent long-term play.

Folded to me in the small blind with A♣ J♦ — a strong opening hand. I want to take the initiative, isolate the button and big blind, and avoid playing multiway out of position. A standard open to ~3x the big blind (300 total) gives decent fold equity and discourages loose calls while keeping post‑flop flexibility with our deep stack. Plan: if called I’ll c‑bet favorable flops (A‑high or dry boards) and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards; if I face a large, polarized 3‑bet I’ll generally fold, while I’ll consider calling smaller 3‑bets depending on sizing and opponent tendencies.

I have Kc 9h in the Big Blind. Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. In blind-vs-blind situations, the opener's range is typically wide. K9o is a strong enough hand to defend with, especially since I will have position for the rest of the hand. While 3-betting is an option, calling is a solid play that allows me to realize my equity and use my positional advantage post-flop without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a relatively deep stack.

I raised pre‑flop to 300 and we’re heads‑up vs the BB who just called. The flop (6♣ 2♦ 8♣) is fairly dry and doesn’t hit many strong parts of his calling range — he’ll have lots of unpaired broadways and medium hands. I hold A♣J♦: no pair but two overcards plus the A♣ is a strong blocker to Ax and club combinations. Against a wide defending range this is a textbook spot to take the initiative and c‑bet for fold equity. I’ll bet about half the pot to charge his weak holdings and deny equity to any speculative hands. If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (we only have backdoor club potential and overcards), and I will fold to significant aggression unless the turn improves me (Ace/Jack/club) or the raise is very small.

In this blind-vs-blind situation, Minni's 300-chip continuation bet on a 6c-2d-8c board is a standard play with a wide range of hands. I have Kc 9h, which gives me two overcards and a backdoor club flush draw. While I haven't connected with the board, my hand has decent equity against Minni's air and semi-bluffs. Calling here allows me to see a turn card with positional advantage. If the turn is a King, Nine, or a Club, my hand improves significantly. Additionally, if Minni checks the turn, I can potentially take the pot with a bet, utilizing my position to 'float' the flop.

Turn pairs the board (6c 2d 8c 6h). I hold A♣J♦ — no pair, only the Ace blocker and backdoor club potential. I bet the flop and was called, so the caller’s range includes pocket pairs, sets (88, 22), single‑pair hands like A6/K6 and club draws. The turn massively improves any 6 in his hand (trips/quads) and keeps sets/strong pairs ahead of me. Betting now has poor fold equity against that calling range and will often get value‑called by better hands while bloating a pot I’m usually behind in. Checking controls the pot, preserves my ability to see a river (and possibly make a club/A on the river), and keeps bluff options open on a safe runout. If the opponent bets the turn I’ll give up to significant aggression (per my earlier plan) unless it’s a tiny probe. Therefore: check.

Following Minni's check on the turn, which pairs the six, I have the opportunity to take a free card. My King-high currently has limited showdown value but possesses decent equity with two overcards and a backdoor flush draw (Kc). Betting here is unlikely to fold out better hands like an eight or a pocket pair, and checking back allows me to see the river for free, potentially improving my hand or winning at showdown if Minni is also on a draw or a lower Ace-high.

Check. I opened pre with A♣J♦, c‑bet the dry flop and was called. The turn pairs the board (6h) and I checked to control the pot — the caller’s range that called flop includes lots of pocket pairs (66,88,22), single‑pair hands and some floats. The river 7h doesn’t improve me — I only have Ace‑high and the A♣ is a useful blocker but not enough to credibly represent a value hand against a player who called the flop. Bluffing the river here is thin: many of his holdings that call a flop c‑bet will still call a river bluff (pairs, 8/6, 7s) so betting risks bloating a pot I’m usually behind in.

I’ll check to take the showdown or induce a thin bluff from him. Plan if he fires: call very small probes when pot odds justify it, fold to meaningful value bets (I have no river showdown value and can’t comfortably call large river aggression).

On a board of 6c-2d-8c-6h-7h, Minni has checked for the second street in a row. My King-high (Kc 9h) has very little showdown value, but it does beat some missed club draws (like QJ of clubs). However, it loses to all Ace-high hands and any pair. In this blind-vs-blind battle, Minni's range contains many Ace-high hands that she might check-call with on the river if I bet, given the relatively small pot and the lack of obvious completed draws besides a straight with 95. Turning my hand into a bluff is a possibility, but checking back is the most prudent play to see if my King-high can win against her air, while avoiding further losses if she holds a better hand like a weak pair or a better Ace-high.

Preflop UTG in 5-handed play with A5s, a suited Ace with excellent playability, straight potential (wheel), flush draw potential, and Ace blocker. This hand is well within a standard UTG opening range for short-handed play (equivalent to early/mid position full-ring). Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative; folding wastes equity. Raise to standard 3x BB size (300 total) to build pot, deny equity to worse hands, and take control. Pot currently 150 from blinds; post-raise pot ~450, good for value and initiative. Stacks are deep (70BB effective vs Pro), supporting standard sizing. Opponent tendencies from history show aggression but no specific exploits here warrant deviation.

I am holding Jack-Seven offsuit in UTG+1, facing a raise from the UTG player (Elon). J7o is a statistically weak hand that plays poorly against a UTG opening range, which typically consists of stronger hands like pocket pairs, big aces, and broadways. Calling would be a losing play in the long run, and bluffing (3-betting) with this hand from early position is too risky with three players still to act behind me. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Elon opened UTG in a 5-handed game (300) — that's a relatively strong, unopened range. My hand Q♦2♣ is very weak and frequently dominated by an UTG opening range. There are still the SB and BB to act behind me who can call or reraise, turning this into a multiway or a tough postflop spot. This is not a spot to invest 300 with minimal equity or fold equity; preserve my deep stack for better spots. I will fold.

I am holding 6s 2h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Elon has raised from UTG, typically indicating a strong range. There is no justification for calling or raising with this hand, especially out of position. I will fold and preserve my stack.
94o is a very weak holding to defend against an UTG raise. Even getting 2.25:1 pot odds, this hand plays poorly postflop - no connectivity, no high card value, and I'm out of position. Easy fold.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Qc 2d). This is a very weak hand with no connectivity or suited potential. Given my position, I have four opponents left to act who could easily dominate me. My stack is healthy at around 65 big blinds, so there is no pressure to make loose plays. Folding is the only standard and profitable decision here.

Q♦J♦ in UTG+1 (5-handed) is a strong, playable suited Broadway — excellent flop equity and maneuverability postflop. With deep stacks (~157bb) and a relatively loose/aggressive table, I want to take the initiative, deny cheap flops to the blinds, and build a pot where I have good equity. Standard open-sizing here is ~3x the BB; a raise to 300 is sized to get value from worse hands, price out some speculative hands, and retain fold equity against the Button/SB. I will open to 300 and re-evaluate on any 3-bet (fold to very large 3-bets from tight players, consider calling or 4-betting depending on opponent and sizing).

I am on the button with 6-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from an early position (UTG+1). Even with the advantage of position, 6-4 offsuit does not play well against an early position opening range. Given my healthy stack size and the tournament progress, there is no reason to defend or bluff with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the most prudent action.

K4o is a weak offsuit hand in the small blind facing a raise from Minni, who has been active. I'm out of position and would have the BB behind me. This hand doesn't play well OOP and isn't strong enough to 3-bet as a bluff. Easy fold.

In the big blind facing a standard 3x open from UTG+1 (Minni), with 89o, a speculative offsuit connector.
