Pot is 450, calling an additional 200 (pot odds ~2.25:1, needing ~31% equity). 89o has straight potential and plays well multiway but here heads-up OOP. Deep stacks (70BB effective) provide implied odds if I hit. Minni has opened wide in past hands and defended BB calls frequently. Folding too tight in BB defense strategy to maximize stack long-term; calling to realize equity and play post-flop.

Flop Qh3h5s completely misses my 89o. No pair, no flush draw (backdoor heart and spade at best), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely, open-ender impossible). Pure air against Minni's UTG+1 opening range, which is strong (TT+, AJo+, KQo+ typically). OOP heads-up in deep-stack spot (68BB effective), pot 650. Standard play is check-fold to any reasonable bet; no reason to bluff here as board favors her value range (sets, two-pair, flush draws like AhXh/KhXh, QX) and she can have equity. Realizing pre-flop equity was the plan, but missed hard—now preserve stack for better spots with 58 hands left in tournament.

I have top pair (Qd Jd) on Qh–3h–5s — strong hand vs a single BB caller. Elon called preflop from the big blind, so his range is wide (Qx, medium pairs, hearts, suited connectors). The board has two hearts so there are heart draws to charge; I also want to extract value from worse queens, Jx, and small pairs while keeping the pot manageable out of position. A half‑pot c-bet (~325 into 650) achieves that balance: it prices drawing hands correctly and gets value from worse hands without bloating the pot. Plan: if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (barrel blanks for value/protection, pot-control or check when a heart or scary card comes); if faced with a large check‑raise I’ll respect a polarized range and fold/re-assess based on sizing and reads.

Flop Qh3h5s completely whiffs my 8s9h (offsuit connector from BB defense). No pair, no flush draw (backdoor heart/spade), no straight draw (89 too high for wheel, no open-ender/gutshot). Pure bluff-catcher at best, but OOP heads-up vs UTG+1 Minni's strong opening range (TT+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways, pocket pairs). Her 1/2-pot bet (325 into 650) polarizes to value (Qx, sets, two-pair) or strong draws (AhXh, KhXh, 24x for wheel), where my equity is <25% (pot odds ~3:1 require ~25%). Deep effective stacks (68BB), but no fold equity for a bluff-raise (her range crushes board, callers stationy in past). Pre-flop call realized minimal equity already—standard check-fold preserves stack for 57 hands remaining in tournament, avoiding spew.

Holding T6 offsuit (Td6s) UTG in a 5‑handed game is a very weak, easily dominated hand with poor playability out of position. The pot is only 150 and calling 100 to see a flop will put me OOP against the button and blinds and likely invite raises from aggressive players (Flash/Claude). Raising here would be over‑playing a marginal hand. With a healthy stack (16k) I should preserve chips for better spots later. Fold.

I have been dealt pocket eights in UTG+1. This is a strong starting hand in a 5-handed game. My stack is deep at over 160 big blinds, and since the action has folded to me, I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300 chips). This sizing is consistent with my previous opening ranges and allows me to take control of the pot early or narrow the field.

K6o is a weak hand even on the button. Flash opened from UTG+1, which typically indicates some strength. While I have position, K6o doesn't play well postflop - it's dominated by better kings and has poor playability. The 300 call represents 2.1% of my stack for a marginal spot. Better to fold and wait for a stronger hand.

Preflop from the small blind facing an early position raise to 300 (to call 250 into 450 pot). My hand 7c8h is offsuit, marginally connected at best, with poor playability out of position. Flash's UTG+1 raise indicates a strong range. Pot odds are roughly 1.8:1 effective (calling 250 to win 700), but the hand realizes little equity against a strong range OOP, and implied odds are limited with my 6775 stack (about 67BB) but shorter than others. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 56 hands left; speculative calls OOP from SB vs EP raise are -EV long-term.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 3c, facing a raise to 300 from Flash in UTG+1. The pot is 450, and I need to call 200, offering odds of roughly 3.25:1. However, 53 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em with very poor playability and equity, especially against an early position opening range. Being out of position further decreases my ability to realize equity. Despite the decent pot odds, the reverse implied odds and low hand strength make this a clear fold.

I am UTG in a 5-handed game with 8h 6h. While suited one-gappers have some playability, opening them from the first position is generally too loose, especially with aggressive players like Claude and Minni still to act. Given my healthy stack and the need to maintain a solid image after several recent raises, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.
T3o is a very weak hand, especially in early position (UTG+1). No playability - offsuit, not connected, one low card. With 141 BBs, no reason to play marginal hands. Easy fold.

Preflop on the button in an unopened pot after UTG and MP folds. J2s is a speculative suited hand with excellent playability (gutshot straights, flush draws, wraps) and position advantage. Standard play is to raise for fold equity against blinds or to build a pot heads-up in position postflop. All stacks deep (60+BB), so no ICM pressure. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) is balanced size: large enough for isolation/fold equity, small enough to get called by worse. Limp or fold suboptimal; this maximizes EV.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 2d, facing a 3bb raise from the Button (Elon). My hand is extremely weak (92 offsuit), and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a poor holding. Defending here would be a losing play in the long run. The correct decision is to fold immediately.

Elon opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the big blind with J♦6♠. Stacks are deep (my stack 15,925; Elon 6,475 — effective ~65bb) and this is a standard 3bb BTN open, which is typically wide. J6o is marginal and not a hand I want to 3-bet/bluff (a small 3-bet to 500 risks a call/4-bet and I’m OOP), but it’s worth defending cheaply vs a wide opening range. Calling the extra 200 (total 300) gives reasonable pot odds (~30.8%) and lets me see a flop while keeping the pot controllable. Postflop plan: play cautiously OOP — fold to heavy continuation if I miss, continue only with top pair, strong draw, two pair/better, or good turn improvement.

I defended the BTN’s standard 3bb open in the big blind with J♦6♠ to see a flop cheaply. Flop is K♣ 3♥ 2♣ — I have no pair and no meaningful club backdoor; only 6 outs (three J’s and three 6’s) ≈ ~24% to improve by the river. I’m out of position and Elon’s BTN open is typically wide, so he’ll c-bet a lot; with this weak holding my priority is pot control and realizing equity, not bluffing or building a big pot. Plan: check and fold to sizeable aggression. If Elon bets small (~≤1/3 pot ≈ 200–250) I’ll call to see a turn since my equity is borderline with that price; I’ll fold to medium/large bets (≥~1/2 pot) and won’t check-raise or lead here. If he checks back I can re-evaluate on the turn and continue only with a pair, strong draw, or good improvement.

Flop: 3h 2c Kc. I hold 2s Js, giving me bottom pair (deuces) with Jack kicker on a king-high, draw-heavy board (clubs flush draw, gutshots/open-enders like 45, A4). Preflop, I raised to 300 as button isolation with suited speculative hand J2s, Minni defended BB wide (calls often seen in history: suited connectors, Ax, pairs). She checked flop, standard for her passive tendencies post-call. Pot 650, position advantage. C-bet thin for value vs worse (overcards, weak Kx, draws like clubs/spades suited), protection vs pure draws/improvers, deny equity to floats/chasers. Avoid check-back free cards to Kx/draws that beat us when they hit. Deep stacks (65BB), half-pot size balances range (value: sets/KQ/KJ, bluffs/polarized). Bet 325 total (half pot) to build pot w/ showdown value or extract from worse.

Elon (BTN) opened wide preflop and is c‑betting small: 325 into a 975 pot (~1/3 pot). I’m OOP with J♦6♠ — no pair, no club backdoor, only ~6 outs to hit a pair by the river (~24% equity). After his bet the pot is 1,300; calling 325 would make the pot 1,625, so my immediate pot odds are ~20% (325/1625). Given Elon's wide BTN range and the small sizing, a flat call to realize equity and keep the pot controllable is the correct play. I won’t bluff‑raise OOP with this holding; plan is to call the flop, evaluate the turn, and fold to any sizable turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a very strong draw.

I’m OOP with J♦6♠ on 3♥ 2♣ K♣ 5♥ — no pair, no club backdoor, only ~6 outs (~24% to pair by river). Pot is already large (1,300) after I called Elon’s small flop c-bet to control the pot. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity against a wide BTN range and would bloat a pot I’m behind in; I have no draw to justify building the pot. Best line is to check and see the river for free, folding to any meaningful turn or river aggression unless I pair or improve. Action: check and re-evaluate on the river.

Turn board: 3h 2c Kc 5h. Holding 2s Js for bottom pair (deuces) + J kicker. Preflop button isolation raise, thin value flop c-bet called by BB defender (wide range: Ax, suited connectors, pairs, Kx). Her check-call flop, check turn = passive OOP line consistent with history (stationy check-calls). Pot 1300, position. Board scarier now: hearts draw live, clubs draw, completed some 4x straights (2345), open-enders like 46/A4. Bottom pair still ahead of most of her range (overcards, draws, weak pairs); blocks some broadway. Bet thin value/protection vs improvers (clubs/hearts), deny equity, build pot for river. Vs passive opponent, expect calls from worse (QJ, ATc, flush draws, weak K). Half-pot ~650 sizes well: polarized (value/sets bluffs), extracts from floats. Check risks free cards (6/A/4/hits clubs) where she improves past us often. Deep stacks (60BB+), exploit position/passivity aggressively to max EV long-term tournament.

I’m OOP with J♦6♠ on 3♥ 2♣ K♣ 5♥. Elon barreled flop (small c-bet) and now fired 650 on the turn into a pot of 1950 – calling 650 would make the pot 2600. I have only 6 outs (three J’s, three 6’s) => 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river. The break-even equity to call is 650/(1950+650) ≈ 25%, so a call is -EV purely on equity. Elon’s line (wide BTN open, small flop c-bet, then a larger turn bet) is consistent with many value hands (Kx, 5x, turned two-pair/sets) and is not a strong candidate for laying down to a light raise; bluff-raising OOP is also poor because I have no backdoor and limited fold equity, and a raise risks getting committed by a stronger holding. With poor equity, wrong pot odds, and a clear chance he has value, the correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.
6s9c is a weak off-suit hand in UTG position - the tightest spot at the table. This hand has poor playability and little equity against the ranges that will call or raise me. Easy fold.

Preflop from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game with 3c6c, a weak suited connector. This hand has poor playability out of position, low equity against ranges, and is not worth entering the pot early when there are strong players behind with deep stacks. Standard fold to avoid speculative situations OOP and preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with J7o, a weak off-suit hand. The action folded to me. While the Button is a steal position, my opponents in the blinds (Minni and Flash) are deep-stacked and have shown capability to defend or 3-bet. J7o is below the threshold for a profitable opening range in this configuration, especially against deep stacks who can put pressure on me post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket queens in the small blind are a premium hand and I want to take the initiative heads-up against the big blind. With a deep stack (~153 BB) I’ll raise to a standard 3x the big blind (300) — larger than the min-raise to charge draws and speculative hands but small enough to keep postflop maneuverability. If the BB 3-bets I will re-evaluate (4-bet for value vs a light 3-bet or call to play postflop depending on sizing and tendencies).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation against the Small Blind. Minni has been quite active throughout the session, and her raise from the Small Blind likely represents a wide range of hands. By 3-betting to 900, I can take initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or build value while playing in position for the rest of the hand. ATo is well ahead of a typical Small Blind opening range, making this a profitable spot for a re-raise.

I opened to 300 from the small blind with QQ and Flash (BB) has 3‑bet to 900. QQ is a premium but I'm out of position — calling 600 and playing OOP with deep stacks surrenders initiative and gives the BB good odds to see flops. I want to both get value and deny equity to broadways/small pairs, and Flash has shown the willingness to 3‑bet light at times, so I’ll 4‑bet to a meaningful size to generate fold equity and isolate heads‑up. A raise to 3,000 (total) is large enough to force many marginal 3‑bets/defenses to fold while still getting called by worse AK/AQ/JJ/TT types; if he 5‑bets I will re‑evaluate based on sizing and his image (fold to an obvious value shove, call if I think he can over‑jam bluffs).

I initially 3-bet to 900 with ATo to put pressure on Minni's Small Blind opening range. However, Minni has responded with a 4-bet to 3000. In this tournament, Minni has shown significant aggression, but a 4-bet usually indicates a premium range (JJ+, AK, and occasionally A5s or similar as bluffs). My ATo is dominated by many of these hands and is generally too weak to call a large 4-bet or to 5-bet jam against her remaining stack. Folding preserves my stack of 15,725 and avoids a high-variance spot where I am likely behind.

UTG with 9c3d, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. In a 5-handed game, even with deep stacks, folding is mandatory from early position to avoid playing out of position post-flop with a weak range. No history suggests limp-folding or raising is profitable here. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am in UTG+1 (Cutoff) with Q6 offsuit. With three players yet to act and my stack being the shortest at the table (though still ~64 BB), this hand is too weak to open raise profitably. It is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with A♣K♣ — a top-tier, suited broadway hand. Pot is 150 and it’s folded to me with only the blinds left to act; stacks are deep (~160 BB) so I want to take the initiative and build the pot while keeping fold equity. I’ll open-raise to 3x the BB (300) — large enough to pressure the blinds and isolate, but small enough to play postflop in position if called. Plan: c-bet most favorable flops; call small-to-medium 3-bets to see the flop; re-evaluate (and potentially 4-bet or fold) against a large, polarized 3-bet depending on opponent tendencies.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 4h. The Button (Minni) has opened for 300. 5h 4h is a decent speculative hand that plays well post-flop, especially in a blind-vs-button situation where ranges are wider. However, calling from the SB puts me out of position against a loose-aggressive opponent like Minni. While I could 3-bet as a bluff, Minni has shown a willingness to 4-bet and play back lately. Calling is acceptable to keep the pot smaller and see a flop with a suited connector, though I must be wary of Claude in the Big Blind squeezing. Given the pot odds (250 to call into 450), I have a reasonable price to see a flop and realize my equity.

5-3 offsuit is a very weak hand. While I'm getting ~3.5:1 pot odds (200 to win 700), this hand has poor playability post-flop and will rarely make strong hands. Even in the BB with a discount, folding is correct here - no need to waste 200 chips on such a marginal spot.

On a flop of Jc-7d-9s, my hand (4h 5h) has no direct equity. I have no pair, no draw, and only very thin backdoor possibilities. The board connects well with a Button opening range (hands like T8, QJ, KJ, 98, 76s). As the Small Blind, I am out of position against a loose-aggressive opponent. My best course of action is to check and prepare to fold to almost any bet, as I have virtually no way to continue profitably on this texture.

Preflop I opened A♣K♣ to 300 and SB (Flash) called; pot is 700. Flop is J♣ 7♦ 9♠. I missed a pair but I have two overcards (A,K) and a backdoor club possibility (Ac,Kc with Jc on board). SB’s calling range from the small blind is fairly wide (broadways, suited connectors, some pairs), so I have decent equity and good fold equity. In position I should continue representing my opening range and apply pressure — this is a favorable spot to c-bet. I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (350) to fold out weaker pairs and unpaired hands, charge draws, and keep the pot manageable when called. If called I’ll use position to re-evaluate on the turn (barrel if favorable or pot-control if I miss), and fold to a large check-raise depending on sizing.

I have 5-high with no pair and no straight or flush draws on a board of Jc-7d-9s. This board texture connects very well with Minni's button-opening range, which includes many Jacks, Nines, and straight draws like T8 or 86. Since I have no equity and am out of position against a loose-aggressive player, there is no justification for calling or attempting a bluff. Folding is the only sensible play.

I am in UTG position with 9h 4h. This is a very weak hand (suited two-gapper) that falls well outside the standard opening range for this position in a 5-handed game. My stack is healthy enough (63BB) that I do not need to take desperate risks with marginal holdings. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Early position (UTG+1) in a 5‑hand game with K5 offsuit — a weak, easily dominated hand. Calling 100 to see a flop puts me out of position in a likely multiway pot; raising to the minimum (200) is too small to effectively steal and will get me 3‑bet or called by better hands. I have a deep stack and plenty of hands left in the tournament, so I’ll conserve chips and wait for a stronger, better‑positioned spot.

I am on the button with Ten of Hearts and Deuce of Diamonds (T2o). This is an extremely weak starting hand with very poor playability and equity. While I am in the best position, T2o is not a hand worth attempting to steal the blinds with, especially since Claude (SB) and Elon (BB) have shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet. Given the goal of maximizing the stack over 150 hands, folding here is the most disciplined and standard play to avoid unnecessary chip loss.

Folded to me in SB with Q6o against only the BB. This is a marginal hand but in a heads-up steal situation, Q-high is playable. I should raise to try to take down the pot preflop rather than completing and playing out of position with a weak hand.

Preflop in BB facing isolation raise from SB after folds. Held 3d4s is a trash hand: low, offsuit, poor playability, dominated by any calling range. No strong draws possible. Pot odds ~3:1 but equity too low vs SB wide raise range. 3-bet would require fold equity but hand too weak postflop. Folding preserves stack for tournament survival with 73BB effective.

Fold. Q♦9♦ from UTG is marginal — easily dominated by better Qx and broadway hands and hard to play out of position. Calling 100 just to see a flop will likely leave me OOP multiway against aggressive players (Flash, Claude) and opening (200+) invites 3-bets from frequent raisers. With a deep 167 BB stack there's no need to gamble with a marginal hand in early position; wait for a better spot to attack.

I am in UTG+1 with Eight of Spades and Six of Diamonds. This is a very weak starting hand, especially in a five-handed game where there are still three players to act behind me. Even though the UTG player folded, opening with 86o from this position is not profitable. I should preserve my stack for better opportunities.
On the button with 97o, folded to me. This is a decent spot to steal the blinds with position. While 97o isn't premium, button opens should be wide. Standard 2.5x raise to put pressure on the blinds.

Preflop in SB with K8o facing a button raise to 250 after two folds. K8o is a marginal hand OOP, especially with BB yet to act who could squeeze or call, leading to multiway pots where I'll be out of position. Pot odds are 2:1 (200 to call into 400), but postflop playability is poor with offsuit king-high, vulnerable to domination by better kings or broadways in Claude's wide button range. Claude raises frequently from history, but folding avoids tough spots deepstacked (73BB). Save chips for better opportunities to maximize stack long-term.

I am in the Big Blind with J4o facing a Button raise to 250 from Claude. The pot odds are decent (calling 150 to win 400, needing about 27% equity), and the Button's range is wide. However, J4o is at the very bottom of my range with poor playability and reverse implied odds out of position. Against a competent opponent like Claude who plays post-flop aggressively, defending this hand is likely -EV compared to folding. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit in the UTG position in a 5-handed game. This is a strong hand that merits an opening raise. Looking at my past history in this tournament, I have consistently opened for 300 (3x BB) with strong hands. AJs is well within my opening range here to take control of the pot and potentially narrow the field. If I get 3-bet by the aggressive stacks behind me (Claude or Minni), I will re-evaluate based on sizing, but for now, the standard play is to raise.
6d8c offsuit is a weak hand. Facing a UTG raise of 300 with players behind me, this hand doesn't play well OOP. Not suited, has a gap - easy fold.

9s4d is a complete trash hand with no playability, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing an UTG raise from Flash (early position, typically strong range like pairs, broadways, suited connectors), calling would be a loose speculative play committing 300 into a 750 pot (pot odds ~2.5:1, but post-flop out of position risks and poor equity). Raising is a bluff with no blockers or fold equity justification given stacks and position. With 73BB effective stack and 50 hands left, folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV in late tournament phase.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Three suited (K3d). An Early Position (UTG) player has raised to 300 (3BB). My hand is weak, easily dominated by an UTG opening range (which often contains stronger Kings), and I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. With a stack of 62BB, I have no incentive to get involved in a bloated pot with a marginal hand against a strong range. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

I'm on the BB with Jh7d facing an UTG open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (total commitment will be 300). I only need ~31% equity to break even (200/(450+200)). J7o is a marginal hand but against a typical UTG open in this 5-handed game it has roughly that ballpark equity and the opener (Flash) has shown frequent preflop aggression, so calling to see a flop is justified. I don't want to 3-bet as a bluff out of position into an early-position raiser — that creates a bigger pot with a hand that's hard to play postflop. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to significant C-bets on dry boards, and keep playing aggressively only if I pair J, hit a strong draw, or pick up favorable action. Therefore I will defend the blind and call (total 300).

Flop: A♣ T♠ 4♥; my hand J♥7♦. I have no pair and essentially no realistic backdoor (with only one heart on board I can’t make a 5‑card flush), so this is a dead, dry board for my holding. Flash was the UTG raiser and is likely to continuation‑bet a high frequency; I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand or try a thin bluff out of position. Plan is to control the pot size: check and fold to any sizable c‑bet, only calling very small probes that offer correct pot odds (e.g. ~<=1/3 pot) to see a cheap turn where I can improve to a pair or re-evaluate. If opponents check through I’ll check the turn and look to proceed only if I pair up or pick up a clear draw. Therefore I will check now.

I started the hand by raising pre-flop with Ace-Jack offsuit from UTG and was called only by Minni in the Big Blind. The flop comes Ace-high, giving me top pair with a solid kicker. The board (Ac-Ts-4h) is relatively dry with no immediate flush draws and limited straight possibilities (only gutshots for KQ, QJ, or KJ). Minni has checked to me. I should place a continuation bet here for value against her range, which likely includes middle pairs, Tens, and weaker Aces. A bet of roughly 45% of the pot (300 into 650) is a standard sizing that keeps her marginal hands in while providing protection against overcards or potential draws.

Flop: A♣ T♠ 4♥; I hold J♥7♦. Flash (UTG preflop raiser) bets 300 into a 950 pot; I must call 300 to continue. Calling requires ~24% equity (300 / (950+300) ≈ 24%). My hand has essentially no showdown value and no clean draw — only live outs if I pair a J or 7 (very marginal) — so vs a very strong c-bet range I’d be behind. However Flash has shown frequent preflop and postflop aggression in earlier hands, so his continuation range here can be reasonably wide (Ax, Tx, some broadway/backdoor hands and small pairs). Given the pot odds and his tendency, a call is a cheap way to see a turn where I can realistically improve or get a free card if he checks. Raising is a poor option: I’m out of position, I have poor blockers and no fold‑equity against an early‑position raiser, and I don’t have any value to extract. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to a large turn bet unless I pair or pick up a clear draw, and re-evaluate if the action or board texture changes.

Board is 4♥ T♠ A♣ T♥ and I hold J♥7♦. I have no pair and no real draw — essentially zero showdown value. Pot is 1250 and there is nothing to call, so this is a decision about whether to probe-bluff or control the pot. Flash is an aggressive UTG raiser who can have Ax, Tx, slow‑plays or a wide c‑betting range; his check on the turn can indicate weakness or a trap, but I have no blockers or fold equity to push him off value. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position with a very marginal hand. With the biggest stack I should avoid unnecessary variance and keep the pot small. Plan: check and fold to a meaningful river bet; only consider calling very small probes or bluffing the river if the card and action make a credible story.

The turn brings a second Ten to the board (Ac-Ts-4h-Th). While I still have top pair with an Ace and a Jack kicker, the Ten is a card that likely connects with Minni's big blind defending range. If I bet again and get raised, I'm in a difficult spot against potential trips. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and keep her range wide, including weaker Aces and pocket pairs that might fold to a second barrel but could lead out or call on the river.

Check. My J♥7♦ has no pair and no meaningful showdown value on A♣ T♠ T♥ 4♥ 2♠ — the river 2♠ doesn’t change that. Pot is only 1,250 while stacks are deep, and I have very little fold equity versus an aggressive UTG opener who likely holds an A, T, or pair. Betting here would bloat the pot with a junk hand and invite calls from better hands; the +EV line is to preserve my stack. I’ll check and concede to any meaningful river bet (fold), taking the free showdown if everyone checks through.

On the river, the board reads Ac-Ts-4h-Th-2s. I have top pair with a Jack kicker (Two Pair, Aces and Tens). Minni checked the river for the third time. While my hand is strong, the Ten on the turn is a dangerous card that connects well with a Big Blind defending range. By checking back the turn, I successfully controlled the pot size. On the river, checking behind is the most prudent move to realize my showdown value. If I bet and get raised, I'm forced into a guessing game against potential trips. Since Minni's range contains several Tens (JTs, QTs, KTs, T9s) that she might be check-trapping with, I will take the free showdown and expect my Ace to be the winner most of the time against her middle pairs or weaker Aces.
J3o in UTG is a clear fold. This is way outside any reasonable opening range from early position. Need to maintain discipline and wait for better spots.

9c3d is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor connectivity. From UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game, this hand has zero playability preflop. Standard fold to avoid spew and preserve stack for better spots. Pot is small at 150, but risking 100 (1.4% of stack) for no equity is -EV. Deep stacks don't change this; discipline is key to maximizing long-term stack.

I am on the Button with a weak offsuit hand (7h Td). The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal blinds, T7o is below the threshold for a profitable opening range, especially with two deep-stacked opponents (Minni and Flash) in the blinds who are capable of defending or 3-betting. My stack is 62 BBs, so I am not desperate to make moves with marginal holdings. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

Heads-up from the small blind with A♠4♦ — a clear raising hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to complete; folding is too weak and just calling is passive (gives the big blind good odds to see flops). I want initiative and fold equity versus Flash (who defends fairly widely), so I’ll raise to the min (200 total) to isolate and play the flop as the aggressor. Postflop plan: c-bet a lot on Ace-high or favorable boards, control pot size on connected/brick boards, and re-evaluate vs a 3-bet based on sizing (fold to a large 3-bet, consider calling a small one given stacks).

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Everyone folded to the Small Blind, Minni, who raised to 200 (a min-raise). I only need to commit 100 more chips to see a flop in a pot that will be 400 total. Given the 3:1 pot odds and the fact that I will have position for the rest of the hand, Ten-Seven offsuit is a standard defend against a small blind open.

I'm heads-up with A♠4♦ after raising preflop and getting Flash to call from the big blind. The flop 6♣ 8♦ J♣ is relatively dry and doesn't connect well with a wide calling range, so as the preflop aggressor I should continue to apply pressure and take advantage of my fold equity. I have position, so a c-bet lets me deny equity to many unpaired broadways and charge the occasional combo draw (two clubs or connectors). I choose a half-pot continuation bet (200 into 400) — big enough to get folds and price out marginal hands, but small enough given the deep stacks to keep the pot controllable if called. Plan: if he folds we take the pot; if he calls I'll re-evaluate on the turn (bet for value if I improve to an ace or check/control versus aggression). If he check-raises large, treat it as strong (Jx/pair/sets) and fold unless the action or runout justifies continuing.

On a flop of 6c-8d-Jc, I have a gutshot straight draw needing a nine. Minni's lead of 200 into 400 (half-pot) gives me 3:1 pot odds. While the direct odds to hit the straight on the turn (approx. 8.5%) don't quite justify a call based on immediate value, I am in position and have some backdoor equity with the 7 of clubs. Given the small sizing and my position, I will call once to see if the turn improves my hand or provides a better opportunity to take the pot away.

I c-bet the dry 6♣-8♦-J♣ flop and Flash called. The turn 9♠ does not improve my A♠4♦ — it actually helps a lot of hands in his calling range (pairs like Jx/9x/8x/6x, club draws, and some two-card connectors). Flash has shown a tendency to defend and call flop c-bets, so a turn bluff is unlikely to fold enough to be +EV and risks getting check-raised or called down by better hands. With position and deep stacks I prefer to control the pot size: check behind, see a free river, and re-evaluate. If he bets the turn small I’ll consider a light call as a blocker/catch; if he fires big or check-raises I’ll fold. If the river brings an Ace I can turn it into value; otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously.

The turn 9s has completed my straight (7-8-9-10-J). While QT makes a higher straight, I am holding the second nuts. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might be pot-controlling a Jack or has missed the board. I will bet 500 into the 800 pot to extract value from one-pair or two-pair hands and to charge any club flush draws or gutshots.

I checked the turn to control the pot with A♠4♦ and Flash fired a smallish bet (500 into a 1300 pot). That sizing gives me ~28% pot odds to call (500 to win ~1800), so I only need ~28% equity to justify a call. Flash defended the big blind and called my flop c‑bet, so his range is wide — Jx, single pairs (9x/8x/6x), club draws, some two‑card connectors and occasional air. My Ace blocks a lot of Ax value combos and A‑high will show down reasonably often against his thin/defensive range. Raising here as a bluff feels too risky (I have no backdoor club or straight draw) and folding is too weak given the price and my blockers. I’ll make the light call to keep the pot controllable, retain position for river, and re‑evaluate based on the river card and any bet sizing — fold to large river aggression unless I pair my Ace, fold to very strong lines, or extract value if the river brings an Ace or a very clear weaker holding.

Check. On the Q♦ river my A♠4♦ is just ace‑high. Flash’s line — defending the big blind, calling my flop c‑bet, then betting the turn — is weighted toward value (Jx, 9x/8x/6x pairs, Qx and 10x straights) and will beat A‑high most of the time. I don’t have a plausible value‑bet target on this runout and I have no backdoor draw to turn a bluff into a credible shove/raise. Betting or raising risks getting raised or called by better hands; checking keeps the pot controlled and lets me see a showdown. If he now bets after my check I’ll fold to a sizeable bet (only consider calling a very small bet), but with no bet to face I check to see the showdown.

The river Queen completes an 8-9-10-J-Q straight for any hand containing a Ten. Since I hold the Ten of Spades, I have the second-best possible hand (only beaten by King-Ten, which makes a 9-K straight). Minni's check-call on the turn and check on the river suggest she is likely holding a marginal hand like top pair, two pair, or a set, rather than the nuts. I will bet 1200 into the 1800 pot to extract value from those hands. If she also holds a Ten, we will likely split the pot, but I need to charge her non-straight hands.

I hold A♠4♦ on 6♣ 8♦ J♣ 9♠ Q♦. I was the preflop aggressor, c‑bet the flop and Flash called; he then bet the turn (500) and I called. His line is consistent with medium‑strength value (Jx, 9x, 8x, Qx) but also contains missed club draws and wide, defensive calling hands since he defends the BB frequently. He sizes small on the river (1200 into the ~3000 pot). Calling 1200 to win ~4200 requires ~28.6% equity — an affordable price given his range and my blocker to some Ax combos. Raising as a bluff is unattractive (I have no credible club/straight blocker to fold out enough of his value hands) and folding is too tight given the pot odds and his wide tendencies. I’ll make the light call to reach showdown and avoid bloating the pot with a bluff.

UTG in 5-handed play with A3o (offsuit). This is a marginal ace but dominated by better aces and not suited or connected for multiway potential. Deep stack (73BB), no need to open weak hands from early position where we'll face position disadvantage postflop. Opponents have deep stacks and shown aggression in past hands; folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV in tournament.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with Ten-Six offsuit, a very weak hand. With three players remaining to act behind me, including two large stacks in the blinds, the likelihood of stealing the blinds is low, and the hand has poor playability post-flop. Standard strategy dictates a fold here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with KQs (suited broadway) — a premium hand in position facing only the blinds. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call; with 14k (≈140BB) and two deep, somewhat aggressive blinds, standard strategy is to open-raise to seize initiative, build the pot, and isolate. I don't want to min-raise (200) because that's too small and invites loose defense; a 3x open to 300 balances value and fold equity while keeping postflop playability in position. If I get a 3-bet I'll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent; if called I'll C-bet favorable flops and leverage position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Button (Minni) has opened with a standard raise to 300. While Ace-Four is not a premium hand, I am getting good pot odds to defend my blind against a Button opening range which is likely wide. A 3-bet is an option to seize the initiative, but given my chip lead and the fact that I'll be out of position, I'll choose to call and see a flop, keeping the pot manageable and utilizing my stack advantage to navigate post-flop.

I have T6 suited in the BB with 200 to call into a 700 pot (3.5:1 odds). Minni raised and Flash called. T6s is marginal but the suited aspect gives flush potential and I'm getting good odds to see a flop multiway. Easy call here.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a King-high board. In a three-way pot where I am out of position, checking is the most prudent play. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) with a marginal hand like bottom pair rarely accomplishes much, as I'll likely only be called by better hands. I will check to evaluate the action from the Button and Big Blind, aiming to keep the pot small and reach showdown cheaply.
I have T6 of hearts on a K-8-4 board with two spades. No pair, no draw (backdoor hearts only). Flash checked, so I can check behind and see a free turn. No reason to bet with this weak holding.

I have K♠Q♠ on 4♠ K♥ 8♠ — top pair with the best kicker plus the nut-flush draw. I was the preflop raiser and both opponents checked, so this is an ideal spot to c-bet for value and to charge spade draws/overcards. A bet around 2/3 pot builds the pot when I'm likely ahead, protects my hand by denying correct odds to draws, and gets value from Kx, 8x, and drawing hands. If called I'll continue using position; if I face a large raise I'll re-evaluate (top pair + nut draw can call small-to-medium raises but I can fold to extreme overbets).

In this three-way pot, Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) has fired a continuation bet of 600 into 900 on a King-high board. While I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker, calling a 2/3 pot bet out of position against two opponents is generally a losing play. Minni is betting into two players, which typically indicates a range of King-x, strong draws, or pocket pairs that beat my fours. Furthermore, Claude is still to act behind me, and if I call, I may be forced to face further action on the turn while out of position. Given the board texture and the multi-way action, my hand is too weak to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I have T6 of hearts on a K84 board with two spades. I have no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), and only very weak backdoor straight potential. Minni bet 600 into a 1500 pot after we both checked. I'm getting 2.5:1 but with essentially no equity - just high card ten. This is a clear fold.

I am in UTG (effectively Hijack in 5-handed play) with King-Jack offsuit. With 62 BBs, this hand is strong enough to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. It is within the standard opening range for this position. I will raise to 2.5BB (250) which is a standard sizing to put pressure on opponents while keeping the pot manageable if 3-bet.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from Pro while I’m UTG+1, J♣8♥ is a marginal offsuit hand with poor post‑flop playability and frequently dominated by a UTG raising range. Calling 250 into a ~400 pot out of position gives weak immediate pot odds and little reason to invest chips against a likely strong opener; a small raise would just build a marginal pot OOP and can easily be 3‑bet by deeper stacks. With a healthy 14,600 stack I should preserve chips for better spots and wider position — fold and wait for a stronger or better‑positioned opportunity.

I am on the button with 2h 6h, a very weak hand. Pro has opened with a raise from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. Although the cost to call is relatively low and I would have position post-flop, this hand has very poor playability and is likely dominated. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have 9h8s (offsuit) in the small blind facing a UTG raise to 250. This is a marginal hand - 98o plays poorly out of position against an UTG opening range which is typically tight. While the pot odds are decent (200 to call into 400), being OOP for the rest of the hand with a speculative hand that needs to hit to win makes this an easy fold. UTG raises typically indicate strength, and I don't want to play a mediocre hand OOP.

Pre-flop in the big blind facing an UTG raise to 250 from Pro (5975 stack). My hand K6o is offsuit with a weak kicker, dominated by better kings in Pro's UTG range (likely 88+, ATs+, AJo+, KQs+, KQo). Equity against a tight UTG range is around 25-30%, but post-flop playability is poor out of position with reverse implied odds. Pot odds are 400:150 (~27% needed), but folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 103/150). Pro's history shows disciplined play; no need to defend marginally here. Fold to avoid tough OOP situations.

3♦6♥ is a weak, unplayable hand from UTG. Out of position against four opponents, it has poor equity, poor playability and few reasonably profitable post‑flop lines. Facing a 100 call (min raise to 200) into a multiway field, there’s no reason to invest chips with this hand when my stack (14,600) and future opportunities are plentiful. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG+1 with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Since Minni (UTG) has folded and there is no reason to open with such a low-equity hand from early/middle position, I will fold to preserve my stack.

On the button with 64 suited after two folds. This is a marginal hand but I have position and only the blinds to act. A standard steal raise here is profitable - I'll either win the blinds uncontested or play a raised pot in position with a hand that has some playability (suited connector can make straights and flushes).

5c6d is a low suited connector with some playability in multiway pots or in position, but preflop from the small blind facing an isolation raise from the button (Claude, who frequently raises and shows aggression), out of position with the big blind yet to act. The pot odds are roughly 3:1 (calling 200 into 400), but postflop playability is poor OOP against a wide button range. Effective stacks are deep, but folding avoids difficult spots and preserves stack for better opportunities late in the tournament (hand 104/150). Standard fold to avoid spew.

I am in the Big Blind with T3s facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 150 to win 400, needing ~27% equity), T3s is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. The large gap between cards makes straight possibilities remote, and playing a weak top pair or bottom pair out of position is difficult. Given I am the shortest stack (though still deep at ~63BB), avoiding marginally EV- or neutral spots out of position against an aggressive player is prudent. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with a very weak hand, 9-4 offsuit. In a 5-handed game, this hand is far below the opening range for this position. Raising or even calling would be a waste of chips, especially with strong players behind me. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

As6s is a suited Ace - playable from UTG+1, especially with Flash already folding. I'll open with a standard raise to 250. This hand has potential for nut flush draws and can hit decent top pair hands. With a healthy stack of 141 BBs, I can afford to play this hand for a small raise.

KsJh is a premium offsuit broadway hand on the button facing a cutoff raise from Claude. Position is excellent, and Claude's CO opening range is wide enough (Ax suited, broadways, suited connectors) that KJo blocks strong kings, dominates many of his calling hands like KQo, AJo, QJo, and has good playability multiway or heads-up. 3-betting here applies pressure, can fold out equity like Ax, suited broadways, or induce folds from blinds. Pot is 400, calling 250 is loose OOP potential vs 2 callers, better to 3-bet for value/protection/isolation. Sizing to 750 is ~3x the raise accounting for dead money, standard and balanced. Folds post-flop likely high if missed, stacks allow (71BB effective). Late tournament, maximizing EV by 3-betting wide from BTN.

I hold 2c 5d in the Small Blind, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing an open from UTG+1 and a 3-bet from the Button, my hand has virtually zero equity and playability. The pot odds do not justify a call, and bluffing against two opponents showing strength is ill-advised. Folding is the only correct play.

Claude opened UTG+1 to 250 and Elon 3‑bet the button to 750. I’m in the BB with K♦4♦. Calling 650 into a 1,150 pot (making it 1,800) requires ~36% equity to break even. K4o is frequently dominated by UTG/3‑bet ranges and plays very poorly out of position against two opponents. A small 4‑bet to 1,250 is not a reasonable bluff here with two live opponents and deep stacks, and calling commits me to a large, marginal spot postflop. Preserve chips and fold to the 3‑bet.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon on the button with As6s. While I have a suited ace with nut flush potential, A6s is at the bottom of hands I'd want to continue with against a 3-bet, especially out of position. Elon has shown aggression throughout the tournament. The 500 to call into a pot of 1150 gives decent odds, but playing OOP with a weak kicker makes this marginal at best. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

Js9s in UTG - a suited one-gapper with decent playability. While UTG is early position, this hand has good implied odds with flush and straight potential. At 138BB deep, I can open this hand with a standard raise. Will raise to 250 (2.5x).

78o is a speculative hand with poor playability out of position. Facing a raise from UTG (Claude, who raises frequently but has positional advantage), the correct play is to fold preflop to preserve stack and avoid difficult postflop spots OOP with a weak hand. Deep stacks don't justify a loose call here, as pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 400 gives ~3:1 but equity against UTG range is low, ~30-35%). Prioritize tight play early in hand to maximize EV long-term.

Current situation: 5 players remaining. Claude raises to 250 (2.5BB) from UTG (effectively Hijack/Cutoff in 5-handed). I am on the Button with Qh Jd. While playing 5-handed widens ranges, QJo is still a marginal hand facing an early position open, as it is frequently dominated by hands like AQ, AJ, KQ, and KJ. Even though I have position, calling invites the blinds to squeeze and leaves me navigating a pot with a hand that has significant reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve stack for better spots.

UTG opened to 250 and I’m in the small blind with A♣2♣. A2s is worth playing — it has blocker value to Ax hands and good post‑flop playability — but I’m out of position versus an UTG opening range that’s likely strong. A tiny 3‑bet to 400 has poor fold equity and a larger 3‑bet is too committal with a marginal hand. With deep stacks I prefer to see a flop and realize equity: I’ll call the 200 more (250 total) and proceed postflop only with an ace, a decent draw, or a favorable texture; I’ll fold to heavy 3‑bet aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with K4 offsuit. Claude raised from UTG and Minni called from the Small Blind. I need to call 150 chips into a 600-chip pot, giving me 4-to-1 odds. While K4o is a weak hand, the pot odds and my position in the Big Blind make this a standard defensive call to see a flop. I have a deep stack and don't need to overcomplicate the pre-flop action with a raise.

I have top pair (A♣2♣) but a very weak kicker and I’m out of position versus the UTG opener (Claude). The flop Q♠5♦A♦ is two‑toned and there are many hands in Claude’s opening range that already beat me (AQ, two pair, sets) or have strong equity (Qx, diamond draws). Betting OOP here would bloat the pot and commit me to difficult decisions against pressure; my hand is good for showdown value but vulnerable. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my equity, and see how the opponents behave. Plan: call a small, single bet (about ≤1/2 pot) to see a turn, but fold to large aggression or a check‑raise that represents two pair/sets; if it checks through I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and value‑bet or protect as appropriate.

I have King-high on a board featuring an Ace and a Queen, which both likely hit the ranges of the pre-flop raiser (Claude) and the small blind (Minni). I have no pair and no real draw. Since Minni checked and I am out of position relative to the aggressor, I should check and prepare to fold to any significant action. There is no merit in bluffing into two opponents on this board.

I have Js9s on Qs-5d-Ad. I missed completely - no pair, only a backdoor spade draw. However, as the preflop raiser, this A-high board is excellent for my perceived range. Both opponents checked to me, showing weakness. A small c-bet (around 1/3 pot) should take this down frequently as I can represent Ax or AQ easily.

Claude (UTG) is a strong opener and this flop (Qs–5d–Ad) contains lots of hands that already beat or have strong equity vs my A♣2♣ (AQ, A5, sets, Qx, diamond draws). I have top pair but a very weak kicker and no diamond — vulnerable OOP. Claude’s bet is very small (250 into a 1000 pot ≈ 1/4 pot), so raising would bloat the pot and commit me to difficult decisions against his continuing range; folding a single small c-bet would be too nitty given my showdown value. Calling preserves pot control, realizes equity, and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his range. Plan: call the 250, see the turn, and fold to strong turn aggression (or if the turn brings a diamond/queen that completes obvious draws or two‑pair lines); if the turn is a blank and checked to me I can consider a small value bet or checking for pot control.

I have King-high on a board of A-Q-5. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 250 into two opponents, and Minni has already called that bet. This board heavily favors the ranges of both opponents, especially Claude's UTG raising range. I have no pair and no draws. Continuing in this hand would be a significant mistake as I have virtually zero equity and am facing strength from two players. I will fold.

Turn gives me two pair (A2: A♣2♣ on Q♠–5♦–A♦–2♥). Claude was the UTG opener and c‑bet a small 250 on the flop, which can be a wide/medium range (many worse A‑x, Qx, medium pairs) but also contains the few hands that beat me (AQ, A5, or sets). The pot is ~1250 and it’s checked to me, so this is a spot to extract value and deny a free river card. I’ll make a modest value bet (~1/2 pot) to charge worse A‑hands and medium pairs and to price out free cards that could improve Claude’s range. If Claude raises large I’ll give credit to a better two‑pair/set and fold; if he makes a small/medium raise I’ll call and reevaluate on the river. Bet 600.

I have Js9s on Qs-5d-Ad-2h. Complete air - jack high with no draws (the backdoor spade draw never materialized). Minni check-called my c-bet on the flop and now donk leads 600 on the turn. This betting pattern typically indicates strength - they're representing Ax, Qx, or possibly a diamond draw. With zero equity (no pair, no draws), calling would be a mistake. Easy fold.

UTG with KJo in a 5-handed game at 75BB effective depth. KJo is within the standard opening range from early position short-handed: strong high-card strength, straight potential, dominates weaker kings/jacks, and plays well post-flop for top pair/top kicker. Raising takes initiative, builds the pot, and applies pressure on the shorter-stacked Pro behind (62BB) and others who call light based on history. Limping is weak; folding too tight late in tournament with 44 hands left. Standard raise sizing to 3x BB for value and fold equity.

I am in UTG+1 (MP) facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the UTG player (Elon). There are 3 players left to act behind me, including two deep stacks who have shown aggression. While Pocket Sixes (66) have set-mining potential, the 3x sizing worsens my direct pot odds and implied odds. Additionally, cold-calling here leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the Button or Blinds, which would force me to fold and forfeit the 300 chips. Without closing the action or having a better position/price, folding is the standard, low-variance decision to preserve my 62bb stack for better opportunities.

I’m on the button with Tc2s facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450). Calling 300 into 450 requires ~40% equity (300 / (450+300)). T2o off-suit is heavily dominated by a typical UTG opening range in a 5-handed game and has very poor post‑flop playability — not enough equity to justify calling. 3-betting as a bluff is also unattractive: the hand has little fold equity vs an UTG range and is a terrible hand to build a bigger pot with, especially with other healthy stacks at the table. My stack (15,250) is comfortable; no need to gamble marginally. Fold and wait for a better spot in position with a hand that can realize equity or apply pressure.

Elon raised from under-the-gun (UTG), which typically indicates a strong range including high pairs and strong broadway cards. I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit, a hand that is easily dominated by UTG's opening range (like AJ, KJ, AT, KT). Playing out of position for the remainder of the hand makes this a difficult spot to realize equity. As the current chip leader, I will opt for a disciplined fold to avoid unnecessary chip loss in a marginal, out-of-position situation.

I have 7s5s in the BB facing a 300 raise from Elon (UTG). I need 200 to call into a pot of 450, giving me about 2.25:1 pot odds. Suited connectors have good implied odds when they hit - can make straights and flushes. Elon's UTG range is likely fairly tight, but I'm getting reasonable odds and will have position postflop. This is a marginally profitable call with the potential to stack him if I hit well.

I flopped middle pair (5s) on Ts 5d 4d board. I'm out of position against Elon's UTG raise. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to lead out. Standard play is to check and let Elon c-bet if he wants, then I can call and evaluate the turn. Two diamonds on board means flush draws are possible for villain.

Heads-up pot against Claude in BB after my UTG raise with KJo. Flop Ts5d4d is draw-heavy (open-ended straight draws like 62o, 36s, flush draws with diamonds). I have king-high overcard with backdoor diamond flush draw. Missed the flop but preflop raiser range (high pairs, AK, KQ, broadways) is perceived to connect decently here (e.g., TT+, sets, KTs, diamond Ax). Claude's check after calling pre suggests no top pair or better likely, more speculative hands/draws that missed. C-bet to deny equity to draws (flush/straight), get value from weaker Ax/Jx/high cards that call light, and maintain aggression in tournament with 44 hands left to build stack. Sizing ~60% pot (400 into 650) balances value/protection/fold equity without overbetting OOP. Checking risks giving free cards to draws and realizes no equity.

I flopped middle pair (5s) with a weak kicker on Ts 5d 4d. Elon bet 400 into ~650 pot. I'm getting about 2.6:1 pot odds (need ~28% equity). Against Elon's UTG range, my pair is marginal - I'm behind overpairs and top pair, but ahead of overcards and roughly flipping vs flush draws. I have a backdoor spade flush draw. Calling one street to see a turn card is reasonable given pot odds, but I'll need to be prepared to fold to aggression on later streets unless I improve.

I turned two pair (7s and 5s) on the 7c turn. This is a significant improvement from my flop middle pair. The board still has a diamond flush draw (5d 4d) that I need to charge. Leading out for value and protection makes sense - I want to get value from Elon's top pairs, overpairs, and charge his draws. A bet around 60% pot (~850-900) accomplishes this goal.

Facing a turn lead from Claude after he check-called flop on a draw-heavy board (Ts5d4d). My KJo is pure king-high with no pair, draw, or showdown value. Preflop as UTG raiser, my range is strong (KK+, AKo, broadways), but this board hits draws hard (diamond flush draws, 62/63/23 straight draws). Claude's BB call preflop, check-call flop (misses many strong hands like overpairs), and now turn donk-lead (850 into 2300) into me suggests: improved hands (7x like A7s, 87, 76; sets; two-pair combos like 75, T7); completed draws semi-bluffing; or trapped value. Turn 7c bricks my backdoors but completes gutshots (e.g., 86) and adds 7-pair possibilities. Pot odds ~3.7:1 require ~21% equity, but king-high has <20% vs his polarized range (folds worse like missed draws, beats bluffs but loses to value). Late tournament (hand 107/150, 5-handed), preserve 68BB stack vs aggressive Claude (11.8k stack); folding avoids spew, live for better spots to build stack. Raising bluff too thin (he calls light historically); call bloat pot OOP with no equity. Standard fold to protect range.

I am in UTG position with 8h 6c, which is a very weak unsuited one-gapper. From early position, my opening range should be tight (pairs, broadways, strong suited connectors). This hand falls well outside of a profitable opening range, especially with 5 players left to act. With 62 big blinds, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better spots. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

3♦2♥ is a very weak, unplayable hand from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. It has almost no showdown value, poor straight/flush potential and will perform terribly out of position against the active raisers here (Flash and Claude have been opening/3-betting frequently). There’s no raise yet but calling 100 or opening to 200 is negative EV — it invites isolation or a squeeze. With a healthy 15250 stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a much stronger spot. Fold.

I am on the button with 9d 7h. While the button is the best position to attempt a steal, 9-7 offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Both Claude (SB) and Elon (BB) have shown significant activity in previous hands, suggesting they are unlikely to fold their blinds to a standard raise without a fight. Given the marginal nature of my hand and the risk of being outplayed post-flop or re-raised pre-flop, I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have pocket 9s in the small blind with everyone folding to me. This is a premium hand heads-up against the big blind. I should raise to either take down the blinds or play heads-up with a strong pocket pair. Standard raise to 300.

AdJd is a premium hand from the BB facing a SB open-raise after multiple folds. SB ranges are wide in this spot (any ace, broadway, suited connectors, pairs). This hand has excellent equity, dominates much of their calling range, plays well postflop with flush and straight potential, and we want to 3-bet for value, build the pot, and isolate/deny equity to worse hands. Standard 3-bet sizing around 3x the open (900 total). Pot will be 400 + 900 = 1300, putting Claude to a 600 into 1300 decision (~3.4:1). Deep stacks allow for postflop flexibility. Folding or flat-calling is too weak.

Elon 3-bet me to 900 after my open with pocket 9s. He has about 60bb effective stack. 99 is a solid hand but I'm out of position. 4-betting risks getting it in against QQ+/AK where I'm crushed. Folding is too tight. Calling to see a flop and play postflop is the standard play - I can hit a set or play carefully on low boards. If the flop comes favorable I can continue, otherwise I can control the pot.

I flopped top set (999) on a dry 5-4-9 board. This is an excellent spot. Elon 3-bet me preflop and likely has overpairs (TT-AA) or AK. With his stack at ~6000 remaining, I want to build the pot. A bet around 50-60% pot will get value from overpairs while keeping him in. If I check, he might check back with AK and I lose value.

Preflop we 3-bet AdJd as a premium hand with great playability and equity vs SB calling range. Flop 5d4h9s smashes our range: nut diamond flush draw (12 outs), live A/J overcards for pair/top pair, backdoor straight outs. Effective nuts or close. Claude's SB donk-lead into our 3-bet pot suggests polarized range: strong made hands (sets, two-pair, 97s, 65s) or draws (spade flush draw 9s, wheel draws, combo draws). However, his lead size (900 into 2700 ~1/3 pot) is smallish and doesn't scream monster. Raising extracts value from worse made hands/draws, provides fold equity vs pure draws/bluffs, denies equity, builds pot when ahead, and maintains initiative with position OOP. Flat is too passive (gives free turn cards to draws, hard to get value later); call allows check-raise. Fold impossible. Sizing: ~2.5x his bet (2400 total) balances value/protection, leaves room for further action (stack 5950 > pot post-raise ~5100), puts Claude to tough decision (2400-900=1500 more into ~5100, ~3.4:1 but vs our strong range). Tournament stage late (108/150), ICM-neutral, deep effective stacks favor aggression.

I flopped top set (999) on a dry 5-4-9 board and Elon is raising into me. He 3-bet preflop and is now putting more money in - he almost certainly has an overpair (TT-AA) or is bluffing with AK. With only 3550 behind after his raise, he's essentially pot-committed. I have the nuts here and should raise all-in to maximize value. He's not folding an overpair at this stack depth.

AdJd on 5d4h9s flop: only two live overcards (A/J for top/second pair, 6 outs ~12%) + backdoor diamond FD (~4% runner-runner), no straight draw. Missed completely despite suited broadway preflop. Preflop 3bet good, flop raise overly aggressive (bluff/value mix, but here pure air). Claude's line (SB wide open-call 3bet, small donk flop ~1/3 pot into 3bet pot, then massive 4bet jam to 5950 when pot ~5100, leaving me all-in for 3550): strongly polarized to nuts/near-nuts (sets 99/55/44, two-pair 95/54, trips unlikely) or premium semi-bluffs (spade FD like ATs/KTs/9xs spade with equity, 76/87 gutshot+draws), but jam size commits and denies further play—looks like value. Equity vs value range ~15-20% (6 outs), vs draws ~40%, blended <25%. Pot odds ~3.8:1 require 21% equity, marginal at best, but likely shortfall. Late tourney (108/150, 5-handed), my 35BB stack playable, others deeper (Claude eff 20BB post, but preserves fold equity elsewhere), ICM favors tight, avoid cooler/speculative shove. Fold preserves stack for better spots; hero call risky with trash equity.

Fold. 3♦4♠ is one of the weakest hands and from UTG in a 5‑hand game it’s out of position against the entire table. There’s no price advantage here (calling 100 just to limp) and the table has frequent open-raises (Flash/Claude) and a mid/short stack (Elon) who can create tough spots post‑flop. Raising as a bluff is unnecessary with a deep stack and marginal hand. Preserve chips and wait for a playable spot in position.

I am in the UTG+1 position (effectively the Cutoff) with 9c 5c. While suited, this hand is too weak to open 5-handed, especially with aggressive players like Claude on the button and short stacks behind me who may shove. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack leader position.
JTo on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a clear raise spot - I have position and a playable hand. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

J6h is a weak suited hand, not strong enough to defend out of position from the small blind against a button raise from Claude, who has been aggressive throughout the tournament. My stack is 35BB, so I need to preserve chips for better spots. Pot odds to call 200 into 400 aren't compelling considering postflop playability OOP and potential action from BB. Folding is standard and disciplined.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit facing a standard 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). Although I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550, requiring roughly 27% equity realization), T7o performs poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive chip leader who can apply significant post-flop pressure. T7o is typically just outside the profitable defense range for 2.5x opens, as it suffers from domination and poor playability. Preserving my stack of roughly 60BB is preferable to playing a marginal hand from a disadvantageous position.

I am UTG with 9-8 offsuit. In a five-handed game, this hand is too weak to open from the first position, especially with aggressive players like Claude and Minni still to act behind me. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
K7o in UTG+1 is a weak hand that should be folded. It's easily dominated by stronger kings and has poor playability. No reason to open this from early position.

84o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low card strength. Preflop on the button with folds to me, the standard play is to open raise a wide range for steals, but 84o is below the playable threshold even here due to its lack of equity against calling ranges and bad postflop playability, especially multiway or vs 3-bets. With 35BB stack and 40 hands left, better to preserve chips for premium spots rather than risk a tough spot. SB and BB have large stacks and may defend wide. Folding is optimal.

I am in the Small Blind with J6o. The action has folded to me. The Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader with over 150 BBs, while I have about 60 BBs. J6o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, even in a Blind vs Blind scenario. Against a deep-stacked opponent who has shown aggression and willingness to defend, raising or limping with this hand risks entering a difficult post-flop spot or facing a pre-flop 3-bet. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Q7o in UTG is a clear fold. This hand is too weak to open from early position - it's easily dominated by better queens and lacks playability. With 17,550 chips I'm in good shape and don't need to take marginal spots.

Preflop from UTG+1 with 5 active players, T3o is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity, and no domination potential. Standard tight range dictates a fold to preserve stack (35BB effective). No position advantage, and opening weak hands here invites trouble from later positions and blinds. With tournament progressing (hand 111/150) and goal to maximize stack, avoid marginal spots.

I am on the Button with 9d 8s. It is folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal blinds, 98 off-suit is at the very bottom of or outside a standard opening range, especially against two opponents in the blinds (Minni and Flash) who have significantly larger stacks (150bb+) and can apply pressure with 3-bets or sticky calls. Playing a marginal hand off-suit out of position post-flop (if called by BB) or facing a 3-bet is not ideal for my 60bb stack. A fold is the disciplined, low-variance play here.

We're in the small blind with A♦K♥ and only the big blind (Flash) left to act. This is a premium hand worth opening for value and fold equity even though we'll be out of position. I want a standard sized open to isolate the BB and deny him cheap odds to see a flop — raise to 3x the big blind (total 300). That sizing gets value from worse aces/broadways, folds out some junk, and keeps the pot controllable OOP. Plan: if called, continuation-bet appropriate flops (big on A-high or dry boards, check/plan turn on coordinated boards); if 3-bet, re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies (often call vs small 3-bet or 4-bet shove vs very large/isolating 3-bets).

I am in the Big Blind holding 9h 7d. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2-to-1 odds to see a flop. Additionally, I will have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. Given the favorable pot odds and my positional advantage, 97o is a standard defending hand against a likely wide Small Blind opening range.

Heads-up on a dry Q♠ 6♠ 2♣ flop after I opened from the SB with A♦K♥ and got called. I have two overcards and the betting initiative, so a continuation bet is standard — it gets value from worse broadways/Kx/Ax that will call, folds out many small pairs and complete air, and denies a free turn card that could pair the board. The flop is relatively uncoordinated (only two spades, no obvious straight), so I don't need to fear big multi-street action; I prefer a half-pot c-bet to balance fold equity and keeping the pot manageable OOP. Bet 300 (≈1/2 pot). Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn — continue against a blank or barrel selectively, but fold to a large check-raise which will usually represent a made hand.

On a flop of Qs 6s 2c, I am holding 9h 7d, which provides no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Minni, who raised from the Small Blind pre-flop, has led out with a half-pot continuation bet of 300. Given that I have zero meaningful equity and my 9-high is unlikely to win a showdown or improve sufficiently to continue, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision.

45s suited connectors are speculative and best played from late position where we can realize equity in multiway pots or with fold equity. UTG in 5-handed play is too early; opening risks isolation against deep-stacked opponents who can reraise light. Limping is passive and invites trouble. With 35BB stack, better to preserve chips for premium hands or better positions. Folding maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

I am in UTG+1 (Hijack) in a 5-handed game with 75 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand with poor playability and low equity. Given the deep stacks of the players behind me and the aggressive nature of the game, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips for better spots.

On the button with Q♠8♣ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Only the small and big blinds to act, so this is a good spot to attempt a steal and play post‑flop last if called. I’ll use a standard 3x BB open (300) to apply pressure and avoid the passivity of limping; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold this holding. Raising to 300 is consistent with my table image and gives the best chance to pick up the blinds or play a heads‑up pot in position.

Holding Ten-Five offsuit in the small blind against a button open from Minni is a clear folding situation. This hand has very poor equity and playability, especially when out of position against an aggressive player. Given my healthy stack and the tournament stage, there is no reason to defend such a weak range.

I have 9d2s in the BB facing a button raise to 300. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Even with 2.25:1 pot odds, 92o doesn't have enough equity or postflop playability against a button opening range to defend. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Eight offsuit (Th 8d). This is a statistically weak hand with poor playability, especially from early position where I have to act first post-flop against four potential opponents. My stack is healthy (60 BB), so there is no desperation to make a move. The standard and correct play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

7♠3♦ (7-3 offsuit) is one of the weakest starting hands. From UTG+1 with four players left to act, neither calling 100 nor opening to 200 is +EV — I'm out of position and dominated by almost every opener/3‑bet range. Deep stacks behind me and a short BB who could shove create messy spots where this hand plays terribly. Preserve a healthy 15,750 stack for better spots later and fold.

I am on the button with 9h 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. While it's generally good to be aggressive on the button, 94o is too weak to open, especially with Elon in the Big Blind holding a relatively short stack of 34 big blinds, making him more likely to defend or shove over a steal attempt. Folding is the most prudent play.
AQo is a premium hand. Folded to me in the SB against a short-stacked BB (Elon with 34BB). I should raise for value here. Standard raise to 300.

Facing a raise from Claude in the small blind to 300 total (200 to call into 400 pot), with J5o offsuit in the big blind. This is a poor speculative hand with low playability, dominated equity against Claude's likely wide stealing range which includes many strong hands. My stack is 34BB, but folding marginal hands here preserves stack for better spots late in the tournament. No fold equity if raising, and postflop position is bad. Pot odds require ~25% equity, which J5o doesn't provide. Standard fold.

5-max dynamics: QcJd is a reasonable open from UTG in a 5-handed game — playable, takes initiative, and has good postflop playability deep (≈157 BB). Table tendencies show standard open sizes around 3x BB, and I want to deny cheap flops to the blinds and define ranges. Raising the minimum (200) is too small; a 3x open to 300 is a standard, constructive size here.

Plan: Raise to 300 to seize initiative. If called, proceed cautiously OOP — c-bet appropriate flops and fold to heavy resistance when out of equity. If I face a large polarized 3-bet (a big 3-bet from Claude/Flash, e.g. ~3x my raise or more), I will fold QJo OOP. Versus a short‑stack shove from the SB (Elon, ~33 BB) I’ll decide based on shove sizing/read, but generally treat it as a fold/call spot depending on how wide I put his shove. Overall the +EV play is to open-raise to 300.

I am holding K3o in UTG+1. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG. K3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly against an UTG opening range, even in a five-handed game. I am currently the chip leader or near it, and there is no reason to defend or 3-bet with such a marginal hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q7 suited on the button facing Minni's UTG raise. Decent speculative hand with flush potential and position. Minni has been very active but also capable of 4-betting (seen in hands 13, 41). Calling to see a flop in position is the safest play with this marginal suited hand - 3-betting risks facing a 4-bet from an active opponent.

A9o is a marginal hand from the small blind facing an UTG raise from Minni (who often shows strength with UTG opens based on history) and a button call from Claude. Calling OOP multiway (Pro BB still to act) commits us poorly with poor playability and kicker issues. Pot odds are about 3:1 (25% equity needed), but our equity vs. strong ranges is insufficient, and position is terrible for postflop. Raising risks too much stack (3350 effective) with a non-premium hand. Best to fold and wait for better spots late in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (4d 8h). The action involves a raise from UTG (Minni) and a call from the Button (Claude). Although I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 750, roughly 4.75:1), my hand has extremely poor playability and equity against a UTG opening range and a Button calling range. Being out of position multiway with 8-high offsuit makes equity realization difficult and reverse implied odds high. Folding is the standard and prudent play.

Heads‑up vs Claude on the button, I'm out of position with QcJd on a K♥ T♠ 5♥ flop. I have an open‑ended Broadway straight draw (A or 9 completes) — 8 outs, ~30–31% to make the straight by the river — and decent equity vs a wide calling range. Claude called my 3x open preflop, so his range contains Kx, Tx, broadways and some pairs/draws. Checking gives him free cards to realize equity and lets many Kx/Tx take the pot for free. A semi‑bluff c‑bet is the best line: it can fold out hands with no pair, charges his weaker draws, and keeps the pot manageable while using my ~31% equity if called. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet to balance fold equity and price (pot 750 → bet ~375). If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate and lean to folding OOP with only a draw; if he calls I can continue with equity to improve and on favorable turns can barrel or check/realize depending on action.

I flopped a flush draw with Qh7h on a 5h Kh Ts board - 9 outs to the second nut flush. Minni bet 375 into 750 (half pot). Pot odds: 375 to win 1125 = ~25% needed. My flush draw gives me ~35% equity to hit by river. Easy call in position with this draw. Raising as a semi-bluff is an option but calling keeps pot controlled.

Board now 5♥ K♥ T♠ K♣; my hand Q♣ J♦. I c‑bet the flop and was called, so Claude’s calling range is weighted toward Kx, Tx, two‑pair/set combos, and some broadway pairs — the turn K puts his range ahead much more often than mine. I only have an 8‑out straight (A or 9) to the river: 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to improve. The pot is 1,500 and my stack is deep (15,075), so a turn bluff needs substantial fold equity to be +EV (e.g. a half‑pot bluff requires >33% fold frequency). Given Claude’s line and table history (sticky, calls down frequently), I don’t expect him to fold enough. Betting here will mostly get called by better hands or get me raised, which I must usually fold. Checking preserves equity, gives me a free river to hit my 8 outs, and lets me control the pot size. Plan: check, see Claude’s action on the river; if I hit the straight I’ll bet for value, if I miss I’ll fold to significant aggression or call small probes selectively.

Minni checked on the turn after the board paired with kings. This could be a trap with a king or weakness. I still have my flush draw with 9 outs (~18% to hit on river). With a paired board, even if I hit my flush, I could lose to a full house. Taking a free card here makes sense - I'm in position and can re-evaluate on the river. No need to bet and potentially get check-raised when I can see the river for free.

I missed — Qc Jd has no pair on the river (board 5h Kh Ts Kc 4s). I c-bet the flop and was called, then checked the turn when a second King came. Claude's line (call flop, check turn) is weighted toward Kx, Tx and other medium-strength hands or slowplays; he is also a sticky caller in prior hands. Betting the river as a bluff would need to fold out a lot of Kx/TX combos to be +EV, but given his tendencies and the line he took I don't expect enough fold equity. I also have essentially no showdown value, so a thin value bet isn't available.
