Best play is to check and take the showdown (and if he were to bet after my check I would fold to a medium/large bet and only call very small probes). Preserve the deep stack for better spots.

Missed my flush draw - I have Queen high with no showdown value. Minni checked turn and river on the paired board, which could indicate weakness (missed draw, small pair) or a trap with trips. Given Minni's tendency to be aggressive, their passive line could mean they're giving up OR slow-playing. Bluffing here is risky - if Minni has any pair (Ten, pocket pair) they'll likely call a bet. With Queen high I can't win at showdown regardless, but the risk of getting hero-called by Minni with marginal holdings outweighs the potential gain. Check back and concede the pot.

I am in the UTG position with 5c 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Given my stack size and position, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such low-equity cards. I will wait for a better opportunity.

5h9h suited in UTG+1 is too weak to open. It's a marginal hand that plays poorly from early position. With 4 players left to act behind me, including some active opponents like Minni, I should fold rather than open this speculative hand.

Pocket Tens (TT) is a strong premium hand on the button in 5-handed play after UTG and UTG+1 have folded. Excellent playability postflop with position on SB (Pro) and BB (Minni). Raising standard 3x BB to 300 builds the pot, denies equity to worse hands, and isolates against likely calling ranges. My stack of 3350 (33.5BB) supports this; deeper stacks behind can call but TT crushes typical calling ranges. Late tournament (hand 115/150), value aggression to maximize stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 5d facing a 3bb (300) raise from the Button (Elon). Elon has a stack of roughly 30bb. Q5s is a marginal hand. While suited, it suffers from domination issues and poor playability out of position. The 3x raise size worsens my pot odds compared to a min-raise. Defending here often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am out of position. Against a standard button opening range, Q5s does not have sufficient equity to justify a call, and it is too weak to 3-bet for value or effectively bluff given the stack depths. Folding is the disciplined and standard play.

I’m in the big blind with 8♦2♦ facing a button open to 300 (to‑call 200). This is basically the worst playable suited holding: heavily dominated by a BTN opening range, very poor equity and very unplayable out of position. Calling 200 into a 450 pot requires ~30.8% equity (200 / (450+200) ≈ 30.8%), and 8‑2s is well below that versus a reasonable BTN range. 3‑betting or shoving to exploit Elon (≈30 BB) is unnecessarily high variance with almost no fold equity and very poor showdown equity. With deep tournament life and plenty of hands left, the +EV choice is to conserve chips and fold rather than play multi‑street OOP with a junk hand.
AQo is a premium hand in UTG position. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to build the pot while maintaining fold equity against marginal hands.

5-handed game, QTs suited is a strong speculative hand with straight and flush potential. Facing a UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB) from Claude, who raises frequently from early position based on history. Pot is 400, calling 250 gives ~1.6:1 direct odds but postflop implied odds are strong with deep stacks ahead (my 35BB effective). Folding is too tight 5-handed; 3-betting OOP is possible as a bluff but QTs plays better multiway postflop. Calling to realize equity and control pot size OOP.

I am on the Button with 4c 5d, a very weak off-suit one-gapper. The action started with an UTG raise to 250 and a call from UTG+1. Given the strong opening range of UTG and the multi-way nature of the pot, my hand has very little equity and poor playability. Calling would be unprofitable, and 3-betting as a bluff is ill-advised against two active opponents, one of whom (UTG) likely has a premium hand. Preserving my stack of ~59BB is prioritized over playing junk hands in difficult spots.

I’m in the small blind with 8♠5♥ — a weak, unsuited, often-dominated hand. Claude opened UTG to 250 and Elon called, so I’m facing a raise + a caller and would be out of position postflop. Calling 200 into a 650 pot requires ~23.5% equity; against an UTG raising range and a caller my hand’s equity is well below that and I have poor playability in a multiway pot. With deep stacks there’s no need to gamble or spew chips with a marginal hand, so I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Four of Spades. Claude raised from UTG to 250, and Elon called from UTG+1. The pot is 650, and it costs me 150 to call. Despite the decent pot odds (over 4 to 1), T4o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position and multi-way, especially against an early-position raiser. I will fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

I have top pair top kicker on a monotone club board. I don't have a club, making me vulnerable to flushes and flush draws. Should c-bet for value and protection against Elon (short-stacked at 3250). Betting ~60% pot to charge draws and get value from worse aces/pairs while keeping the pot manageable given the wet board texture.

Flop of 5c Ac 8c is extremely wet with flush draws and straight draws possible, heavily favoring Claude's UTG raising range which includes Ax, suited broadways, pocket pairs, and clubs. My Qs Ts is queen-high with no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw (gutshot at best but unlikely). Equity against his continuing range is very low (<15%). Pot odds 400 into 1450 (~28%) not sufficient. Facing cbet in position from strong range; folding preserves my ~32BB stack for better spots with 35 hands left.

UTG with J5 suited, a marginal speculative hand at best, but too weak for early position in a 5-handed game. My stack is 32.5BB, not desperate enough to shove or raise this trash. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots, especially with deep opponents behind.
I am in UTG+1 with 8s2s, which is a very weak hand (trash). With 59 BBs, I have no reason to get involved with such low equity cards in early position. Folding is the only standard play here.

6♣3♠ is a very weak, unsuited, low hand with poor playability and little equity post‑flop. Even though I’m on the button, both blinds are deep and fairly active — a small open will likely be defended or 3‑bet, leaving me in tough spots. Calling 100 to see a flop is just chasing with little chance to realize equity and exposes my deep stack unnecessarily. I’ll preserve chips and fold to wait for a better spot to leverage position and stack depth.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack suited (AsJs), a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Since everyone folded to me, I will raise to 300 to either win the blinds immediately or build the pot against the Big Blind (Claude). This sizing is consistent with my standard opening raise throughout the tournament.

Facing a min-raise from Flash in SB with K5o in the BB. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400). K5o is marginal but closing the action with decent odds makes a call reasonable. I can hit top pair or a strong draw occasionally.

I have flopped second pair with the nut kicker on a monotone board (three hearts). While Ace-Jack is a strong hand, the presence of three hearts makes the board extremely dangerous since I do not hold any hearts myself. I am out of position against Claude. Checking here allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Claude responds. If he bets, I have a standard call given my pair and kicker, but betting into a board that likely connects well with a Big Blind defending range (which includes many suited hearts and connectors) is risky.

I flopped top pair with Kd5s on a monotone heart board (2h-Jh-Kh). Flash checked to me. I have no heart so I'm vulnerable to any flush draw completing. I should bet for value and protection - charging draws and getting value from Jx or weaker kings. Checking gives free cards on this dangerous board. Betting around 50-60% pot is appropriate.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with the nut kicker (Ace) on a monotone board of hearts. While I do not hold a heart myself, making my hand vulnerable to any further heart on the board, folding second pair to a single bet in a blind-versus-blind situation is too conservative. Claude's range as the Big Blind is very wide and can include bluffs, semi-bluffs with a single heart, or weaker Jacks. Calling allows me to control the pot size and see how the turn develops while keeping his bluffs in. I will re-evaluate on the turn, especially if a fourth heart or an overcard appears.

On the turn, the 9s is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete the heart flush or the most likely straight draws (like QT). I currently have middle pair (Jacks) with the nut kicker, but the board remains extremely dangerous given the three hearts. Since I am out of position and Claude took the lead on the flop after I checked, I will check again to control the pot size. This allows me to see how Claude reacts; if he checks back, I can likely get to a showdown cheaply, and if he bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing to determine if my pair of Jacks is still ahead of his range, which includes bluffs or semi-bluffs with a single high heart.

Turn brought 9s - a blank. Flash checked again. I have top pair (K5o) on a three-heart board with no heart in my hand. Flash called my flop bet on this monotone board - they likely have either a made flush, a flush draw with one heart, or a pair. If they had a flopped flush, they probably would have raised the flop. Checking back is the best play here - I'm way ahead or way behind. Betting risks getting check-raised by a flush, and I don't gain much value since worse hands that called flop might fold. Control the pot and see a safe river.

On the river, a fourth heart (Qh) has appeared, making any heart in Claude's hand a flush. Additionally, any Ten now completes a straight. I hold second pair (Jacks) with no hearts, which is an extremely weak hand on this specific board texture. Given I am out of position and Claude checked back the turn, I will check the river. If Claude bets, I must fold, as he is very likely to have either a heart or a Ten in his Big Blind defending range. If he checks back, I hope to win at showdown against a hand with no hearts and no Ten, though this is unlikely.

River brought Qh completing a 4-heart flush on board. I have Kd5s with no heart - my top pair is now nearly worthless since anyone with ANY single heart beats me. Flash checked, so I should check back. Betting accomplishes nothing - I only get called by flushes that beat me. This is a clear check to showdown and hope Flash also has no heart.

I am in UTG (first position) with King-Five offsuit (K5o) and a stack of roughly 59 big blinds. This is a 5-handed table. K5o is a very weak hand to open from early position due to its poor playability and the likelihood of being dominated by calling ranges or 3-bet ranges from the players left to act, particularly the deep stacks behind me and the short stack in the big blind who might shove. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from UTG. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

UTG+1 with Q♦4♠ — weak, offsuit, and easily dominated by standard opening ranges. We're in early position with three players to act behind (Button, SB, BB); raising to 200 would be a light/fragile play and calling 100 puts us out of position into likely aggression or a shove from shorter stacks. Small absolute cost but high reverse implied odds and awkward postflop decisions multiway. Prefer to conserve a healthy stack and wait for a stronger, more playable spot — fold.

Holding 6c 3d on the button is an extremely weak hand. While it's a potential stealing position, the hand has almost no equity if called and Elon (BB) is short-stacked enough to defend aggressively. It is best to fold and wait for a better spot.
Folded to me in the SB with JTo - a solid hand heads up against the BB. Elon is short-stacked at 3150 (about 31 BBs). I should raise to put pressure on him. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

Facing a small raise from the SB to 250 (2.5BB) into a 350 pot as BB, with T9o. This hand has straight potential, broadway potential, and good playability postflop. Pot odds are excellent: calling 150 more into 350 gives ~2.3:1 direct, plus implied odds. SB ranges are wide in late position aggression (from history, Claude raises frequently). Folding is too tight in BB defense strategy. Raising or jamming (31BB deep) risks vs potential 4bet with a marginal hand. Prefer cheap flop OOP with position heads-up postflop (SB acts first but we close action). Maximizes stack EV long-term with hands like this.

Flopped top pair with jack kicker on 3c 2c Td. Good flop for my hand. Elon only has 3000 behind. I should c-bet for value and protection against the flush draw. Betting around 60% pot (300) to extract value and deny equity.

Facing a 300 bet into 800 pot on the flop (37% pot) from Claude (SB, aggressive raiser preflop). My T9o hit top pair (Th pairs Td) with a 9 kicker. Board is drawy: two clubs (flush draw possible), 2-3-T low connected cards (straight draws like 45, JQ). Pot odds excellent: calling 300 to win 1100 (~2.67:1 effective, plus implied). Claude's SB open + cbet range is polarized/capped: value like sets (unlikely), two pair (A3,A2,32 rare), strong clubs (A3s+, suited broadways), but mostly bluffs/missed draws (Axo, KQo, suited connectors, blockers). I block some Tx (Th) and have showdown value. Raising OOP risks vs better Tx/Ax/sets; folding top pair too tight in BB defense (we called pre wide). Heads-up deep (30BB vs 173BB), call realizes equity, controls pot, sees turn to evaluate flush/straight completion. Late tournament (118/150), preserve stack but exploit villain's aggression history (frequent cbets, shown down medium strength). EV+ call maximizes stack long-term.

Turn brought Qc completing the club flush draw. I have top pair with TcJh but the board now has 3 clubs. I hold the Tc which blocks some flush combos, but checking for pot control makes sense here. If Elon has a flush I don't want to build the pot. If he checks back I can evaluate river. My hand still has decent showdown value.

On the turn, the Qc completes many straight draws (KQ, JQ) and adds another club, making the board very drawy (3c-2c-Td-Qc: flush draws heavy, straights possible). My Th9c now holds top pair (Tens, good kicker) plus a strong club flush draw (four clubs to nut flush potential, as no Ac visible). I called flop OOP with positionally disadvantaged T9o, which hit decently, and villain's check after flop c-bet suggests weakness, polarization (missed draws, air, or traps like sets which are rare). Heads-up ~27BB effective depth (my 2700 vs villain's deep 17375), pot 1100. Checking risks giving free cards to villain's draws or allowing cheap showdown; villain checked, so betting extracts value from worse pairs (Qx, Tx weaker), missed draws (JQ suited, 45), floats/A-high. Value bet sizing ~60-70% pot (~650-800) balances value/protection, denies equity to draws (8 outs ~32% river), exploits villain's aggressive history (frequent cbets but folds to pressure). Late stage (118/150), building stack EV+ by realizing equity and charging draws. Bet 700 to commit controlled size OOP.

Elon bets 700 into 1100. I have top pair with the Tc blocker, which is significant - it reduces his flush combos and gives me a draw to the nut flush if another club comes. Pot odds are 700 to win 2500 (~28% needed). Against a made flush I'm nearly dead, but my Tc blocker makes that less likely. He could be betting Qx for value, a worse ten, or semi-bluffing. With only 2000 behind, he's committed. Folding top pair with the Tc seems too exploitable here. Calling to see the river and re-evaluate.

River paired the board with 2h. I have tens with jack kicker and the Tc blocker. Elon has 2000 behind. The board pairing actually helps me slightly - full houses now beat flushes. With my Tc blocker reducing flush combos, checking to induce a bluff or for pot control is best. If I bet, worse hands fold and better hands call. Checking lets me call a reasonable bet or win at showdown if he checks back.

River 2h pairs the deuces, doesn't complete flush or new straights, but board is draw-heavy (club flush possible with two clubs, prior straight draws like JQ/TJ filled on turn Qc). Hold top pair Tens (Th-Td) with 9 kicker, decent showdown value. Effective stack 20BB short, pot 25BB. Claude checked turn (possible weakness post-flop cbet), called 700 turn bet (polarized: strong hands like sets/32s/Q3s/trips, flushes (two clubs), or stubborn draws/medium strength), now checks river again (caps range, unlikely nuts). Checking risks free showdown with worse (Ax, Qx, Tx weaker, missed draws), villain may have showdown value. Shove 2000 (~80% pot) extracts value from worse pairs/draws, max fold equity vs marginal calls (his deep stack but late tournament pressure). History: Claude aggressive pre/flop but folds to pressure (e.g., Hand 24, 43). BB defense play: realize equity aggressively short-stacked. EV+ shove builds stack, exploits history.

Elon is all-in for 2000 into 4500 (now 6500 total). I need ~31% equity to call. I have top pair (tens) with jack kicker and the crucial Tc blocker which significantly reduces his flush combos. The river 2h paired the board so full houses beat flushes. His range includes: flushes (reduced by my Tc blocker), Qx for two pair, worse tens, or bluffs. Given pot odds, my blocker, and that folding top pair with a key blocker is exploitable, I should call.

Fold. 2♣3♣ is too weak to open from UTG. From early position this tiny suited connector has poor absolute strength, is frequently dominated, and plays badly multiway and out of position. Limping (call 100) or making a small open to 200 simply invites callers and potential squeezes from deeper, aggressive stacks (Claude/Flash), creating difficult post‑flop decisions. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position.

I have been dealt pocket Kings (Kc Kd), which is the second strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My goal is to build the pot and protect my hand. Given the current tournament situation and stack sizes, a standard opening raise is appropriate. I will raise to 300 chips to extract value and potentially narrow the field, while remaining ready to commit more if any of the shorter stacks decide to shove.
72 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even with position on the button, this hand has no playability against a UTG+1 open. Easy fold.

Pocket 22 in SB facing an UTG+1 raise to 300 (call 250 into 450 pot). Excellent pot odds (~3:1 direct, better implied). 22 has ~50% equity vs typical EP range and great set-mining potential with deep effective stacks (Flash 16175). OOP but short stack (32BB) favors calling to realize equity rather than folding a premium short-stack hand or over-shoving (poor risk/reward, low fold equity from EP raiser). BB still to act but unlikely to squeeze wide. Plan: call, check-fold most flops unless set or overcards hit.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c5d, facing a raise to 300 from Flash (UTG+1) and a call from Elon (SB). The pot is 700 and I need to call 200, offering me pot odds of roughly 22%. However, 95o is one of the weakest starting hands, being unsuited and disconnected. Furthermore, I would be playing a multi-way pot out of position against the original aggressor (Flash). Being 'sandwiched' and holding a hand with very poor playability and equity realization makes this a clear fold despite the attractive price. Conserving chips is better than engaging in a marginal negative EV spot.

Flop: Jc 5s As. Heads-up vs Flash (UTG+1 raiser with strong range: pairs 77+, AQo+, suited broadways/connectors) OOP first to act. Pocket 22 is an underpair with minimal showdown value and no strong draws (2s provides weak spade blocker). Board heavily favors villain's range (many Ax, Jx, sets, flush draws). Equity ~20-30% vs range but poor playability OOP. Previous plan was check-fold non-set flops. No value/semi-bluff to lead (short stack, deep villain likely to raise better). Check to induce bluffs or pot control, fold to bet.

I have pocket Kings, which was a premium hand pre-flop. However, the flop has come Ace-high (As), which is a scare card for Kings. Elon called from the Small Blind and has checked to me. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to maintain initiative and extract value from hands like Jacks, pocket pairs lower than Kings, or spade draws. A bet of 350 (half the pot) is a balanced size that allows me to define where I am in the hand. If Elon calls or raises, I must proceed with caution given his short stack and the presence of the Ace.

Flop Jc5sAs is a villain's dream board for his UTG+1 raising range (heavy on Ax, KQs, broadway pairs, Jx). Pocket 22 is a naked underpair with a weak spade blocker but no real draw or playability OOP heads-up. Equity ~20-25% vs his range, but pot odds are marginal at 350 into 1050 (~3:1, needs ~22%), and villain's bet sizing (1/3 pot) is standard value/protection with his strong hands, unlikely to be a bluff given position and range advantage. Calling commits ~12% of my 30BB stack OOP with no initiative, poor implied odds (underpair unlikely to improve ahead), and vulnerable to turn aggression. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 119/150). Plan from pre-flop was check-fold non-sets, executed correctly. Fold.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong starting hand in a five-handed game, especially from UTG. I have a healthy stack and want to maintain an aggressive posture. My standard opening raise throughout the tournament has been 300 chips (3x the big blind), and I will continue with that sizing to build the pot and potentially thin the field or take down the blinds and antes immediately.

I have QQ, a premium hand, facing a standard open from Flash UTG. This is a clear 3-bet for value. I'll raise to around 850-900 to build the pot and isolate. Flash has been active and may call or 4-bet light occasionally.

Facing a UTG raise from Flash and a 3-bet from Claude UTG+1, both deep-stacked players with aggressive histories. My J7s is a speculative suited connector but too weak to call or 4-bet against strong ranges from early position. Pot odds are poor (900 into 1350, ~1.5:1), and committing over 30% of my 2950 stack leaves me vulnerable postflop out of position relative to their strength. On button position helps, but folding preserves my stack for better spots late in the tournament (hand 120/150). Min raise to 1500 also commits too much without equity advantage.

I hold 6h 2s in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. The action ahead involves an UTG open to 300 and a 3-bet from UTG+1 to 900. Facing a 3-bet with a trash hand out of position makes this a trivial decision. I am getting poor odds to call, and the hand has almost no playability or equity against the ranges of an opener and a 3-bettor. Preserving my 5,775 stack is the only logical move.

Facing an UTG open to 300 and an immediate UTG+1 3‑bet to 900. I’m on the BB with Kc6c; pot is 1,350 and it costs me 800 to call. Claude’s sizing and tendencies from the log indicate a fairly strong 3‑bet range from this position (high pairs, AK/AQ, strong broadways) and my hand is largely dominated and plays very poorly out of position. Calling requires roughly 800/(1350+800) ≈ 37% equity — well above what K6s will have vs this range, especially if Flash (the opener) peels or Claude continues aggressively postflop. A light 4‑bet to 1,500 would be a thin bluff with deep stacks and is unlikely to fold enough better hands. Preserve the stack and fold to this 3‑bet.

I opened UTG with Ace-Jack offsuit, a strong hand in a five-handed game. Claude, who has been very active and aggressive throughout the tournament, 3-bet me to 900. While AJo is often at the bottom of a continuing range against a 3-bet, we are playing five-handed and I have a very deep stack (over 160 big blinds). Folding immediately seems too tight given Claude's frequency of play. I'm getting good pot odds (600 to call into a pot of 1350) to see a flop. Although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, my hand has high card strength and can flop top pair reliably. I will call the raise and evaluate the flop texture before committing more chips.

On this paired board of 5-8-8, I am out of position as the caller of Claude's pre-flop 3-bet. While Ace-Jack is a strong high-card hand, it has missed the flop. Claude, as the 3-bettor, has a range advantage that includes high overpairs (99-AA) and strong Ace-high hands like AK and AQ. Leading into the aggressor on this texture is generally not advisable. By checking, I allow Claude to continue with his wide range of bluffs or semi-bluffs, and I can evaluate his bet sizing. My hand still has significant value if the action stays relatively cheap, and I don't want to bloat the pot without at least a pair.

I have QQ as an overpair on a dry 5d-8h-8s board. Flash called my 3-bet and checked. This board doesn't hit many hands in Flash's 3-bet calling range (likely AK, AQ, broadways, medium pairs). My overpair is very strong here - I'm only behind 88 or 55 which are unlikely. I should bet for value to extract from hands like TT, JJ, AK, AQ. Betting around 60-65% pot.

Claude's large continuation bet (over 60% of the pot) on a dry, paired board (5-8-8) is quite polar. While my Ace-Jack has high-card value, it is significantly behind the majority of a standard pre-flop 3-betting range, which includes overpairs (99-AA) and stronger Aces (AK, AQ). Calling out of position with only Ace-high and no backdoor equity is risky, especially given Claude's aggressive sizing. I will fold here to preserve my stack for more favorable situations where I have a clearer equity advantage.
2d9s is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity. From UTG position, I need to play a tight range. This hand has no place in an opening range - easy fold.

5-handed play at 29BB effective stack. Ah6h is a standard opening hand from UTG+1: suited ace with good playability, blockers to premium hands, potential for strong postflop equity multiway or heads-up. No action before me except blinds. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) for value and to build pot/take initiative. Limping bad OOP; folding too tight late in tournament with playable hand. Opponents deep but my sizing controls pot.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qh Qs) on the Button, a premium hand. Elon has opened to 300 from UTG+1 with a relatively short stack of 2,650 (approx 26.5 BB). My goal is to extract value and isolate the opener, preventing the blinds from seeing a cheap flop and potentially cracking my hand. Flat calling would invite multi-way action, which is less desirable with high pairs. A 3-bet is the standard play here for value. Given the open size of 300, a 3-bet to around 850 is appropriate. This size puts significant pressure on Elon's short stack (setting up an SPR of ~1 if he calls) and likely commits him to the pot if he continues, while also discouraging the deep-stacked blinds from entering lightly.

I’m in the small blind with 4♠2♣ facing a button 3‑bet to 850 after an UTG+1 open to 300. This is a completely unplayable hand out of position with almost zero post‑flop equity against the opener + 3‑better. Calling 800 would require ~28% equity to justify it and likely leaves me in tough multiway spots; a 3‑bet bluff is possible but unnecessary here given opponent ranges and stack dynamics. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o. Elon (a short stack) opened to 300, and Pro (a medium-short stack) 3-bet to 850. My hand is very weak and clearly behind the ranges of both the original raiser and the 3-bettor. I am out of position against both. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are only 30 hands left in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to commit chips with junk in this spot. I will fold.

Facing a 3-bet to 850 (needing 550 to call) from Pro on the button after my UTG+1 open to 300. Pot is 1300, my stack 2650 (~26BB). A6hh is a suited ace with playability and nut flush potential, but OOP against a button 3-bet range (likely JJ+, AK, AQ, some bluffs suited broadways/connectors), my equity is around 35-40% heads-up but plays poorly postflop OOP with reverse implied odds due to kicker. Calling commits ~20% of stack into a pot where I'll be out of position, facing further aggression. 4-betting (min 1400 or shove 2650) overplays it vs a calling range and risks stack without fold equity guarantee. Late tournament (hand 121/150, 5-handed), preserve stack for better spots like premium hands or steals. Tight fold standard here to maximize EV long-term.

UTG position with 5d8s offsuit, a marginal trash hand with poor playability, low equity against any range, and no suited/connected value for speculative calling. Pot is 150, to-call 100 gives odds of about 2:1 but hand realizes equity poorly multiway. Late tournament (hand 122/150), stack 26.5BB, better to preserve chips for premium spots rather than enter with weak holding first in. Standard fold.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (Ad Ks) in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with approximately 62 BB. This is a premium hand that warrants an open raise for value. My standard opening size from this position in previous hands has been 2.5x (250), which balances well with my range. The opponents behind me are deep-stacked, so I want to build the pot but not overcommit pre-flop unnecessarily against a 3-bet, though I am willing to continue against aggression.

Button with Ad9d vs a 250 open from UTG+1 — this is a playable suited Ace in position. I want to take the initiative, isolate the opener and avoid a multiway pot that dilutes my fold equity and postflop maneuverability. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard, constructive size: big enough to deny cheap action from the blinds and apply pressure to Pro, yet small relative to my deep stack so I can continue postflop in position if called. If I face a large 4-bet shove I can fold and preserve my stack; if I'm called I have good playability and equity to realize in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. Pro (UTG+1) opened for 2.5x and Minni (Button) has put in a 3-bet to 900. While Minni has shown high frequency and aggression throughout the tournament, A6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raise and a 3-bet. I am likely dominated by Minni's range, and Pro still has to act behind me if I were to call. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.
Q8o is a weak hand facing a raise and a 3-bet. This is a clear fold - I'm out of position with a dominated hand against a strong 3-betting range from Minni who has been aggressive.

I hold AKo, a premium hand, in the Cutoff (UTG+1) and faced a 3-bet to 900 from the Button (Minni). My stack is roughly 60 BB. A 4-bet shove would be a massive overbet (approx 60BB into a 13BB pot), likely only getting called by hands that flip with or dominate me, while folding out Minni's bluffs. A smaller 4-bet would commit a significant portion of my stack and create a very low SPR pot. Calling allows me to keep Minni's range wide, including dominated hands like AQ/AJ or bluffs, and control the size of the pot while minimizing variance at this tournament stage. Although I will be out of position, the SPR of ~2.6 allows for reasonable post-flop play. Therefore, I call the additional 650 chips.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with a stack-to-pot ratio of approximately 2.7. The board is 7h-6h-7d, which is a paired, low, semi-wet texture. While my AK high has showdown value, it does not connect strongly with this board. The standard play against a pre-flop aggressor who 3-bet from the button is to check and see what they do. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands (overpairs) or strong draws, while folding out hands I dominate or chop with (AQ, AJ, other AK). Checking keeps my range balanced and allows me to evaluate the opponent's sizing if they choose to bet.

I 3-bet preflop to isolate and seize initiative. The flop (7h 6h 7d) is paired with two hearts — I’ve missed with Ad9d but I block A♦ and have a small backdoor diamond draw. As the preflop aggressor I should use initiative and make a small continuation bet to fold out air (broadway hands, some unpaired Ax/Kx, and weaker connectors) and charge heart draws, while keeping the pot controllable. A 1/3-pot c-bet (≈650) accomplishes that: it applies pressure, preserves stack depth for later streets if called, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. If I face a significant raise I’ll give it up (likely behind to a set/two-pair); if called I’ll proceed cautiously in position.

I hold Ace-King offsuit on a Flop of 7h-6h-7d. I am out of position against a pre-flop 3-bettor (Minni) who has c-bet 650 (1/3 pot). While the price is relatively cheap, the board texture is poor for my hand (low, paired, semi-connected), and I lack any flush draws (holding As Kd). My opponent's range retains all overpairs (88-AA) which crush me, and I will struggle to realize equity or withstand further aggression on the turn and river while out of position. Without a pair or strong draw, and with a stack of ~53 BBs that I wish to preserve for better opportunities, the disciplined play is to fold rather than float with just two overcards.

I am playing 5-handed in the UTG position (effectively Hijack) with Ace-Five suited (Ac 5c). With 53 big blinds, this hand is strong enough to open-raise for value and board coverage, as it has good playability post-flop (flush and straight potential) and blocks Ace-X hands. I will open to 2.5bb (250 chips), which is my standard sizing, to put pressure on the blinds and the short stack in the Big Blind.

Facing an UTG open to 250 while I'm UTG+1 with 9s2s — essentially one of the worst playable hands. I'm out of position to the Button and the blinds; calling 250 into the current pot gives poor odds and very little post‑flop playability (high chance of being dominated and suffering reverse implied odds). A 3‑bet bluff here is unnecessary and likely to be called or 4‑bet by better ranges; with a healthy stack I should conserve chips for stronger holdings or better position. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. A player in UTG (which is effectively the Cutoff in a 5-handed game) has opened for 2.5x. While I have position, A6o is a relatively weak hand that is easily dominated by an UTG opening range. Additionally, there is a short stack (Elon) and a deep stack (Claude) behind me in the blinds; if I call, I risk being squeezed out of the pot. Given my comfortable stack and the stage of the tournament, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid a marginal and potentially expensive post-flop situation.

8d6d suited in SB facing UTG raise. This is a weak suited connector - while it has some playability, it's not strong enough to defend against an UTG open. Elon could squeeze from BB, and even if he doesn't, I'll be out of position postflop. With 18825 chips and good stack, no need to get involved with marginal hands. Fold.

Facing an UTG raise from Pro to 250 (2.5x BB), which indicates a strong range. My hand 4c9h is offsuit, unconnected trash with no high card value or playability. In the BB, I have no position post-flop. Pot odds to call 150 into 400 are about 2.67:1, but equity against UTG range is low (~20-25%), and I'll be OOP with a weak hand on any board. Late in tournament (hand 123/150), stack 2550 (~25BB), better to preserve chips for better spots rather than defend marginally. Standard fold.

UTG with 2♣4♠ is a very weak, unplayable hand. Calling 100 (limping) or raising out of position would commit chips in a multiway pot where I have poor equity and will be easily dominated. There are four players to act behind, and I should preserve my 17,325 stack for better spots. Folding is the disciplined +EV choice here.

I am holding Ten-Nine offsuit in UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. While the table is short-handed, T9o is generally too weak to open-raise from this position, especially with two short stacks in the blinds (Elon and Pro) who are likely in shove-or-fold mode. Additionally, Claude is a big stack on the button who has shown aggression. I will fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

On the button with 67o after two folds. This is a good steal spot - I have position and a decent stack. While 67o isn't premium, it plays well postflop if called and I can take down the blinds often here. Standard button open to 250.

82o is one of the worst starting hands possible, with terrible equity against any raising range. Claude's button raise could be wide, but even against a loose range, 82o plays poorly postflop out of position. In SB with 25BB effective stack, preserving chips for better spots is crucial this late in the tournament (hand 124/150). No fold equity for a squeeze with this hand, and calling commits too much of stack to a speculative hand with poor implied odds. Standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind with QTo facing a standard 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). Claude is an aggressive player with a wide opening range in late position. QTo is a strong enough hand to defend effectively, offering roughly 48% equity against a typical button opening range. I am getting favorable pot odds, needing to call 150 to win a pot of 400 (roughly 2.67:1). Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary as I can realize equity by calling and playing post-flop, albeit out of position. Therefore, I will call to see a flop.

I am out of position on a dry, paired board (5s Jd 5h) with Queen-high and no significant draws. The pre-flop raiser (Claude) has a range advantage here, holding overpairs and strong Jacks. Standard strategy is to check to the aggressor. Leading out serves little purpose as I would fold out worse hands and get action from better ones. I will check and evaluate Claude's action.

Paired board with 5-5-J. I raised preflop and Pro just check-called from BB. This is a standard c-bet spot - paired boards favor the preflop aggressor, and I have position. A small sizing around 1/3 pot should be effective here. If I get raised, I can easily fold with no equity.

I am holding QTo in the Big Blind on a flop of 5s Jd 5h. Claude (Button) has c-bet 200 into a pot of 550. This is a dry, paired board where Claude has a range advantage and is expected to c-bet frequently with a wide range. Although the bet is small (roughly 1/3 pot) offering me favorable odds to call, my hand has very poor playability. I hold Queen-high with no flush draws or significant straight draws (no backdoor flush equity either). I am currently losing to all pairs, Ace-highs, and King-highs. While I have 6 outs to a pair, hitting a Ten or Queen does not guarantee the best hand and I am out of position, making it difficult to realize my equity or bluff on later streets. Against a generic button c-betting range, QTo without a backdoor flush draw is a fold.

I am under the gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game. Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o) is a marginal hand that typically falls just outside of a standard opening range from early position, even in short-handed play. Opening here would likely result in being out of position against deep stacks like Claude or Minni if they call, or facing a potential shove from the short stacks (Elon or Pro) which would put me in a difficult spot. Given that I want to preserve my stack as we approach the final hands of the tournament, folding is the most disciplined play.

J8o from UTG+1 is a weak hand in early position. With 25 hands remaining and a healthy stack of 19125, there's no need to play marginal holdings from early position. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Qd4c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor postflop playability, low equity against calling ranges, and easily dominated by better queens or any ace. On the button with folds from UTG and UTG+1, there's some steal potential, but with a 25BB stack late in the tournament (hand 125/150), risking chips on a marginal spot against deep-stacked blinds (Pro 51BB+, Minni 172BB+) is unnecessary when better spots will arise. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 offer only 2:1 (including fold equity negligible for call), but EV is negative long-term. Folding preserves stack for stronger hands.

I am in the Small Blind with [9h 7d], a weak offsuit connector (97o). The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader with a massive stack and has shown activity in previous hands. Playing this hand out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive player is likely -EV. While BvB ranges are wide, 97o is generally below the threshold for a profitable limp or raise, especially given the stack dynamics. Folding preserves my ~52 BB stack for better opportunities.
K3o is too weak to open from UTG. Even with a king, the 3 kicker gives me poor playability and I'll often be dominated by better kings. Folding is the correct play from early position.

Preflop from UTG+1 (early position) with 6s8d offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor playability and low equity against ranges. My stack is 25BB, but folding trash early preserves chips for better spots. Opponents have deep stacks; no need to speculate with marginal hand. Pot is small at 150, to call 100 offers poor odds for this hand. Standard fold to maximize long-term stack.

I am on the Button with J4o, a very weak hand. Action has folded to me. While the Button is a stealing position, J4o is at the very bottom of the range and generally not profitable to open, even in an unopened pot. The players in the blinds (Minni and Flash) are deep-stacked (>150 BBs) and can easily defend or 3-bet, putting me in a difficult spot post-flop or forcing a fold pre-flop. With a stack of ~52 BBs, I have plenty of patience to wait for a better hand to open. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I’m in the small blind with 3♠9♥ (trash, unsuited) and only the big blind (Flash) remains. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to see the flop — calling will commit a total of 100 chips (I’ve already posted 50). Calling is the low-cost, correct defense here: the call gives me ~25% break-even equity (50 into a 200 pot after my call), so it’s worth seeing a flop heads-up against a wide BB defending range. Raising would be a pure bluff with a poor hand and folding surrenders too much for just 50 chips. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to significant preflop aggression (3-bet), and postflop only continue with a pair/strong draw or favorable board texture — otherwise control the pot and let stronger hands show down.

Minni completed from the small blind. I'm in the big blind with 8h 5c. This is a weak hand, but I get to see a free flop. There's no reason to raise here and potentially play a larger pot with a marginal holding. I'll check and see what the flop brings.

I have top pair (9s) with a very weak 3 kicker on Qc-9c-6h. Heads-up vs the big blind’s wide defending range, many worse hands (weaker nines, small pairs) and lots of club draws will call a reasonable c-bet. I want to get value from worse and charge club draws, so c-bet a clear but not huge size. Betting ~half-pot (100 into a 200 pot) achieves both value and protection. If he raises big (polarized to Qx/sets/strong draws) I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold to large, pot-sized+ raises; consider calling small raises only if odds and reads justify it). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue vs draws/weak pairs or pot-control vs heavy action.

On a flop of Qc 9c 6h, I have 8h 5c, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Minni leads for 100 into a 200 pot. While the price to call is small, my hand has very little equity (approximately 8-9% to hit the gutshot on the turn) and 8-high is extremely weak. Even if I hit the straight, it's not the nuts, and the board has a flush draw. In a limped pot with a healthy stack and 25 hands remaining, there's no reason to float or chase a marginal draw with no overcard equity.

UTG in 5-handed play with 65o offsuit, an extremely weak hand with poor playability, low equity, and vulnerable to domination. Multiple strong opponents behind with deep stacks, including Minni on the button who is active. My 25BB stack is playable but no need to risk it with trash. Standard fold to preserve chips for premium hands late in the tournament (hand 127/150).

In a 5-handed game, sitting in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with Ace-Ten offsuit after the UTG player folds is a standard opening spot. My stack of roughly 52 BB allows me to play comfortably. ATo blocks strong Ax hands and plays reasonably well as a steal or for value against wider blind defense ranges. I will raise to 250 (2.5 BB) which is consistent with my previous opening sizes and offers a good balance between applying pressure and minimizing loss if 3-bet.

I’m on the button with 7♥8♥ against a standard UTG+1 open to 250 (pot 400). This is a playable suited connector with great post‑flop playability and position on the raiser and the blinds. Calling 250 keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and gives me implied odds to hit straights/flushes — while a small min 3‑bet (to 400) would be too thin to fold out hands and a larger 3‑bet would commit too much with a marginal hand. The raiser (Pro) has a shorter stack (~4.9k) which slightly reduces implied odds versus him, but the blinds are deeper and could come along. 250 is a small investment relative to my stack (~1.4%), so I’ll call and evaluate on the flop: proceed aggressively when I hit strong equity/draws, and fold to heavy resistance if I miss and face big pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. Pro (UTG+1) has raised to 250, and Minni (Button) has called. While the pot odds are tempting (200 to call into a pot of 650), K5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against two active opponents, especially in a multi-way pot. Claude is still to act in the Big Blind and could potentially squeeze. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a more profitable situation rather than risk chips with a marginal hand that has poor playability post-flop.
K9o in BB facing a 250 raise from Pro with Minni calling. Getting 4.3:1 pot odds (150 to win 650). K9o has decent playability and I'm closing the action. Easy call to see a flop.
Flopped nothing with K9o on 4s3c8s. I have king-high against two opponents. No reason to bet into two players with air. Check and take a free card - I can improve if a K or 9 comes.

I raised pre-flop with AT offsuit and got two callers (Button and BB). The flop comes 4s 3c 8s, a low board with a flush draw. In a 3-way pot, c-betting with just Ace-high on this texture is risky as it connects well with calling ranges (sets, pairs, straight draws, flush draws). My hand has some showdown value and I hold the As which gives me backdoor flush potential, but I have no immediate made hand or strong draw. Checking allows me to pot control, avoid getting check-raised, and potentially realize equity on the turn. If checked to, I will check back.

I have top pair (8♥) with a decent kicker (7♥) and position on both opponents after the flop (4♠ 3♣ 8♠). Both the UTG+1 opener (Pro) and the big blind checked, which signals weakness and gives me an opportunity to take the pot now. I’ll bet a small-to-medium amount (~1/3 pot) to extract value from worse pairs, charge any two‑card spade draws, and deny free cards that could outdraw my pair — while keeping the pot manageable against Pro’s shorter stack. If I face a sizable check‑raise (especially all‑in) I’ll fold or re-evaluate tightly since sets/overpairs can beat me; if I get single callers I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and continue to apply pressure when appropriate.

Facing Minni's 300 bet on 4s3c8s with K9o - complete air. I have two overcards but no pair, no draw. Getting 3.7:1 pot odds but I'm out of position with Pro still to act behind me. Even if I call, I need to hit a K or 9 (6 outs ~12%) and then likely face more action. Not worth chasing with king-high here against an aggressive player who could have connected with this board or have a pocket pair. Fold and wait for a better spot.

The pot is 1100 (including Minni's 300 bet and the preflop pot of 800). I need to call 300, getting pot odds of roughly 3.6:1 (21.4% equity required). The board is low (4s 3c 8s) and the button's small bet (300 into 800) is a common stab size after two checks, often representing a wide range including draws, weak pairs, or pure bluffs. I hold AsTc, giving me Ace-high which is currently ahead of semi-bluffs and air. I also have the Ace of Spades, giving me a backdoor nut flush draw and blocking the nut flush draw in Minni's range. With two overcards and the backdoor draw, my equity against a typical button range is sufficient to justify a call given the excellent price.

The turn brings a 3d, pairing the board (4s 3c 8s 3d). As the pre-flop aggressor who checked-called the flop, I am out of position against Minni. My hand (AsTc) has showdown value as Ace-high, and the 3d is unlikely to have improved Minni's range significantly unless they held a 3 specifically (less likely given pre-flop flat and small flop bet). Leading out (donking) here would likely only get called by better hands (pairs, trips) and fold out worse hands (missed draws, air), accomplishing little. Checking is the standard play to pot control, induce bluffs, or get to a cheap showdown. My goal is to realize my equity and see the river.

Turn: 4♠ 3♣ 8♠ 3♦ — I hold 8♥7♥ (top pair, weak kicker) and Pro (UTG+1 opener) is the only remaining caller after I bet the flop. The turn pairs the board and massively increases the chance he’s sitting on a set/full house (pocket 3s/4s/8s) or another disguised very strong holding. With a marginal top pair on a paired board and a relatively short-stacked caller behind me, I don’t want to inflate the pot and give him easy shove/raise spots when I’m often behind. I’ll check for pot control and to allow him to bluff — plan is to call reasonable small-to-medium bets to reach showdown but fold to large check-raises or all-ins that represent full houses. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate the river for a thin value bet or showdown depending on the card.

On the River, the board is [4s 3c 8s 3d 5d]. I hold [Tc As], giving me Ace-high (technically a pair of 3s with an Ace kicker on the board). The action went: I raised pre-flop, checked-called a small bet on the flop, and the turn was checked through. Minni's range likely consists of missed spade draws, weak pairs (4x, 8x, pocket pairs), or perhaps a straight draw that got there (6x, 2x). My hand beats missed draws but loses to any pair. Betting here is unlikely to fold out better hands (like a pair of 4s or 8s) and only folds out hands I already beat. Checking allows me to potentially realize my showdown value against missed draws if Minni checks back, or evaluate if Minni decides to bluff. Given the board texture and action, checking is the standard line to minimize loss and get to showdown cheaply.

Check. I have top pair (8♥) with a weak 7 kicker on a paired board (4♠ 3♣ 8♠ 3♦ 5♦). Pro called my flop bet but then led a very passive line — checking turn and checking the river — so his range likely contains medium pairs, underpairs, or the occasional slow‑played strong hand (sets/full houses are possible). Betting here risks inflating the pot and allowing a short‑stack shove (or a check‑raise) that I often need to fold to; a thin value bet would only get called by a small subset of worse hands. Checking controls the pot and gets to showdown cheaply against hands I beat; if he bets a small amount I’ll call for value, but I’ll treat a large shove as a fold versus a likely full house.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 5-handed game with a stack of roughly 46 big blinds. My hand is 4h 5h, a low suited connector. While suited connectors have playability, opening them from the earliest position is generally loose, especially with active players and larger stacks behind who will have position on me post-flop. The Big Blind (Elon) is also short-stacked enough (24 BB) that he might jam over an open, forcing me to fold and lose equity. Given the stage of the tournament (Hand 128/150) and my stack size, preserving chips for better positional spots or stronger hands is the prudent play. I will fold.

Ten-four offsuit in UTG+1 is a marginal hand with very little playability — often dominated by standard opening ranges from later positions. The pot is just the blinds (150) so calling 100 would be an unprofitable limp and raising to the min (200) is a thin open that invites 3-bets or an iso-shove from the short BB (Elon, 2400) or aggression from Button/SB. With a healthy stack (18,325) and several hands left in the tournament, I should preserve chips and wait for a much stronger spot. Fold.

I am on the button with 2s3s and it has folded to me. While this is a prime stealing position, 2s3s is at the very bottom of any opening range. Elon is in the Big Blind with a relatively short stack of 24 BBs and is in a position where he might shove or defend frequently. Claude in the Small Blind is deep-stacked and has played aggressively. Given the stack sizes and the poor post-flop potential of my hand, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads up against the big blind with T7o. This is a weak hand but completing is cheap at only 50 more chips. Elon is short-stacked at 2400. T7o has some playability with straight potential. I'll complete rather than raise with this marginal hand out of position.

Preflop in BB with J6o facing a limp from SB (Claude). Unraised pot, pot 200. J6o is a marginal hand with some straight potential but offsuit and not strong. Claude has a massive stack (18775) and acts first postflop. With ~24BB effective stack, checking to see flop cheaply is best rather than risking a raise with a weak hand that plays poorly OOP if called. Avoid bloating pot OOP with mediocre holdings. Will reassess postflop.

Board is Qc Js Jh - paired board with jacks. I have T7o with nothing - no pair, just a weak gutshot (K gives me KQJ10). Against short-stacked Elon heads up, checking is correct. I have no equity to bet for value, and bluffing into a paired board where he could easily have a J or Q is -EV. Free card.

Flopped top set of Jacks (Jd with Js Jh on board Qc Js Jh) heads-up against Claude who limped from SB and checked the flop. This is an absolute monster hand, likely the nuts or very close (beats any two-pair, overpairs, etc.; only loses to unlikely QQ or pocket JJ). Board is paired, reducing straight draws, but protects against some turns. With only 2400 stack (~24BB) and deep opponent, need to get value from weaker Jacks, Qx, Tx for straight draws, etc. Claude's limp-check suggests weak range. Betting for value and thin value; pot-sized bet builds the pot without scaring off callers. Avoid checking to pot control as this hand wants action. No need to shove yet with small pot.

I have T7o with no pair on Qc Js Jh board. Just a gutshot (need K for KQJT = 4 outs, ~8%). Elon bet 200 into 200, I'm getting 2:1 pot odds but need ~12:1 for the gutshot. No showdown value, no draw worth chasing. Easy fold.

UTG with Qc3c — a weak, easily-dominated hand. From early position this has poor playability and a bad kicker against any Queen or higher; calling to limp just invites raises and multiway pots where Q3s performs very poorly. Opening here would be too loose: players behind (Flash, Claude, Pro, and a short-stacked SB) are capable of 3-bets or coming along, leaving me OOP. Preserve my healthy 18k stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I have Ace-Seven suited (As 7s), which is a strong starting hand in a 5-handed game. Since the first player (Minni) folded, I am the first to act. Opening with a standard raise to 300 is a consistent strategy for my playstyle and appropriate for this hand's strength to build a pot or win the blinds.

K2o is a weak hand even on the button. Flash opened to 300 and while I have position, K2 offsuit doesn't play well postflop - when I hit a king I'm often dominated, and the deuce provides no backup. This is a clear fold against a standard opening range.

Hand 129 pre-flop, SB with K4s vs UTG+1 raise to 300 from deep-stacked Flash (15375). K4s is a marginal suited king, but poor playability OOP, especially against an early position raise which suggests strength. My stack is shortest at 2550 (~25BB), Pro BB has 4525. Calling 250 OOP risks multiway pot with dominated hand. 3-betting has fold equity but I'm OOP and hand doesn't play well post-flop. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 129/150).

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 2s, which is statistically one of the worst hands in poker. Flash raised to 300 (3BB) from early position (UTG+1), indicating a potentially strong range. Although I only need to call 200 to win a pot of 450, offering decent pot odds, my hand has very low equity and poor playability out of position against an early position raiser. With 45 BBs, I have a healthy stack and should not bleed chips with trash hands. Folding is the only reasonable option.

In this 5-handed game, holding Ace-Six offsuit (Ah6c) from the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position is a marginal but playable hand, especially given the current stack dynamics. I have a significant stack of 15,825 chips, making me one of the big stacks. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening that exerts pressure on the smaller stacks like Elon and Pro, while also defining the strength of my hand. If I am 3-bet by the larger stacks like Minni or Claude, I can comfortably fold without damaging my tournament position, but often this will take the blinds down or let me play a pot with initiative.
J5o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Not profitable to call or 3-bet with this offsuit hand against an early position opener. Easy fold.

Preflop on the button with Ts7c offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor playability. Flash raised from UTG (early position), indicating strength. With only 20 hands left and my stack at ~25BB, preserving chips for better spots is key. Pot odds ~1.5:1 after call, but equity against UTG range is low (~25-30%). Standard fold to avoid tough postflop situations.

I hold a premium hand (AKs) in the Small Blind facing an UTG raise from Flash. Flat calling is suboptimal as it invites the Big Blind to come along, leaving me out of position in a multi-way pot. 3-betting is the standard play here to build the pot for value, isolate the opener, and take the initiative. Given I am out of position and want to reduce the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) to simplify post-flop decisions, a larger 3-bet size is appropriate. I will raise to 1100 (approximately 3.6x the open), which commits about 25% of my stack, setting up a profitable shove or easy commit on favorable flops.

UTG (Flash) opened to 300 and the SB (Pro) bumped it to 1100. I have Tc Jc in the BB — a suited broadway with good flop playability and backdoor club/straight potential. Calling 1,000 more into a 1,500 pot (~40% breakeven equity) is cheap relative to my 18,225 stack and gives me the chance to realize equity postflop. 3-betting here with TJs is thin versus an active 3-bettor and folding is too tight given the price and implied odds. I'll call to see a flop and then fold to extreme aggression or a clear 4-bet shove if I miss and the action indicates I'm dominated.

I am facing a 3-bet from Pro (the small blind) and a cold call from Minni (the big blind) after my initial UTG raise. While I only need to call 800 more into a pot of 2500, my hand (Ah6c) is likely heavily dominated by both opponents' ranges. Pro's 3-bet from a short stack of 34BBs suggests a strong linear range, and Minni's cold call of a 3-bet from the big blind is particularly concerning, often indicating a premium hand trying to trap. Being out of position against a deep-stacked Minni and facing significant strength from two players makes this an easy fold to protect my stack.

I hold AdKd on a board of As-9s-3d. I have Top Pair Top Kicker. The pot is 2500 and I have 3425 behind, creating a Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of roughly 1.37. Given the low SPR and the presence of a flush draw (spades), my goal is to get stacks in. Since I do not hold a spade, it is more likely my opponent holds a flush draw, which I want to charge. Checking risks giving a free card to a draw or checking through and missing a street of value. I will bet approximately 36% of the pot (900). This gets value from worse Aces and flush draws, and leaves me with ~2525 behind to comfortably shove the turn into a pot of 4300.

I called preflop with Tc Jc to see a flop; on As 9s 3d Pro (the preflop raiser) leads for 900 into a 3,400 pot. Calling 900 to win ~4,300 gives ~21% pot odds. I have missed the flop (no pair) but have ~6 clean pair-outs (J/T) plus backdoor club/runner-runner straight possibilities — total equity in the mid-20% range — so a call is justified rather than folding. Raising as a bluff is too risky versus a preflop 3-bettor who can have many Aces/pairs and still has enough stack to shove; I’ll call to see the turn and will fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (or pick up a strong read), and will proceed aggressively only if I pair/flush/appropriate card arrives.

I hold AdKd on a board of As-9s-3d-Kc, giving me Top Two Pair. The pot is 4,300 and I have 2,525 remaining in my stack (SPR ~0.6). The Turn card (Kc) improves my hand significantly, beating everything except sets of 9s or 3s, which are less likely given the pre-flop action (cold call of 3-bet). My opponent, Minni, likely holds a flush draw (which I unblock), a weaker Ace (AQ, AJ), or possibly a lower two pair like A9. With such a low SPR and a strong value hand vulnerable to flush draws, the optimal play is to move all-in to maximize value and deny equity to draws. Checking risks giving a free card or losing value if the opponent checks back. I am committed and shove for value.

Pro shoved the turn for 2,525 into a 6,825 pot after 3-betting preflop and betting the flop. To call requires ≈2525/(4300+2525)=2525/6825 ≈ 37% equity. I have TcJc and have not improved on the turn. My clean river outs: Qx (4) gives Broadway; J/T (6) make only a single pair. Naively that’s 10 outs → ~10/44 ≈ 23% equity, but most of the pair-outs are “dirty” vs the preflop 3‑bettor’s value-heavy line (AK, A9, sets, two‑pair). Against that realistic range only the 4 queens are reliably live (~9%); a fair adjusted estimate of my equity is well under the ~37% needed. Calling 2,525 (~15% of my stack) here is negative‑EV; I already planned to fold to significant turn aggression unless I improved. I’ll fold and preserve chips for better spots.

K7o from UTG is a weak hand. As the chip leader with 187 BBs, I don't need to take marginal spots. K7o is not profitable to open from early position - it's dominated by better kings and lacks playability. Easy fold.

4s8h is a trash hand: offsuit, low cards, gapped connectors at best but too weak. UTG+1 in 5-handed is early position, vulnerable postflop. Stack is 25BB, short but not desperate; pot odds to call 100 into 150 are poor for speculative hand without position or suitedness. Opponents deep except me; folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 131/150).

I am on the Button with Jd 6s. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, J6o is a very weak hand (bottom 35% of hands). Given that the blinds (Minni and Flash) have large stacks and have been relatively active, and my own stack is healthy at ~68 BBs, there is no need to force a steal with such poor equity. The expected value of opening here is likely negative compared to folding.

Action folded to me in the small blind, heads-up vs Flash (BB). I hold QcTd — a playable, fairly strong heads-up hand with good flop playability. Calling 50 (completing to 100) is passive and gives the BB good odds to see flops OOP against me. A min-raise to 200 is too small and invites many speculative calls. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure, and deny correct odds to many of Flash’s calling hands, while keeping stacks deep for postflop play. A standard 3xBB size (raise to 300) achieves that: good fold equity (win the 150 pot immediately if he folds), isolates heads-up when called, and leaves plenty of chips behind to play postflop. If he 3-bets big I can fold QTo; if he calls I plan to c-bet appropriate textures or proceed cautiously when massively raised. Raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with Q8o. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 300. This is a standard blind-vs-blind situation. Getting 3:1 odds (200 more to call into a pot of 400), Q8o is well within the defending range against a Small Blind open. I have position for the rest of the hand, and my hand has decent high-card value. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and position. A raise is unnecessary and might bloat the pot against a chip leader, so I will call to see a flop.

Heads-up vs the BB after my 3x preflop raise (I opened to 300). Flop 6d–4c–2d is very dry and disconnected; it missed a lot of a BB’s calling range (small pairs, weak broadways, suited connectors). I hold QcTd: two clean overcards and one diamond (backdoor/runner potential) — not a made hand but a good candidate for a high-frequency continuation bet heads-up. Betting takes the initiative, folds many unpaired holdings, and denies profitable pot odds to the primary immediate draws (opponent’s two-diamond combos). With the pot at 600 I want meaningful fold equity, so I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot: bet 400. Plan: if he folds we take it down; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (check and control if a brick, or continue barreling only if I pick up equity or he shows weakness). If he raises large I’ll fold QTo to avoid bloating the pot with just overcards.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 8d. The flop is 6d 4c 2d. The Small Blind (Minni) has bet 400 into a pot of 600. I have Queen-high and no direct draws, although I do have a backdoor diamond draw. This board is quite low and doesn't connect well with my Q8o. Calling here would be a float with very little equity, and since I am facing a bet from a large stack who has shown aggression, it is best to let this hand go and wait for a more favorable situation rather than committing more chips with just overcards.

UTG with 5s2h, a very weak suited hand with poor playability and low equity multiway or against raises. Standard fold from early position to preserve my ~25BB stack for better spots late in the tournament (hand 132/150). No reads from opponents justify entering with this trash.

I hold 7d Tc in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with 5 players remaining. The UTG player has folded. T7o is a weak hand with poor playability and is generally not profitable to open-raise even from late position, especially with three deep-stacked opponents (Button, SB, BB) left to act who can easily defend or 3-bet. Given I have a healthy stack of roughly 68 big blinds, there is no need to force a steal with such marginal equity. Folding is the standard and prudent play.

I'm on the button with T5 hearts — a playable suited hand with decent postflop playability and backdoor heart/straight potential. Only the small and big blinds remain, so this is a textbook spot to attempt a steal and take the initiative. I want to avoid a bare min-raise (which invites 3-bets) but also keep the raise size reasonable given deep stacks, so I open to ~3x the big blind. This gives strong fold equity versus the blinds, and if I'm called I have position and playable equity to continue on many flops. If either blind 3-bets big, I'll fold this hand rather than get into a large contest out of position.

I am holding 9d 4c in the Small Blind. Minni has opened from the Button to 300. This hand is extremely weak and plays very poorly out of position. There is no strategic value in defending such a wide range hand here against a standard open. Folding is the correct play to preserve my stack.

J4 suited in the BB facing a min-raise from Minni on the button. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds. J4s is a reasonable defend here - suited hands with some high card value are playable against wide button opens. Minni has been active throughout. Calling 200 to see a flop.

Completely whiffed this flop - no pair, no draw (board has spades, I have diamonds). I'm in position to check first. Standard check here with zero made hand or draw. If Minni bets, I'll fold. No reason to put chips in with J-high and no backdoor draws.

I am the preflop aggressor (button) with T♥5♥ on a Q♠7♠2♦ flop. I have no pair and, because the flop contains zero hearts, I cannot make a five-card heart flush by the river — so this hand has little intrinsic showdown value. The board is relatively dry aside from the two spades, and my preflop range credibly contains many Qx/broadway hands that I can represent. A standard continuation bet should fold out unpaired A/K/J-type hands and small pairs while keeping the pot manageable against spade draws and made pairs. I'll make a ~50% pot c-bet (325) to apply pressure and gather information. If Claude folds I take the pot; if he calls I will proceed cautiously on a spade or a card that pairs the board; if he check-raises large on the flop I will fold this marginal hand.

Missed the flop completely - J-high with no pair and only a weak backdoor diamond draw. Minni c-bet 325 into 650. Getting 3:1 odds but I have almost no equity here. No reason to float with this weak holding. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position with 9h 5s, which is an extremely weak hand (9-5 offsuit). With 5 players remaining and a healthy stack of over 68 big blinds, there is no strategic incentive to open the pot with trash cards from early position. The risk of being called or raised by the deeper stacks behind me or the short stack in the big blind is not worth the low probability of winning the blinds or hitting the flop hard. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I’m UTG+1 with Qc7s — a weak offsuit queen from early position in a 5-handed game. Opening or limping here often means facing aggression from the button/SB/BB and playing out of position; the button and SB have shown frequent raises and the BB (Elon) is shortish (~24 BB) and could shove or call a raise, putting me in a tough spot with a marginal hand. Calling 100 to limp-in is also unattractive since it invites multiway action and postflop play OOP. With deep effective stacks I can wait for a much stronger spot to open-raise. Fold and conserve chips for better position/hands.

I am on the button with T2o, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While there is an opportunity to steal the blinds, the Big Blind (Elon) is relatively short-stacked at 24.5 BBs and may be looking for a spot to shove or defend aggressively. Claude in the Small Blind has also shown a willingness to play. Risking chips on a pure steal with zero post-flop equity or playability is unnecessary at this stage of the tournament. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

Holding 9h4c in the small blind with everyone folding to me. This is heads up against the big blind (Elon with 2450). 94o is a very weak hand with poor playability. Even though it's only 50 to complete, this hand plays poorly post-flop. Elon is short-stacked and might defend or shove over a raise. I'll fold and save the 50 chips rather than play a marginal spot with a trash hand.

UTG with 2h3h suited is a very weak, speculative hand and I will be out of position for the entire hand. The pot is only the blinds (150) and calling 100 to limp in invites raises and multiway pots where 2-3s has poor equity and is frequently dominated. There is also a short stack (Elon) on my left who can shove over a limp, forcing uncomfortable all-in decisions with a marginal hand. With a healthy stack (16975) I should conserve chips for better opening spots (pocket pairs, suited broadways, or stronger connectors) and avoid playing OOP speculative hands from UTG. Fold and wait for a higher EV spot.

I am in UTG+1 with Ten-Six offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, especially in a five-handed game. My position is early, and there are three players behind me to act, including a large stack on the button. There is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand, as it lacks both high-card value and connectivity. I will fold to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

7c3c on the button is a very weak hand. While I have position and could attempt a steal, 73s plays poorly post-flop even when suited. With 16 hands left and a healthy stack of 18425, no need to get fancy with marginal holdings. Folding and waiting for better spots.

Folded to me in the small blind with 9h7s, a marginal offsuit hand but playable for a steal given my 25BB stack late in the tournament (hand 134/150). The pot is 150, and I only need to risk an additional 150 to raise to 200 total, potentially winning 150 uncontested. Pro in BB has 67BB but may fold weak hands to a raise. 97o has straight potential and fold equity; if called, I can play post-flop selectively. Raising maintains aggression and builds stack without needing premium hands.

I am in the Big Blind with As3s facing a min-raise from the Small Blind (Elon). Elon has a relatively short stack (24 BBs). In a Blind vs Blind scenario, ranges are wide. A3s is a very strong hand to defend with given the 3:1 pot odds and position. I have excellent equity against a wide stealing range and the hand plays well post-flop. A 3-bet is an option, but calling keeps the pot manageable and keeps Elon's wide range in play while utilizing my positional advantage.

Flop board Kh Ad 5h gives me a weak flush draw with 9h7s (9 outs to the nut flush if Ah doesn't come). As the pre-flop raiser from the SB, I have fold equity and position advantage post-flop doesn't matter first in. Pro's BB calling range is wide (pairs, suited connectors, Ax, Kx, draws). C-betting half pot continues my aggression, represents AK/Ax/sets, and can win the pot immediately often. With 24BB stack late in tournament (134/150), protecting equity and building stack by semi-bluffing in positionally strong spot. If called, ~20BB left behind pot for turn shove if improves or as bluff. Check-fold too passive given initiative.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (Aces, 3 kicker) on a Kh-Ad-5h board in a Blind vs Blind encounter. The opponent (Small Blind) bets half-pot (200 into 400). Given the wide ranges in BvB play, my hand is well ahead of his bluffs (flush draws, straight draws) and weaker value hands (Kx), though behind better Aces. Raising would likely only get called by better hands or force out bluffs that I want to keep in. Calling allows me to use my position to navigate future streets and keeps the pot manageable relative to my opponent's effective stack of 22 BBs. The price is excellent (getting 3:1 on a call).

Turn 4c doesn't improve my hand; I still have a non-nut flush draw (10 hearts left, ~22% equity raw) but no pair or straight draw. The board is now very coordinated with possible straights (6x now made), club flush draw possible, and Pro's range after calling preflop raise and flop c-bet includes strong hands like Ax, Kx, sets (AA, KK, 55), 65s-type straights, or stubborn draws. Continuing aggression with a bet risks getting called or raised by better hands, and my short stack (22BB effective remaining ~18BB after previous bets) doesn't have great implied odds for the flush. Shoving ~1800 into 800 pot requires ~31% equity to break even, but vs Pro's capped strong range, equity is likely 20-25% with reverse implied. Checking preserves stack, takes a free card if Pro checks back (possible with medium strength), or allows a disciplined fold to a bet (Pro likely values with made hands). Late tournament (134/150), 5-handed, better to wait for better spots than bluff into likely strength. Check-fold or check-call small if any.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (Aces, 3 kicker) and a gutshot wheel draw (needs a 2). The board is Kh-Ad-5h-4c. My opponent, the Small Blind, bet the flop but has now checked the turn. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, ranges are wide. His check likely indicates a marginal hand like a King (Kx), a draw, or a give-up, though a trap is possible. Betting here with a weak Ace risks isolating myself against better Aces or getting check-raised, forcing a fold. Checking back controls the pot size, denies a check-raise opportunity, and allows me to realize my equity comfortably. I can re-evaluate on the river, either calling a bet to catch bluffs or value betting if checked to again. Given the medium stack depth of the opponent (SPR ~2.25), pot control is preferred.

River 10h completes obvious heart flushes (board now has K♥, 5♥, 10♥), adding significant value hands and draws to Pro's calling range from BB (Ax♥, Kx♥, suited connectors with hearts). My 9h7s is complete air: no pair, no flush (only one heart), no straight. Preflop SB raise and flop c-bet represented strength (AK/Ax/sets), but turn check signaled possible missed draw or medium strength. Shoving 2200 into 800 pot (~2.75x pot overbet) or betting requires massive fold equity, but Pro's range after calling flop c-bet likely contains Ax, Kx, pairs, and now many flushes; unlikely to fold showdown value or improved hands late in tournament. My equity vs that range is <20%, and reverse implied odds if called. With 22BB stack (hand 134/150), preserving for better spots (e.g., premium hands or better bluffs) is optimal rather than risking stack with zero showdown value. Check allows free showdown if Pro checks weak/medium (possible), or disciplined fold to value bet. No bluff value as first to act after checking turn.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (Aces, 3 kicker) on a board of Kh-Ad-5h-4c-Th. The river completes a flush draw (hearts) and puts 3-to-a-broadway straight on the board (Q-J would make a straight). My opponent, the Small Blind, showed aggression on the flop but checked the turn and now checks the river. His range is likely capped, containing second pairs (Kx), missed draws, or weak Aces. While I likely have the best hand against Kx or underpairs, betting is thin value because he may fold those hands on this scary river (flush and straight possibilities). Furthermore, if I am called, it is often by a better Ace (splitting with A2 is the best case for other Ax) or a flush/two-pair that checked to trap. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity and win against his bluffs or weaker pairs without risking being check-raised or value-owning myself against a better Ace.

I am in the UTG position with 6c 4h, which is a very weak hand. In a 5-handed game, this hand has almost no equity and is not worth playing from the earliest position. With a healthy stack and only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips on a sub-optimal hand or attempt a high-variance bluff from this position.

K7o is a weak hand from UTG+1. Even with a healthy stack of 18,425 and 16 hands remaining, this is a fold. K7o doesn't play well postflop and I'd be out of position against most remaining players. No need to get involved with marginal holdings from early position.

Preflop on the button with J9o after two folds ahead. Excellent stealing position with fold equity against the blinds.
