J9o has good playability postflop with straight potential and decent high card value. My stack is 22BB, so accumulating chips is crucial late in the tournament (hand 135/150). Blinds are deep but will fold often to a standard raise. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) balances bluffs and value, applies pressure, and builds the pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs 7c, facing a 3x raise (300) from Elon on the Button. Elon is short-stacked with about 19 big blinds. Q7 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against a larger-than-minimum raise which reduces my pot odds. Additionally, the Big Blind (Minni) has a massive stack and could easily squeeze, forcing me to fold even if I called. Given the poor playability of the hand, the positional disadvantage, and the stack dynamics, folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack of roughly 72 big blinds.

Elon (button) opened to 3bb (300) but is a short ~19bb stack — his raise sizing is a standard steal/shove range. I’m in the big blind with Jc4c: it’s suited but very low-value, easily dominated by the button’s range and plays poorly out of position. Calling 200 into a 450 pot gives marginal pot odds but poor post‑flop playability and high likelihood of folding to pressure or being crushed when called. A min‑raise to 500 is too thin a 3‑bet with this hand (and could induce a shove or create a difficult spot). With a big stack I should preserve chips for better, higher‑EV spots. Fold.
AQo is a premium hand in UTG. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB). This is a strong hand that plays well heads-up or multiway, and I have position to act first in a strong spot.

Preflop, UTG+1 facing an UTG raise to 250 into a pot of 400 (to call 250). My hand is Ad2d, a weak suited ace with poor playability and dominated often by better aces in Claude's strong UTG range. My stack is 2350 (~23BB), short but not desperate for ICM purposes this late (hand 136/150). Calling offers poor implied odds due to reverse domination risks and multiway potential. 3-betting A2s is suboptimal as it's not a strong enough polarizing hand and leaves me committed with marginal equity. Folding preserves stack for better spots against shorter effective stacks.

I am on the Button with 9c 6h, a weak unsuited hand. The player in UTG (Claude) has opened with a raise to 250. Given the positional disadvantage of the opener (suggesting a stronger range) and the poor quality of my hand, I have no incentive to enter this pot. Calling or raising would be -EV. The correct play is to fold.

Claude opened UTG to 250 in a 5-handed game. In the small blind with K♥T♠ I'm out of position and facing a reasonably strong opener — KTo is playable but vulnerable to domination vs UTG. I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by 3-betting (BB will likely defend and Claude can 4-bet), nor is folding necessary. Calling 200 keeps the pot controlled, allows me to realize equity and see a flop where I can proceed based on texture: c‑bet/raise/fold depending on the board and opponents. I'll fold to a large 3‑bet preflop and play cautiously OOP postflop (look to fold to heavy aggression if I miss).

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Claude opened from UTG with a raise to 250, and Minni called from the Small Blind. I am being offered pot odds of 5-to-1 (150 to call into a 600 pot) to close the action. While T7o is a sub-optimal hand, it is mathematically defensible given the price and the fact that I am closing the pre-flop action. My stack is healthy enough to afford this speculative call and see if the flop connects with my range.

Flop: 9♠ 3♣ 8♣. I’m OOP in the small blind with K♥T♠. I’ve missed — no pair, just two overcards (K and T), and I have no club. This is a coordinated flop that hits an UTG open-range and the BB caller well (clubs, 9x/8x, sets, etc.). Pot control is priority: I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with only overcards and weak backdoor outs. Check to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and see how the preflop raiser and the BB proceed. Plan: fold to significant c-bets or raises; call a small probe (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 250) as a bluff-catcher since I have ~6 clean pair-outs (~24% to hit by river) and decent showdown equity vs some bluffs; if checked through I’ll take a free turn and only start betting if I pair (K or T) or pick up a very clear draw.

On the flop of 9s-3c-8c, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and an overcard (Ten). Minni has checked. Claude, the original pre-flop raiser, is next to act behind me. Given the board texture which includes a flush draw and several straight possibilities, checking is the standard and most prudent play here from the Big Blind to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts before committing any chips with only a draw.

I have AQo on a 9s 3c 8c board - complete miss with just two overcards. However, both opponents checked to me as the preflop aggressor. This is a standard c-bet spot. The board is relatively dry with a club draw possible. A small c-bet around 40-50% pot should take this down often. I'll bet 350.

Claude (UTG) leads with a small c‑bet of 350 into 1100 on a 9♠ 3♣ 8♣ flop. I’m OOP with K♥ T♠ and have missed — only two overcards and no club. I have ~6 clean outs (three Ks, three Ts) ≈24% to hit by river, and the board has two clubs so draws/flushes are plausible. Calling 350 is cheap (~2% of my stack and ~30–35% of the pot) to realize equity and keep the pot controlled out of position. Folding is too nitpicky for the price; raising OOP with only overcards and no strong blockers is thin and likely to get punished. Plan: call to see a turn, fold to large turn/raise unless I improve (pair or a clear draw), and use position on later streets to proceed selectively.

On the flop of 9s-3c-8c, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Jack) and one overcard (the Ten). Claude has made a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 750, and Minni has already called that bet. This makes the total pot 1450, and it costs me 350 to call. I am getting 4.14-to-1 pot odds. My hand has some equity and I am closing the action on this street. Given the price and the fact that a Jack would likely give me the best hand, calling to see a turn card is a mathematically sound decision.

Check. I’m out of position with K♥T♠ and missed the flop; I called Claude’s small c‑bet on the flop to keep the pot under control and realize equity. The turn (6♦) gives me a one‑card straight draw (a 7 on the river makes 6‑7‑8‑9‑T) plus the original pair‑outs (3 kings + 3 tens) — roughly 10 outs (~22% to improve on the river) — but many of those outs are “dirty” (hitting K/T can still be behind a 9/8 or two‑pair, and a club on the river can give an opponent a flush). Claude (UTG raiser) and Flash (BB) are both in and have shown lines consistent with 9/8x, clubs, or medium pairs/flush draws, so bluffing the turn or bloating the pot OOP is unlikely to succeed frequently and will get called. With two opponents and healthy stacks, pot control is the priority. I’ll check and take my free river to try to improve. If someone now leads into me on the turn I’ll fold to meaningful pressure (only consider calling very small probes that give correct pot odds against my ~22% equity); if checked through I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and value‑bet only when I actually pair or make the straight, otherwise fold to aggression.

On a board of 9s-3c-8c-6d, my T7o has successfully completed a 10-high straight (6-7-8-9-T). This is currently the absolute nuts, as no higher straight is possible with only two hole cards using the cards present on the board. Minni has checked from the Small Blind. I am in the 'sandwich' position with Claude, the original aggressor, still to act behind me. By checking, I am playing my monster hand deceptively, hoping to induce a second barrel from Claude. If Claude bets and Minni calls or folds, I can then decide whether to check-raise to build the pot immediately or continue slow-playing to the river, depending on the bet size and Minni's action. This maximizes the potential to extract value from Claude's overpairs or bluffs.

Two players called my flop c-bet on a 9-8-3-6 board with clubs. The 6d doesn't help me - I still have just ace-high. With two callers showing interest, they likely have pairs, draws, or both. Continuing to barrel without a made hand is burning money. I'll check behind for pot control and see a free river - my ace-high might even have showdown value if they're on draws.

I turned top pair (K♥) on the river after checking through. Claude (UTG) opened and led a small flop c‑bet that both Flash and I called, then everyone checked the turn — lines that look like medium pairs (9x/8x/6x), missed club draws, or occasional air rather than a very strong concealed hand. My hand (K T) now beats most of those holdings but loses to sets/two‑pair and Kx with a better kicker. With two opponents I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position, but I should still extract thin value from worse one‑pair hands that will often call. A modest value bet of about one‑third of the pot (~600 into 1800) balances getting called by worse while keeping my downside limited. If I face a substantial raise I’ll concede the pot (those raises will mostly be from sets/two‑pair or better Kx), but if I get called I expect to be ahead often and will take it to showdown.

On the river, the board is 9s-3c-8c-6d-Kd. I hold 10d-7h, which made a 10-high straight (6-7-8-9-T) on the turn. While the King on the river technically makes a higher straight (QJ for a Queen-high straight) possible, my hand remains extremely strong. Minni has led out for a small bet of 600 into an 1800 pot. This 'blocker' or value bet often indicates a hand like top pair (Kings), two pair, or a set that didn't want to see a large bet. Since I slow-played the nuts on the turn by checking, I must now raise for value. I'll raise to 1800, which is a 3x raise of her bet, to extract maximum value from her likely two-pair or set holdings while still being able to evaluate if she or Claude (who is still to act) pushes back with the rare QJ.

I have AQo and completely whiffed except the King hit the river. But I have no pair - just Ace-high. Minni led 600 and Flash raised to 1800. With two players showing significant strength on a board where straights are possible (T7 makes a straight), flush draws were present, and I have zero showdown value beyond Ace-high, this is an easy fold. I'm not calling 1800 with nothing.

I bet a small one‑third pot on the river to get thin value from worse one‑pair hands. Flash (BB) — who check‑called the flop and turn — now raises my 600 to 1800. Calling 1,200 more into a 4,200 pot requires ~22% equity. Given his line and the board texture, his raising range is weighted toward Kx (that just improved), sets/two‑pair, and only a limited number of bluffs. I’m out of position, have a marginal kicker, and don’t have the pot‑odds or blockers to justify calling a substantial 3x raise; this sizing is the kind of action I planned to concede to. Fold and preserve the bulk of my stack for better spots.

UTG position with 67o (offsuit, mediocre connectivity). This is a standard fold in early position, especially in a 5-handed game. Hand has poor playability post-flop, dominated often, and requires premium strength from UTG to open. My stack is 23.5BB, not desperate; better to wait for stronger hands like pairs, suited connectors in position, or high cards. Opponents have deep stacks, so speculative hands from EP are -EV. Folding preserves chips for superior spots near tournament end.

Current situation: 5-handed, Hand 137/150. I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with 4d5d. The player UTG folded. My stack is approx 72BB. The three players acting after me (Button, SB, BB) all have massive stacks (150BB+) covering me significantly. While 54s is a candidate for opening from the Cutoff in a standard game, the stack dynamics here discourage it. The deep-stacked opponents can easily apply pressure with 3-bets or floating in position, making it difficult to realize equity with a speculative hand. To maximize stack size at this stage, avoiding marginal spots against chip leaders is prudent. I will fold and preserve chips for better positional spots or stronger holdings.

I have K♣Q♠ on the button — a strong, playable hand with great positional advantage against only the blinds. Standard +EV play is to open-raise and seize initiative: it picks up the blinds a fair amount of the time and lets me play postflop in position when called. I’ll avoid the minimum (200) and use a standard 3xBB sizing to 300 total — big enough to get fold equity and discourage limp/call fields, but not so large that I overcommit with KQo deep against potential 3-bets. If Claude (BB) 3-bets big I’ll generally fold without further reads; if I get one caller I’ll c-bet favorable boards and pot-control on dry, A‑high or heavily connected boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a strong Broadway hand that plays well against a button opening range. Minni, on the button, has been quite active throughout the tournament. While 3-betting is an option to seize the initiative, calling the 250 chips (to match the 300 raise) provides excellent pot odds and keeps the pot manageable while playing out of position. Given that we are approaching the end of the tournament (Hand 137 of 150) and I have a healthy stack, I will take the lower-variance route of calling to see a flop.

Holding 9-2 offsuit in the BB facing a raise and a call. This is a trash hand with no playability. Even getting 3.5:1 pot odds (200 to win 700), 92o is too weak to defend - it's dominated by virtually any hand and will rarely make a strong holding. Easy fold.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw with Queen-Jack on a King-Ten-Six rainbow board. While I am out of position against Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, this is a strong draw that I want to play cautiously but profitably. Checking to the raiser is the standard play here. If Minni bets, I will likely call to see the turn, as the pot odds for an eight-out draw are usually favorable, and I have potential implied odds if I hit my straight on the next street. Leading into the pre-flop raiser on a King-high board is generally not advisable as it hits their range quite well.

I have top pair with the best kicker (K♣Q♠) on a dry, rainbow K♦‑T♣‑6♠ board. I was the preflop aggressor (raised to 300) and the SB just called, so c‑betting preserves initiative and extracts value from worse Kx, Tx, and small pairs in his wide calling range. The board is thin on draws, so a half‑to‑two‑thirds pot bet is good — it charges the hands that can continue and folds out pure air. Pot is 700; I’ll bet 400 (~57% pot): big enough to get value and deny equity, but not so large that I overcommit if I face a strong check‑raise. Plan: if called I’ll reassess on the turn and continue for value on safe cards; if I face a large, polarized check‑raise I’ll give credit and fold more often, but I’ll call or raise smaller, non‑polarized pressure depending on sizing.

On this King-high board, I have flopped an open-ended straight draw with my Queen-Jack. Minni, who raised pre-flop on the button, has made a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 1100. The pot odds are approximately 2.75-to-1, meaning I need about 26.6% equity to call profitably. With 8 outs to a straight (4 Aces, 4 Nines), I have about a 16% chance of hitting on the turn and roughly a 32% chance by the river. Given the potential implied odds if I complete my straight—as it may be well-disguised and Minni's range contains many hands that will pay off a bet—calling is the most mathematically sound and strategically sensible play. I will call the 400 to see the turn.

On the turn, the board has become King-Ten-Six-Five rainbow (with two clubs). I still have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs). Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here. I want to see how Minni reacts to this blank turn card. If Minni checks behind, I get a free look at the river to hit my straight. If Minni bets, I will re-evaluate based on the bet sizing, but given my equity and the potential for implied odds should I hit an Ace or a Nine, I am likely to call a standard-sized bet.

I have top pair with the best kicker (K♣Q♠) on a very dry K♦‑T♣‑6♠‑5♣ board. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop; SB called, so his continuing range is wide (T‑x, small/medium pocket pairs, Kx, broadways). The turn 5♣ is essentially a brick — it doesn’t complete any realistic draws and, with one card to come, a club flush for me is impossible (I already hold one club and there are only two clubs on board), so I’m still usually ahead. This is a clear spot to keep extracting value rather than checking and giving free cards. I want a sizing that gets called by worse Kx, Tx and medium pairs but isn’t so large I only get action from hands that beat me. With a 1,500 pot I’ll make a ~50% pot value bet to charge weaker pairs and tens while keeping worse hands in. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed with thin value on blanks or exercise pot control on scary rivers (A or paired rivers); if faced with a big, polarized check‑raise I’ll give credit and fold KQ most of the time.

On the turn, the board is King-Ten-Six-Five rainbow. I have an open-ended straight draw with Queen-Jack, giving me 8 outs (Aces and Nines). Minni has bet 750 into a pot of 1500, offering me pot odds of 3-to-1 (meaning I need 25% equity to call). While my mathematical probability of hitting the straight on the river is approximately 17.4%, the implied odds in this situation are significant. Minni has been aggressive throughout the tournament, and if I hit my straight on the river, there is a high likelihood of extracting a much larger bet, especially if she holds a King or a Ten. Given that I have a substantial stack of over 16,000 chips, calling 750 to potentially win a much larger pot is a standard and profitable play for a professional player. I will call the bet and look to hit my draw on the river.

On the river, the board completes as King-Ten-Six-Five-Eight rainbow with three clubs. Unfortunately, my open-ended straight draw (Queen-Jack) failed to materialize, leaving me with only Queen-high. Minni has been the aggressor since pre-flop, betting both the flop and the turn. In this position, I have zero showdown value, but I have also not shown any strength that would make a river bluff particularly credible after check-calling two streets. The most logical and professional play is to check. If Minni bets, I will fold, as my hand cannot beat any part of her value range and she is unlikely to be bluffing with a hand worse than Queen-high given the action.

I have top pair with the best kicker (K♣Q♠) but the river (8♣) completes a three‑club board and also gives some hands a paired 8. Flash called my 400 flop c‑bet and 750 turn bet, so his continuing range contains Kx, T‑x, medium pockets and also suited club combos that now have a flush. If I bet I’d get value from some worse K/T/pair hands, but I’ll also get called (or check‑raised) by rivered flushes and 8x that beat me — and the break‑even call rate on a thin bet (e.g. 1k into 3k) is only ~25%, which is risky given the uncertainty. He checked the river (showing either weakness or a trap), so the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check back and take my showdown — preserve chips and avoid getting value‑checked by hands that now beat me. Action: check.

I am in UTG position with a very weak hand, 2h 8s (Deuce of Hearts and Eight of Spades). With 71 big blinds, I have a comfortable stack and no reason to play such a poor starting hand, especially from early position where I would be out of position against most callers. This is a standard fold.

UTG+1 with J♠9♥ (offsuit). In a 5‑handed game with deep stacks (≈172 BB) I want to take the initiative rather than limp and play a multiway pot out of position. Js9o is marginal but has good postflop playability and is a reasonable open in this spot. I'll open‑raise to 300 (about 3x the BB) — big enough to deny cheap flops and to match table raising patterns, but still keeps the pot manageable. If I face a large 3‑bet from a deep stack I'll often fold; if the short BB shoves (≈22 BB) I'll seriously consider calling because his shove range will be wide and my hand has decent equity. Postflop I'll cbet appropriate textures and re-evaluate based on opponents' responses.

I have 6c-2d on the button. Minni, an active player, has raised to 300 from early position (UTG+1). With the short-stacked Elon in the Big Blind likely looking for an opportunity to commit his remaining chips, and my hand being one of the weakest possible starting hands, there is no reason to continue. Folding is the only viable play.

Holding Ac2c in the small blind facing a 300 raise from Minni. Suited aces have good playability with nut flush potential. Minni has been an active raiser throughout the tournament. With 250 to call into a 450 pot, I'm getting decent odds. A2s is a good speculative hand to defend rather than 3-bet or fold.

I'm in the Big Blind with 8d5d, a speculative suited connector but quite weak. Minni raised from early position (UTG+1), indicating a strong range, and Claude called from the Small Blind, adding another strong hand to the mix. The pot is 700, and it costs 200 more to call (pot odds about 3.5:1), but post-flop I'll be out of position against two players with strong ranges. My stack is 2250 (~22BB), so while not short, I can't afford speculative calls OOP in a late-stage tournament hand (#138/150). 85s plays poorly multiway without position and has poor equity against typical calling/raising ranges here. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

Flopped the nut flush draw with Ac2c on Tc Qd 3c. I'm out of position against the preflop raiser (Minni). Checking makes sense here - if Minni c-bets, I can call with my strong 9-out draw. If they check back, I get a free card to the turn. No need to lead into the aggressor when I have a drawing hand that benefits from keeping the pot controlled while building implied odds.

Flop: T♣ Q♦ 3♣. I hold J♠9♥ — I have an open‑ended straight draw (need an 8 or K; ~8 outs, ≈32% to river) and I'm in position vs Claude (the SB) who called preflop and checked the flop. Claude's call preflop is fairly wide (Qx, Tx, pocket pairs, broadways, suited connectors), so a continuation bet here functions well as a semi‑bluff: it can take down many unpaired hands and builds a pot I can win when I hit. I don't have a club so no flush draw, so I want a bet size that gets some fold equity but keeps the pot manageable if called. I’ll make a standard half‑pot c‑bet (350). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I still have good equity to improve and will re‑evaluate on the turn — continue when I hit (K/8 or pair), and otherwise proceed cautiously to avoid bloating the pot against strong made hands.

Nut flush draw with Ac2c on a Tc Qd 3c board. Getting 3:1 odds (350 to win 1050) with 9 outs to the nuts. I have ~35% equity to make my flush by river, so calling is mathematically correct. Implied odds are excellent if I hit - I can stack Minni with the nut flush. No need to raise as a semi-bluff here; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize my equity.

Turn 9s doesn't complete my flush. I still have 9 outs to the nut flush. Checking is standard here - if Minni bets again, I can evaluate pot odds to call. If they check back, I get a free river card. No reason to lead out with a pure draw when I can control pot size and see their action first.

Turn: T♣ Q♦ 3♣ 9♠ — I hold J♠9♥, so I just improved to second pair (nines) with a decent jack kicker. Claude called preflop and called my 350 c-bet on a dry-ish T Q 3 with two clubs on board, so his range is wide (Qx, Tx, medium pocket pairs, club combos, some broadways). He checked the turn, showing weakness or a draw. Betting here protects my hand against club draws (if he has two clubs he has ~9 outs) and extracts thin value from worse pairs and draws that will call a reasonable-sized bet. I’ll make a size around half the pot (~700) — big enough to charge draws and get value from smaller pairs, but not so large that I commit unnecessarily against hands that beat me (Qx/TX). If Claude raises big I’ll assume a very strong hand and will fold or re-evaluate based on sizing; if he calls I’ll re-assess river.

Nut flush draw with Ac2c. Pot is 2100, facing 700 bet - getting 3:1 odds (need 25% equity). I have 9 outs = ~19% to hit on river. Pure pot odds slightly against a call, but with strong implied odds when I hit the nuts against an aggressive Minni who has been betting three streets, this is a clear call. If I hit, I can likely extract significant value on the river.

The river Qc completed my nut flush! I have Ac2c with Tc, 3c, Qc on board - that's the absolute nuts. Minni has been the aggressor throughout this hand and showed strength on all streets. With the nut flush, I need to bet for value. If Minni has Qx for trips, a straight (KJ), or even a smaller flush, they'll likely call. Betting around 60% pot (~1700) balances getting called by strong hands while extracting maximum value.

River decision: board Tc Qd 3c 9s Qc, I hold Js9h (second pair, J kicker). Pot is 4500; Claude (SB) just made a small river bet of 1700. He called preflop, check‑called my 350 c‑bet on T♣Q♦3♣ and then check‑called my 700 on the 9♠ turn — a line consistent with medium strength (Qx, single T, medium pocket pairs) or clubs draws. The river Q♣ completes possible club flushes and also gives trips/full houses to Qx or to sets (TT, 33, 99) that were in his calling range. That said, his river sizing is small (≈<½ pot), which is often used as a thin value or blocker bet rather than a polarizing shove — it will get called by weaker hands (and sometimes induce bluffs).

Pot odds and equity: calling 1700 to win about 6200 (4500+1700) requires ≈27% equity to breakeven. I have no improvement outs on the river, so this is a pure showdown call — I need to believe Claude is bluffing or value‑betting thin at least ~27% of the time. Given his passive calls on earlier streets and the small river sizing, there is a reasonable chance he is betting thin or trying to buy the pot rather than always holding a nut/near‑nut hand.

Risk management: 1700 is only ~10% of my 15,875 stack — a reasonably small cost to realize showdown with a hand that still beats missed draws and some weaker holdings. Raising would be irresponsible (I’m unlikely to fold out better hands and will face stronger ranges if re‑raised).

Action: call 1700 to get to showdown. If he then raises to the minimum (3400) I will strongly lean to folding to a polarized raise.

Holding 5♥4♠ UTG in a 5‑handed game is a very weak, unplayable hand from first to act. It has poor equity and post‑flop playability, and opening it will often face stronger ranges from players behind (Flash/Claude) or a shove from the short SB (Elon, ~22bb). Limping or a small open invites multiway action that destroys fold equity and reduces realized equity. With a healthy stack (14175) and many better spots to pick, the +EV decision is to fold and conserve chips for higher‑value situations.

I am holding Jack-Ten suited in UTG+1 in a five-handed game. This is a strong starting hand that plays well post-flop and is well within my opening range for this position. Minni has already folded from UTG. My standard opening raise in this tournament has been 3x the big blind, which is 300 chips. I should be aware that Elon is quite short-stacked with only 2,200 chips and may look to shove over my open, but JTs is strong enough to consider calling a short-stack shove depending on the odds, or at least it's worth the risk of opening.
6h 2c is a very weak hand - one of the worst in poker. Facing a 3x raise from Flash, I have no business calling or 3-betting with this garbage even from the button. Easy fold.

Facing a raise to 300 from Flash in UTG+1 (early position) with KJo offsuit from SB. My stack is 2200 (~22BB), making me somewhat short but not desperate. Pot odds are favorable (250 to call into 450 pot, ~26% equity needed), but post-flop OOP with offsuit broadway is tough. Flash's EP raise range is strong (likely JJ+, AK, AQ+), against which KJo has poor equity (~25-30%, often dominated by AK/AQ). No strong blockers or suited connectivity for 3-bet/fold or shove. BB still to act could 4-bet or call, reducing fold equity. Standard play is to fold marginal offsuit broadway OOP vs EP raise to preserve stack late in tournament (hand 139/150). Avoid speculative OOP spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 6c, facing a 3x raise (300) from Flash in UTG+1. My hand is a weak unsuited one-gapper (64o). The pot odds are approximately 30.7% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650), but realizing equity with this hand out of position against a strong early-position opening range is very difficult. Defending here would likely be a losing play in the long run. With a healthy stack of over 70 BBs and nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 139/150), there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot. The standard and correct play is to fold.

In a 5-handed game, Jack-Ten offsuit is a playable hand from UTG, though it's on the lower end of a standard opening range. Given my current stack size and the stage of the tournament (Hand 140 of 150), I want to maintain an aggressive posture to pick up blinds or control the action. I will open with my standard 3x raise to 300, while remaining cautious of potential 3-bets from the larger stack (Claude) or a shove from the short stack (Elon).
AQo is a premium hand facing a UTG open. Flash has been active and I have position on them. Standard 3-bet for value here - sizing around 3x the open to 850.

Hand #140 on the button with 6s9h (offsuit, speculative but poor). Facing UTG raise to 300 by Flash and 3-bet to 850 by Claude (strong range: QQ+, AKo etc.). Pot 1300, calling 850 gives ~1.7:1 odds but equity ~25-30% OOP against tight range, potential multiway pot. My 2200 stack (22BB) is short; committing 40% to a marginal hand risks stack unnecessarily late in tournament (140/150 hands, 5 players). Position helps but not enough vs early aggression. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I hold As5s in the Small Blind. The action began with an UTG raise to 300 from Flash, followed by a 3-bet to 850 from Claude in UTG+1. This configuration (UTG vs UTG+1) typically indicates very strong ranges from both opponents. Cold calling from the Small Blind would leave me Out of Position against two strong ranges with a hand that is easily dominated (by AK, AQ, AJs, stronger flushes) and would cost more than 10% of my stack. A cold 4-bet bluff is too risky given my stack size (7025) and the likelihood that Claude has a premium hand. The most prudent play is to fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

Flash opened UTG to 300 and Claude 3-bet to 850. I’m in the big blind with 8♥4♦ — a poor, unplayable holding out of position with almost no fold equity or postflop playability. Calling 750 requires roughly 36% equity to break even (750/(1300+750)) and 8-4o doesn’t have that vs a 3-bet/raisers’ ranges, especially with Flash still to act. A shove/4-bet as a bluff is too risky against two deep, competent stacks. Preserve my 14k+ stack for better spots — fold.

I opened UTG with JTo, which is a marginal hand in this position. Claude, the chip leader sitting to my immediate left, has 3-bet me to 850. Calling this raise out of position with JTo is risky, as I'll likely be behind his range and will have difficulty navigating post-flop without the lead. Given we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 140 of 150) and I have a healthy stack, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than bleed chips in a sub-optimal spot against the leader.

52 suited is way too weak to open from UTG, even with the suited diamond potential. I have a healthy stack at 21,325 and only 10 hands remaining. No need to play speculative trash from early position.

Hand 141/150, short stack at 22BB (2200). Position UTG+1 (early), holding J7o offsuit - weak hand with poor playability, dominated by better jacks, low equity multiway. No action ahead except blinds, but folding preserves stack for better spots like premium hands or late position steals. Opponents deep-stacked; shoving risks too much equity loss if called by better ranges. Standard fold to maximize EV long-term.

I am on the Button with Pocket Eights (8h 8c), a strong starting hand in a 5-handed game. With action folded to me, this is a clear spot to open-raise for value and to potentially steal the blinds. My stack is healthy (70 BBs), allowing me to navigate post-flop comfortably in position if called. A standard open size of 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the blinds while keeping the pot manageable.

I have K♥9♥ in the small blind facing a button open to 250. K9s is a defendable hand — suited, good playability, and can make top pair or strong backdoor draws. Calling the extra 200 (total commitment 250) keeps the pot controlled and lets me see a flop without bloating the pot out of position. A 3-bet is an option but is too high-variance here: the big blind (Flash) is behind and could squeeze or call, and I don't want to play a big pot out of position with a marginal hand. Postflop plan: proceed cautiously — continue on king/heart/favorable boards, fold to heavy action on dry boards when I miss. Therefore I will call the raise.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q7o. Pro raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. I am being offered 4:1 pot odds to call (150 chips into a 600-chip pot) and I will be closing the action pre-flop. While Q7o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, the price is too good to pass up in a multi-way pot. I intend to see a flop and will proceed with caution if I don't improve significantly.

I hold K♥9♥ on 6c 9s 3s — top pair with a very good kicker. This is a multiway pot (button opened preflop, big blind called) so I want to both extract value from worse 9x/6x/3x hands and protect against spade draws. Betting a modest-sized amount (~300, ~40% pot) accomplishes that: it gets value from worse pairs and keeps the pot manageable OOP. If I face a big raise I'll fold to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position; I’ll call small/medium resistance and re-evaluate on the turn.

On a flop of 6c 9s 3s, I am holding Qh 7d, which is just Queen-high with no draws. Minni has led into the pot for 300 chips after calling from the Small Blind pre-flop. The original raiser, Pro, is still to act behind me. Given that I have no equity or backdoor potential on this board and am facing a lead into a multi-way field, calling or raising is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold Pocket Eights (8h 8c) on a 6-9-3 board with two spades. This gives me second pair (an overpair to the 6 and 3, but under the 9). The Small Blind (Minni) has led out (donk bet) for 300 into a pot of 750. This bet size is relatively small (40% pot) and can often indicate a draw, a middle pair like A6/76, or a weak top pair (T9/89) trying to prevent a check-back or set a price. My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet on this texture, as I am well ahead of flush draws and lower pairs. However, raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (9x or better) and fold out bluffs or weaker hands. Being in position, calling is the best line to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and Minni's subsequent action.

I have K♥9♥ and the turn Kc gives me two pair (Kings and Nines). Pro (the button) called my flop bet, so his range contains hands like single pairs (9x, 6x, 3x), pocket pairs, spade draws, and some overcards/floaters. Two-pair is a very strong hand here but vulnerable to sets/trips/full houses (33/66/99 are possible) and I’m out of position, so I want to both extract value and charge spade draws. The pot is 1,350 — a bet of roughly half the pot denies correct pot odds to spade draws: calling 700 to win a ~2,750 pot requires ~25.5% equity, while a turn-only spade draw has ~19.6% to hit the river. I’ll make a value/protection bet of 700 to get calls from worse made hands and fold out most semi-bluffs; if I face significant raise I’ll re-evaluate (large raises are usually sets/trips and should be treated cautiously).

On the flop, I called a lead with second pair (88 on 9-6-3). The turn brings a King, which is an overcard to my pair, downgrading my hand to third pair. The King also connects well with the Small Blind's defending range (Kx suited, K9, etc.) or gives them a good scare card to barrel a bluff. Minni's line of leading the flop and betting half-pot on the turn represents strength. Without a strong read or a draw, calling down with 88 here is losing too much EV against a range that likely includes top pair or better 9x. Preserving my 64 BB stack is the prudent play.

Pocket Jacks (JJ) is a premium hand, top of our range from UTG in 5-handed play. With 22BB stack late in the tournament (hand 142/150), we raise to build the pot, thin the field, and apply pressure. Standard sizing is 3x the BB (300 total), which is above min-raise (200) but not overcommitting our stack. Limping is too passive and invites multiway pots; folding is incorrect. Opponents have varying stacks but JJ plays well postflop. Aim to maximize EV by raising for value and fold equity.

I am holding 7c 5s in UTG+1. This is a very weak hand (7-high, one-gapper, offsuit). Elon has raised to 300 from UTG. Facing an early position raise with a trash hand, the only correct play is to fold immediately. I have no reason to get involved in this pot.

I’m on the button with A♥6♣ facing an UTG open to 300 from a 19bb short stack. With deep stacks behind (SB/BB) and position, this hand plays much better seeing a flop than as a light 3‑bet. 3‑betting risks isolating against deeper callers or facing a shove from the short and a cold 4‑bet from the big stacks; folding is too nitty for an Ace in position. I’ll call 300 to take advantage of my positional and blocker value: if the blinds fold I’ll have heads‑up position vs the short and can apply pressure on many flops; if they defend I’ll proceed cautiously postflop, barrel strong Ace flops and fold or check‑call on dry boards. If Elon shoves I’ll re‑evaluate but have decent showdown equity vs a short’s shove range.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 5h. Elon, who is the table's short stack with only 1,900 chips remaining, has raised to 300 from UTG. Minni has called on the button. While I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call, 85o is a very weak hand with poor playability, and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents. Given the healthy size of my stack and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 142/150), there is no strategic reason to defend with such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have T9s in the BB facing a raise to 300 from short-stacked Elon (1900 chips) with Minni cold-calling. I need 200 to call into a 750 pot, getting ~3.75:1 odds. T9 suited is a solid speculative hand that plays well multiway. With these pot odds and a connected suited hand, calling is the clear play. Could consider squeezing, but with Elon so short, he'd likely just shove if I 3-bet, and I'd rather see a cheap flop.

I flopped top pair with T9s on 5c Tc 7h. This is a solid hand but vulnerable to flush draws (two clubs on board) and straight draws. I should bet for value and protection. Betting around 60% pot (550-600) makes sense to charge draws and get value from worse pairs or draws. Elon is very short-stacked and may shove with various holdings.

Pocket Jacks remain very strong as an overpair on a 5c-Tc-7h flop, which is relatively dry with possible straight draws (e.g., 89) and a weak club flush draw, but no strong made hands likely from Claude's BB calling range facing my UTG raise. His donk-bet of 550 into a ~950 pot (now showing 1500 with bet) is often polarized: draws, floats, or weak pairs (e.g., 88, 99, A-high). With only 1900 behind (~19BB late in tournament), pot odds to call are decent (~3:1), but calling invites Minni to realize equity multiway and keeps pot small. Raising isolates, protects against draws improving, and extracts value from worse (e.g., 98s, JTs, small pairs). Shoving all-in maximizes fold equity against Claude's likely weak range while getting called by overpairs/set mining that beats worse. Minni's button call is wide but unlikely to snap-call a jam. Committing stack with premium hand late in tournament to build stack.

Fold. On the flop (5♣ T♣ 7♥) I have A♥6♣ — only Ace‑high with a very weak backdoor plan. Claude c‑bet 550 and Elon shoved all‑in for 1,900. Pot is 3,400 and I must call 1,900, so I need ~1,900/(3,400+1,900) ≈ 35.8% equity to break even. My realistic outs are 3 Aces + 3 Sixes = 6 outs (~24% to pair by river) plus a tiny ~3% chance for running clubs — total well under the ~36% needed. Elon opened UTG (short stack) and his shove over a c‑bet is weighted toward made hands (T, sets, two‑pair, strong Ax), and Claude can still be involved — a multiway call would only make my equity worse. I have a healthy stack and should preserve chips rather than make a marginal call, so fold here.

I have top pair (Ts9s on 5c Tc 7h) and Elon has shoved his remaining stack (1900 total). Pot is 3400 and I need 1350 to call, getting ~3.5:1 odds (need ~28% equity). Against Elon's short-stack shoving range here (sets, overpairs, top pair better kicker, two pair, flush draws, straight draws), my top pair with decent kicker has sufficient equity at these pot odds. He could have better (TJ, TQ, overpairs, sets) but also could have flush draws, 89 for OESD, or weaker tens. Easy call given the math.

I am in UTG with a very weak hand, Jack-Seven offsuit. With four players left to act behind me, opening this hand is loose and unprofitable. My stack is healthy at ~65 BBs, so I have no pressure to make moves with marginal holdings. Standard play is to fold immediately.

Hand: A♣7♥ in UTG+1 with a deep 145bb stack in a 5-handed game. Limping is weak and invites multiway action; folding is too tight for an Ace that plays well postflop. I want initiative and fold equity, so I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300). That sizing pressures the blinds and keeps the pot manageable if Claude (SB) or short-stacked Elon (BB) defends — I’ll proceed cautiously vs a large 3‑bet (likely fold or fold-to-heavy 4-bet) and plan to C-bet favorable flops or realize equity heads-up.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours. Minni has opened from UTG+1 for 300. Pocket fours are a classic set-mining hand, and with 150 hands in the tournament nearly finished, I want to keep my stack healthy while looking for high-value opportunities. Calling here is standard to see a flop in position. If I hit a set, I have high implied odds; if not, I can easily fold to aggression on most boards. Raising against an early position open with a small pair is unnecessary risk.

K6o is a weak hand to play out of position multiway. Even with 3:1 pot odds, this hand doesn't play well postflop against two opponents. I'm chip leader with 19K, no need to get involved in marginal spots. Folding saves 250 chips.

Hand 143/150, in BB with 4650 stack (46BB). Holding J9h suited, a speculative hand with straight and flush potential. Pot 750, calling 200 more (total 300 committed) into what will be 950 pot offers excellent pot odds of ~4.75:1. Minni raised from early position (UTG+1), Flash flat-called from button (likely wide range). Claude folded SB. Defending BB wide here due to position, odds, and implied odds if we hit. Multiway pots suit connectors well. Folding too tight late; calling maximizes EV without overcommitting stack. Raise unnecessary as hand plays well postflop multiway.

Hand 143/150, very late in tournament with short stacks around (mine 44BB). Flopped bottom pair (9s) + open-ended straight draw (8 or K makes straight) on Tc Qc 9c flop, a highly coordinated monotone club board. This is a monster multiway: ~30-40% equity vs strong ranges due to draw + showdown value. Acting first postflop in BB vs Button (Flash, loose caller) and UTG raiser (Minni, stronger range). Lead betting extracts value from weaker pairs (e.g., Jx, Tx, overcards with draws), charges flush/straight draws heavily, and protects against villain turns. Check risks free cards to nutted flushes or runner-runner bombs. Sizing 2/3 pot (600 into 950) balances value/protection, invites calls from worse, discourages pure air. Builds pot for ICM when ahead late tournament; folding equity thins field if raised.

Preflop I opened A♣7♥ to 300 from UTG+1. Flop is T♣ Q♣ 9♣ and Elon (BB) leads for 600 into a 1550 pot. I don’t have a made hand yet, but I hold the A♣ which is a hugely important blocker and I have a very strong single-card nut-flush draw (there are 9 clubs left). Calling 600 would give me ~21.8% pot odds to see further streets, but my true equity is substantially higher (9 club outs to the turn plus backdoor pair/ace outs — ~35%+ to make a winning hand by river against many hands). Raising here is the right play: a min‑raise to 1200 (total bet 1200) generates fold equity vs single-pair and overcard holdings, isolates the bettor, and builds the pot when I have the best redraw/blocking card. If Elon jams I have good equity vs his shove range and can call; if he calls and a club arrives I can barrel for value, if the turn bricks I can pot‑control or proceed cautiously depending on action. I’ll raise to apply pressure and maximize EV with this strong blocker + draw.

The flop of Tc Qc 9c is a nightmare for pocket fours. Not only is it a monotone board where any club higher than the 4c (which is all of them) beats me if a flush completes, but the board is also heavily connected for straights (KJ, J8). Elon leading into two players and Minni raising him signals extreme strength from both opponents, likely representing made flushes, straights, or sets. My pair of fours has almost no equity here, and even my 4c is the worst possible draw. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

Late tournament (hand 143/150), effective 38BB deep. Flopped bottom pair + OESD (8 straight outs: 4x8, 4xK; ~32% equity standalone) + backdoor FD on monotone flop. Led for value/protection, Minni 2x raise small (1200 total into ~1550), Flash folds. Pot odds massive: 600 to call into 3350 (~5.6:1 direct, need ~18%). Equity >> vs Minni's UTG range (flushes, sets, TP/two, combo draws AxKx clubs, overpairs); crush worse, live vs nuts. Raising commits ~62% stack (min 1800, potty), fold equity low vs strong range. Call realizes equity OOP, keeps pot manageable (turn pot ~3350, SPR~1 with my 3250 behind), re-eval (implied if hit, villain often barred turn). ICM favors not jamming marginal but defend wide here. Standard call maximizes EV.

Turn 8c completes my straight (Q-J-T-9-8), giving the second-nut straight, but critically completes the flush on a four-club board (Tc Qc 9c 8c), making any two clubs a flush. Minni's UTG preflop raise followed by a raise over my flop lead on a monotone board strongly polarizes her range to nutted hands: made flushes (A/K/Q-clubs), sets (TT/QQ/99), or combo draws that hit (e.g., KJcc). My hand is crushed by flushes and K-high straights (KQJT9), live only vs bare lower pairs/straights or bluffs (unlikely from her). Pot 3350 vs my 3250 stack creates SPR <1. Leading OOP risks jamming into nuts and getting snapped or check-raised off equity; no fold equity vs her strong range. Checking controls pot size OOP, potentially wins at showdown if she has QQ/TT overpair or checks villainously, induces bluffs/mistakes late tournament. If bet/jam (likely), massive pot odds (~1:1) warrant hero-call with ~33% equity needed but realistic vs polarized range including value bluffs/overpairs. ICM late (8 hands left), but dead money in pot justifies realizing equity. Check maximizes EV without initiative loss.

Board T♣ Q♣ 9♣ 8♣; I hold A♣7♥ — that is the nut (ace‑high) flush on the turn. The only realistic hands that can beat me are straight‑flush combinations that use J♣ (or very specific two‑club combos), which are possible but very rare. Elon’s line (BB lead 600 on the flop, called my raise) is consistent with club draws, single‑pair (Q/T/9) or two‑club flushes; his check on the turn hands me the initiative to charge. I want to extract value from worse flushes, club combos and single pairs and to deny a free river that could change dynamics, while knowing I can call any shove with this nut. I’ll bet a sizable value bet — 3000 (≈ pot) — to build the pot and get thin calls or shoves from his mid‑strength holdings. If he jams I will call (only the extremely unlikely straight‑flush combos beat me); if he calls and we see a river I’ll continue extracting on blank rivers and re‑evaluate only if the river specifically completes obvious straight‑flush lines (e.g. a J♣).

Facing a 3000 bet into 6350 pot on a four-club board with my second-nut straight (8-9-T-Q-J). Minni's UTG+1 preflop raise, flop raise over my lead on monotone board, and now turn donk bet after check polarizes her to nut flushes (A/K clubs), K-high straights (KQ/KT etc.), or sets/full houses. My hand is crushed by flushes and higher straights, live only vs bluffs/overpairs unlikely from her strong history. Pot odds ~24% equity needed to call 3000 (leaving 250, committed river), but realistic equity <20% vs her range. Shove min-raise to 3250 has negligible fold equity vs nuts. Late tournament (143/150, 32BB effective), ICM favors folding strong-but-second-best hands to preserve stack for bubble/final table push vs shorter stacks (Pro 65BB? Wait stacks vary). Realizing no equity post-river maximizes survival/EV.

Q♦8♣ is a weak, offsuit queen in first-to-act (UTG) position. With three players left to act behind me and two deeper stacks that open-raise frequently, this hand is not strong enough to open or limp profitably — limp invites raises and playing OOP multiway is negative EV. I’ll conserve my 16.4k stack for better spots and stronger holdings in position. Fold.

8-4 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand. From UTG+1, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot, especially with several players remaining to act and the tournament approaching its final hands. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

On the button with 75o, two folds in front. This is a marginal steal spot - 75o is at the bottom of a button opening range, but with position and only the blinds left to act, a steal attempt is reasonable. I have the largest stack and can apply pressure. Standard 2.5x open to try to take the blinds.

Preflop in SB with 87o facing a button raise to 2.5BB (250). My hand is offsuit connectors with mediocre playability. Effective stack 32BB. Button range is wide but calling OOP is poor due to position disadvantage and reverse implied odds. BB still to act. Pot odds ~2:1 direct but folding is standard GTO to avoid tough postflop spots. Preserve stack late in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 3s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button (Claude) to 2.5bb, I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win 400), requiring about 27% equity. However, 23o has very poor equity and playability, especially out of position. With only 6 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack of 63bb, there is no incentive to defend such a marginal hand and bleed chips. The standard and correct play is to fold.

With pocket threes in a 5-handed game at UTG, this is a standard opening range hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially see a flop or take down the blinds and antes. Given we are near the end of the tournament (hand 145 of 150), maintaining pressure is key.
Holding 2h7d - one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a UTG raise of 300, this is a clear fold. No playability, no high cards, not suited. Easy fold.

K4o is a marginal offsuit king-high hand with a poor kicker. Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who likely has a strong range, and with SB and BB yet to act (both deep-stacked), calling risks multiway pots where the hand plays poorly postflop. My 32BB stack is playable but not desperate; folding preserves chips for better spots near the tournament's end (hand 145/150). Raising is off-table due to hand strength. Standard fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o), a very weak hand. The player in UTG (Flash) has raised to 300. Facing an UTG raise, which typically indicates a strong range, and being out of position, Q2o is nowhere near playable. The pot odds do not justify a call, and the reverse implied odds are high. With 63 big blinds, I have plenty of chips to wait for a better spot. Folding is the only standard play here.

I’m in the big blind with 8c8d facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450). To call I need to put in 200 more (300 total) which means I need ~200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8% equity to break even. With deep stacks (~160bb) pocket eights have good implied odds for set-mining and enough equity vs a typical UTG opening range to justify a straight call. 3-betting is an option, but a small/min raise to 500 is too weak to meaningfully fold out his range, and a larger 3-bet just builds a big pot OOP and risks a 4-bet from a tighter UTG range — not ideal with a medium pair. Plan: call to see a flop, look to extract if we hit a set or favorable board; if we miss and face heavy action I’ll give it up or proceed cautiously as a blocker/short-term bluff-catcher. Action: call 300 total.

Board is A♦ 5♠ 4♠ and I hold 8♣8♦ — a medium pocket pair that missed the Ace and the spade draw. Flash opened UTG preflop, so his range contains many A‑x hands and overpairs that will continue on this flop. With deep stacks and being out of position I don’t want to bloating the pot with a marginal hand; 88 has decent showdown value versus bluffs and some broadway holdings, and I can call reasonable continuation bets to see a turn or fold to heavy pressure. Plan: control the pot and induce bluffs or loose c‑bets by checking. Action: check (0).

I am the pre-flop aggressor and an Ace-high board is generally very favorable for my perceived range. Although my pocket threes are weak, especially with the straight and flush possibilities on the board, a continuation bet is standard here to fold out hands that missed the flop entirely (like K-Q, K-J, or small pairs). I have the 3 of spades as a minor blocker to some spade draws and an inside straight draw to a 2. I will place a small c-bet of approximately 38% of the pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I am called.

UTG (Flash) opened and continuation‑bet small (250 into 900) on A♦5♠4♠. I hold 8♣8♦ — a medium pocket pair that missed the Ace and the spade draw but has good showdown value vs bluffs and worse pairs. The c‑bet is small (cheap to call) and UTG’s range includes a lot of A‑x and some air; raising OOP with a medium pair is risky and will often bloat the pot or get me 4‑bet/folded by better hands. Calling 250 controls the pot, lets me realize equity and see a turn for little cost; I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (proceed if a blank or check back, fold to heavy turn/river pressure unless I improve to a set or face clear bluffs).

Board A♦ 5♠ 4♠ 6♥, I hold 8♣8♦. I called the small UTG open and the small flop c‑bet, so my line has been pot‑controlling. My eights are a medium pair with decent showdown value vs bluffs and worse broadways, but they are behind any A‑x or better pair and vulnerable OOP. The turn 6♥ is mostly a blank — it doesn’t complete obvious straights or the spade draw — so there’s no compelling reason to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Betting here would often fold out worse hands I want to see (or commit me to a bigger pot where I’m behind to many of his continuing hands) and could get raised by better hands. I’ll keep the pot under control and see how Flash reacts: check and call reasonable turn bets, fold to large raises or overbets unless I improve to a set. If he checks back I go to river with showdown value.

The turn 6h has improved my hand significantly by giving me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 2 or 7) to go along with my pair of threes. Minni has checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. Given the Ace on the board, Minni's flop-calling range is likely centered around Ace-x hands or flush draws. Since she is unlikely to fold an Ace to a second barrel and I have a high-equity draw, checking back to realize my equity for free is the most strategic play. This allows me to potentially hit my straight on the river without having to navigate a check-raise on the turn.

River: board A♦ 4♠ 5♠ 6♥ 6♠, my hand 8♣8♦. I now have two pair (8s & 6s) — a decent but vulnerable holding. Flash opened UTG and c‑bet small on the A♣4♠5♠ flop, then checked the turn; that line is heavy in A‑x and scattered spade combos (both of which now beat me: A‑x makes A+6 two pair, any 6 makes trips/full house, and any two spades in hand make the completed spade flush). A river bet here would often be called by the very A‑x and spade hands that beat me and will fold out the weaker hands I actually beat, so a value bet is thin and multiway extraction unlikely. With a large proportion of the raiser’s range still beating me and the pot relatively small (1150) versus my deep stack, the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and go to showdown. If Flash now decides to bet, I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing but will give weight to calling medium bets with two pair and folding only to extreme overbets when the line indicates trips/fullhouse/flush.

The board has run out with a pair of sixes and three spades. My pocket threes are now two pair (6s and 3s) with an Ace kicker. However, this is a very weak hand given the board texture. Minni's line of calling pre-flop and on the flop, then checking the turn and river, suggests she likely has a marginal made hand like a weak Ace or a middle pair (4x, 5x) that she is looking to take to showdown. Since I checked the turn, my perceived range is somewhat capped. A bluff here is risky because an Ace is unlikely to fold, and I have minimal showdown value. Checking back is the safest play to see if my small pair is somehow good against missed straight draws.

K2s from UTG is too weak to open. While suited kings have some playability, K2s is at the bottom of that range and UTG requires a tighter opening range. With 5 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack of 19k, no need to play marginal hands from early position.

9h2d is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no playability from UTG+1 position. Multiple strong opponents behind, including deep-stacked blinds and button. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 offer minimal equity against likely calling ranges. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and my stack at 32BB, folding preserves chips for premium spots to maximize stack.

I am on the Button with [3h 8h] (Eight-Three suited). The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds, 83s is a very weak hand, sitting well outside a standard opening range even for a loose aggressive strategy. The gap between the cards reduces connectivity, and the high card strength is low. With active players in the blinds (Minni and Flash have large stacks and defend often), opening here is likely negative EV. Given there are only 4 hands left in the tournament and my stack is healthy (63 BB), there is no desperate need to force action with sub-par holdings. I will fold.

I’m in the small blind with A♠9♠ and everyone folded to me — only the big blind (Flash) remains. This is a strong, playable hand in short-handed play and I want to take the initiative rather than limp/call out of position. I’ll open to 300 (3x the big blind) — large enough to get fold equity and isolate the BB, but small relative to our deep stack. If Flash folds we scoop the 150. If called, I’ll C-bet on dry/A-high flops, and be prepared to fold or proceed cautiously if I face heavy resistance or a large 3‑bet; I’ll evaluate further based on sizing and board texture.

With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and holding one of the weakest possible starting hands (7-3 offsuit), there is no strategic reason to defend the big blind against a raise from Minni. My priority is to preserve my stack and maintain my current standing in the final few hands. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

AcTc is a strong suited ace hand from UTG in 5-handed play, especially with a 32BB stack. It plays well post-flop and has good equity against calling ranges. Standard open-raise size of 3x the BB to build the pot or take down blinds uncontested. Near the end of the tournament (hand 147/150), but no need to deviate from optimal play. Minni, Flash, and Claude are deep-stacked, Pro has 63BB; raising applies pressure while keeping fold equity high.

Current hand is 147/150. I hold 7d 6h (off-suit connector) in UTG+1. Elon (UTG) opened to 300 (3BB). 76o is a weak hand that plays poorly against an UTG opening range. With only 3 hands left after this, preserving my 63BB stack is better than entering a pot with a marginal hand and a positional disadvantage against the opener. Cold calling is -EV, and 3-betting as a bluff is risky against a 29BB stack that might shove. Folding is the correct standard play.

Elon opened UTG to 300 (about 3bb) and still has only ~29bb — his raise is likely a reasonably strong opening range. I’m on the button with 2♣8♥, one of the worst starting hands: offsuit, unconnected, very low playability and frequently dominated. Calling 300 into a 450 pot would put me in a marginal multiway pot with the SB/BB yet to act and give me essentially no realizable equity. A 3‑bet bluff with 2‑8o is a poor spot (easy to get isolated or 4‑bet by deeper stacks). I have a large stack (≈171bb) and should preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Queen suited, a very strong hand in a 5-handed game. Elon, who has a relatively short stack of 2900 (29 BBs), has raised to 300 from under-the-gun. Given the hand count (147 of 150) and the stack sizes, I want to isolate the short-stacked opener and potentially end the hand pre-flop or go heads-up. Calling would invite the Big Blind (Claude) into the pot and leave me playing out of position. A 3-bet to 900 puts pressure on Elon's remaining stack while remaining a standard sizing that allows me to comfortably call a shove if he decides to commit his remaining chips.
Q4o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. Flash 3-bet over Elon's UTG open, indicating strength. Q4o has poor playability and is likely dominated. Clear fold despite the pot odds.

AcTc is a premium suited ace hand with excellent playability, straight potential, flush blocker, and strong equity against Flash's SB 3-bet range, which is often polarized (value like QQ+, AK or bluffs). Pot odds are favorable: calling 600 into 1300 gives ~3.5:1 implied odds, requiring only ~22% equity, which AcTc exceeds vs both value and bluffing ranges. Folding is too tight late in tournament with 29BB stack and only 4 hands left; we want to realize equity. 4-betting OOP risks bloating pot with deep-stacked opponent (132x BB), committing us awkwardly post-flop out of position. Calling keeps pot manageable, allows flop realization, and exploits SB's wide range while preserving fold equity for later streets if needed. Maximizes EV without unnecessary risk.

I have an open-ended straight draw and two overcards on a Jack-high board after 3-betting pre-flop. The board (J-T-6) is quite good for my perceived range. Elon called my 3-bet and is now playing with a very short stack relative to the pot (SPR of approximately 1.2). With a pot of 1900 and Elon having 2300 left, I have significant equity against almost his entire range (roughly 45-50% against a Jack) and am only in bad shape against a set. I will lead out with a half-pot bet to exert pressure on his remaining chips and potentially get a fold from hands like small pairs or Ace-high. If he shoves, I have the necessary pot odds and equity to call.

AcTc flops middle pair (Tens) with top kicker (Ace) on a Jd Ts 6c board, giving us strong showdown value and blocker to top pair hands like AJ, AT. Flash's SB 3-bet range is polarized (strong hands like JJ+, AJs+, sets, or bluffs like suited connectors, Axs), and he c-bets frequently on this coordinated board (straight draws like Q9, 98, 87 possible). Pot is 2850, bet 950 (~1/3 pot), leaving SPR ~0.35 after calling with our 23BB stack. Calling OOP short-stacked risks tough turn decisions facing further aggression. Raising exploits his wide range: gets value from weaker pairs/draws (worse Tx, Jx, Ax), protects against draws (e.g., Q9s, 98s, KQ), and has high fold equity vs air/bluffs. Shoving 2300 (~0.8 pot + to-call) is ideal late in tournament (hand 147/150) with short stack: maximizes fold equity while realizing equity when called (~35-45% vs typical calling range like JJ+, AJs, TJs). Avoids multi-street OOP play; builds stack or ends hand favorably.

Elon has shoved his remaining stack for a total of 2300 on the flop. The pot is currently 5150, and I need to call 1350 more. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.8:1, meaning I need roughly 21% equity to make a profitable call. With an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any Nine or Ace), I have approximately a 32% chance of completing my straight by the river. Even if Elon has a strong hand like top pair (AJ or KJ) or even a set, my straight draw alone provides more than enough equity to justify the call. Therefore, I am mathematically committed to the pot and will call.

I'm UTG in a 4-handed game with A♠3♦. In 4-max this is a hand I want to open — it plays well as the bettor (can take the pot down, get value from worse Aces, and isolate the short-stacked BB). I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300 total) to apply pressure on the Button/SB and to deny a free showdown to the short Pro in the big blind. 300 is big enough to generate fold equity but small relative to my deep stack so I don't overcommit if somebody 3-bets or the short stack shoves; I'll re-evaluate if either opponent gives strong resistance.

With only three hands remaining in the tournament, I need to be cautious about which pots I enter, especially with marginal hands. I am currently on the button with K6 offsuit. Minni, who has been very active and aggressive throughout the session, has raised to 300 from under-the-gun. While K6o has some high card value, it is easily dominated by Minni's opening range and does not play well post-flop in a raised pot. Additionally, Claude (SB) and Pro (BB) are still behind me, with Pro holding a relatively short stack that might look to shove. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary chip loss.

4h8h is a weak suited hand. Facing an UTG raise from Minni (who has been active but tends to have real hands from early position), this hand doesn't have enough playability to defend. The suited nature gives some flush draw potential but 48s is too weak to call profitably here. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7c 2c, which is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG. The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 450, requiring about 30.7% equity. Against a reasonable UTG range, 72s has approximately 29-30% raw equity, which is borderline, but my equity realization will be very poor playing out of position with such a weak hand. Given that there are only 3 hands left in the tournament and I am the short stack, I cannot afford to bleed chips in negative expectation spots. Folding is the clear correct decision.

With only two hands remaining in the tournament, I am currently UTG with a very weak hand (2h 5d). Given the stack sizes and the late stage of the tournament, there is no strategic reason to open or call with such a poor hand. Folding preserves my stack for the final hand.

I have 8-2 offsuit on the button - one of the worst hands in poker. While I'm in a good stealing position with only the blinds left, 82o is below my opening range even from the button. Minni has been aggressive with 3-bets throughout this tournament, and risking chips with such a weak hand with only 2 hands remaining isn't worth it. I have a healthy stack at 19,075, so no need to take unnecessary risks.

Current Hand 149/150. Four active players. I am in the Small Blind with Q7o (Queen-Seven offsuit). Action has folded to me. My stack is 6175 (shortest), while the BB (Minni) has 17175. Q7o is a marginal hand, generally near the bottom of a stealing range in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Given that Minni is a large stack and has shown a tendency to defend her blinds (e.g., Hand 16, Hand 141), opening this hand creates a high risk of playing a bloated pot out of position with a weak holding. Furthermore, since the button moves to the left, I will be on the Button for the final hand (Hand 150), which is a much more advantageous position to commit chips. Preserving my stack to utilize the positional advantage in the final hand is the superior strategic play compared to forcing a marginal spot now. Therefore, I fold.
Last hand of the tournament. I'm UTG with 8c4s - a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity. I have a solid stack of 19,075 chips. No reason to open this trash hand from early position. Easy fold.

It is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). I am on the Button with A6o, a marginal hand. I am currently in 4th place with ~6,100 chips, while the other three players have >17,000, meaning I cannot improve my rank regardless of the outcome of this hand. The players in the blinds (Minni and Flash) are separated by only 50 chips and are fighting for position, which may lead to them playing more aggressively or unpredictably to secure rank. Given my hand is merely an off-suit Ace with a weak kicker, and considering the specific dynamics where the blinds have strong incentives to play, the marginal expected value of stealing is outweighed by the risk of losing chips. Folding locks in my current stack size.

I'm in the small blind with 5♣4♦. UTG and the button have folded, so I'm heads-up vs Flash in the big blind (100). I've already posted 50; calling costs 50 more (100 total). With the pot at 150, calling 50 to see a flop gives very favorable pot odds and is +EV with a speculative hand heads-up. Flash defends a fairly wide range, so a min-raise to 200 would bloat the pot with a weak offsuit hand and likely get called; I prefer to see a cheap flop and proceed only with real equity (pair, two pair, good draw) or otherwise fold to heavy action. I will call to see the flop and evaluate on texture and opponent reaction.

This is the final hand of the tournament (Hand #150). I am in the Big Blind with 5-2 of diamonds. Minni has limped from the Small Blind. I am currently in 2nd place overall, trailing Claude by approximately 1,750 chips. Since Claude has already folded this hand, the only way to overtake him for 1st place would be to win a significant pot here, but with a speculative hand like 5-2 suited and only 200 chips in the pot, a check is the most prudent and standard play to see a flop for free. Raising here would introduce unnecessary risk without a high probability of swinging the lead.

I have 5♣4♦ on K♣ J♦ 3♠ — I completely missed. There are no real draws: no backdoor flush (even two more clubs on turn+river still leaves only four clubs total for me), and the only straight route is a remote two‑card runner (need both a 2 and a 6). I do have ~24% chance to pair a 4 or 5 by the river, but those low pairs will usually be behind against Flash’s defending range (Kx, Jx, pocket pairs, broadways). Pot is 200 and Flash checked the flop; I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot and plan to fold to a standard continuation bet (especially anything >~1/3 pot). If he checks back I’ll see a cheap turn and reassess only if I improve.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am currently in 2nd place with 17,325 chips, trailing Claude (who has 19,075 and has already folded) by 1,750 chips. To take 1st place, I would need to increase my stack by more than 1,750 chips in this single hand. The current pot is only 200 chips. With 5-high on a King-Jack-Trey board, I have no made hand and very little potential. While I need to win a large pot to overtake Claude, attempting to build a nearly 2,000-chip pot from a 200-chip start with this hand is extremely risky and likely to result in me losing 2nd place to Minni (who is only 100 chips behind me) rather than gaining 1st. I will check and see if a miracle turn card improves my situation.

I have 5♣4♦ on K♣ J♦ 3♠ J♥ — I completely missed. No real draws (flush impossible, straight remote), and the turn pairing the board makes trips (Jx) or two‑pair (Kx) much more likely in Flash’s range. Pot is only 200 while stacks are ~17k each, so any meaningful bet from him on this street or the river would be priced to fold with nothing. Flash checked the flop and checked back the turn; that can be weakness but can also be slowplay with a Jack or a K. Leading as a bluff here is low EV — many hands that call a small bet (Kx, Qx, Ax, many broadways) still beat me by the river or call down. I’ll check to control the pot and save chips; plan is to fold to any realistic bet and only continue if I pair/improve on the river or he gives a very weak, tiny bet that’s easy to exploit.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am currently in 2nd place with 17,325 chips. Claude is the leader with 19,075 chips and has already folded, meaning I need to gain at least 1,751 chips to take 1st place. The current pot is only 200 chips. Minni, who is in 3rd place, is only 100 chips behind me. With a board of K-J-3-J and my 5-high, I have no hand and almost no equity. Attempting to bluff or build a pot large enough to overtake Claude is extremely risky and far more likely to result in me losing 2nd place to Minni than it is to secure 1st. My priority is to protect my current position. I will check to see the river for free.

River summary: I have 5♣4♦ on Kc Jd 3s Jh 6h — no pair, no draw. Pot is 200 and both players are deep (~17k stacks). Flash checked the flop and turn, showing passivity (could be missed broadways, weak Kx, or a slowplayed Jx). Betting river as a bluff has very thin fold equity — many hands that will fold (A‑high, some Qx) are marginal, while hands that beat me (Jx, Kx) or will call for showdown (Qx, Tx, small pairs) are common and will often call a standard river bet. With deep stacks and such a dry board, risking chips for a low chance to steal is negative EV. I’ll check and go to showdown; if Flash bets into me I will fold to any realistic bet size (only consider calling an absurdly tiny bet).

This is the final hand of the tournament. My current stack is 17,325, trailing Claude (the leader at 19,075) by 1,750 chips. Claude has already folded. To overtake him, I would need to increase my stack by at least 1,751 chips. The current pot is only 200 chips. With 5-high on a board of K-J-3-J-6, I have no showdown value. To win enough chips from Minni to pass Claude, I would have to make a large bet, hope she calls with a worse hand, and win. Since it is nearly impossible for 5-high to be the best hand here, my focus shifts to maintaining my current position. Minni is only 100 chips behind me. Checking is the safest option. If I win at showdown, I secure 2nd place. If I lose, I finish 3rd. Bluffing into a small pot provides no path to 1st place and risks unnecessary losses.
